topics.title: Generalized Co-ordinates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generalized Co-ordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Coordinates** are defined as a *minimal set of independent scalar variables* $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ whose values *uniquely specify the configuration* (position and orientation) of a mechanical system, such as a robot manipulator, at any instant of time.

Mathematically, the configuration of the robot is represented as a point in an $n$-dimensional space called the **Configuration Space** or **Joint Space**, denoted by:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

The integer $n$ is the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the robot and represents the *minimum number of generalized coordinates* required to describe the motion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Arm as a Robot**
> 
> Think of how you reach out to grab a coffee mug. Your brain does not consciously compute the $x, y, z$ coordinates of your palm in 3D space. Instead, it commands your **shoulder angle**, **elbow angle**, and **wrist angle**. These three independent joint angles are the *generalized coordinates* of your arm. Each unique combination of these three angles corresponds to exactly one configuration of your hand. This is the essence of generalized coordinates in robotics: we describe complex spatial motion through the *natural, independent control variables* of the system.

### 1.3 Why Generalized Coordinates Matter in Robotics

- They are the **natural variables** for control engineers, because actuators (motors) are mounted at the joints.
- They decouple the mathematical description from the absolute Cartesian world.
- They reduce the number of variables by eliminating *redundant* or *constrained* motion.
- The standard for robot dynamics (Lagrangian and Newton–Euler formulations) is built upon them.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention Used in KTU Robotics:**
> - Generalized coordinate symbol: $q_i$ where $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$
> - Generalized velocity: $\dot{q}_i = \dfrac{dq_i}{dt}$
> - Generalized acceleration: $\ddot{q}_i = \dfrac{d^2 q_i}{dt^2}$
> - Generalized force/torque: $\tau_i$ (associated with $q_i$)
> - Number of DOF of a serial chain with $n$ joints: typically **$n$** (one DOF per joint).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Conceptual Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Identify All Possible Motions:** List every joint variable present in the mechanical system. For a 6-joint serial manipulator, this yields 6 variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_6$ (or $d_i$ for prismatic joints).
- **Step 2 — Identify Constraints:** Check whether the variables are *independent*. A *holonomic constraint* of the form $f(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n) = 0$ reduces the count of independent variables.
- **Step 3 — Determine the Independent Set:** The remaining independent variables form the **generalized coordinate set**. Their number equals the *Degrees of Freedom (DOF)*.
- **Step 4 — Validate Uniqueness:** Verify that for every value of $q$, there is **one and only one** physical configuration of the robot. If multiple configurations map to the same $q$, the description is incomplete.

### 2.2 Types of Coordinates in Robotics

| Coordinate Type | Symbol | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generalized (Joint) Coordinate | $q_i$ | Independent joint-level variable | Dynamics, Control, Simulation |
| Cartesian (Operational) Coordinate | $X, Y, Z, \phi, \theta, \psi$ | Task-space pose of end-effector | Path planning, Trajectory |
| Dependent Coordinate | $q_d$ | Related to others by a constraint equation | Eliminated during formulation |
| Redundant Coordinate | — | Extra variable beyond DOF | Used in Lagrangian formulation |

### 2.3 Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Constraints

- **Holonomic Constraint:** Expressible as $f(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n, t) = 0$. Reduces the count of *independent* generalized coordinates. Example: A 4-bar closed linkage where $l_1 + l_2 - l_3 - l_4 = 0$.
- **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** Involves velocities; cannot be integrated to a position-level equation. Example: The rolling-without-slipping condition of a car wheel: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. Non-holonomic constraints *do not reduce* the number of generalized coordinates; they only restrict the *admissible velocities*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Generalized coordinate vector | $q = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$ | $n \times 1$ configuration vector |
| Number of DOF (Kutzbach/Grübler) | $DOF = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | $N$ = links, $J_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $J_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| DOF (planar mechanism) | $DOF = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | Planar Grübler formula |
| Generalized velocity | $\dot{q} = dq/dt$ | First time derivative of $q$ |
| Generalized acceleration | $\ddot{q} = d^2 q / dt^2$ | Second time derivative of $q$ |
| Holonomic constraint count reduction | $n_{indep} = n_{total} - n_{holonomic}$ | Independent coordinates |
| Position of end-effector | $X = f(q)$ | Forward kinematics mapping |
| Joint–task Jacobian relation | $\dot{X} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Velocity mapping between spaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule for KTU Exams:** For a simple open-chain serial manipulator with $n$ single-DOF joints, the number of generalized coordinates is **always equal to $n$** because there are no closed-loop constraints.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Robot Control:** Generalized coordinates form the state vector for the joint-space PD, PID, and computed-torque controllers.
- **Dynamic Modeling:** The Lagrangian $L = T - V$ is always expressed in terms of $q$ and $\dot{q}$, leading to the Euler–Lagrange equation:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$
- **Trajectory Planning:** Polynomial interpolation (cubic, quintic) is performed on $q(t)$ directly to ensure smooth joint motion.
- **Singularity Analysis:** The Jacobian determinant $\det J(q) = 0$ identifies *losing generalized coordinates* (loss of DOF) at singular configurations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a Planar Mechanism (Kutzbach Criterion)

Consider a planar mechanism with $N$ rigid links (including the ground link), $J_1$ lower pairs (revolute and prismatic), and $J_2$ higher pairs (e.g., cam–follower).

**Step 1 — Total Unconstrained DOF in the Plane**

In a 2D plane, every rigid body has 3 degrees of freedom (translation in $x$, translation in $y$, and rotation $\theta$ about the $z$-axis). Excluding the ground (which is fixed), the total unconstrained DOF is:

$$F_{total} = 3(N - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Constraints Introduced by Joints**

- A **Class 1 lower pair** (1-DOF joint: revolute or prismatic) removes 2 degrees of freedom.
- A **Class 2 higher pair** (e.g., cam) removes 1 degree of freedom.

Hence the total number of removed DOF is:

$$F_{removed} = 2J_1 + J_2$$

**Step 3 — Net Available DOF (Generalized Coordinates Count)**

$$DOF = F_{total} - F_{removed} = 3(N - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2$$

This number equals the count of *independent* generalized coordinates.

### 3.2 Worked Example: 4-Bar Planar Linkage

A planar 4-bar linkage has $N = 4$ links, with $J_1 = 4$ revolute joints, and $J_2 = 0$.

$$DOF = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** The mechanism needs exactly **one** generalized coordinate (the input crank angle $\theta_1$) to describe its configuration fully.

### 3.3 Derivation: Joint-Space to Task-Space Velocity Mapping

Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^m$ be the task-space coordinate (end-effector pose) and $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ the joint-space generalized coordinate. The forward kinematics equation is:

$$X = f(q)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{dq}{dt}$$

Recognizing the partial derivative as the **Jacobian Matrix** $J(q)$:

$$J(q) = \frac{\partial f(q)}{\partial q} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$$

The velocity mapping becomes:

$$\dot{X} = J(q) \dot{q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This equation reveals that the joint velocities $\dot{q}$ (the time derivatives of generalized coordinates) are linearly mapped to the task-space velocities $\dot{X}$ via the configuration-dependent Jacobian matrix.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Common Robots to Generalized Coordinates

| Robot Type | $N$ (links) | $J_1$ | DOF | Generalized Coordinates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2R Planar Arm | 3 | 2 | 2 | $q_1, q_2$ (two revolute angles) |
| 3R Planar Arm | 4 | 3 | 3 | $q_1, q_2, q_3$ |
| RR Robot (2R vertical) | 3 | 2 | 2 | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |
| RRP Cylindrical | 4 | 3 | 3 | $\theta_1, d_2, d_3$ |
| SCARA (RRPR) | 5 | 4 | 4 | $\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3, \theta_4$ |
| 6R Articulated (PUMA) | 7 | 6 | 6 | $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ |
| Stanford Arm (RRPRRR) | 7 | 6 | 6 | $\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Physical System to Generalized Coordinates

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a physical robotic mechanism is reduced to its mathematical description using generalized coordinates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Robot Mechanism] --> B[Identify All Joint Variables]
    B --> C{Holonomic Constraints Present?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Constraint Equations]
    C -- No --> E[Direct Listing of Variables]
    D --> F[Eliminate Dependent Variables]
    E --> G[Form Generalized Coordinate Vector q]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Configuration Space Rn]
    H --> I[Compute DOF using Kutzbach Formula]
    I --> J[Form Lagrangian L T minus V in q and dq by dt]
    J --> K[Derive Equations of Motion via Euler Lagrange]
    K --> L[Generalized Forces tau as Control Inputs]
```

### 4.2 Joint Space vs Task Space Mapping

This block diagram shows the role of generalized coordinates in bridging joint-level actuator commands with end-effector motion in Cartesian space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Joint Actuator Commands] --> B[Generalized Coordinates q1 to qn]
    B --> C[Forward Kinematics f of q]
    C --> D[End Effector Pose in Task Space X Y Z phi theta psi]
    B --> E[Jacobian Matrix J of q]
    E --> F[Task Space Velocity dX by dt]
    F --> G[Trajectory Planner and Controller]
    G --> A
```

### 4.3 DOF Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Count Links N] --> B[Count 1-DOF Joints J1]
    A --> C[Count 2-DOF Joints J2]
    B --> D[Apply Kutzbach Formula]
    C --> D
    D --> E{DOF equals 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Structure: No Motion Possible]
    E -- No --> G[DOF equals 1]
    E -- No --> H[DOF greater than 1]
    G --> I[Single Generalized Coordinate q1]
    H --> J[Multiple Generalized Coordinates q1 to qn]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define generalized coordinates. How is the number of generalized coordinates related to the degrees of freedom of a robotic system?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Generalized coordinates are a *minimal set of independent scalar variables* $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ whose instantaneous values uniquely define the configuration (position and orientation) of every link of a mechanical system.
> 
> **Relation to DOF (1 Mark):** The number of generalized coordinates is equal to the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the system, provided the system is free of redundant or dependent coordinates. Mathematically, $n = DOF = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ for a planar mechanism.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with one example each in the context of robot manipulators.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> | Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
> |---|---|---|
> | Equation form | $f(q, t) = 0$ (position level) | Involves velocities, not integrable |
> | Effect on DOF | *Reduces* number of independent generalized coordinates | *Does not reduce* DOF, only restricts velocity |
> | Robot example | Closed-loop parallel manipulator | Wheeled mobile robot (rolling constraint) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of generalized coordinates with reference to a 2R planar robot manipulator. Identify the generalized coordinates, configuration space, and the corresponding forward kinematics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Kutzbach criterion, derive the degrees of freedom of (i) a 3R planar manipulator and (ii) a four-bar planar linkage. Comment on the number of generalized coordinates required in each case. **(7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
> 
> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> 
> *Conceptual Setup (2 Marks):* A 2R planar manipulator consists of two rigid links connected by two revolute joints mounted on a fixed base. The end-effector moves in the XY plane.
> 
> *Generalized Coordinates (2 Marks):* The two joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ form the generalized coordinate vector:
> 
> $$q = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> The configuration space is $\mathbb{R}^2$, and the DOF = 2.
> 
> *Forward Kinematics (3 Marks):* Using the link lengths $l_1, l_2$:
> 
> $$\begin{aligned}
> x &= l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
> y &= l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
> \end{aligned}$$
> 
> The end-effector pose vector is $X = [x, y]^T = f(q)$.
> 
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> 
> *(i) 3R Planar Manipulator (3 Marks):* Here $N = 4$ (ground + 3 links), $J_1 = 3$ revolute joints, $J_2 = 0$.
> 
> $$DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 0 = 9 - 6 = 3$$
> 
> Three generalized coordinates are needed: $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$.
> 
> *(ii) Four-Bar Linkage (3 Marks):* $N = 4$, $J_1 = 4$, $J_2 = 0$.
> 
> $$DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 9 - 8 = 1$$
> 
> Only one generalized coordinate (the driver crank angle) is required.
> 
> *Comment (1 Mark):* The count matches the intuitive observation that the 3R arm has 3 independent actuators, while the four-bar has only 1 independent input.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of generalized coordinates in robot dynamics formulation. Show how the Euler–Lagrange equation is expressed in terms of generalized coordinates and forces. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planar mechanism has 6 links and 7 single-DOF lower pairs. Determine its DOF and the minimum number of generalized coordinates. Justify whether the mechanism can be fully controlled with a single actuator. **(7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
> 
> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> 
> *Role in Dynamics (3 Marks):* Generalized coordinates $q$ are the natural variables for robot dynamics because:
> - Actuators apply forces/torques *at the joints* (generalized forces $\tau$).
> - The kinetic energy $T$ and potential energy $V$ are scalar functions of $q$ and $\dot{q}$.
> - The Lagrangian $L = T - V$ is a function of $(q, \dot{q}, t)$.
> 
> *Euler–Lagrange Equation (3 Marks):*
> 
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
> 
> where $\tau_i$ is the generalized force (torque for revolute, force for prismatic) conjugate to $q_i$.
> 
> *Block Diagram (1 Mark):*
> 
> ```mermaid
> flowchart LR
>     A[Generalized Coordinates q] --> B[Compute Kinetic Energy T]
>     A --> C[Compute Potential Energy V]
>     B --> D[Form Lagrangian L = T minus V]
>     C --> D
>     D --> E[Apply Euler Lagrange Equation]
>     E --> F[Generalized Forces tau as Output]
> ```
> 
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> 
> *DOF Calculation (4 Marks):* Given $N = 6$ and $J_1 = 7$, $J_2 = 0$.
> 
> $$DOF = 3(N - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2 = 3(5) - 2(7) = 15 - 14 = 1$$
> 
> *Generalized Coordinates (2 Marks):* The minimum number of generalized coordinates equals the DOF = **1**. A single coordinate (e.g., the input crank angle) suffices.
> 
> *Control Feasibility (1 Mark):* **Yes**, a single actuator is sufficient to fully control the mechanism because its DOF is 1. However, the end-effector trajectory is *kinematically constrained* by the linkage geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the *number of links* $N$ with the *number of moving links*. Always include the ground in $N$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting DOF = number of joints without checking for closed-loop constraints. Only open-chain serial arms follow this naive rule.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to subtract the unit of joint DOFs in the Kutzbach formula. A revolute joint removes *2*, not 1, degrees of freedom in the plane.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Stating that non-holonomic constraints reduce DOF — they do not.
> - **Marks typically lost:** 1–2 marks per mistake in derivation steps; 3 marks for skipping the constraint analysis in part (a) of Question A.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Generalized coordinates** are a *minimal* and *independent* set of variables specifying a robot's configuration.
- The count of generalized coordinates equals the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the mechanism.
- For a standard open-chain $n$-joint serial manipulator, the generalized coordinate vector is $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Kutzbach's planar formula:** $DOF = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper.
- **Holonomic constraints** reduce the count of independent coordinates; **non-holonomic constraints** do not.
- Forward kinematics maps $q \rightarrow X$ (joint space to task space).
- Velocity mapping: $\dot{X} = J(q)\dot{q}$ — the Jacobian $J(q)$ is configuration-dependent.
- The **Euler–Lagrange equation** in generalized form is the foundation of analytical robot dynamics.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det J(q) = 0$, causing a *loss of effective DOF* in the task space.
- Always state the configuration space dimension (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^n$) when defining generalized coordinates in exam answers.
- For KTU answer writing: clearly *box the final DOF value* and *list the generalized coordinate vector explicitly*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generalized Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of robotic manipulator dynamics, a **Generalized Force** is defined as the scalar (or vector) quantity $Q_i$ that is *conjugate* to a **generalized coordinate** $q_i$, such that the total virtual work done on a mechanical system can be expressed as:

$$
\delta W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta q_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PERAT523 Module 1):**
> The generalized force $Q_i$ corresponding to a translational joint displacement $\delta q_i$ has units of **Newton (N)**, while the generalized force corresponding to a rotational joint angle $\delta q_i$ has units of **Newton-meter (N·m)**, i.e., a torque. This dual interpretation is the cornerstone of unified manipulator analysis.

For an $n$-Degree-Of-Freedom (DOF) robotic manipulator with joint coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the vector of generalized forces is:

$$
\mathbf{Q} = [Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n
$$

and the joint-actuator torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is, by direct identification, the generalized force vector itself:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} \equiv \mathbf{Q}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Right Lever" Idea

Imagine you are pushing a heavy door open. The door is connected to a robotic arm. The handle is at the end of the arm. Now:

- If you push the handle **perpendicular to the door surface** (in the direction the door moves), 100% of your effort produces motion. The generalized force is at its **maximum** (equal to your push force).
- If you push the handle **along the door's surface** (a useless direction), zero percent of your effort moves the door. The generalized force is **zero**.

The generalized force is essentially a measure of *"how much of a real, Cartesian-world force/torque actually drives motion along the chosen coordinate axis."* A simple push on a non-rolling door rotates it about its hinge — so even though you apply a **linear force in Newtons**, the door cares only about the **torque in N·m** about the hinge axis. That torque is the generalized force conjugate to the angular coordinate of the door.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Name "Generalized"?**
> The term "generalized" exists because the same mathematical framework handles BOTH linear displacements (m) AND angular rotations (rad) as coordinates. The conjugate quantity $Q_i$ is automatically a force (N) for a translational coordinate and a torque (N·m) for a rotational coordinate — the framework "generalizes" across the two.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The standard dimensional relationships used throughout this module are:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Linear Position / Displacement | $x, \delta q$ | **m (meter)** |
| Angular Position / Displacement | $\theta, \delta q$ | **rad (radian)** |
| Cartesian Force | $\mathbf{F}$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Cartesian Moment | $\mathbf{M}$ | **N·m (Newton-meter)** |
| Generalized Force | $Q_i$ | **N** or **N·m** (depends on $q_i$) |
| Joint Torque | $\tau_i$ | **N·m** |
| Mass | $m$ | **kg** |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Quick-Recall:** In KTU board exams, you will save marks by stating *explicitly* the units of each generalized force you compute. Examiners mark for dimensional clarity in robot dynamics problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle: Virtual Work

The derivation of generalized forces is rooted in the **Principle of Virtual Work** (Lagrange's foundational postulate). For a system in static or quasi-static equilibrium:

$$
\delta W_{\text{total}} = 0
$$

For a robotic manipulator subjected to external forces, the total virtual work splits into two parts:

$$
\delta W = \delta W_{\text{applied}} + \delta W_{\text{constraint}} = 0
$$

Assuming **ideal, workless** constraints (the only real scenario in a rigid-link manipulator), all virtual work is done by the **applied generalized forces** $Q_i$ and the **actuator torques** $\tau_i$:

$$
\delta W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta q_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_i \, \delta q_i = 0
$$

Because the virtual displacements $\delta q_i$ are **independent** (the joints are independently actuated), each coefficient must vanish independently:

$$
\tau_i = Q_i \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

## 2.2 Why Generalized Forces Exist — The Core "Why"

- **Robot joints are independent**: Each $q_i$ can move without affecting the others' ability to move.
- **Cartesian world is coupled**: A single Cartesian force can produce motion in *multiple* joints simultaneously.
- **We need a one-to-one mapping**: Between *one* real-world force and *n* joint torques. The Jacobian transpose provides this mapping.

## 2.3 Construction of the Generalized Force Formula — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Define the position of any point $P$ on the manipulator in terms of joint coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{r}_P(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the differential position for a virtual displacement:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r}_P = \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{J}_P(\mathbf{q}) \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}_P \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **position Jacobian** of point $P$.

**Step 3:** The virtual work done by an external Cartesian force $\mathbf{F}_P$ applied at $P$:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{F}_P^T \delta \mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{F}_P^T \mathbf{J}_P \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify with the definition $\delta W = \mathbf{Q}^T \delta \mathbf{q}$:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}_P^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}_P}
$$

**Step 5 (6-DOF extension):** For a spatial manipulator where the end-effector can apply both a force $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ and a moment $\mathbf{M} \in \mathbb{R}^3$, the virtual work includes rotational contributions:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{M}^T \delta \boldsymbol{\phi}
$$

Stacking into a **wrench vector** $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{F}^T, \mathbf{M}^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^6$ and using the geometric Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{W}}
$$

This is the **fundamental force–torque transformation** in robotics.

## 2.4 Generalized Forces from a Potential Field

When forces arise from a **conservative potential** $V(\mathbf{q})$ (e.g., gravity, spring potential), the generalized forces are obtained as the **negative gradient** of the potential:

$$
Q_i = -\frac{\partial V(\mathbf{q})}{\partial q_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\mathbf{Q} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} V(\mathbf{q})}
$$

### 2.4.1 Gravity Loading (Standard KTU Problem)

For a manipulator with link masses $m_i$ and centers of mass at $\mathbf{r}_{C_i}(\mathbf{q})$, the gravity potential is:

$$
V_g(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{C_i}(\mathbf{q})
$$

with $\mathbf{g} = [0, -g, 0]^T$ (or $[0, 0, -g]^T$ depending on convention). The gravitational generalized force is:

$$
\mathbf{Q}_g = -\left[\frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_1}, \frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_n}\right]^T
$$

### 2.4.2 Spring Force at the End-Effector

If a spring of stiffness $k$ connects the end-effector to a fixed point $\mathbf{r}_0$:

$$
V_s(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} k \, (\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{r}_0)^T (\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{r}_0)
$$

The spring's generalized force is $\mathbf{Q}_s = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} V_s = -k \, \mathbf{J}^T (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}_0)$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam Day Lifesaver)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for Module 1 of PERAT523. Memorize the last two columns; they are tested in nearly every KTU board cycle.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Vector Form | Used For |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $Q_i = \mathbf{F}^T \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_i}$ | Definition | $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ | Single point force |
| 2 | $Q_i = \mathbf{M}^T \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\phi}}{\partial q_i}$ | Couple on body | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{M}$ | Pure moment |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{W}$ | Spatial wrench | $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ | 6-DOF end-effector |
| 4 | $Q_i = -\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Conservative force | $\mathbf{Q} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} V$ | Gravity / Springs |
| 5 | $V_g = \sum m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{C_i}$ | Gravity potential | — | Mass loading |
| 6 | $\mathbf{J}_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial q_j}$ | Jacobian entry | $\mathbf{J} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ | Velocity / Static force |
| 7 | $\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Cartesian force | Requires $\mathbf{J}^T$ invertible | Inverse statics |
| 8 | $P_{\text{diss}} = \mathbf{Q}_{\text{fric}}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Friction power | $Q_{\text{fric},i} = -b_i \dot{q}_i$ | Viscous damping |

> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (3) is the workhorse of **force control** in industrial robots (e.g., KUKA LWR, ABB YuMi). By commanding $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{cmd}} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{desired}}$ with force feedback from a wrist sensor, the controller makes the robot behave as a programmable **virtual spring** — the foundation of compliant assembly and safe human–robot collaboration.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Generalized Force from a Single Point Force

This is the most fundamental derivation of Module 1. We derive $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ from first principles.

**Given:**
- An $n$-DOF manipulator with generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- A point $P$ on the manipulator at position $\mathbf{r}_P(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^3$.
- An external Cartesian force $\mathbf{F}_P \in \mathbb{R}^3$ acting at $P$.

**To Find:** The generalized force vector $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

### Step 1: Position of Point P

The position vector of the point of force application is a smooth function of the joint coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{r}_P(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)
$$

For example, for a 2-link planar arm:

$$
\mathbf{r}_P = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Differential of the Position

Apply the chain rule of partial differentiation:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r}_P = \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_1} \delta q_1 + \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_2} \delta q_2 + \cdots + \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_n} \delta q_n
$$

In compact matrix form:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r}_P = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial q_n} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \delta q_1 \\ \delta q_2 \\ \vdots \\ \delta q_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

Defining the **Jacobian matrix**:

$$
\mathbf{J}_P(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_P}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}
$$

we obtain:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{J}_P(\mathbf{q}) \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

### Step 3: Virtual Work Done by the Force

By definition, the virtual work done by a Cartesian force is the dot product of force and virtual displacement:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{F}_P^T \, \delta \mathbf{r}_P
$$

Substituting the Jacobian expression:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{F}_P^T \, \mathbf{J}_P(\mathbf{q}) \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

Because $\mathbf{F}_P^T \mathbf{J}_P$ is a $1 \times n$ row vector and $\delta \mathbf{q}$ is an $n \times 1$ column vector, we can rewrite:

$$
\delta W = \left( \mathbf{J}_P^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}_P \right)^T \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

This uses the matrix identity $(\mathbf{A} \mathbf{B})^T = \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{A}^T$ applied to a scalar product, where the result is a $1 \times n$ row vector.

### Step 4: Identification with the Definition

By the very definition of generalized forces:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{Q}^T \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

Equating the two expressions (valid for all arbitrary $\delta \mathbf{q}$):

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}_P^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}_P}
$$

This is the **Jacobian Transpose Relationship** — a cornerstone result in robot dynamics, force control, and haptic rendering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Transpose"?** The transpose converts the *rows of the Jacobian* (which map joints to Cartesian directions) into *columns* (which map Cartesian forces to joint contributions). It is the natural dual operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Generalized Force for a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

This is a **high-frequency KTU problem** and demonstrates end-to-end computation.

**Given:**
- 2-DOF planar arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.
- Joint angles $q_1$ (shoulder) and $q_2$ (elbow).
- External force $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y, 0]^T$ at the end-effector.

**To Find:** The generalized forces $Q_1$ and $Q_2$.

### Step 1: End-Effector Position

From forward kinematics of the 2-link planar arm:

$$
x(q_1, q_2) = L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y(q_1, q_2) = L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

### Step 2: Compute Partial Derivatives

**Partial derivative of $x$ with respect to $q_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -L_1 \sin(q_1) - L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Partial derivative of $x$ with respect to $q_2$:**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Partial derivative of $y$ with respect to $q_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Partial derivative of $y$ with respect to $q_2$:**

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Jacobian

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Transpose the Jacobian

$$
\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Apply $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6: Final Componentwise Results

$$
Q_1 = F_x \left[-L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)\right] + F_y \left[L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

$$
Q_2 = F_x \left[-L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)\right] + F_y \left[L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ (KEE Exam Tip):**
> - $Q_1$ is the **shoulder torque** required to hold the arm against the external force.
> - $Q_2$ is the **elbow torque**.
> - Notice $Q_2$ depends only on the **relative angle** $(q_1 + q_2)$ — the elbow doesn't "know" the absolute shoulder angle. This decoupling is exploited in computed-torque controllers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Generalized Forces from a Potential Field (Gravity)

This is the standard approach for **gravity loading**, the most-tested conservative force in KTU papers.

**Given:**
- A 2-DOF planar arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$.
- Centers of mass at mid-link: $L_{C_1} = L_1/2$ and $L_{C_2} = L_2/2$.
- Gravity acts in $-y$ direction with magnitude $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1: Position of Each Center of Mass

$$
\mathbf{r}_{C_1} = \begin{bmatrix} L_{C_1} \cos q_1 \\ L_{C_1} \sin q_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_{C_2} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos q_1 + L_{C_2} \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \sin q_1 + L_{C_2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Construct the Gravity Potential

The gravity potential of a point mass at height $h$ is $V = m g h$. Since $y$ is the height coordinate and gravity points in $-y$:

$$
V_g = m_1 g \, y_{C_1} + m_2 g \, y_{C_2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_g = m_1 g \, L_{C_1} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \, \left[ L_1 \sin q_1 + L_{C_2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]
$$

### Step 3: Grouping Terms

$$
V_g = (m_1 L_{C_1} + m_2 L_1) \, g \, \sin q_1 + m_2 L_{C_2} \, g \, \sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

### Step 4: Compute Generalized Forces

**For $Q_1$:**

$$
Q_1 = -\frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_1} = -g \left[ (m_1 L_{C_1} + m_2 L_1) \cos q_1 + m_2 L_{C_2} \cos(q_1+q_2) \right]
$$

**For $Q_2$:**

$$
Q_2 = -\frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 L_{C_2} \, g \, \cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Warning (KTU Valuation):** Gravity in the $-y$ direction means $V = +m g y$ (higher $y$ = larger potential). Then $Q = -\partial V / \partial q$ gives a **negative** generalized force when the arm is horizontal and gravity tries to *decrease* the joint angle (in some conventions). Always draw a free-body diagram and check the sign by intuition.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — End-to-End Force Computation

**Problem:** A 2-DOF arm with $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m is at $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$. An external force $\mathbf{F} = [10, -5, 0]^T$ N is applied at the end-effector. Compute the joint torques.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Compute the Trigonometric Values

$$
\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \cos 30° = 0.8660, \quad \sin 45° = 0.7071, \quad \cos 45° = 0.7071
$$

$$
q_1 + q_2 = 75° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin 75° = 0.9659, \quad \cos 75° = 0.2588
$$

### Step 2: Build the Jacobian Numerically

Row 1 (x-components):

$$
J_{11} = -1 \cdot 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot 0.9659 = -0.5000 - 0.4830 = -0.9830
$$

$$
J_{12} = -0.5 \cdot 0.9659 = -0.4830
$$

Row 2 (y-components):

$$
J_{21} = 1 \cdot 0.8660 + 0.5 \cdot 0.2588 = 0.8660 + 0.1294 = 0.9954
$$

$$
J_{22} = 0.5 \cdot 0.2588 = 0.1294
$$

So:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Transpose

$$
\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & 0.9954 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Multiply by $\mathbf{F}$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & 0.9954 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Row 1:**

$$
Q_1 = (-0.9830)(10) + (0.9954)(-5) = -9.830 - 4.977 = -14.807 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Row 2:**

$$
Q_2 = (-0.4830)(10) + (0.1294)(-5) = -4.830 - 0.647 = -5.477 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{Q_1 \approx -14.81 \text{ N·m}, \quad Q_2 \approx -5.48 \text{ N·m}}
$$

The negative signs indicate that the joint motors must apply **clockwise** torques (in this sign convention) to hold the arm in static equilibrium against the applied force.

---

## 3.5 Generalized Forces from Multiple Sources — Superposition

When the manipulator experiences **multiple external influences simultaneously** (e.g., gravity, a payload force, joint friction, and a spring), the total generalized force is the **sum** of all individual contributions:

$$
\mathbf{Q}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{Q}_{\text{gravity}} + \mathbf{Q}_{\text{payload}} + \mathbf{Q}_{\text{friction}} + \mathbf{Q}_{\text{spring}} + \cdots
$$

This is a direct consequence of the **linearity** of the virtual work principle and the fact that each $\delta q_i$ is independent.

### Example: Viscous Friction at Joint $i$

For a rotational viscous damper at joint $i$ with damping coefficient $b_i$:

$$
Q_{\text{fric}, i} = -b_i \dot{q}_i
$$

This is a **non-conservative** generalized force (cannot be derived from a potential). It is obtained by equating the dissipated power $P_{\text{diss}} = -b_i \dot{q}_i^2$ to $\mathbf{Q}_{\text{fric}}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Generalized Forces in Robotics

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows how the various types of forces in a manipulator are unified under the **generalized force framework**. This is a *functional architecture map* (not a physical drawing), as the topic is mathematical and conceptual.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical System n-DOF Manipulator] --> B[Generalized Coordinates q1 q2 ... qn]
    B --> C[Position Map r q]
    C --> D[Jacobian Matrix J q]
    D --> E[Velocity Relationship r-dot equals J q-dot]
    D --> F[Static Force Relationship tau equals J-transpose F]
    A --> G[External Inputs]
    G --> G1[Cartesian Force F at End-Effector]
    G --> G2[Cartesian Moment M at End-Effector]
    G --> G3[Gravity Field g]
    G --> G4[Spring Potential Vs]
    G --> G5[Viscous Friction b]
    G1 --> H[Virtual Work delta-W]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[Identification with delta-W equals Q-transpose delta-q]
    I --> J[Generalized Force Vector Q in R-n]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Joint Actuator Torques tau equals Q]
    K --> L[Robot Motion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left side of the diagram shows the *geometric pipeline* (coordinates → position → Jacobian). The right side shows the *force pipeline* (external forces → virtual work → generalized forces → joint torques). The two pipelines meet at the **Jacobian transpose**, which is the natural "bridge" between Cartesian and joint spaces.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Force-Transformation Pipeline

The following diagram illustrates the **data flow** in computing generalized forces for a real robotic controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Forward Kinematics Module] --> B[Joint Position q]
    B --> C[Jacobian Computation J q]
    C --> D[Jacobian Transpose J-transpose q]
    E[Force/Torque Sensor at Wrist] --> F[Wrench Vector W]
    G[Gravity Compensation Database] --> H[Link Masses and COM]
    H --> I[Potential Energy Vg q]
    I --> J[Gradient of V wrt q]
    D --> K[Static Generalized Force Q-static = J-transpose W]
    J --> L[Gravitational Generalized Force Q-grav = minus gradient Vg]
    K --> M[Total Generalized Force Q-total]
    L --> M
    N[Viscous Friction Model b q-dot] --> M
    M --> O[Joint Torque Command tau]
    O --> P[Motor Drivers]
    P --> Q[Robot Joints]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Generalized Force Computation for a 2-DOF Planar Arm

This diagram shows the **specific computation** for the 2-DOF example derived in Section 3.2. It maps every arithmetic operation to its result.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: q1, q2, Fx, Fy, L1, L2] --> B[Compute trig functions sin q1, cos q1, sin q1+q2, cos q1+q2]
    B --> C[Compute J11 = minus L1 sin q1 minus L2 sin q1+q2]
    B --> D[Compute J12 = minus L2 sin q1+q2]
    B --> E[Compute J21 = L1 cos q1 plus L2 cos q1+q2]
    B --> F[Compute J22 = L2 cos q1+q2]
    C --> G[Build 2x2 Jacobian Matrix J]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Transpose J to get J-transpose]
    H --> I[Q1 = J-transpose[1,1] Fx + J-transpose[1,2] Fy]
    H --> J[Q2 = J-transpose[2,1] Fx + J-transpose[2,2] Fy]
    I --> K[Output: Joint Torques tau1 and tau2]
    J --> K
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "compute the generalized forces" in the KTU exam, follow this pipeline **explicitly** in your answer. Examiners reward clear, step-by-step derivations. Always (i) state the Jacobian, (ii) explicitly transpose, (iii) show the matrix multiplication, (iv) write the final scalar expressions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define generalized forces. How are they related to the joint coordinates of a robotic manipulator?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Generalized forces $Q_i$ are scalar (or vector) quantities conjugate to the generalized coordinates $q_i$ of a mechanical system. For an $n$-DOF manipulator, they are defined through the **Principle of Virtual Work**:
> $$\delta W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta q_i$$
> They represent the force or torque along/about each joint axis that produces virtual work equivalent to that of the actual external forces in Cartesian space. For a force $\mathbf{F}$ applied at a point with position $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{q})$, the generalized force is $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$, where $\mathbf{J}$ is the Jacobian. The units of $Q_i$ are **N** for a translational $q_i$ and **N·m** for a rotational $q_i$.
> 
> *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: virtual work statement, 1 mark: Jacobian relation + units)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the principle of virtual work as applied to a robotic manipulator. How does it lead to the concept of joint torques?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The principle of virtual work states that for a system in equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all forces during a virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is zero:
> $$\delta W_{\text{applied}} + \delta W_{\text{constraint}} = 0$$
> For an ideal-constraint rigid manipulator, the only forces doing work are the external Cartesian forces $\mathbf{F}$ and the joint actuator torques $\tau_i$. Applying a virtual displacement $\delta \mathbf{q}$:
> $$\sum_i \tau_i \delta q_i = \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q}$$
> Equating coefficients of the independent $\delta q_i$ gives $\tau_i = Q_i$ — the joint torques are the generalized forces conjugate to the joint coordinates. *(Full 3 marks: principle + manipulator specialization + identification of $\tau = Q$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme requires Module-wise internal choice. The two questions below are independent alternatives — a student answers **one** of them.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** With the help of the **Principle of Virtual Work**, derive the expression for the generalized forces of an $n$-DOF robotic manipulator when an external Cartesian force $\mathbf{F}$ is applied at the end-effector. Show clearly that the joint torque vector is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, the joint variables are $q_1$ and $q_2$. Derive the **Jacobian matrix** and compute the generalized forces $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ when the manipulator is at $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = 30°$ and is subjected to an external force $\mathbf{F} = [20, -10, 0]^T$ N at the end-effector. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Setup the position of the end-effector (1 mark):**

For an $n$-DOF manipulator, the end-effector position in Cartesian space is a smooth function of the joint coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{r}(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n) \in \mathbb{R}^3
$$

**Step 2: Compute the virtual displacement (1 mark):**

Using the chain rule, an infinitesimal virtual displacement of the end-effector is:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_i} \delta q_i
$$

In matrix form, defining the **Jacobian**:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_n} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}
$$

we obtain:

$$
\delta \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the principle of virtual work (2 marks):**

For the manipulator in equilibrium, the virtual work done by the external force and the actuator torques must balance:

$$
\delta W = \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{r} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} = 0
$$

Substituting $\delta \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \, \delta \mathbf{q} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q}
$$

**Step 4: Use independence of $\delta q_i$ to identify terms (1 mark):**

Since the $\delta q_i$ are independent virtual displacements:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^T = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}
$$

**Step 5: Concluding remark (2 marks):**

This is the **Jacobian transpose relationship**. It is the dual of the velocity relationship $\dot{\mathbf{r}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Physically, it says that the joint torques required to balance an external Cartesian force are obtained by projecting that force through the transpose of the velocity Jacobian.

**(b) Numerical Computation for 2-DOF Arm (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Forward Kinematics (1 mark):**

$$
x = L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$
$$
y = L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Step 2: Partial Derivatives and Jacobian (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $L_1 = 0.5$, $L_2 = 0.4$, $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = 30°$:

We need $\sin 60° = 0.8660$, $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.8660$, $q_1 + q_2 = 90°$ → $\sin 90° = 1$, $\cos 90° = 0$.

$$
J_{11} = -0.5(0.8660) - 0.4(1) = -0.4330 - 0.4 = -0.8330
$$

$$
J_{12} = -0.4(1) = -0.4000
$$

$$
J_{21} = 0.5(0.5) + 0.4(0) = 0.2500
$$

$$
J_{22} = 0.4(0) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Transpose the Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8330 & 0.2500 \\ -0.4000 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8330 & 0.2500 \\ -0.4000 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ -10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
Q_1 = (-0.8330)(20) + (0.2500)(-10) = -16.66 - 2.50 = -19.16 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
Q_2 = (-0.4000)(20) + (0)(-10) = -8.00 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5: Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{Q_1 = -19.16 \text{ N·m}, \quad Q_2 = -8.00 \text{ N·m}}
$$

The negative signs indicate the joint motors must apply torques in the clockwise (negative) direction to maintain static equilibrium.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of **generalized forces** arising from a **potential field**. Derive the relation $Q_i = -\partial V / \partial q_i$ and apply it to compute the gravitational generalized forces for a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link masses $m_1, m_2$ and link lengths $L_1, L_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF RP (Revolute-Prismatic) manipulator** whose end-effector position is given by:
$$
x = q_1 + L \cos q_2, \quad y = L \sin q_2
$$
where $q_1$ is the prismatic joint variable and $q_2$ is the revolute joint variable, derive the Jacobian and compute the generalized forces $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ when an external force $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y]^T$ acts at the end-effector. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Generalized Forces from Potential Field (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Definition of Conservative Forces (1 mark):**

A force is **conservative** if it can be derived from a scalar potential function $V(\mathbf{q})$ such that the virtual work done equals the negative change in potential:

$$
\delta W = -dV = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} \delta q_i
$$

**Step 2: Identification with Generalized Forces (1 mark):**

Comparing with $\delta W = \sum Q_i \delta q_i$:

$$
Q_i = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{Q} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} V(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 3: Gravity Potential for 2-DOF Arm (2 marks):**

The potential energy of the manipulator under gravity (acting in $-y$ direction) is:

$$
V_g = m_1 g \cdot \frac{L_1}{2} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[ L_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]
$$

$$
V_g = g \left[ \left(\frac{m_1 L_1}{2} + m_2 L_1\right) \sin q_1 + \frac{m_2 L_2}{2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate to find $Q_1, Q_2$ (2 marks):**

$$
Q_1 = -\frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_1} = -g \left[ \left(\frac{m_1}{2} + m_2\right) L_1 \cos q_1 + \frac{m_2 L_2}{2} \cos(q_1+q_2) \right]
$$

$$
Q_2 = -\frac{\partial V_g}{\partial q_2} = -\frac{m_2 L_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Step 5: Discussion (1 mark):**

These are the joint torques needed to **counteract gravity**. Notice that $Q_1$ depends on BOTH joint angles (because shoulder carries the weight of both links), while $Q_2$ depends only on the **relative angle** $q_1 + q_2$.

**(b) Jacobian and Generalized Forces for RP Manipulator (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Position Kinematics (1 mark):**

$$
x = q_1 + L \cos q_2, \quad y = L \sin q_2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Partial Derivatives (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -L \sin q_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = L \cos q_2
$$

**Step 3: Assemble the Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -L \sin q_2 \\ 0 & L \cos q_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Transpose the Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -L \sin q_2 & L \cos q_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Generalized Forces (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -L \sin q_2 & L \cos q_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_1 = F_x, \quad Q_2 = -L \sin q_2 \, F_x + L \cos q_2 \, F_y = L(F_y \cos q_2 - F_x \sin q_2)}
$$

**Step 6: Physical Interpretation (Bonus for clarity):**

- $Q_1 = F_x$ — the prismatic joint force equals the $x$-component of the applied force. This makes sense: the prismatic joint moves the arm only in the $x$-direction, so only $F_x$ does work on it.
- $Q_2$ is the revolute joint torque — it depends on the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from the joint to the line of action of the force.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Jacobian Transpose:** Students often write $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{F}$ instead of $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board papers. Always remember: *velocity* uses $\mathbf{J}$ (no transpose), *force* uses $\mathbf{J}^T$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Transpose Property:** $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ is valid in any dimension; $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{F}$ is dimensionally inconsistent unless $\mathbf{J}$ is square.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** $F_x, F_y$ in **Newtons**; $L$ in **meters**; final $Q_2$ must be in **N·m**. Always state units.
> 4. **Confusing Virtual Work and Real Work:** The principle applies to *virtual* (imaginary, instantaneous, constraint-consistent) displacements, not real finite motions.
> 5. **Gravity Sign Mistakes:** If gravity acts in $-y$ but the student uses $V = -m g y$ instead of $V = +m g y$, the entire problem is sign-flipped.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Generalized Forces (Module 1)**

- **Core Definition:** $Q_i$ is the scalar conjugate to $q_i$ in the virtual work expression $\delta W = \sum Q_i \, \delta q_i$.
- **Master Equation:** $\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}}$ (and its 6-DOF extension with the wrench $\mathbf{W}$).
- **Jacobian Definition:** $\mathbf{J} = \partial \mathbf{r} / \partial \mathbf{q}$ — a $3 \times n$ (or $6 \times n$) matrix.
- **Force-Transpose Duality:** $\dot{\mathbf{r}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ (kinematics) vs. $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (statics). These are **dual** to each other.
- **Conservative Forces:** $Q_i = -\partial V / \partial q_i$. Use this for gravity and springs.
- **Gravity Potential:** $V_g = \sum m_i \, \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{C_i}$. Differentiate to get joint torques.
- **Non-Conservative Forces:** Viscous friction $Q_{\text{fric},i} = -b_i \dot{q}_i$ — *cannot* be derived from a potential.
- **Superposition:** $\mathbf{Q}_{\text{total}} = \sum_k \mathbf{Q}_k$ (linearity of virtual work).
- **Units Matter:** $Q$ is **N** if $q$ is a translation, **N·m** if $q$ is a rotation. State explicitly.
- **Inverse Statics:** When $\mathbf{J}$ is square and invertible, $\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\tau}$. Used to find the Cartesian force equivalent to a given joint torque.
- **Singularities:** When $\det(\mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^T) = 0$, the manipulator is in a singular configuration, and $\mathbf{J}^T$ cannot be inverted. Force transmission is lost in certain directions — a critical safety concern in robot design.
- **Engineering Applications:** Force control ($\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{desired}}$), gravity compensation, haptic devices, bilateral teleoperation, and hybrid force/position control all rely on the generalized force framework.
- **Past KTU Trends:** Expect a Part-A definition question + a Part-B numerical on a 2-DOF or 3-DOF manipulator. The numerical almost always tests the Jacobian transpose, often with a gravity sub-part.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** For a 2-DOF arm at $q_2 = 90°$ (elbow fully bent), the $y$-component of $F$ contributes **zero** to $Q_2$ (verify this in the example). This is the moment-arm intuition.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equations of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ Module 1 | Equations of Motion — Core Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The **Equations of Motion (EOM)** of a robotic manipulator are the mathematical relationships that describe how the joint variables (positions, velocities, and accelerations) evolve in time under the influence of applied joint torques/forces and external loads. They form the cornerstone of robot dynamics and are indispensable for simulation, control design, trajectory planning, and stability analysis.

---

## 📘 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **dynamic model** (or equations of motion) of an *n*-DOF serial-chain robotic manipulator in **joint space** is canonically expressed as:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})
$$

where every term has a precise physical meaning tied to KTU syllabus outcomes.

| Symbol | Mathematical Meaning | Physical Interpretation | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Vector of generalized joint coordinates | Configuration of the manipulator | **rad** / **m** |
| $\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Joint velocities and accelerations | Rate of motion | **rad/s, m/s** |
| $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite **mass/inertia matrix** | Encodes kinetic energy of the system | **kg·m²** |
| $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | $n \times n$ **Coriolis–centrifugal matrix** | Captures velocity-dependent forces | **kg·m²/s** |
| $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times 1$ **gravity vector** | Gravitational torques at joints | **N·m** |
| $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | $n \times 1$ **friction vector** | Viscous + Coulomb friction | **N·m** |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | $n \times 1$ generalized force/torque vector | Actuator inputs | **N·m / N** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric** $\left(\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^{T}\right)$ and **positive-definite**, which guarantees that the kinetic energy is always non-negative and that the system is **energetically well-posed** for control synthesis (e.g., Lyapunov stability proofs).

---

## 🍎 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why Does the Robot Move Like That?"

Imagine you are pushing a friend sitting on a **rotating office chair** while holding a **heavy backpack**.

- If the friend leans forward (changing configuration $\mathbf{q}$), the **effort** you must exert to spin them changes — this is the role of the **inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$**. A robot arm behaves identically: extending a link outward increases its rotational inertia about the base.
- If the friend is already spinning and tries to swing their arm tangentially, you feel an additional sideways push — this is the **Coriolis–centrifugal force** $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- Gravity always tugs the backpack down; if the chair is tilted, that downward tug creates a **torque about the pivot** — this is the **gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$**.

> [!TIP]
> The dynamic equation is essentially **Newton's Second Law for robots**: "Sum of torques equals mass × acceleration, plus velocity effects, plus gravity."

---

## 🎯 1.3 Why Equations of Motion Matter in KTU Robotics

1. **Forward Dynamics** — Given $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$, compute $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t)$ for **simulation**.
2. **Inverse Dynamics** — Given a desired $\mathbf{q}(t), \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t), \ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t)$, compute required $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ for **feedforward control**.
3. **Controller Design** — Model-based controllers (Computed Torque, Adaptive Control, Sliding Mode) require explicit $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$.
4. **Energy / Power Budgeting** — Predict actuator loading and battery sizing.
5. **Mechanical Design** — Select gear ratios, motor torques, and structural stiffness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Module 1 | Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Formulations (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 1 mandates a deep understanding of **both** formulations, with derivations expected in Part B questions.

### 🔹 (A) Lagrangian (Energy-Based) Formulation

This is an analytical, **closed-form**, and elegant approach. It uses **scalar energy functions** rather than vectorial forces.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Assign generalized coordinates** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^{T}$ (joint angles for revolute, joint displacements for prismatic).
2. **Compute the position of the center of mass** of every link $i$ via forward kinematics:
   $$
   \mathbf{p}_{ci} = \mathbf{p}_{ci}(\mathbf{q})
   $$
3. **Differentiate to obtain velocity** $\mathbf{v}_{ci} = \mathbf{J}_{v_i}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, where $\mathbf{J}_{v_i}$ is the **linear Jacobian** of link $i$.
4. **Form the total kinetic energy** $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$, where
   $$
   T_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\mathbf{v}_{ci}^{T}\mathbf{v}_{ci} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{T}\,\mathbf{I}_{i}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}
   $$
   After substituting Jacobians and exploiting the identity $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{A}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}(\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{T})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, we obtain the compact form:
   $$
   T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\,\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
   $$
5. **Form the total potential energy** due to gravity:
   $$
   V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,\mathbf{p}_{ci}(\mathbf{q}) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{q}}
   $$
6. **Construct the Lagrangian**:
   $$
   \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})
   $$
7. **Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation** for each coordinate $q_j$:
   $$
   \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_j} = \tau_j, \qquad j = 1, \ldots, n
   $$
   In vector form:
   $$
   \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}\right)^{T} - \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\right)^{T} = \boldsymbol{\tau}
   $$

### 🔹 (B) Newton–Euler (Force-Balance) Formulation

This is a **recursive, vectorial** approach favoured for **real-time implementation** because of its $O(n)$ computational complexity.

**Two Recursions:**

- **Forward recursion** (base → tip): compute ${}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}$, ${}^{i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i}$, ${}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{i}$, ${}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{c_i}$ (angular velocity, angular acceleration, linear acceleration of frame origin, and acceleration of link CoM).
- **Backward recursion** (tip → base): compute ${}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i}$, ${}^{i}\mathbf{n}_{i}$ (forces and moments on each link), then project onto joint axes to obtain $\tau_i$.

---

## 2.2 Structure of the Inertia Matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$

The $(j,k)$ element is given by the **mass distribution tensor**:

$$
M_{jk}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=\max(j,k)}^{n} \left[\, m_i\,(\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{(j)})^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{(k)} + (\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{(j)})^{T}\,{}^{i}\mathbf{I}_{i}\,\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{(k)} \,\right]
$$

**Properties (frequently tested in KTU Part A):**

- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric**: $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^{T}$.
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **positive-definite**: $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} > 0, \; \forall\, \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
- The eigenvalues are bounded: $\mu_1 \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^{2} \leq \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \leq \mu_2 \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^{2}$.

---

## 2.3 Coriolis–Centrifugal Term — Two Equivalent Forms

The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts either form:

**Form 1 — Christoffel symbols:**
$$
C_{kj}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i
$$

**Form 2 — Matrix formulation:**
$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{q}}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Skew-Symmetry Property (favourite KTU exam topic):** The matrix $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric**:
> $$
> \mathbf{x}^{T}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q}) - 2\,\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\right]\mathbf{x} = 0, \quad \forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
> $$
> This identity is the **gateway** to Lyapunov-based and passivity-based controller proofs.

---

## 2.4 🚀 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Property | Physical Meaning | Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{F}$ | Standard EOM | All manipulators |
| 2 | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Kinetic energy | All manipulators |
| 3 | $G_j = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial q_j}$ | Gravity torque at joint $j$ | Conservative gravity |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ | Lagrangian scalar | Conservative systems |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\right) - \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_j} = \tau_j$ | Euler–Lagrange equation | All conservative joints |
| 6 | $M_{jk} = \sum_{i} m_i(\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{(j)})^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{(k)} + (\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{(j)})^{T}\mathbf{I}_{i}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{(k)}$ | Inertia matrix element | Rigid-body links |
| 7 | $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^{T} \succ 0$ | Symmetric PD | Structural property |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric | Passivity property | Christoffel form of $\mathbf{C}$ |
| 9 | $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{M}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \tfrac{1}{2}\nabla_{\mathbf{q}}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | Equivalent $\mathbf{C}$ matrix | Always |
| 10 | ${}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{i+1} = {}^{i}\mathbf{R}_{i+1}\!\left[{}^{i+1}\mathbf{a}_{i+1}^{\prime} + {}^{i+1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1}\times({}^{i+1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1}\times{}^{i+1}\mathbf{r}_{i+1,i}) + {}^{i+1}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i+1}\times{}^{i+1}\mathbf{r}_{i+1,i}\right]$ | Forward recursion | Newton–Euler |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial 6-DOF arms (KUKA, ABB):** Inverse dynamics computed at 1 kHz for trajectory tracking.
- **Humanoid robots (Atlas, Asimo):** Full-body EOM with $n \geq 28$ DOF for balance control.
- **Space manipulators (Canadarm):** Micro-gravity eliminates $\mathbf{G}$ but $\mathbf{M}$ and $\mathbf{C}$ dominate.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci):** Friction $\mathbf{F}$ and accurate $\mathbf{G}$ are critical for haptic feedback.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Module 1 | Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the full derivation chain — partial credit is heavily penalised.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard EOM for a Single Rotational Joint

Consider **Link $i$** of a serial manipulator rotating about the $z_{i-1}$ axis of its predecessor. Its mass is $m_i$, and its rotational inertia about the centre of mass is $\mathbf{I}_i$.

### Step 1 — Position of the centre of mass
Using forward kinematics, the position vector of link $i$ CoM in the base frame:
$$
\mathbf{p}_{c_i} = \mathbf{p}_{i}(\mathbf{q}), \qquad \mathbf{p}_{c_i} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{3}
$$

### Step 2 — Velocity by chain rule
Differentiating:
$$
\mathbf{v}_{c_i} = \frac{d\mathbf{p}_{c_i}}{dt} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}_{c_i}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \equiv \mathbf{J}_{v_i}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Here $\mathbf{J}_{v_i}$ is the $3 \times n$ **linear velocity Jacobian** of link $i$ CoM.

### Step 3 — Kinetic energy of link $i$

The kinetic energy of a rigid body has translational and rotational parts:

$$
T_i = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\mathbf{v}_{c_i}^{T}\mathbf{v}_{c_i}}_{\text{Translational}} \; + \; \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{T}\,\mathbf{I}_{i}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}}_{\text{Rotational}}
$$

Substituting the velocity expression and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_i &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}\mathbf{I}_{i}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\!\left[\,m_i\,\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}\mathbf{I}_{i}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\,\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the **link inertia contribution**:
$$
\mathbf{M}_i(\mathbf{q}) \;\triangleq\; m_i\,\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}\mathbf{I}_{i}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Total kinetic energy of the manipulator

Summing over all $n$ links:

$$
T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\!\left[\,\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{M}_i(\mathbf{q})\,\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This recovers the canonical inertia matrix:
$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\,m_i\,\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}_{v_i}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{I}_{i}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}(\mathbf{q})\,\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Potential energy and gravity vector

The potential energy of link $i$ (taking the base frame $z$-axis as vertical gravity reference, $\mathbf{g} = [0,0,-g]^{T}$):

$$
V_i = -m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_{c_i}(\mathbf{q}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(\mathbf{q}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_{c_i}(\mathbf{q})
$$

### Step 6 — Form the Lagrangian

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_{c_i}(\mathbf{q})
$$

### Step 7 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}$

Since $\mathbf{M}$ depends only on $\mathbf{q}$, not on $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}\right)$

Using the product rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}\right) &= \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{d\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})}{dt}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \\[4pt]
&= \dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total derivative of $\mathbf{M}$ is:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})}{\partial q_j}\,\dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 9 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{g}
$$

Since $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is a scalar, its gradient with respect to $\mathbf{q}$ is a column vector:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]_{k} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 10 — Apply Euler–Lagrange and simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\tau} &= \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \\[4pt]
&= \dot{\mathbf{M}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{g}}_{\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the **Coriolis–centrifugal vector**:
$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;\triangleq\; \dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\!\left[\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]
$$

### Step 11 — Final Compact Equation of Motion

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\;}
$$

✅ **This is the KTU gold-standard form** — every part-(a)/(b) numerical answer must terminate at this equation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Setup:** Two links of lengths $L_1, L_2$ and point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends. Joint angles $q_1, q_2$. Gravity acts downward ($g$).

### Step A — Forward kinematics (positions of CoM)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{c_1} &= \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1 \\ \tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{p}_{c_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Velocities (Jacobians)
$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_1} = \tfrac{L_1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -\sin q_1 & 0 \\ \cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) & -\tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) & \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step C — Inertia matrix (after substitution)

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = m_1\,\mathbf{J}_{v_1}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_1} + m_2\,\mathbf{J}_{v_2}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_2}
$$

Evaluating (assumes negligible link rotational inertia; for full inertia include $\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}\mathbf{I}_i\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}$):

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
m_1\tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4} + m_2\!\left(L_1^{2} + \tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + L_1L_2\cos q_2\right) & m_2\!\left(\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) \\[6pt]
m_2\!\left(\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) & m_2\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step D — Gravity vector
$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1\tfrac{L_1}{2} + m_2 L_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 \tfrac{L_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
m_2 \tfrac{L_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step E — Coriolis–centrifugal matrix
$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = m_2 L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -\dot{q}_1 - \dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step F — Final equation of motion
$$
\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix}\tau_1\\ \tau_2\end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\begin{bmatrix}\ddot{q}_1\\ \ddot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1\\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\;}
$$
