# 📝 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE** pattern: Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks each with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the general form of the equations of motion for an n-DOF serial manipulator. Briefly explain the physical significance of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

The joint-space equations of motion for an *n*-DOF serial rigid-link manipulator are:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Physical significance:**

- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Inertial torques arising from joint accelerations.
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Coriolis and centrifugal torques due to coupling between moving links.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — Gravitational torques due to link weights.
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ — Generalized actuator torques/forces required to drive the manipulator.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State two important properties of the inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ that are exploited in robot controller design.*

**Model Answer:**

**Property 1 — Symmetry:** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{M}^{T}(\mathbf{q})$ for all $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

**Property 2 — Positive Definiteness:** $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{x} > 0$ for every $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ and every $\mathbf{q}$. This guarantees that kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} > 0$ for all non-zero velocities.

Both properties are essential in proving stability of computed-torque controllers using Lyapunov functions of the form $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{e}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{e}$.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Two fully independent options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the Euler–Lagrange equations of motion for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends. Assume gravity $g$ acts downward. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the derived model, identify and write explicit expressions for the inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, the Coriolis–centrifugal vector $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, and the gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. Prove that the inertia matrix is positive-definite for all $\mathbf{q}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Generalized coordinates:** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2]^{T}$, both revolute.

**Step 2 — Forward kinematics:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{c_1} &= \bigl[\tfrac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1,\;\tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1\bigr]^{T} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{p}_{c_2} &= \bigl[L_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2),\;L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\bigr]^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocities via Jacobian (shown in §3.2):**
$$
\mathbf{v}_{c_1} = \mathbf{J}_{v_1}\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \mathbf{v}_{c_2} = \mathbf{J}_{v_2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 4 — Kinetic energy:**
$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\!\left[m_1\mathbf{J}_{v_1}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_1} + m_2\mathbf{J}_{v_2}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_2}\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 5 — Potential energy:**
$$
V = -m_1 g \tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 - m_2 g \left[L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = T - V$

**Step 7 — Apply Euler–Lagrange** for $j = 1, 2$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_j} = \tau_j
$$

After carrying out the derivatives (already detailed in §3.1, steps 7–11), the final equation is:

$$
\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix}\tau_1\\ \tau_2\end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\begin{bmatrix}\ddot{q}_1\\ \ddot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1\\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\;}
$$

##### Part (b) — Identification and PD proof

**Inertia matrix:**
$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
m_1\tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4} + m_2\!\left(L_1^{2} + \tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + L_1L_2\cos q_2\right) & m_2\!\left(\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) \\[6pt]
m_2\!\left(\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) & m_2\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Coriolis–centrifugal vector:** $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ with
$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = m_2 L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -\dot{q}_1 - \dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Gravity vector:**
$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1\tfrac{L_1}{2} + m_2 L_1)g\cos q_1 + m_2 \tfrac{L_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt]
m_2 \tfrac{L_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Positive-definiteness proof:** For any $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^{T} \neq \mathbf{0}$,

$$
\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\bigr)\bigl|_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}=\mathbf{x}} = T > 0
$$

since the kinetic energy of a moving rigid-body system with non-zero velocity is strictly positive. Also, the principal minors are positive:

- $M_{11} = m_1\tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4} + m_2\!\left(L_1^{2} + \tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + L_1L_2\cos q_2\right) > 0$ for all $q_2 \in [0, 2\pi]$.
- $\det(\mathbf{M}) = \tfrac{m_1 m_2 L_1^{2} L_2^{2}}{16} + \tfrac{m_2^{2} L_2^{2}}{16}\!\left(L_1^{2} + \tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + L_1L_2\cos q_2\right) - m_2^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right)^{2} > 0$ by Sylvester's criterion.

Hence $\mathbf{M} \succ 0$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **omit the explicit evaluation** of $\det(\mathbf{M})$ and only assert "$\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric, hence PD." This loses 2–3 marks. You **must** invoke **Sylvester's criterion** (positive leading principal minors) to clinch the PD argument.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Newton–Euler formulation for the equations of motion of a serial manipulator. Clearly distinguish between the forward and backward recursion stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single revolute joint driving a rigid link of mass $m$, length $L$, with centre of mass at $L/2$ and inertia $I$ about its CoM, derive the equation of motion using the Lagrangian approach. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Newton–Euler Formulation

The Newton–Euler formulation expresses **Newton's second law** for translation and **Euler's equation** for rotation on every link, subject to joint constraints. It proceeds in **two coupled recursions**:

**1. Forward Recursion (Base → Tip):** For each link $i = 1, \ldots, n$ compute:

- Angular velocity: ${}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i} = {}^{i}\mathbf{R}_{i-1}\bigl[{}^{i-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i\,{}^{i-1}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}\bigr]$
- Angular acceleration: ${}^{i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i} = {}^{i}\mathbf{R}_{i-1}\bigl[{}^{i-1}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i\,{}^{i-1}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} + {}^{i-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times\dot{q}_i\,{}^{i-1}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}\bigr]$
- Linear acceleration of origin: ${}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{i} = {}^{i}\mathbf{R}_{i-1}\bigl[{}^{i-1}\mathbf{a}_{i-1} + {}^{i-1}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i-1}\times{}^{i-1}\mathbf{r}_{i-1,i} + {}^{i-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times({}^{i-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times{}^{i-1}\mathbf{r}_{i-1,i})\bigr]$
- Linear acceleration of CoM: ${}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{c_i} = {}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{i} + {}^{i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i}\times{}^{i}\mathbf{r}_{i,c_i} + {}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}\times({}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}\times{}^{i}\mathbf{r}_{i,c_i})$

**2. Backward Recursion (Tip → Base):** For each link $i = n, \ldots, 1$ compute:

- Force on link: ${}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i} = m_i\,{}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{c_i} + {}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i+1}$
- Moment on link: ${}^{i}\mathbf{n}_{i} = {}^{i}\mathbf{I}_{i}\,{}^{i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i} + {}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}\times({}^{i}\mathbf{I}_{i}\,{}^{i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}) + {}^{i}\mathbf{n}_{i+1} + {}^{i}\mathbf{r}_{i,c_i}\times{}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i} + {}^{i}\mathbf{r}_{i,i+1}\times{}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i+1}$
- Joint torque: $\tau_i = {}^{i}\mathbf{n}_{i}^{T}\,{}^{i}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ (revolute) or $\tau_i = {}^{i}\mathbf{f}_{i}^{T}\,{}^{i}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}$ (prismatic)

**Advantages over Lagrangian:** $O(n)$ computation, suitable for real-time control at kHz rates. **Disadvantages:** No insight into energy properties.

##### Part (b) — Lagrangian Derivation for Single Link

**Setup:** A single revolute joint rotates a uniform link of mass $m$, length $L$, about a vertical axis. The joint angle is $q$.

**Step 1 — Position of CoM:** $p_c = (L/2)\sin q$ (vertical), $h_c = (L/2)(1-\cos q)$ (height) — for a pendulum-like model.

**Step 2 — Velocity:** $v_c = \dot{p}_c = (L/2)\cos q\,\dot{q}$, so
$$
v_c^{2} = \tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q\,\dot{q}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy:**
$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}m v_c^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}I\dot{q}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I\right]\dot{q}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Potential energy:**
$$
V = m g h_c = m g\tfrac{L}{2}(1-\cos q)
$$

**Step 5 — Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = T - V$

**Step 6 — Compute partial derivatives:**
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}} = \left[m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I\right]\dot{q}
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) = \left[m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I\right]\ddot{q} - m\tfrac{L^{2}}{2}\cos q\sin q\,\dot{q}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = -m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos q\sin q\,\dot{q}^{2} - m g\tfrac{L}{2}\sin q
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Euler–Lagrange:**
$$
\tau = \left[m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I\right]\ddot{q} - m\tfrac{L^{2}}{2}\cos q\sin q\,\dot{q}^{2} + m g\tfrac{L}{2}\sin q
$$

Identifying:

- $M(q) = m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I$
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = -m\tfrac{L^{2}}{2}\cos q\sin q\,\dot{q}^{2}$
- $G(q) = m g\tfrac{L}{2}\sin q$

**Final equation of motion:**

$$
\boxed{\;\tau = \left[\,m\tfrac{L^{2}}{4}\cos^{2}q + I\,\right]\ddot{q} - \tfrac{m L^{2}}{2}\sin q\cos q\,\dot{q}^{2} + \tfrac{m g L}{2}\sin q\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **include the rotational inertia $I$** in $M(q)$. The link is **not a point mass**; the rotational kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}I\dot{q}^{2}$ is mandatory. Failing to do so loses **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Equations of Motion**

- ⭐ The **canonical EOM** is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.
- ⭐ Two formulations: **Lagrangian (energy-based, symbolic)** vs **Newton–Euler (force-based, recursive)**.
- ⭐ **Inertia matrix** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric positive-definite** — guarantees well-posedness.
- ⭐ **Skew-symmetry** of $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is the cornerstone of **passivity-based** and **adaptive** controllers.
- ⭐ The **Coriolis matrix is not unique** — any matrix satisfying $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{C}^{T}$ is valid.
- ⭐ The **gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \partial V/\partial \mathbf{q}$ has no $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ dependence.
- ⭐ Forward dynamics → simulation; Inverse dynamics → control.
- ⭐ Christoffel symbol formula for $C_{kj}$ is the KTU favourite for 3-mark derivations.
- ⭐ For **single-link pendulum**: $M(q)$ depends on $q$ if CoM is offset; $\mathbf{G}(q) \propto \sin q$.
- ⭐ For **planar 2-DOF**: $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ has cosine-coupling in off-diagonal terms; centrifugal terms $\propto \sin q_2$.
- ⭐ Always include **rotational inertia $I_i$** about CoM for every link, not just translational mass.
- ⭐ Use **Sylvester's criterion** (positive leading principal minors) to prove $\mathbf{M} \succ 0$ explicitly.
- ⭐ The Newton–Euler algorithm runs in $O(n)$ — preferable for **real-time** inverse dynamics.
- ⭐ In **micro-gravity** (space), $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$, but $\mathbf{M}$ and $\mathbf{C}$ still dominate.
- ⭐ **Computed-torque controller** design is impossible without the explicit $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Forces in Manipulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Forces in Manipulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Forces in Manipulators** is the branch of manipulator mechanics that studies the relationship between the **generalized forces** (forces and torques) applied at the end-effector of a robotic manipulator and the **joint torques/forces** required to maintain **static equilibrium**, when the manipulator is either stationary or moving at constant velocity (quasi-static conditions).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is derived from the principle of **virtual work** applied to the kinematic chain. The fundamental mapping is established through the **transpose of the Jacobian matrix** ($\mathbf{J}^T$), which relates end-effector wrench $\mathbf{F}$ to joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \mathbf{F}$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the joint torque/force vector
- $\mathbf{J}^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 6}$ is the Jacobian transpose (for a 6-DOF spatial manipulator)
- $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 1}$ is the Cartesian force/moment (wrench) at the end-effector
- $\mathbf{q}$ is the vector of joint variables

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crowbar and the Door:**
> Imagine you are trying to open a heavy door (the **end-effector load**) by pushing near the hinges versus pushing near the handle. When you push near the handle (favorable configuration), the door opens easily — your hand force maps to a small joint effort. When you push near the hinge (unfavorable configuration), the same door feels extremely heavy — the same hand force requires huge joint effort.

This is exactly what static force analysis in manipulators predicts. The Jacobian transpose $\mathbf{J}^T$ acts as a **mechanical transformer** — it amplifies or reduces the joint torques required for a given end-effector load, depending on the **manipulator's posture** (configuration).

**Key Physical Insight:**
- When the manipulator is **stretched out** (near singular configuration) → $\mathbf{J}$ becomes ill-conditioned → small end-effector forces map to **huge** joint torques.
- When the manipulator is **folded compactly** → better force transmission → smaller joint torques required.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Joint Torque (revolute) | $\tau_i$ | **N·m (Newton-meter)** |
| Joint Force (prismatic) | $f_i$ | **N (Newton)** |
| End-effector Force | $\mathbf{F}$ | **N** |
| End-effector Moment | $\mathbf{M}$ | **N·m** |
| Manipulability Measure (force) | $w_F$ | dimensionless |
| Condition Number | $\kappa(\mathbf{J})$ | dimensionless, $\kappa \geq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Static force analysis is foundational for Module 1 because it precedes dynamic analysis. The student must master the **virtual work principle** and the **force-ellipsoid** concept before moving to forward/inverse dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Virtual Work — The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Virtual Work"?** Because at static equilibrium, the manipulator does no real work (no motion). We use the *virtual* (imaginary) infinitesimal displacements to derive the force relationship.

For a manipulator in **static equilibrium**, the total virtual work done by all external forces (joint + end-effector) must equal zero:

$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$$

where:
- $\delta \mathbf{q}$ = virtual displacement in joint space
- $\delta \mathbf{x}$ = virtual displacement in Cartesian space
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ = generalized joint forces/torques
- $\mathbf{F}$ = generalized end-effector force/moment

Since the differential relationship between joint and Cartesian velocities is:

$$\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \delta \mathbf{q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \delta \mathbf{x}$$

Substituting:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q}$$

For arbitrary virtual displacement $\delta \mathbf{q}$:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}}$$

This is the **Static Force Equation** of a robotic manipulator — the central result of this topic.

## 2.2 Force Ellipsoid (Force Manipulability)

The **Force Ellipsoid** describes the set of all end-effector forces $\mathbf{F}$ that a manipulator can generate with joint torques bounded by $\Vert \boldsymbol{\tau} \Vert \leq 1$ (unit ball in joint torque space).

| Concept | Velocity Ellipsoid | Force Ellipsoid |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Domain | Joint velocities | Joint torques |
| Constraint | $\Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^2 = 1$ | $\Vert \boldsymbol{\tau} \Vert^2 = 1$ |
| Map | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ |
| Image | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}} \leq 1$ | $\mathbf{F}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) \mathbf{F} \leq 1$ |
| Eigendirection | Principal force directions | Same as velocity ellipsoid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (Yoshikawa, 1985):** The **principal axes** of the velocity ellipsoid and the force ellipsoid are the **same**, but their **magnitudes are reciprocal**. If $\lambda_i$ is an eigenvalue of $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$, the velocity ellipsoid semi-axis is $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ and the force ellipsoid semi-axis is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}$.

**Force Manipulability Measure:**

$$w_F = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)} = \frac{1}{w_V}$$

where $w_V$ is the velocity manipulability.

## 2.3 Force Decomposition in Singular Value Form

Using SVD: $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T$, with singular values $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$.

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^T \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{F}$$

- Forces along directions $\mathbf{u}_i$ (left singular vectors) are transmitted with "gain" $\sigma_i$.
- **Largest force** that can be generated along $\mathbf{u}_1$ (direction of max singular value).
- Along singular directions ($\sigma_i \to 0$), **infinite** force is theoretically required — this is the **singularity problem**.

## 2.4 Solvability and Force Closure

| Concept | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---------|------------|---------------|
| **Solvability** | Given $\mathbf{F}$, can we find $\boldsymbol{\tau}$? | Always solvable (just compute $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$) |
| **Force Closure** | Can the manipulator resist any arbitrary external wrench? | Requires the manipulator to be in a non-singular configuration with full row-rank Jacobian |
| **In the Null Space** | $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_0 = 0$ for some $\mathbf{F}_0 \neq 0$ | These forces produce no joint torque — they are **internal preloads** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Industrial Assembly:** Predicting joint motor sizing during heavy payload operations.
> - **Surgical Robots:** Ensuring force feedback accuracy in minimally invasive surgery.
> - **Humanoid Robots:** Computing ankle/hip torques for standing balance.
> - **Force-Controlled Machining:** Maintaining constant contact force in polishing/deburring.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the boxed equations. They appear every year in Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q}$ | Differential kinematic relation |
| 2 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ | **Static force-torque mapping** (CORE) |
| 3 | $\mathbf{F}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) \mathbf{F} \leq 1$ | Force ellipsoid equation |
| 4 | $w_F = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ | Force manipulability index |
| 5 | $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}}$ | Condition number (force transmission isotropy) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{F}_0 \in \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J}^T) \Rightarrow \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ | Internal wrench in null space |
| 7 | $F_{\max} = \frac{\tau_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}}$ | Max force along weakest direction |
| 8 | $w_V \cdot w_F = 1$ | Velocity-force manipulability duality |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** Absolute values and norms are written as `\Vert x \Vert` and `\vert x \vert` (not vertical pipes), to preserve markdown table integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator — Static Force Derivation

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, joint angles $q_1$ and $q_2$. The end-effector applies a wrench $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y]^T$ at the tool tip. Derive the joint torques $\tau_1, \tau_2$ required to maintain static equilibrium.

### Step 1: Forward Kinematics (Position)

The end-effector position is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y &= l_1 \sin(q_1) + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$

Taking partial derivatives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} &= -l_1 \sin(q_1) - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} &= -l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} &= l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} &= l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\
l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & l_2 c_{12}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $s_1 = \sin q_1$, $c_1 = \cos q_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(q_1 + q_2)$, $c_{12} = \cos(q_1 + q_2)$.

### Step 3: Apply the Static Force Equation

Using the principle of virtual work, the joint torque vector is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}
$$

Compute the Jacobian transpose:

$$
\mathbf{J}^T =
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \\
-l_2 s_{12} & l_2 c_{12}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Explicit Joint Torque Expressions

Multiplying $\mathbf{J}^T$ by $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= -(l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12}) F_x + (l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12}) F_y \\
\tau_2 &= -l_2 s_{12} F_x + l_2 c_{12} F_y
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= (l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12}) F_y - (l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12}) F_x \\
\tau_2 &= l_2 c_{12} F_y - l_2 s_{12} F_x
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Observation:** The term $(l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12})$ is the moment arm of $F_y$ about joint 1. The term $l_2 c_{12}$ is the moment arm of $F_y$ about joint 2. This matches the static moment balance from rigid body statics — a **powerful sanity check** for the KTU examiner.

## 3.2 Derivation: Force Ellipsoid from Joint Torque Bound

**Assumption:** Joint torques are bounded: $\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2 + \dots + \tau_n^2 \leq 1$ (unit ball in joint torque space).

### Step 1: Write the Static Relation

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Vert \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \Vert^2 \leq 1
$$

### Step 2: Expand the Squared Norm

$$
\Vert \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \Vert^2 = (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F})^T (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}
$$

### Step 3: Define the Constraint Inequality

$$
\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \leq 1
$$

### Step 4: Identify the Quadratic Form

This is a **quadratic form** in $\mathbf{F}$. The matrix $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$ is symmetric positive semi-definite. The set $\{\mathbf{F} \mid \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{F} \leq 1\}$ is a **generalized ellipsoid** in force space.

### Step 5: Principal Axes via Eigendecomposition

Let $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{U}^T$ with $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n)$. Change of variable $\mathbf{F}' = \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{F}$:

$$
\lambda_1 F_1'^2 + \lambda_2 F_2'^2 + \dots + \lambda_n F_n'^2 \leq 1
$$

This is the **canonical ellipsoid equation** with semi-axes $a_i = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}$.

$$
\boxed{\text{Force Ellipsoid: } \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{F_i'^2}{1/\lambda_i} \leq 1}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| KTU Problem Type | Setup Given | Find | Key Formula |
|------------------|-------------|------|-------------|
| Type 1: Joint Torque | $\mathbf{F}$, $\mathbf{q}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ |
| Type 2: End-effector Force | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$, $\mathbf{q}$ | $\mathbf{F}$ | $\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{J}^T)^{\dagger} \boldsymbol{\tau}$ |
| Type 3: Force Ellipsoid | $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ | Equation of ellipsoid | $\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \leq 1$ |
| Type 4: Manipulability | $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ | $w_F$ | $w_F = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ |
| Type 5: Singularity Test | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ | Singular config | Infinite joint torque |
| Type 6: Null-space Wrench | $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{F}_0$ such that $\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}_0 = 0$ | Solve homogeneous system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-Code Mandate:** As per KTU 2024 engineering exam protocols, all derivations and problem-solving are done via closed-form algebra, linear algebra (matrix inverses, SVD, eigenvalues), and geometric reasoning. No programming/simulation is required in the written exam, although **MATLAB/Python verification** is encouraged for lab internal assessments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Static Force Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[End-Effector Wrench F] --> B[Coordinate Transform Tool Frame to Base Frame]
    B --> C[Compute Jacobian J of q]
    C --> D[Compute J Transpose J Transpose]
    D --> E[Matrix Multiplication tau equals J Transpose F]
    E --> F[Joint Torque Vector tau]
    F --> G{Joint Torque within Limits?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Static Equilibrium Maintained]
    G -- No --> I[Select New Configuration or Reduce Payload]
    J[Configuration q] --> C
    K[Link Geometry l1, l2, ...] --> C
    L[Joint Torque Limits tau_max] --> G
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Inputs (left side):** End-effector wrench, current joint configuration, link geometry, joint torque limits.
- **Central transformation:** Compute Jacobian, transpose, and apply the static force equation.
- **Decision branch (right):** Check feasibility; if not feasible, re-plan trajectory or re-design payload.

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: Velocity vs. Force Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JOINT_SPACE["JOINT SPACE"]
        J1[tau : Joint Torque Vector]
        J2[qd : Joint Velocity Vector]
    end
    subgraph TASK_SPACE["TASK / CARTESIAN SPACE"]
        T1[F : End-Effector Wrench]
        T2[xd : End-Effector Velocity]
    end
    J2 -->|J| T2
    T1 -->|J Transpose| J1
    J1 -.Feedback.-> M1[Motor Sizing]
    T2 -.Feedback.-> M2[Trajectory Planning]
    T1 -.Feedback.-> M3[Force Control Law]
    J2 -.Feedback.-> M4[Servo Control Law]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- **Upper path (Velocity):** Used for trajectory planning, motion control, and resolving joint velocities from Cartesian commands.
- **Lower path (Force):** Used for force control, compliance, and motor sizing.
- The same Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}$ governs both mappings — but with **transpose** swapping the direction.

## 4.3 Force Ellipsoid Visualization (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unit Ball in Joint Torque Space] -->|J Transpose Map| B[Force Ellipsoid in Task Space]
    A1[Torque Constraint: tau dot tau <= 1] --> A
    B --> B1[Principal Axes: Singular Vectors of J]
    B --> B2[Semi-Axes: 1 divided by sigma_i]
    B --> B3[Ellipsoid Volume related to Manipulability wF]
    C[Singular Configuration: sigma_min approaches 0] --> D[Force Ellipsoid Stretches to Infinity]
    C --> E[Velocity Ellipsoid Collapses to Zero]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `M1`), avoiding reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, etc. Node labels contain raw text only (no markdown bold/italics) inside double-quoted strings.

## 4.4 Force Transmission Quality Across Configurations

| Configuration | Jacobian Rank | Force Ellipsoid Shape | Engineering Implication |
|---------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Stretched (Elbow Up, Arm Extended)** | Full | Highly elongated | Good force in some directions, poor in others |
| **Compact (Folded Elbow)** | Full | Near-spherical | Isotropic force transmission |
| **Singular (Elbow Straight at $180°$)** | Rank deficient | Degenerate (line/plane) | Infinite torque required along lost direction |
| **Near Boundary (Workspace Edge)** | Reduced | Highly elongated | Motor saturation likely |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are **short-answer conceptual** questions, typically requiring a definition, a formula, or a one-step derivation.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: State the principle of virtual work as applied to a robotic manipulator in static equilibrium. Derive the relation between end-effector forces and joint torques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Principle of Virtual Work:** For a manipulator in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by joint forces and end-effector forces over any virtual displacement must vanish.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta W &= \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \delta \mathbf{x} = 0 \\
\delta W &= \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J} \delta \mathbf{q} = 0 \\
(\boldsymbol{\tau}^T - \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}) \delta \mathbf{q} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\delta \mathbf{q}$ is arbitrary:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}}$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define force ellipsoid. How are its principal axes oriented with respect to the singular vectors of the Jacobian?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The force ellipsoid is the set of all end-effector forces $\mathbf{F}$ that can be generated by a manipulator when the joint torques are constrained within a unit ball: $\mathbf{F}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) \mathbf{F} \leq 1$.

> **Principal Axes Orientation (1.5 marks):** The principal axes of the force ellipsoid are aligned with the **left singular vectors** $\mathbf{u}_i$ of the Jacobian $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$. The semi-axis lengths are $\frac{1}{\sigma_i}$ where $\sigma_i$ are the singular values.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and $l_2 = 0.4\,\text{m}$, compute the joint torques $\tau_1, \tau_2$ when the end-effector applies a force $\mathbf{F} = [10\,\text{N}, -5\,\text{N}]^T$ at configuration $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define and derive the **Force Ellipsoid** for a 2-DOF planar manipulator. Show that the principal axes of the force and velocity ellipsoids are reciprocal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 (1 mark): Compute the trigonometric values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= 30°, \quad q_2 = 45° \\
q_1 + q_2 &= 75° \\
c_1 = \cos 30° &= 0.866, \quad s_1 = \sin 30° = 0.500 \\
c_2 = \cos 45° &= 0.707, \quad s_2 = \sin 45° = 0.707 \\
c_{12} = \cos 75° &= 0.259, \quad s_{12} = \sin 75° = 0.966
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 (2 marks): Compute the Jacobian.

$$
\mathbf{J} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\
l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & l_2 c_{12}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\mathbf{J} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-(0.5)(0.5) - (0.4)(0.966) & -(0.4)(0.966) \\
(0.5)(0.866) + (0.4)(0.259) & (0.4)(0.259)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.636 & -0.386 \\
0.537 & 0.104
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 (2 marks): Compute $\mathbf{J}^T$.

$$
\mathbf{J}^T =
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.636 & 0.537 \\
-0.386 & 0.104
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 (2 marks): Compute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.636 & 0.537 \\
-0.386 & 0.104
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
10 \\ -5
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
(-0.636)(10) + (0.537)(-5) \\
(-0.386)(10) + (0.104)(-5)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-6.36 - 2.685 \\
-3.86 - 0.52
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_1 = -9.045\,\text{N·m}, \quad \tau_2 = -4.38\,\text{N·m}}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 (2 marks): Joint torque constraint.

$$
\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2 \leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Vert \boldsymbol{\tau} \Vert^2 \leq 1
$$

Step 2 (2 marks): Substitute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.

$$
(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F})^T (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}) \leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} \leq 1
$$

Step 3 (2 marks): SVD-based reciprocal property.

Using $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$, the velocity ellipsoid (in rotated coords $\dot{\mathbf{x}}'$) is $\sum_i \sigma_i^2 \dot{x}_i'^2 \leq 1$ with semi-axes $\frac{1}{\sigma_i}$. The force ellipsoid becomes $\sum_i \frac{F_i'^2}{1/\sigma_i^2} \leq 1$ with semi-axes $\frac{1}{\sigma_i}$.

Hence the principal axes of both ellipsoids are aligned with $\mathbf{U}$ (left singular vectors), and the semi-axes are **reciprocals**. ∎

Step 4 (1 mark): Conclusion.

> **Reciprocal Property:** $\sigma_i^V = \sigma_i$ (velocity), $\sigma_i^F = 1/\sigma_i$ (force). Thus $w_V \cdot w_F = \prod_i \sigma_i \cdot \prod_i (1/\sigma_i) = 1$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternate Model]

**(a)** Explain the **force manipulability** measure. For a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with $\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the force manipulability index and comment on the configuration quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **force closure** and **null-space wrenches** in manipulator statics. Why do internal wrenches require no actuator effort? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 (2 marks): Definition.

> The **force manipulability** measure quantifies the manipulator's ability to generate end-effector forces in various directions, given bounded joint torques. It is defined as:

$$w_F = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$$

Step 2 (2 marks): Compute $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$.

$$
\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T =
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.6 & -0.4 \\
0.5 & 0.1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.6 & 0.5 \\
-0.4 & 0.1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.36 + 0.16 & -0.30 - 0.04 \\
-0.30 - 0.04 & 0.25 + 0.01
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.52 & -0.34 \\
-0.34 & 0.26
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 (1 mark): Compute the determinant.

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = (0.52)(0.26) - (-0.34)^2 = 0.1352 - 0.1156 = 0.0196
$$

Step 4 (1 mark): Compute $w_F$.

$$w_F = \sqrt{0.0196} = 0.14$$

Step 5 (1 mark): Comment.

> **Comment:** The low manipulability index (0.14) indicates the manipulator is **near a singular configuration**, where force transmission is poor. The force ellipsoid is highly elongated, meaning the manipulator can apply large forces in some directions but very small forces in others. The condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ will be high.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 (2 marks): Force closure definition.

> **Force Closure** is the condition where a manipulator (or gripper) can resist any arbitrary external wrench applied at the end-effector through appropriate joint torques. Mathematically, the columns of $\mathbf{J}^T$ must span the entire 6-D wrench space — i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}^T) = 6$ (or full task dimension).

Step 2 (2 marks): Null-space wrenches.

> **Null-Space Wrenches** are forces $\mathbf{F}_0 \neq \mathbf{0}$ such that $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_0 = \mathbf{0}$. These wrenches exist in the **right null space** of $\mathbf{J}^T$, equivalently the **left null space** of $\mathbf{J}$. They satisfy $\mathbf{F}_0 \in \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J}^T)$.

Step 3 (2 marks): Physical interpretation.

> **Why no actuator effort?** A null-space wrench $\mathbf{F}_0$ produces zero joint torque because it lies in the kernel of $\mathbf{J}^T$. From $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, the actuators register no load. Physically, this represents a **self-balanced internal preload** — for example, two fingers of a gripper squeezing a box generate external clamping force but no net joint torque (in the symmetric case). The motors do not "see" this force; only structural members experience it.

Step 4 (1 mark): Engineering use.

> **Application:** Null-space wrenches are exploited in **grasping theory** (force closure grasps) and **cooperative manipulators** (internal force regulation between two arms holding a common object).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1, Static Forces):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\mathbf{J}$ vs. $\mathbf{J}^T$:** A very common error is writing $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}$ instead of $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$. This loses **2 marks** instantly. **Always** use the **transpose** for force mapping.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $\tau$ for revolute joints is in **N·m** (not N). For prismatic joints, the joint "force" is in **N** (not N·m). Mixing units loses **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Negative torques are physically valid (e.g., gravity compensation). Do not mistakenly "correct" them to positive.
>
> 4. **Singularity oversight:** When the problem asks for force at a configuration, **always check** $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) \neq 0$. If $\det = 0$, the configuration is singular and the question is ill-posed — comment on this in the answer.
>
> 5. **Incomplete derivation in Part B:** Examiners allocate **2 marks** for the virtual work statement, **2 marks** for the kinematic substitution, and **2–3 marks** for the algebraic simplification. Skipping the initial statement of virtual work loses easy marks.
>
> 6. **Force ellipsoid without bounding inequality:** Students often write only $\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F} = 1$ (without "$\leq 1$") and forget the unit-ball assumption on $\boldsymbol{\tau}$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Core Equation:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{F}$ — the static force-torque relation.
- [x] **Origin:** Derived from the **Principle of Virtual Work** for static equilibrium.
- [x] **Jacobian Transpose Property:** Force mapping uses $\mathbf{J}^T$, NOT $\mathbf{J}$ (which is for velocity mapping).
- [x] **Force Ellipsoid:** $\mathbf{F}^T (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) \mathbf{F} \leq 1$, obtained from unit joint-torque ball.
- [x] **Velocity-Force Duality:** Principal axes are the same; semi-axes are reciprocals.
- [x] **Force Manipulability:** $w_F = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$. Higher = better force transmission.
- [x] **Condition Number:** $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min} = 1$ (isotropic, ideal), $\kappa \to \infty$ (singular).
- [x] **Singularities:** $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ → manipulator cannot resist forces in some directions.
- [x] **Null-Space Wrenches:** $\mathbf{F}_0 \in \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J}^T) \Rightarrow \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (internal preloads).
- [x] **Force Closure:** Requires $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}^T) = $ full task-space dimension.
- [x] **Units:** Revolute joint torque $\to$ N·m; Prismatic joint force $\to$ N.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Negative torques are valid (e.g., gravity support).
- [x] **Real-World Apps:** Motor sizing, force control, grasp planning, surgical robotics, humanoid balance.
- [x] **Course Outcome Linkage (CO1/KTU 2024):** Static force analysis supports **CO1: Apply principles of statics and kinematics to derive manipulator force/torque relationships** (Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze).
- [x] **Module Context:** This topic is a **prerequisite** for Module 2 (Dynamics — Newton-Euler / Lagrangian formulations), where joint torques will include inertial and Coriolis terms in addition to the static component.
- [x] **Exam Frequency:** Static force mapping ($\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$) appears in **almost every KTU end-semester paper** of this course — guaranteed 7-mark question.

---

**END OF NOTES — Static Forces in Manipulators (Module 1, PERAT523)**

topics.title: Jacobian Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jacobian Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jacobian Matrix (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Jacobian Matrix** of a robotic manipulator is a time-varying matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ that linearly maps the **joint-space velocity** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to the **task-space (Cartesian) velocity** $\mathbf{v}$ of the end-effector. Mathematically:
> $$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$
> where $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of $n$ joint variables, $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the end-effector linear and/or angular velocity, and $m$ is the dimension of the task space (typically $m = 6$ for spatial manipulators: 3 linear + 3 angular).

For a **6-DOF spatial manipulator**, the Jacobian is a **6×n matrix** (often $6 \times 6$ for non-redundant arms):

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{J}_v(\mathbf{q}) \\
\mathbf{J}_\omega(\mathbf{q})
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{J}_v$ → **Linear velocity Jacobian** (top 3 rows)
- $\mathbf{J}_\omega$ → **Angular velocity Jacobian** (bottom 3 rows)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Steering & Gear Ratio" Analogy**
> Imagine you are driving a car. The steering wheel and pedals are your "joints." The car's actual speed and direction on the road is the "end-effector velocity." The **Jacobian is like the gear-ratio and steering-translation lookup table** that tells you: *"If I turn the steering wheel by 1° AND press the accelerator by 1 cm, what will be the resulting speed and turning rate of the car?"*
> - When the car is moving straight (no steering input), the Jacobian is simple — acceleration just maps to forward speed.
> - When the car is in a tight turn, the same steering input causes a **much larger change** in the car's direction. The Jacobian captures this **configuration-dependent sensitivity**.

In robotics terms: the same joint velocity $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ produces **different end-effector speeds** depending on the current pose of the arm. The Jacobian encodes that pose-dependent sensitivity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - The Jacobian is **dimensionless** when joint angles are in radians and lengths in consistent SI units (meters).
> - **Singularity condition** (must be checked in every exam): $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ or equivalently $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < m$.
> - Standard KTU assumption: **Newton-Euler** or **DH parameterization** is used to derive $\mathbf{J}$.
> - The Jacobian relates two vector spaces → it is the **best linear approximation** of the nonlinear forward kinematics map $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$, computed via partial derivatives.

## 1.4 Physical & Geometric Meaning of the Jacobian Columns

Each column $i$ of the Jacobian corresponds to the **instantaneous twist (linear + angular velocity)** that joint $i$ would induce at the end-effector **if all other joints were locked**. This geometric interpretation is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Standard Methods to Derive the Jacobian

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method 1 — Direct Differential Method (Velocity Propagation):**
> Differentiate the forward kinematics equation $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ with respect to time:
> $$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial f(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method 2 — Screw / Cross-Product Method (Geometric, KTU-Favorite):**
> For a **revolute joint** about axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ with position $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ of the joint and $\mathbf{o}_n$ of the end-effector:
> $$\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$$
> $$\mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$$
> For a **prismatic joint** along axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$:
> $$\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}, \qquad \mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{0}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Velocity mapping | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ |
| 2 | Revolute joint column (linear) | $\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$ | Cross product, m·rad/s |
| 3 | Revolute joint column (angular) | $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ | Unit vector, rad/s |
| 4 | Prismatic joint column (linear) | $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ | Unit vector, m/s |
| 5 | Prismatic joint column (angular) | $\mathbf{0}_{3\times 1}$ | Zero vector |
| 6 | Static force relation | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ | $\mathbf{F}$ = wrench at EE |
| 7 | Singularity condition | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ | Loss of DOF |
| 8 | Manipulability index | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ | Yoshikawa's measure |
| 9 | Differential kinematics | $\delta\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\delta\mathbf{q}$ | First-order Taylor approx |
| 10 | Inverse velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$ (square, non-singular case) | KTU frequently asked |
| 11 | Pseudo-inverse (redundant) | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^\dagger \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{v}$ | Moore–Penrose |
| 12 | DH frame origin propagation | ${}^{0}\mathbf{o}_i = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_i \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}$ | For column formulas |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT** write absolute-value bars `|x|` or determinants `|J|` inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\det(\mathbf{J})` instead — otherwise the table parser breaks.

## 2.3 Why the Jacobian Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Real-time motion control**: Industrial robots (KUKA, ABB, Fanuc) compute $\mathbf{J}$ every 1–4 ms inside their servo loop to convert commanded Cartesian paths $\mathbf{v}_d$ into joint commands $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^* = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}_d$.
> - **Singularity avoidance**: Factory pre-programmed trajectories avoid configurations where $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ (e.g., fully extended arm).
> - **Force control & haptics**: The transpose $\mathbf{J}^T$ transforms end-effector forces into joint torques, used in assembly and polishing tasks.
> - **Bilateral teleoperation** (surgical robots like Da Vinci): The master–slave velocity mapping is governed by the Jacobian.
> - **Mobile manipulation & legged robots** (Boston Dynamics Atlas): Whole-body controllers rely on task-priority Jacobian stacks.

## 2.4 Logical Derivation Flow — "Why & How"

- **Why** differentiate FK? Because velocity is the time derivative of position; FK gives position, so its time derivative gives velocity.
- **How** does the chain rule apply? Because FK is a composition of rotations and translations, each depending on one joint variable. The chain rule yields a sum of partial-derivative terms — one per joint — which naturally form the columns of $\mathbf{J}$.
- **Why** cross product appears for revolute joints? Because a rigid body rotating about an axis $\mathbf{z}$ with angular velocity $\dot{q}\,\mathbf{z}$ produces a linear velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$ at any point offset by $\mathbf{r}$ from the axis. This is a **pure kinematics fact**, not a definition.
- **How** to handle base/mount offsets? All vectors $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ must be expressed in the **same global (base) frame**, denoted with a left superscript ${}^0$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Forward Kinematics to Jacobian (General Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting point:** Forward kinematics gives the end-effector pose as a $4\times 4$ homogeneous transform:
> $$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q}) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_n(\mathbf{q}) = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1(\mathbf{q}_1)\,{}^{1}\mathbf{T}_2(\mathbf{q}_2)\cdots{}^{n-1}\mathbf{T}_n(\mathbf{q}_n)$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. time.** Apply the time derivative:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[f(\mathbf{q})\big] = \frac{\partial f(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\,\frac{d\mathbf{q}}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the chain rule structure.** Since $\mathbf{x}$ is a function of $n$ joint variables, the total derivative expands as:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f(\mathbf{q})}{\partial q_i}\,\dot{q}_i
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Jacobian.** Writing the partial derivatives as columns of a matrix:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} =
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial q_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial q_n} \\
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial q_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial q_n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial q_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial q_n}
\end{bmatrix}
}_{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}}
\,
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_n\end{bmatrix}
}_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.** Hence $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Each column $i$ of $\mathbf{J}$ describes the end-effector velocity contribution from a unit velocity of joint $i$ alone.

> This is the **first-order Taylor approximation**:
> $$\delta\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\delta\mathbf{q}$$
> valid for infinitesimal motions — a key KTU short-answer point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Screw-Based Jacobian for a Single Revolute Joint

Consider joint $i$ (revolute, axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$, position $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$), with end-effector at $\mathbf{o}_n$.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity contribution.** A revolute joint rotates with $\dot{q}_i$ about $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$, so:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{n,i} = \dot{q}_i\,\mathbf{z}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity at end-effector.** Using rigid-body kinematics $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$ where $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{n,i} = \dot{q}_i\,\big(\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the per-joint Jacobian column.** Dividing both sides by $\dot{q}_i$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}), \qquad
\mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Stack to form full Jacobian.** For $n$ joints:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{z}_0 \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_0) & \mathbf{z}_1 \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_1) & \cdots & \mathbf{z}_{n-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{n-1}) \\
\mathbf{z}_0 & \mathbf{z}_1 & \cdots & \mathbf{z}_{n-1}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

(all revolute). For mixed manipulators, swap in prismatic columns where appropriate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: 2-Link Planar Manipulator (RR)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **standard KTU 14-mark problem**. Below is the full step-by-step.

**Setup:** Two revolute joints, link lengths $l_1, l_2$. End-effector position:
$$
x = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2), \qquad
y = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate x w.r.t. time.**

$$
\dot{x} = -l_1\sin q_1\,\dot{q}_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\,(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Group $\dot{q}_1$ and $\dot{q}_2$ terms.**

$$
\dot{x} = \big[-l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_1 \;+\; \big[-l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate y w.r.t. time.**

$$
\dot{y} = l_1\cos q_1\,\dot{q}_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\,(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Group y terms.**

$$
\dot{y} = \big[l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_1 \;+\; \big[l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_2
$$

**Step 5 — Write in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x}\\ \dot{y}\end{bmatrix}
=
\underbrace{
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\;\;l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & \;\;l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
}_{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})}
\begin{bmatrix}\dot{q}_1\\ \dot{q}_2\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute determinant for singularity.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = \big[-l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\big[l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big] - \big[-l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\big[l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big]
$$

Simplifying with the identity $\sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B = \sin(A-B)$:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2
$$

**Step 7 — Singularity condition.** $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sin q_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$ — the **fully extended** or **fully folded** configurations. **This is the KTU gold-standard answer.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Static Force Relationship ($\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}$)

**Principle of Virtual Work:** For a small virtual displacement consistent with constraints:

$$
\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \delta W_{\text{task}} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^T \delta\mathbf{x}
$$

Substitute $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\delta\mathbf{q}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{J}\delta\mathbf{q} = (\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F})^T \delta\mathbf{q}
$$

Equating coefficients (valid for arbitrary $\delta\mathbf{q}$):

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}}
$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** between end-effector wrench $\mathbf{F}$ and joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ — heavily tested in KTU Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Jacobian in a Robotic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trajectory Planner\nDesired Cartesian Path x_d] --> B[Cartesian Velocity v_d]
    B --> C[Inverse Jacobian Solver\nq_dot = J_inv times v_d]
    C --> D[Joint Velocity Commands q_dot]
    D --> E[Joint Servo Controllers]
    E --> F[Robot Actuators\nTorques tau = J_transpose F]
    F --> G[Robot Manipulator\nPhysical Plant]
    G --> H[Encoder Feedback\nJoint Angles q]
    H --> I[Forward Kinematics FK]
    I --> J[Current End Effector Pose x]
    J --> K[Compute Jacobian J of q]
    K --> C
    K --> F
    style A fill:#D6E9FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000
    style K fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style F fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style G fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#5B2C6F,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** The Jacobian is **recomputed every control cycle** from the current joint angles. It is the **bridge** between the high-level task space (Cartesian world) and the low-level joint space (motor commands). Both the velocity loop (via $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$) and the torque loop (via $\mathbf{J}^T$) depend on it.

## 4.2 Per-Joint Contribution (Column-wise Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DH Parameters Table] --> B[Compute T_i for i equals 0 to n]
    B --> C[Extract o_i and z_i for each frame]
    C --> D{Joint Type}
    D -->|Revolute| E1[Jv_i equals z_i_minus_1 cross o_n_minus_o_i_minus_1]
    D -->|Prismatic| E2[Jv_i equals z_i_minus_1]
    E1 --> F1[Jw_i equals z_i_minus_1]
    E2 --> F2[Jw_i equals zero vector]
    F1 --> G[Stack Columns to Form J of q]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Validate rank and determinant]
    H --> I{Singular?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Flag Singularity and Replan]
    I -->|No| K[Output Jacobian Matrix]
    style A fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    style G fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    style I fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C65911,color:#000
    style K fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#1F3864,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Chain — 2-Link Planar RR Manipulator (Reference Frame Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[World Frame 0] -->|q1 rotation about z0| J1[Joint 1 at origin o0]
    J1 -->|link l1| J2[Joint 2 at o1]
    J2 -->|q2 rotation about z1| EE[End Effector at o2]
    EE -.->|Position x,y| V[Task Space Velocity v equals J q_dot]
    J1 -.->|Column 1| V
    J2 -.->|Column 2| V
    style W fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2E75B6,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000
    style J2 fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000
    style EE fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    style V fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C65911,color:#000
```
