<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator and state its physical significance.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper SET-A]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is a multi-variable generalization of the ordinary derivative. It linearly relates the **joint-space velocities** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to the **task-space (end-effector) velocities** $\mathbf{v}$:

$$
\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Physical significance:**
1. It maps small joint motions $\delta\mathbf{q}$ to small end-effector motions $\delta\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{q}$ (first-order approximation of FK).
2. Each column represents the **instantaneous twist** induced at the end-effector by a unit velocity of the corresponding joint (geometric interpretation).
3. Its **rank** indicates the available degrees of freedom at the current configuration; **rank deficiency** = singularity → loss of motion in some direction.

---

### **Q2. What is a kinematic singularity? How is it identified using the Jacobian?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kinematic singularity** is a robot configuration in which the manipulator **loses one or more degrees of freedom** in the task space — i.e., the end-effector cannot move in certain directions regardless of the joint velocities applied.

**Identification using the Jacobian:**

For a square Jacobian ($m = n$):
$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0
$$

For a general Jacobian (rectangular, $m \neq n$):
$$
\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0 \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad \text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < \min(m,n)
$$

**Example:** For a 2-link planar RR arm, $\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 = 0$ when $q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$ — i.e., the **fully extended** or **fully folded** configurations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** For a 2-link planar RR manipulator with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$, derive the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ relating joint velocities to end-effector linear velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the derived Jacobian, determine the **singular configurations** of the manipulator. Also, compute the joint velocities $\dot{q}_1, \dot{q}_2$ required to produce an end-effector linear velocity of $\mathbf{v} = [0.5 \;\text{m/s},\; 0\;\text{m/s}]^T$ at the configuration $q_1 = 30^\circ, q_2 = 45^\circ$ with $l_1 = 1\,\text{m}, l_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the forward kinematics.** End-effector position:

$$
x = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
y = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $x$ w.r.t. time.**

$$
\dot{x} = -l_1\sin q_1\,\dot{q}_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{x} = \big[-l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_1 + \big[-l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $y$ w.r.t. time.**

$$
\dot{y} = l_1\cos q_1\,\dot{q}_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = \big[l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_1 + \big[l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\big]\dot{q}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Express in matrix form $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.**

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
-l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\;\;l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & \;\;l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\det(\mathbf{J})$.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2
$$

**Step 2 — Singular configurations.**

$$
l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; q_2 = 0^\circ \;\text{(fully extended)}\quad\text{or}\quad q_2 = 180^\circ \;\text{(fully folded)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** at $q_1 = 30^\circ, q_2 = 45^\circ, l_1 = 1, l_2 = 0.5$:

$q_1 + q_2 = 75^\circ$

Row 1: $[-1\sin 30^\circ - 0.5\sin 75^\circ,\; -0.5\sin 75^\circ]$
$\sin 30^\circ = 0.5,\; \sin 75^\circ \approx 0.9659$

Row 1: $[-0.5 - 0.48295,\; -0.48295] = [-0.98295,\; -0.48295]$

Row 2: $[1\cos 30^\circ + 0.5\cos 75^\circ,\; 0.5\cos 75^\circ]$
$\cos 30^\circ \approx 0.8660,\; \cos 75^\circ \approx 0.2588$

Row 2: $[0.8660 + 0.1294,\; 0.1294] = [0.9954,\; 0.1294]$

**Step 4 — Form Jacobian and invert:**

$$
\mathbf{J} =
\begin{bmatrix}-0.98295 & -0.48295\\ 0.9954 & 0.1294\end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-0.98295)(0.1294) - (-0.48295)(0.9954) = -0.1272 + 0.4806 = 0.3534
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$.**

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.3534}\begin{bmatrix}0.1294 & 0.48295\\ -0.9954 & -0.98295\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3662 & 1.3666\\ -2.8167 & -2.7813\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3662 & 1.3666\\ -2.8167 & -2.7813\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0.5\\ 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.1831\\ -1.4083\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{q}_1 \approx 0.183\,\text{rad/s},\quad \dot{q}_2 \approx -1.408\,\text{rad/s}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **geometric (screw-theory) method** to compute the Jacobian columns for a manipulator having both **revolute and prismatic joints**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-DOF manipulator (RPR configuration) with DH parameters as given below, compute the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ using the cross-product method. Identify its singular configurations. **(7 marks)**

| i | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|---|------------|-------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $q_1$ | 0 | 0 | 90° |
| 2 | 0 | $q_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | $q_3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Geometric (Screw-Theory) Method:**

For each joint $i$, identify:
- the **axis of motion** $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (expressed in the **base frame**)
- the **position of the joint origin** $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ (in the base frame)
- the **end-effector origin** $\mathbf{o}_n$ (in the base frame)

**For a revolute joint (axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$):**
- Linear velocity at EE: $\mathbf{v}_{n,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$
- Angular velocity at EE: $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{n,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad \mathbf{J}_i =
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})\\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1}\end{bmatrix}
\quad \text{(revolute)}
$$

**For a prismatic joint (axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$):**
- Linear velocity at EE: $\mathbf{v}_{n,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$
- Angular velocity at EE: $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{n,i} = \mathbf{0}$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad \mathbf{J}_i =
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{z}_{i-1}\\ \mathbf{0}\end{bmatrix}
\quad \text{(prismatic)}
$$

**Final Jacobian** is the horizontal stacking of all columns:
$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{J}_1 \vert \mathbf{J}_2 \vert \cdots \vert \mathbf{J}_n\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute intermediate transforms.**

- ${}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1$: rotation about $z_0$ by $q_1$, then about $x_1$ by $90°$:

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos q_1 & 0 & \sin q_1 & 0 \\
\sin q_1 & 0 & -\cos q_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$\mathbf{o}_0 = \mathbf{0},\quad \mathbf{z}_0 = [0,0,1]^T$

- ${}^0\mathbf{T}_2 = {}^0\mathbf{T}_1 \cdot {}^1\mathbf{T}_2$ where ${}^1\mathbf{T}_2$ is pure translation along $z_1$ by $q_2$:

$$
{}^0\mathbf{T}_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos q_1 & 0 & \sin q_1 & q_2\sin q_1 \\
\sin q_1 & 0 & -\cos q_1 & -q_2\cos q_1 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$\mathbf{o}_1 = \mathbf{o}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (joint 1 at base), $\mathbf{z}_1 = [\sin q_1, -\cos q_1, 0]^T$
$\mathbf{o}_2 = [q_2\sin q_1, -q_2\cos q_1, 0]^T$, $\mathbf{z}_2 = [\sin q_1, -\cos q_1, 0]^T$

- ${}^0\mathbf{T}_3$: rotation about $z_2$ by $q_3$ (no offset):

$$
\mathbf{o}_3 = [q_2\sin q_1, -q_2\cos q_1, 0]^T,\quad \mathbf{z}_3 = [\sin q_1, -\cos q_1, 0]^T
$$

**Step 2 — Construct Jacobian columns.**

- **Joint 1 (Revolute):**
$$
\mathbf{J}_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{z}_0 \times \mathbf{o}_3\\ \mathbf{z}_0\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}-q_2\cos q_1 \\ -q_2\sin q_1 \\ 0 \\ 0\\ 0\\ 1\end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Joint 2 (Prismatic):**
$$
\mathbf{J}_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{z}_1\\ \mathbf{0}\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}\sin q_1 \\ -\cos q_1 \\ 0 \\ 0\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Joint 3 (Revolute):** Since $\mathbf{o}_3 - \mathbf{o}_2 = \mathbf{0}$:
$$
\mathbf{J}_3 =
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}\\ \mathbf{z}_2\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\0\\ \sin q_1\\ -\cos q_1\\ 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Stack columns.**

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
-q_2\cos q_1 & \sin q_1 & 0 \\
-q_2\sin q_1 & -\cos q_1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \sin q_1 \\
0 & 0 & -\cos q_1 \\
1 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify singularities.** The top-left $3\times 2$ block is rank-deficient (always — top-right column has zero $z$-component by geometry). Note $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) \leq 5$ since the **third row of the linear part is all zeros** → the EE **cannot move in the $z$-direction** regardless of joint motion → permanent singularity in the $z$-direction.

This is expected for the given DH table — the manipulator's task space is effectively **planar in the $xy$-plane plus 1-DOF rotation**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting frame consistency**: Vectors $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}, \mathbf{o}_{i-1}, \mathbf{o}_n$ **must all be expressed in the same (base) frame**. Mixing frames gives wrong answers and zero credit.
> 2. **Sign errors in cross products**: Carefully apply right-hand rule. A common mistake: writing $\mathbf{o}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ instead of $\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{o}_{i-1}$.
> 3. **Mixing up revolute vs prismatic formulas**: The angular row $\mathbf{J}_{\omega}$ for a prismatic joint is **zero**, not $\mathbf{z}$. This is the #1 most common error.
> 4. **Skipping the singularity analysis**: Even if $\mathbf{J}$ is correctly derived, KTU examiners allocate **2–3 marks** explicitly for the singularity discussion. Always compute $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)$ and interpret the physical meaning.
> 5. **Not stating units**: $\mathbf{J}$ entries have mixed units (length for revolute, dimensionless for prismatic). Mention this in derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Jacobian Matrix**

- **Core equation**: $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — velocity mapping; $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}$ — static force mapping.
- **Jacobian is a function of configuration $\mathbf{q}$** — recompute every control cycle.
- **Two derivation methods**:
  1. Differential (differentiate FK)
  2. Geometric / cross-product (column-wise, screw theory)
- **Column interpretation**: Column $i$ = end-effector twist caused by unit velocity of joint $i$ alone.
- **Revolute joint column**: $\big[\mathbf{z}_{i-1}\times(\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1});\; \mathbf{z}_{i-1}\big]$.
- **Prismatic joint column**: $\big[\mathbf{z}_{i-1};\; \mathbf{0}\big]$.
- **All vectors must be in the base (world) frame** — major source of mistakes.
- **Singularity** = rank deficiency of $\mathbf{J}$. Test: $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ for non-square cases.
- **2-link RR planar example**: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$ → singular at $q_2 = 0, \pi$.
- **Redundant manipulators** ($n > 6$): use Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{J}^\dagger = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1}$.
- **Manipulability index** (Yoshikawa): $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ — measures distance from singularity.
- **Linearization**: $\delta\mathbf{x} \approx \mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{q}$ — first-order Taylor approximation.
- **Industrial use**: Real-time servo loop, force control, teleoperation, singularity avoidance.
- **Units**: $\mathbf{J}$ entries are either dimensionless (prismatic) or have units of length (revolute, for linear part).
- **KTU favorite exam tip**: Always end a Jacobian derivation with a singularity check and physical interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacobians in the Force Domain: Cartesian Transformation of Velocities and Static Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jacobians in the Force Domain: Cartesian Transformation of Velocities and Static Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of serial-chain robotic manipulators, the **Jacobian Matrix** $\mathbf{J(\theta)}$ is a configuration-dependent linear operator that maps the **joint-space velocity vector** $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ to the corresponding **Cartesian (task) space velocity vector** $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ of the end-effector.

Mathematically, this velocity mapping is written as:

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J(\theta)}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

In the **force domain**, the Jacobian is used to map forces and moments acting at the end-effector ($\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$) to the equivalent generalized joint torques ($\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$) required to maintain static equilibrium:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\,\mathbf{F}$$

The superscript "$\mathbf{T}$" denotes the **matrix transpose** operation, which arises directly from the **Principle of Virtual Work** (also known as the principle of virtual displacements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Jacobian is the **fundamental bridge** between the joint space and the Cartesian space. For a generic $n$-DOF manipulator operating in an $m$-dimensional Cartesian workspace ($m = 2$ for planar, $m = 3$ for spatial translation, $m = 6$ for full spatial pose), the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is an $m \times n$ matrix.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are steering a car with a steering wheel while pushing a heavy crate:

- The **steering wheel angle** is analogous to the **joint variable** $\theta_i$.
- The **velocity of the car** is the **Cartesian (task-space) velocity** $\mathbf{V}$.
- The **Jacobian** tells you *how much* the car moves for *each degree* you turn the wheel. It is essentially a **multi-variable sensitivity map**.

Now, for the **force domain**:
- If the car gets stuck in mud and the front wheels experience a resistive force $\mathbf{F}$ (a *Cartesian force*), the **equivalent torque** the engine must apply at the steering column is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$.
- The **transpose** of the Jacobian converts *spatial pushing forces* into *rotational torques*, just as a mechanic's wrench converts a long-arm push into a powerful twisting force at a bolt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Torque** is measured in **Newton-meters (N·m)** in SI units.
> - **Force** is measured in **Newtons (N)**.
> - **Angular velocity** is measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
> - **Linear velocity** is measured in **meters per second (m/s)**.

---

## 1.3 Components of the Cartesian Velocity Vector

For a general 6-DOF spatial manipulator, the Cartesian velocity vector combines **linear** and **angular** components:

$$\mathbf{V} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{v} = [v_x, v_y, v_z]^{T}$ is the **linear velocity** of the end-effector origin (m/s).
- $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z]^{T}$ is the **angular velocity** of the end-effector frame (rad/s).

The corresponding 6D Cartesian force/moment vector is:

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{f} \\ \mathbf{n} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$$

where $\mathbf{f}$ is a pure force and $\mathbf{n}$ is a pure moment (torque vector).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics as the Starting Point
The Cartesian position and orientation of the end-effector are functions of the joint variables:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the task-space coordinate vector and $\mathbf{K}(\cdot)$ is the forward kinematics mapping.

### Step 2 — Differentiation with Respect to Time
Differentiating $\mathbf{x}$ with respect to time, applying the **chain rule of multivariable calculus**:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

By definition, the coefficient matrix is the **Jacobian**:

$$\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{\partial \mathbf{K}(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

### Step 3 — Velocity Relationship (The Velocity Domain)
The Cartesian velocity is linearly related to joint velocity through the Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful?**
> This linearized relationship enables **resolved-rate motion control**, **singularity analysis** (when $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$), and **inverse kinematics algorithms** using the **Newton–Raphson** method.

### Step 4 — Static Force Relationship (The Force Domain)
For a **static system in equilibrium** (no acceleration, no inertia), the principle of virtual work states that the work done by joint torques in a virtual joint displacement must equal the work done by Cartesian forces in the corresponding virtual Cartesian displacement:

$$\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \delta W_{\text{Cartesian}}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}$$

Since $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$, substitution yields:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}$$

Transposing both sides:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **force-domain Jacobian** is simply the **transpose** of the **velocity-domain Jacobian**. There is *no separate* force Jacobian — the same matrix, transposed, serves both domains.

### Step 5 — Why the Transpose? (Conceptual Justification)
- The Jacobian maps **small joint motions** to **small Cartesian motions** (forward mapping).
- The transpose maps **Cartesian wrenches** (forces + moments) back to **joint torques** (inverse mapping).
- This is mathematically the **adjoint** of the velocity mapping under the **inner product** (dot product) structure of virtual work.

### Step 6 — Engineering Applications
| Application | Domain Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolved-rate control** | Velocity | Compute $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{V}_{d}$ |
| **Singularity detection** | Velocity | Compute $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})$ |
| **Static force control** | Force | Compute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ |
| **Compliant motion** | Force | Regulate contact forces via $\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{J}^{T})^{\dagger}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ |
| **Haptics / teleoperation** | Both | Bilateral coupling of master–slave systems |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ | Velocity transformation (forward) | $\mathbf{V}$: m/s, rad/s |
| 2 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{V}$ | Inverse velocity mapping (square Jacobian) | Valid only when $m = n$ and $\mathbf{J}$ non-singular |
| 3 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{V}$ | Pseudoinverse for redundant ($m < n$) manipulators | $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ | Static force transformation | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$: N·m, $\mathbf{F}$: N, N·m |
| 5 | $\mathbf{F} = (\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Inverse static force mapping | Square, non-singular Jacobian |
| 6 | $\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}$ | Principle of virtual work | Scalar energy equivalence |
| 7 | $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | Infinitesimal displacement mapping | First-order kinematic relation |
| 8 | $J_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial x_i}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Element-wise definition of Jacobian | $i$ = Cartesian row, $j$ = joint column |
| 9 | $\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_v \\ \mathbf{J}_\omega \end{bmatrix}$ | Partitioned form (linear + angular) | $\mathbf{J}_v$: $3 \times n$, $\mathbf{J}_\omega$: $3 \times n$ |
| 10 | $\text{Singularity when } \det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$ | Rank loss condition | Scalar = 0 indicates a singular configuration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pitfall Reminder:** The Jacobian is **NOT a constant matrix**. It is a **function of joint positions** $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. Evaluated at a new configuration, its entries change.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Jacobian from Forward Kinematics (Position Form)

Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$. The forward kinematics is:

$$x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time using the chain rule:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1}\dot{\theta}_1 + \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2}\dot{\theta}_2$$

Computing each partial derivative explicitly:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

Stacking the partials into the Jacobian matrix:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

The velocity relationship becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The Jacobian can also be interpreted geometrically as the stack of $z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$ vectors for revolute joints, or simply $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ columns for prismatic joints. This is the **screw-axis / Plücker-coordinate** form used in modern robotics texts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force-Domain Relationship from the Principle of Virtual Work

We rigorously derive $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ from first principles.

**Starting point — Principle of Virtual Work:**
For a static system, the total virtual work done by all applied forces through any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints must vanish. Equivalently, the work done by joint torques must equal the work done by end-effector forces:

$$\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \delta W_{\text{task}}$$

**Step 1 — Express joint-side virtual work:**

$$\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_i\,\delta\theta_i$$

**Step 2 — Express task-side virtual work:**

$$\delta W_{\text{task}} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} F_i\,\delta x_i$$

**Step 3 — Equate and use the kinematic constraint** $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Group the $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ terms (valid for arbitrary virtual displacement):**

$$\left(\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} - \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\right)\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = 0$$

Since $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is arbitrary (any small joint motion is kinematically admissible in a static analysis), the bracketed term must vanish:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}$$

**Step 5 — Take the transpose of both sides:**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Equivalence (Dynamic Consistency Check)

The same relationship can be derived from **power conservation**. The mechanical power delivered by joint actuators is:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

The mechanical power delivered to the environment by the end-effector is:

$$P_{\text{task}} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{V}$$

For a lossless kinematic chain (no friction, no energy storage in the Jacobian itself), these must be equal:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{V}$$

Substituting $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

$$\left(\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} - \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\right)\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = 0$$

For arbitrary $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$, this again yields:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This power-based derivation reinforces the idea that the *same Jacobian matrix* governs both velocity and force transformations — a deep duality in robot kinematics.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: 2-DOF Planar Arm

**Given:** $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $\theta_1 = 30^{\circ}$, $\theta_2 = 45^{\circ}$.

**Step A — Compute the Jacobian entries** (in radians: $\theta_1 = \pi/6$, $\theta_2 = \pi/4$):

$$J_{11} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = -\sin(30^{\circ}) - 0.5\sin(75^{\circ})$$

$$J_{11} = -0.5 - 0.5(0.9659) = -0.5 - 0.4830 = -0.9830$$

$$J_{12} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = -0.5\sin(75^{\circ}) = -0.4830$$

$$J_{21} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = \cos(30^{\circ}) + 0.5\cos(75^{\circ})$$

$$J_{21} = 0.8660 + 0.5(0.2588) = 0.8660 + 0.1294 = 0.9954$$

$$J_{22} = L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0.5\cos(75^{\circ}) = 0.1294$$

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step B — Compute Jacobian Transpose for Force Domain:**

$$\mathbf{J}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & 0.9954 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Apply a Cartesian force** $\mathbf{F} = [10\,\text{N}, 0\,\text{N}]^{T}$ (horizontal push):

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & 0.9954 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -9.830 \\ -4.830 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N·m}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 N horizontal push on the end-effector is equivalent to a torque of $\approx -9.83$ N·m at joint 1 and $\approx -4.83$ N·m at joint 2. The negative signs indicate the required motor torques oppose the disturbance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Velocity vs. Force Domain Mapping

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **dual role of the Jacobian** in transforming signals between joint space and Cartesian space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    jointSpace["Joint Space nDOF"]
    cartSpace["Cartesian Space mDOF"]
    JMat["Jacobian Matrix J of theta"]
    JTMat["Transpose Jacobian J Transpose"]
    jointVel["Joint Velocity theta dot"]
    cartVel["Cartesian Velocity V"]
    cartForce["Cartesian Wrench F"]
    jointTorque["Joint Torque tau"]

    jointVel --> JMat
    JMat --> cartVel
    cartVel --> cartSpace
    jointSpace --> jointVel

    cartForce --> JTMat
    JTMat --> jointTorque
    jointTorque --> jointSpace
    cartForce --> cartSpace

    VWEq["Principle of Virtual Work delta W joint equals delta W task"]
    JMat -. maps .- VWEq
    JTMat -. maps .- VWEq
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Forward Kinematics to Force Control

The following diagram shows the **complete information flow** from joint angles to the force domain, with the Jacobian acting as the central operator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Joint Angles theta1 to thetan"]
    B["Forward Kinematics K of theta gives x and orientation R"]
    C["Compute Jacobian J from partial derivatives"]
    D["Velocity Domain Output V equals J times theta dot"]
    E["Force Domain Output tau equals J transpose times F"]
    F["Application: Resolved Rate Control"]
    G["Application: Static Force Control"]
    H["Application: Singularity Detection det J J transpose"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    C --> H
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Mapping Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JS["Joint Space"]
        thetaVec["theta dot in Rn"]
        tauVec["tau in Rn"]
    end

    subgraph CS["Cartesian Space"]
        vVec["V in Rm linear plus angular"]
        fVec["F in Rm force plus moment"]
    end

    subgraph OP["Jacobian Operator"]
        Jblock["J theta is m by n"]
        JTblock["J transpose theta is n by m"]
    end

    thetaVec -->|"forward map: V = J theta_dot"| Jblock
    Jblock -->|"yields"| vVec

    fVec -->|"inverse map: tau = J transpose F"| JTblock
    JTblock -->|"yields"| tauVec

    JS --- OP
    OP --- CS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **Top path** (velocity): the Jacobian maps *from* joint space *to* Cartesian space (forward direction of motion).
> - **Bottom path** (force): the transpose Jacobian maps *from* Cartesian space *to* joint space (inverse direction of force).
> - Both directions share the **same underlying matrix** $\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$, ensuring mathematical duality and energy consistency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator. Explain its significance in mapping velocities from joint space to Cartesian space.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Jacobian $\mathbf{J(\theta)}$ is an $m \times n$ matrix whose $(i,j)$-th element is the partial derivative of the $i$-th Cartesian coordinate of the end-effector with respect to the $j$-th joint variable: $J_{ij} = \partial x_i / \partial \theta_j$.

It is significant because it provides a **first-order linear mapping** between joint velocities and Cartesian velocities: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J(\theta)}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$. This linearization is essential for **resolved-rate control**, **inverse kinematics via Newton–Raphson iteration**, and **singularity analysis** (when $\mathbf{J}$ loses rank, the manipulator cannot move in certain Cartesian directions).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain the relationship between joint torques and end-effector forces using the principle of virtual work.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a manipulator in static equilibrium, the **Principle of Virtual Work** states that the virtual work performed by joint torques through a virtual joint displacement $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ equals the virtual work done by end-effector forces through the corresponding Cartesian virtual displacement $\delta\mathbf{x}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}$$

Since $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$, substituting and using the arbitrariness of $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$$

Thus, the joint torques required to balance a Cartesian wrench are obtained by multiplying the wrench by the **transpose** of the Jacobian evaluated at the current configuration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Jacobian matrix for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ using the differential kinematics approach. Clearly state the velocity relationship. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the principle of virtual work, derive the static force relationship $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ and discuss its physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:**
$$x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives** to form the Jacobian entries:

$$J_{11} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$J_{12} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$J_{21} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$J_{22} = L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the Jacobian:**

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity relationship:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This first-order differential mapping allows joint velocities to be transformed into end-effector Cartesian velocities. **(7 Marks fully allocated)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Virtual work done by joint torques:**
$$\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \tau_1\delta\theta_1 + \tau_2\delta\theta_2 = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Virtual work done by end-effector Cartesian forces:**
$$\delta W_{\text{Cart}} = F_x\delta x + F_y\delta y = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Equate virtual works** (Principle of Virtual Work for a static system):
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the kinematic constraint** $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$:
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\,\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$** (arbitrary virtual displacement):
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}$$

**Step 6 — Transpose:**
$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- This relationship allows a designer to convert **task-space forces** (e.g., contact forces during assembly) into equivalent **joint torques** required from the actuators.
- It forms the foundation of **force control** and **hybrid position/force control** strategies.
- The transpose reflects the **adjoint mapping** between motion and effort in the configuration manifold. **(7 Marks fully allocated)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.75$ m, with $\theta_1 = 60^{\circ}$ and $\theta_2 = 30^{\circ}$. Compute the Jacobian matrix and find the joint torques required to balance a Cartesian force of $\mathbf{F} = [5\,\text{N}, \; 0\,\text{N}]^{T}$ at the end-effector. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to (a), determine:
- (i) The Cartesian velocity when $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1$ rad/s and $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.5$ rad/s. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The determinant of the Jacobian and comment on whether the configuration is singular. **(4 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Convert angles to radians:**
$$\theta_1 = \pi/3 = 60^{\circ}, \quad \theta_2 = \pi/6 = 30^{\circ}$$

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the four Jacobian entries:**

$$J_{11} = -L_1\sin 60^{\circ} - L_2\sin 90^{\circ} = -1(0.8660) - 0.75(1) = -1.6160$$

$$J_{12} = -L_2\sin 90^{\circ} = -0.75(1) = -0.7500$$

$$J_{21} = L_1\cos 60^{\circ} + L_2\cos 90^{\circ} = 1(0.5) + 0.75(0) = 0.5000$$

$$J_{22} = L_2\cos 90^{\circ} = 0.75(0) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Jacobian matrix:**

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.6160 & -0.7500 \\ 0.5000 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Jacobian transpose:**

$$\mathbf{J}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.6160 & 0.5000 \\ -0.7500 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Joint torques via $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.6160 & 0.5000 \\ -0.7500 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8.08 \\ -3.75 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_1 = -8.08\,\text{N·m}, \quad \tau_2 = -3.75\,\text{N·m}}$$

**(7 Marks fully allocated)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Cartesian velocity:**

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.6160 & -0.7500 \\ 0.5000 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\dot{x} = (-1.6160)(1) + (-0.7500)(0.5) = -1.6160 - 0.3750 = -1.9910\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\dot{y} = (0.5000)(1) + (0)(0.5) = 0.5000\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{V} = [-1.9910,\; 0.5000]^{T}\,\text{m/s}}$$

**(3 Marks fully allocated)**

**(ii) Determinant and singularity:**

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-1.6160)(0) - (-0.7500)(0.5000) = 0 + 0.3750 = 0.3750$$

Since $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0.3750 \neq 0$, the configuration is **non-singular**. The manipulator has full rank (rank 2), meaning it can produce velocities in both $x$ and $y$ directions at the end-effector.

> [!NOTE]
> If $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 0^{\circ}$ (arm fully folded back along the first link), $\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 0$ would make $J_{12} = 0$, and the determinant would collapse to zero, indicating a **singular (folded) configuration**.

**(4 Marks fully allocated)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose** — Students often write $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}$ instead of $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$. This is the **most common error** in the force domain. The transpose is non-negotiable.
>
> 2. **Dimension mismatch** — Always check that $\mathbf{J}$ is $m \times n$, $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is $n \times 1$, $\mathbf{F}$ is $m \times 1$, and $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is $n \times 1$. Inconsistent dimensions will not get partial credit.
>
> 3. **Treating the Jacobian as constant** — Examiners specifically test whether students understand that $\mathbf{J}$ depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. If a question specifies a configuration, **evaluate $\mathbf{J}$ at that configuration**; do not leave it symbolic.
>
> 4. **Skipping the principle of virtual work** — A common KTU examiner complaint: students write $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ as a formula without deriving it from $\delta W = 0$. Always show the derivation.
>
> 5. **Mixing up revolute and prismatic column rules** — For a revolute joint $i$, the $i$-th column is $\hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$ (or simply its $z$-component for planar arms). For a prismatic joint, the column is $\hat{z}_{i-1}$. Writing the wrong column structure loses full marks in 14-mark questions.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — Forces must be in Newtons, torques in N·m, angles in radians (not degrees) inside trigonometric functions. Always convert degrees to radians before computing $\sin$, $\cos$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: The Jacobian $\mathbf{J(\theta)}$ is the partial-derivative matrix that maps joint velocities to Cartesian velocities: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.
- **Force Dual**: The static force relationship is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$, derived from the **Principle of Virtual Work** or **power equivalence**.
- **Why the Transpose?**: Because the Jacobian defines an inner-product-preserving linear map between motion and effort — virtual work and power must be conserved.
- **Geometric Construction**: For revolute joints, the $i$-th column of $\mathbf{J}_v$ is $\hat{z}_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$; for prismatic joints, the $i$-th column is $\hat{z}_{i-1}$.
- **Singularities**: Occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$; the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space.
- **Redundancy**: When $n > m$, the manipulator is redundant. Use the **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}$ for minimum-norm joint velocities.
- **Time Dependence**: $\mathbf{J}$ is a **function of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$**, not a constant. Its entries change with manipulator configuration.
- **Power Balance**: $\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{V}$ is the fundamental energy-conservation statement that ties the two domains together.
- **Two Derivation Routes**: (i) Differentiate forward kinematics directly; (ii) Use virtual work. Both must yield the same matrix.
- **Real-World Use**: Resolved-rate motion control, singularity avoidance, force control in assembly, teleoperation haptics, and grasp-stability analysis all rely on $\mathbf{J}$ and $\mathbf{J}^{T}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration of a Rigid Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acceleration of a Rigid Body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In rigid body kinematics, the **acceleration of a rigid body** describes the time-rate of change of the velocity of every point belonging to the body. For a rigid body undergoing general planar or spatial motion, the acceleration field is governed by two independent kinematic descriptors:

1. The **linear acceleration vector** $\vec{a}$ of a reference point (typically the center of mass or a known tracking point), and
2. The **angular acceleration vector** $\vec{\alpha}$, defined as the time derivative of the angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt}$$

The acceleration of *any* arbitrary point $P$ on the rigid body is then expressed using the **rigid-body acceleration equation** (also known as the acceleration transport theorem):
$$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$$

where $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ is the position vector from the known reference point $A$ to the point $P$ of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students must explicitly distinguish between **tangential acceleration** (arising from $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}$) and **normal/centripetal acceleration** (arising from $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$). Both terms are non-zero in general rigid body motion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a spinning plate on your fingertip. Your fingertip (point $A$) may be moving sideways at $2\,\text{m/s}^2$ — that's the **linear acceleration**. At the same instant, the plate is spinning faster, meaning the spin rate is increasing — that's the **angular acceleration**. Now consider a crumb stuck at the rim of the plate (point $P$). That crumb experiences *three* effects:

- It accelerates because your finger accelerates (the **transport acceleration** $\vec{a}_A$),
- It speeds up tangentially because the plate is spinning up (the **tangential acceleration** $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}$), and
- It is constantly being yanked toward the center because it is following a curved path (the **centripetal acceleration** $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$).

The total acceleration the crumb experiences is the **vector sum** of all three. This decomposition is the very heart of rigid body acceleration analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention Used Throughout KTU Exams:**
> - $\vec{a}$ in $\text{m/s}^2$
> - $\vec{\alpha}$ in $\text{rad/s}^2$
> - $\vec{\omega}$ in $\text{rad/s}$
> - $\vec{r}$ in meters (m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Descriptors

A rigid body in 3D space has **six degrees of freedom (DOF)**: three translational (captured by $\vec{a}$) and three rotational (captured by $\vec{\alpha}$). The acceleration of *every* point on the body is fully determined once we know these six quantities and the position of the point relative to a reference.

- **Linear Acceleration $\vec{a}$** — describes how fast the velocity of a chosen body-fixed reference point is changing. Direction is along the tangent of the path of that point.
- **Angular Acceleration $\vec{\alpha}$** — describes how fast the body is "speeding up" or "slowing down" its rotation. Direction lies along the instantaneous axis of rotation (right-hand rule).

### 2.2 Decomposition of the Acceleration of Point P

The total acceleration $\vec{a}_P$ can be split into **four** logical components:

| # | Component | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Transport (linear) acceleration | $\vec{a}_A$ | Acceleration carried over from reference point $A$ |
| 2 | Tangential acceleration | $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$ | Due to change in magnitude of $\vec{\omega}$ |
| 3 | Normal (centripetal) acceleration | $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$ | Due to change in direction of $\vec{\omega}$ (always points toward instantaneous center) |
| 4 | Coriolis acceleration* | $2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{P/A}^{\text{rel}}$ | Appears only when $P$ is moving *relative* to the rotating body |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For a point *fixed* on a rigid body (no relative motion), the **Coriolis term vanishes**, and the equation reduces to a clean three-term expression. This is the version most commonly tested.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Vector Form | Key Use |
|--------------|----------|-------------|---------|
| Angular acceleration definition | $\alpha = d\omega / dt$ | $\vec{\alpha} = d\vec{\omega}/dt$ | Relates $\alpha$ and $\omega$ over time |
| Acceleration transport theorem (general 3D) | $\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$ | Vector triple | Master equation for all rigid body acceleration problems |
| Planar (2D) tangential component | $a_t = \alpha \cdot r$ | $\alpha \hat{k} \times \vec{r}$ | Magnitude of tangential accel. in plane motion |
| Planar (2D) normal component | $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot r$ | $-\omega^2 \vec{r}$ | Magnitude of centripetal accel. toward IC |
| Planar (2D) total | $a_P = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$ | Magnitude sum | Magnitude of total acceleration in plane motion |
| Direction of total accel. in 2D | $\tan\phi = \vert a_t \vert / \vert a_n \vert$ | Angle w.r.t. radial line | Angle of acceleration vector |
| Acceleration of center of mass (Newton–Euler) | $\vec{a}_G = (1/m) \sum \vec{F}$ | Newton's 2nd law | Used in robot dynamics to find CG acceleration |
| Euler's rotational equation | $\vec{M}_G = I_G \vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega})$ | Vector | Links moments to angular acceleration |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency is Critical:** In every KTU numerical problem, students must ensure $\omega$ is in **rad/s** (not RPM or deg/s) and angles in **radians** when substituting into the cross-product form.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **robot dynamics and control**, computing the acceleration of every link of a robot manipulator is the cornerstone of:

- **Inverse dynamics** (computing joint torques from desired trajectories),
- **Trajectory planning** (ensuring actuator forces stay within limits),
- **Vibration and fatigue analysis** of robotic arms in high-speed pick-and-place operations,
- **Control law design** (computed-torque control requires $\vec{\alpha}$ of each link).

> [!NOTE]
> The acceleration transport theorem is essentially the **kinematic backbone** of the Newton–Euler formulation taught later in this very course (PERAT523).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Angular Velocity to Angular Acceleration

We begin with the formal definition of angular velocity for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis (or instantaneously about an axis). Let $\vec{\omega}(t)$ be the time-varying angular velocity vector.

**Step 1: Write angular velocity definition.**

By definition, the angular velocity is the first time derivative of the orientation (rotation tensor) of the body. For a fixed-axis rotation with $\hat{k}$ as the unit axis:
$$\vec{\omega}(t) = \omega(t)\,\hat{k}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time.**

Since $\hat{k}$ is fixed in inertial space (for fixed-axis rotation), only the scalar $\omega(t)$ varies:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} = \frac{d\omega}{dt}\,\hat{k}$$

**Step 3: Generalize to 3D.**

If the axis of rotation is *not* fixed (general 3D motion), then $\hat{k}$ itself is time-dependent, giving:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} = \dot{\omega}_x \hat{i} + \dot{\omega}_y \hat{j} + \dot{\omega}_z \hat{k} + \omega_x \dot{\hat{i}} + \omega_y \dot{\hat{j}} + \omega_z \dot{\hat{k}}$$

In standard inertial Cartesian components, this simplifies to:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \dot{\vec{\omega}}$$

> **Logic Note:** The scalar $\omega$ can change because of (a) speeding up/slowing down of rotation, or (b) the axis itself tilting. Both effects are bundled into $\vec{\alpha}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Acceleration of a Point on a Rigid Body (Master Equation)

**Step 1: Velocity of point P on a rigid body (using previously known result).**

If point $A$ is a known reference on the body and $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ is the position of $P$ relative to $A$, the velocity of $P$ is:
$$\vec{v}_P = \vec{v}_A + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$$

This is the **relative velocity equation** for a rigid body (no slip, no stretch).

**Step 2: Differentiate both sides with respect to time.**

$$\frac{d\vec{v}_P}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{v}_A}{dt} + \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}\bigr)$$

This gives:
$$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times \frac{d\vec{r}_{P/A}}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Identify the angular acceleration term.**

By definition, $\dfrac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} = \vec{\alpha}$. Substituting:
$$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times \frac{d\vec{r}_{P/A}}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate the relative position vector.**

Since $P$ is *fixed* on the rigid body, the vector $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ rotates with the body. The rate of change of a vector rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ is:
$$\frac{d\vec{r}_{P/A}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into the acceleration expression.**

$$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$$

**Step 6: Interpret the three terms physically.**

- $\vec{a}_A$ → the **transport acceleration** (acceleration of the reference point $A$),
- $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$ → the **tangential acceleration** (perpendicular to $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ in plane motion),
- $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$ → the **centripetal acceleration** (always directed from $P$ toward the instantaneous axis, magnitude $\omega^2 r$).

> **Final Master Result (boxed for KTU exams):**
> $$\boxed{\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specialization to 2D (Planar) Motion

**Step 1: Assume motion confined to the $xy$-plane.**

In planar motion, the angular velocity and angular acceleration are along the $z$-axis:
$$\vec{\omega} = \omega \hat{k}, \quad \vec{\alpha} = \alpha \hat{k}$$

**Step 2: Express $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ in plane components.**

$$\vec{r}_{P/A} = r_x \hat{i} + r_y \hat{j}$$

**Step 3: Compute the tangential term.**

$$\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} = \alpha \hat{k} \times (r_x \hat{i} + r_y \hat{j}) = \alpha r_x (\hat{k} \times \hat{i}) + \alpha r_y (\hat{k} \times \hat{j})$$

Using $\hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}$:
$$\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} = -\alpha r_y \hat{i} + \alpha r_x \hat{j}$$

**Step 4: Compute the centripetal term using BAC–CAB rule.**

$$\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}) = \vec{\omega}(\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{r}_{P/A}) - \vec{r}_{P/A}(\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{\omega})$$

Since $\vec{\omega} = \omega \hat{k}$ and $\vec{r}_{P/A}$ lies in the $xy$-plane, $\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{r}_{P/A} = 0$ and $\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{\omega} = \omega^2$:
$$\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}_{P/A} = -\omega^2 r_x \hat{i} - \omega^2 r_y \hat{j}$$

**Step 5: Assemble the planar acceleration components.**

$$\vec{a}_P = (a_{Ax} - \alpha r_y - \omega^2 r_x)\hat{i} + (a_{Ay} + \alpha r_x - \omega^2 r_y)\hat{j}$$

**Step 6: Convert to scalar magnitudes (typical KTU numerical form).**

$$\vert a_t \vert = \alpha \, r_{P/A}, \quad \vert a_n \vert = \omega^2 \, r_{P/A}$$

> **Logic Note:** The tangential component is **perpendicular** to the position vector $\vec{r}_{P/A}$, while the normal component is **along** $-\vec{r}_{P/A}$ (i.e., from $P$ toward the instantaneous center of zero velocity).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Acceleration of the Center of Mass (for Later Modules)

**Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to the entire rigid body.**

The sum of external forces equals the total mass times the acceleration of the center of mass $G$:
$$\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = m \, \vec{a}_G$$

**Step 2: Solve for the CG acceleration.**

$$\vec{a}_G = \frac{1}{m} \sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}}$$

**Step 3: Apply Euler's rotational equation about the CG.**

$$\sum \vec{M}_G = I_G \vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega})$$

**Step 4: Solve for angular acceleration.**

For principal axes (or when $\vec{\omega}$ is along a principal axis), the second term vanishes:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{\sum \vec{M}_G}{I_G}$$

> **Logic Note:** Together, $\vec{a}_G$ and $\vec{\alpha}$ form the **kinematic state** of the rigid body — knowing these is sufficient to find the acceleration of *any* point using the transport theorem derived above.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Acceleration Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical flow of how the acceleration of any arbitrary point on a rigid body is computed from known kinematic inputs. This is a **block-level functional architecture** representing the standard KTU solution methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Reference point A velocity vA and acceleration aA] --> B0[Compute Angular Quantities omega and alpha of body]
    B0 --> C0[Compute relative position vector rPA]
    C0 --> D0[Branch 1: Tangential Term alpha cross rPA]
    B0 --> E0[Branch 2: Centripetal Term omega cross omega cross rPA]
    A0 --> F0[Branch 3: Transport Acceleration aA]
    D0 --> G0[Vector Sum Assembly Stage]
    E0 --> G0
    F0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Output: Acceleration Vector aP of arbitrary point P]
    H0 --> I0[Application 1: Newton Euler Dynamics]
    H0 --> J0[Application 2: Robot Manipulator Inverse Dynamics]
    H0 --> K0[Application 3: Trajectory Planning and Control]
    I0 --> L0[Next Module: Joint Torque Computation]
    J0 --> L0
    K0 --> L0
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map: Decomposition of Acceleration

The diagram below illustrates the three principal contributions to $\vec{a}_P$ and their geometric relationships. This serves as a quick visual recap before solving problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Kinematic State]
        IA[Linear accel aA]
        IB[Angular accel alpha]
        IC[Angular velocity omega]
        ID[Position vector rPA]
    end
    IA --> SUM[Vector Sum]
    IB --> TANG[Tangential Component: alpha cross rPA]
    IC --> NORM[Centripetal Component: omega cross omega cross rPA]
    ID --> TANG
    ID --> NORM
    TANG --> SUM
    NORM --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Result: Total Acceleration aP]
    TANG -. direction .- DIR1[Perpendicular to rPA]
    NORM -. direction .- DIR2[Opposite to rPA toward IC]
    OUT --> APPL[Application in Robot Dynamics]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Schema (Plain Text Schematic)

For a point $P$ at radial distance $r$ from reference point $A$ undergoing planar rotation:

```
                            Tangential accel (a_t)
                                 ↑
                                 |
                                 |
                                 P  •  ──────→  Direction of motion (tangent)
                                 |
                                 |
                                 ↓  Centripetal accel (a_n) — points toward A
                                 •
                                 A
                          (Instantaneous Center / Reference Point)
```

> [!NOTE]
> - $\vec{a}_t$ is **tangent** to the circular path, magnitude $\alpha r$.
> - $\vec{a}_n$ is **radial inward** toward $A$, magnitude $\omega^2 r$.
> - Total $\vec{a}_P$ is the vector sum, lying between the two.
