## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
> **Define angular acceleration of a rigid body. How is it related to angular velocity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **angular acceleration** $\vec{\alpha}$ of a rigid body is defined as the time-rate of change of the angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}$ with respect to time. Mathematically:
$$\vec{\alpha} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt}$$
It is a vector quantity, directed along the instantaneous axis of rotation. In scalar form, for fixed-axis rotation, $\alpha = d\omega / dt$, with units of **rad/s²**. A positive $\alpha$ indicates the body is speeding up; negative $\alpha$ indicates slowing down. The direction is given by the right-hand rule, same as $\vec{\omega}$. (3 marks: definition 1, equation 1, physical interpretation 1)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
> **State the acceleration transport theorem for a rigid body. List all terms with physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The acceleration transport theorem states that the acceleration of an arbitrary point $P$ on a rigid body, in terms of a known reference point $A$, is:
$$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$$
where:
- $\vec{a}_A$ = transport (linear) acceleration of reference point $A$ (1 mark),
- $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$ = tangential acceleration due to angular acceleration $\vec{\alpha}$ (1 mark),
- $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$ = centripetal (normal) acceleration due to angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$, magnitude $\omega^2 r$ directed toward the rotation axis (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
> A robotic link $AB$ of length $L = 0.5\,\text{m}$ rotates in the vertical plane about a fixed pivot at $A$. At a certain instant, the angular velocity is $\omega = 4\,\text{rad/s}$ and the angular acceleration is $\alpha = 2\,\text{rad/s}^2$, both counter-clockwise. Point $B$ is at the tip of the link.
>
> **(a)** Determine the velocity and acceleration of point $B$ at this instant. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** If a payload of mass $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ is attached at $B$, find the net force and the reaction at pivot $A$ needed to produce this motion. Neglect the link's mass. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Identify the reference point and geometry.**

Reference point $A$ is the fixed pivot. The link is along a chosen orientation; take $\vec{r}_{B/A} = 0.5\,\hat{j}\,\text{m}$ (assume link is vertical for the instant, but the result is general via magnitudes).

**Step 2: Compute velocity of B.**

For a body rotating about a fixed point, $\vec{v}_A = 0$. So:
$$\vec{v}_B = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} = (4\,\hat{k}) \times (0.5\,\hat{j}) = -2\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}$$

Magnitude: $\vert \vec{v}_B \vert = \omega L = 4 \times 0.5 = 2\,\text{m/s}$ (directed along $-\hat{i}$, i.e., horizontal). (2 marks)

**Step 3: Compute acceleration of B.**

Since $A$ is fixed, $\vec{a}_A = 0$. Apply the transport theorem:
$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A})$$

**Step 4: Compute the tangential term.**

$$\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} = (2\,\hat{k}) \times (0.5\,\hat{j}) = -1\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

This is the tangential acceleration, magnitude $\alpha L = 2 \times 0.5 = 1\,\text{m/s}^2$. (2 marks)

**Step 5: Compute the centripetal term.**

$$\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}_{B/A} = -(4)^2 (0.5\,\hat{j}) = -8\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

This is the centripetal acceleration, magnitude $\omega^2 L = 16 \times 0.5 = 8\,\text{m/s}^2$, directed from $B$ toward $A$ (i.e., $-\hat{j}$). (2 marks)

**Step 6: Sum the vectors.**

$$\vec{a}_B = -1\,\hat{i} - 8\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Magnitude: $\vert \vec{a}_B \vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06\,\text{m/s}^2$. (1 mark)

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law at point B (the payload).**

The forces on the payload are: tension/compression $\vec{F}_{link}$ from the link (along $AB$) and gravity $m\vec{g}$.

Assume the link is massless, so the link can only carry an axial force $F$ (along the link direction). Let the link be along $+\hat{j}$ (i.e., B is above A). Then:
$$\vec{F}_{\text{on B from link}} = F \hat{j}$$

The gravity force on the payload is $-mg \hat{j} = -2 \times 9.81\, \hat{j} = -19.62\,\hat{j}\,\text{N}$.

**Step 2: Net force on payload equals mass times acceleration.**

$$\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m \vec{a}_B$$
$$F \hat{j} - 19.62\,\hat{j} = 2 \times (-1\,\hat{i} - 8\,\hat{j})$$
$$F \hat{j} - 19.62\,\hat{j} = -2\,\hat{i} - 16\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 3: Equate components.**

- $\hat{i}$ component: $0 = -2$ — **Impossible!**
- This means the link must also carry a *horizontal* force component. (3 marks)

**Step 4: Realistic model — the link is a rigid body (2-force member analysis revised).**

Since the link is massless and pinned at $A$, with a payload at $B$, the *true* force on the link at $A$ and $B$ need not be collinear for a *rigid* body. Let reactions at $A$ be $A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j}$.

Equilibrium of link (massless): $A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} + \vec{F}_{B \to \text{link}} = 0$, so $\vec{F}_{B \to \text{link}} = -A_x \hat{i} - A_y \hat{j}$.

Then force on payload at $B$ from link is the reaction: $\vec{F}_{\text{link} \to B} = A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j}$.

Newton's law for payload:
$$A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} - 19.62\,\hat{j} = 2(-1\,\hat{i} - 8\,\hat{j})$$
$$A_x = -2\,\text{N}, \quad A_y - 19.62 = -16 \Rightarrow A_y = 3.62\,\text{N}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5: Solve for reactions at A.**

Since the link is massless, the link's acceleration is not defined by Newton's law, but by the requirement that net force and net moment on it are zero (D'Alembert). The reaction at $A$ is the negative of the force at $B$:
$$\vec{R}_A = -\vec{F}_{B \to \text{link}} = -(-A_x \hat{i} - A_y \hat{j}) = A_x \hat{i} + A_y \hat{j} = -2\,\hat{i} + 3.62\,\hat{j}\,\text{N}$$

Magnitude: $\vert \vec{R}_A \vert = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (3.62)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 13.10} = \sqrt{17.10} \approx 4.14\,\text{N}$. (2 marks)

> **Final Answer for Q A:** $\vec{v}_B = -2\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}$, $\vec{a}_B = -1\,\hat{i} - 8\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$, $\vec{R}_A \approx (-2\,\hat{i} + 3.62\,\hat{j})\,\text{N}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**
> A disc of radius $R = 0.3\,\text{m}$ rolls without slipping on a horizontal surface. The center $G$ of the disc has an acceleration of $\vec{a}_G = 1.5\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}^2$ to the right and a velocity of $\vec{v}_G = 2\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}$.
>
> **(a)** Determine the angular velocity and angular acceleration of the disc. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Find the acceleration of the contact point $C$ on the rim of the disc (the point instantaneously in contact with the ground). (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Apply the no-slip rolling condition.**

For pure rolling, the velocity of the contact point $C$ must be zero:
$$\vec{v}_C = 0$$

The relation between center velocity and angular velocity is:
$$\vec{v}_C = \vec{v}_G + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}$$

Let $\vec{r}_{C/G} = -R\,\hat{j} = -0.3\,\hat{j}\,\text{m}$ (C is directly below G).

**Step 2: Solve for angular velocity.**

For rolling to the right with $\vec{v}_G = 2\,\hat{i}$, the disc must rotate clockwise (negative $\hat{k}$). Let $\vec{\omega} = -\omega \hat{k}$:
$$\vec{v}_C = 2\,\hat{i} + (-\omega \hat{k}) \times (-0.3\,\hat{j})$$
$$0\,\hat{i} = 2\,\hat{i} + (-\omega)(-0.3)(\hat{k} \times \hat{j})$$
$$0 = 2 + 0.3\omega(-1) \quad \text{(since } \hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}\text{)}$$
$$0 = 2 - 0.3\omega \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{2}{0.3} = 6.67\,\text{rad/s}$$

So $\vec{\omega} = -6.67\,\hat{k}\,\text{rad/s}$. (3 marks)

**Step 3: Differentiate the no-slip condition to get angular acceleration.**

Differentiate $\vec{v}_C = \vec{v}_G + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G} = 0$ with respect to time (note $\vec{r}_{C/G}$ is constant in magnitude but rotates with the body — however, for the *instantaneous* evaluation, we use the rate-of-change of the cross product correctly):
$$\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_G + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{C/G} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}) = 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute known values.**

$\vec{a}_G = 1.5\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}^2$, $\vec{r}_{C/G} = -0.3\,\hat{j}\,\text{m}$, $\vec{\omega} = -6.67\,\hat{k}\,\text{rad/s}$, $\vec{\alpha} = -\alpha \hat{k}$ (assumed clockwise).

Compute $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}$:
$$(-6.67\,\hat{k}) \times (-0.3\,\hat{j}) = (6.67)(0.3)(\hat{k} \times (-\hat{j})) = -2.0\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}$$

Compute $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}_{C/G}$:
$$- (6.67)^2 \times (-0.3\,\hat{j}) = +13.35\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Compute $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}$:
$$(-\alpha \hat{k}) \times (-0.3\,\hat{j}) = -0.3\alpha(\hat{k} \times (-\hat{j})) = 0.3\alpha\,\hat{i}$$

**Step 5: Sum and set equal to zero.**

$$1.5\,\hat{i} + 0.3\alpha\,\hat{i} + 13.35\,\hat{j} = 0$$

$\hat{j}$ component: $13.35 = 0$ — **Contradiction!**

This means the contact point is *not* the instantaneous center of *zero acceleration* in accelerating rolling. The contact point has *zero velocity* (IC of velocity), but *not* zero acceleration.

**Step 6: Re-interpret the problem — find $\alpha$ from the equation of motion, not from the contact constraint.**

The correct procedure: differentiate the *velocity* no-slip condition differently. We have $v_G = \omega R$ (scalar form, with signs), so:
$$a_G = \alpha R \Rightarrow 1.5 = \alpha (0.3) \Rightarrow \alpha = 5\,\text{rad/s}^2$$

So $\vec{\alpha} = -5\,\hat{k}\,\text{rad/s}^2$ (clockwise). (4 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** The instantaneous center of *zero velocity* (ICZV) is *not* the same as the instantaneous center of *zero acceleration* (ICZA) for an accelerating body. The contact point of a rolling disc is the ICZV but has a non-zero acceleration of $\omega^2 R$ directed toward the center.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Use the acceleration transport theorem with G as reference.**

$$\vec{a}_C = \vec{a}_G + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{C/G} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G})$$

**Step 2: Compute each term using values from Part (a).**

- $\vec{a}_G = 1.5\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}^2$
- $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}$: Already computed as $0.3 \times 5\,\hat{i} = 1.5\,\hat{i}\,\text{m/s}^2$
- $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{C/G}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}_{C/G} = -44.44 \times (-0.3\,\hat{j}) = 13.33\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$

**Step 3: Sum the components.**

$$\vec{a}_C = (1.5 + 1.5)\hat{i} + 13.33\,\hat{j} = 3.0\,\hat{i} + 13.33\,\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 4: Magnitude and direction.**

$$\vert \vec{a}_C \vert = \sqrt{3.0^2 + 13.33^2} = \sqrt{9 + 177.7} = \sqrt{186.7} \approx 13.66\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Direction: $\theta = \tan^{-1}(13.33 / 3.0) = \tan^{-1}(4.44) \approx 77.3°$ above the horizontal. (3 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The contact point is *not* stationary in acceleration — it has a large upward acceleration $\omega^2 R$ because the disc center is "rising" relative to the contact point in a curved path. This is a classic KTU problem to test whether students blindly apply the ICZV = ICZA misconception. (4 marks for the realization and correct calculation)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ICZV with ICZA:** The instantaneous center of zero velocity is *not* the center of zero acceleration for an accelerating body. Question B explicitly tests this. Loss: 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the centripetal term:** Many students write $\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A}$ and forget the $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$ term. Loss: 2–3 marks per instance.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Using RPM instead of rad/s for $\omega$ when computing $\omega^2 r$. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in cross products:** In 2D, $\hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}$. Mixing these up gives wrong directions. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not drawing a free body / kinematic diagram:** KTU examiners award partial credit (typically 1 mark) for a clearly labeled diagram. Skipping it forfeits this.
> 6. **Mixing up $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{\alpha}$:** Linear and angular accelerations are dimensionally and physically distinct — never equate them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two descriptors** fully characterize rigid body acceleration: linear acceleration $\vec{a}$ of a reference point and angular acceleration $\vec{\alpha}$ of the body.
- The **master equation** (acceleration transport theorem) is:
  $$\vec{a}_P = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/A} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/A})$$
- In **2D planar motion**, the three scalar magnitudes are: transport $a_A$, tangential $a_t = \alpha r$, and centripetal $a_n = \omega^2 r$.
- **Tangential acceleration** is perpendicular to $\vec{r}_{P/A}$; **centripetal acceleration** points from $P$ toward the instantaneous axis of rotation.
- The **Coriolis term** $2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{P/A}^{\text{rel}}$ appears *only* when $P$ moves relative to the rotating body — not for points *fixed* on the body.
- **ICZV ≠ ICZA** in general. The instantaneous center of zero velocity has zero velocity but non-zero acceleration for an accelerating body.
- For a **rolling wheel**, the contact point is the ICZV but has acceleration $\omega^2 R$ directed toward the center.
- **Angular acceleration is the time derivative of angular velocity**: $\vec{\alpha} = d\vec{\omega}/dt$.
- **Units:** $\vec{a}$ in $\text{m/s}^2$, $\vec{\alpha}$ in $\text{rad/s}^2$, $\vec{\omega}$ in $\text{rad/s}$, $\vec{r}$ in m.
- In **robot dynamics**, computing $\vec{\alpha}$ of each link is the prerequisite step for inverse dynamics and computed-torque control.
- The **centripetal term** is also written as $-\omega^2 \vec{r}_{P/A}$ — both forms are equivalent and accepted in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Distribution
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mass Distribution

# ⚙️ 1. Mass Distribution in Robot Dynamics — Core Definition & Intuition

In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, every rigid link of a manipulator is a distributed mass system, not a point particle. To write the equations of motion (Newton–Euler or Lagrangian), we must mathematically *characterize* how that mass is spread throughout the volume of the link. This characterization is collectively called **Mass Distribution**.

Formally, **Mass Distribution** of a rigid body is described by three hierarchical inertia properties:

1. **Scalar Mass (m)** — the total amount of matter (0th moment of mass).
2. **Center of Mass (COM, $\mathbf{r}_c$)** — the *first moment* of mass, locating the "balance point."
3. **Inertia Tensor ($\mathbf{I}$)** — the *second moment* of mass, describing resistance to rotational acceleration about any axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** "Mass distribution parameters — mass, center of mass, moment of inertia, product of inertia, inertia tensor, parallel axis theorem, principal axes, radius of gyration."

## 🤖 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a cricket bat. If you grip it at the **handle (near COM)**, you can swing it easily with a flick of the wrist. If you grip it at the **blade end (far from COM)**, the same swing feels much heavier and harder to control — even though the *mass* of the bat is identical. Why?

- The **mass** hasn't changed.
- The **distance from the rotation axis to the COM** has increased.
- Hence, the **moment of inertia** about your wrist is much larger (since $I = m k^2$, and $k$ has grown).

This is why *where* the mass is located — not just *how much* mass there is — is the central concern of robot dynamics. A 6-DOF industrial arm with poorly distributed link masses will have sluggish response, motor saturation, and oscillatory behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** In KTU 2024 scheme problems, when a robot link is described as "uniform," the COM lies at the geometric centroid, and standard textbook inertia formulas (rod, disk, block, cylinder) apply directly. When it is "non-uniform" or "arbitrary," you must fall back on the integral definitions:
> $$\mathbf{r}_c = \frac{1}{m}\int_V \mathbf{r}\, dm, \qquad m = \int_V dm$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Mass Distribution

### (a) Total Mass — $m$
A **scalar** quantity representing the integrated mass density over the body volume $V$.
$$m = \int_V \rho(\mathbf{r})\, dV$$
- $\rho(\mathbf{r})$ = mass density at point $\mathbf{r}$ (kg/m³).
- For a **homogeneous (uniform)** body, $\rho$ is constant, so $m = \rho V$.

### (b) Center of Mass — $\mathbf{r}_c$
A **first moment vector** that identifies the unique point about which the weighted sum of position vectors vanishes.
$$\mathbf{r}_c = \frac{1}{m}\int_V \mathbf{r}\, dm = \frac{1}{m}\int_V \mathbf{r}\, \rho(\mathbf{r})\, dV$$

> [!NOTE]
> The COM coincides with the **Center of Gravity** only in a uniform gravitational field — a standard KTU assumption for terrestrial robots.

### (c) Mass Moment of Inertia (Scalar Form) — $I$
A **scalar** describing resistance to angular acceleration about a *specific axis* $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$.
$$I_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}} = \int_V (\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{r} - (\mathbf{r}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{u}})^2)\, dm = \int_V r_{\perp}^2\, dm$$
where $r_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance from the mass element $dm$ to axis $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$.

### (d) Product of Inertia — $I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx}$
These are **mixed second moments** that vanish only when the reference axes are aligned with the **principal axes** of the body.
$$I_{xy} = \int_V x\, y\, dm, \qquad I_{yz} = \int_V y\, z\, dm, \qquad I_{zx} = \int_V z\, x\, dm$$

### (e) Inertia Tensor — $\mathbf{I}$ (3×3 Symmetric Matrix)
A compact, axis-independent representation of all six independent second-moment quantities.

In any frame $\{A\}$ with origin at the COM:
$$\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & -I_{xy} & -I_{xz} \\ -I_{xy} & I_{yy} & -I_{yz} \\ -I_{xz} & -I_{yz} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix}_{A,c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative signs** on off-diagonals are a *convention* (the "inertia tensor" form). The *products of inertia* $I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{xz}$ are positive when defined as $\int xy\,dm$. Always use the convention specified in your KTU textbook (most Kerala textbooks follow the negative-off-diagonal form).

### (f) Radius of Gyration — $k$
The **equivalent distance** at which the entire mass $m$ could be concentrated to produce the same moment of inertia.
$$I = m k^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{m}}$$

### (g) Principal Axes & Principal Moments of Inertia
The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{I}$ are the **principal moments** $I_1, I_2, I_3$, and the corresponding eigenvectors define the **principal axes** — a body-fixed frame in which the inertia tensor is purely diagonal:
$$\mathbf{I}_{principal} = \begin{bmatrix} I_1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **symmetric body** (uniform rod, cylinder, sphere, rectangular block centered at origin), the principal axes align with the geometric symmetry axes, so the off-diagonal products of inertia are **zero by inspection**. This saves substantial calculation time in KTU exams.

## 2.2 The Parallel Axis Theorem (Steiner's Theorem)

This is the **single most-tested theorem** in Module 1 of PERAT523. It relates the inertia about an *arbitrary* point $P$ to the inertia about the **COM**.

### Scalar Form (2D, about parallel axes):
$$I_{P} = I_{c} + m\, d^{2}$$
where $d$ is the perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes.

### Vector / Tensor Form (3D, about a parallel-axis displaced frame):
$$\mathbf{I}_{P} = \mathbf{I}_{c} + m\!\left[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\,\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\otimes\mathbf{d}\right]$$
where $\mathbf{d}$ is the vector from COM to point $P$, $\mathbf{E}$ is the 3×3 identity matrix, and $\otimes$ is the outer (dyadic) product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Industrial robot links are designed with COM offsets for motor placement. The **Parallel Axis Theorem** allows the controller to dynamically shift the inertia reference between joint axes (e.g., the motor housing) and the link's geometric center — a calculation that must be performed *every control cycle* in adaptive control schemes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Definition / Formula | Units | Special Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m$ | $\int_V \rho\, dV$ | kg | Scalar |
| $\mathbf{r}_c$ | $(1/m)\int_V \mathbf{r}\, dm$ | m | First moment vector |
| $I_{xx}$ | $\int_V (y^2 + z^2)\, dm$ | kg·m² | About X-axis through origin |
| $I_{yy}$ | $\int_V (x^2 + z^2)\, dm$ | kg·m² | About Y-axis through origin |
| $I_{zz}$ | $\int_V (x^2 + y^2)\, dm$ | kg·m² | About Z-axis through origin |
| $I_{xy}$ | $\int_V x y\, dm$ | kg·m² | Product of inertia (positive) |
| Parallel Axis (scalar) | $I_P = I_c + m d^2$ | kg·m² | Only for **parallel** axes |
| Parallel Axis (tensor) | $\mathbf{I}_P = \mathbf{I}_c + m[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T]$ | kg·m² | Full 3D form |
| Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{I/m}$ | m | Equivalent point-mass distance |
| Uniform Rod (center, ⊥) | $I_c = (1/12)\, m L^2$ | kg·m² | About perpendicular bisector |
| Uniform Rod (end, ⊥) | $I_{end} = (1/3)\, m L^2$ | kg·m² | Apply Steiner with $d = L/2$ |
| Rectangular Block (corner ⊥ to face) | $I = (1/12) m (b^2 + c^2)$ | kg·m² | $a$ along axis, $b,c$ transverse |
| Solid Cylinder (central axis) | $I_{axial} = (1/2) m R^2$ | kg·m² | Symmetry axis |
| Solid Cylinder (diametral) | $I_{diam} = (1/4) m R^2 + (1/12) m L^2$ | kg·m² | Through center, ⊥ to axis |
| Thin Spherical Shell | $I = (2/3) m R^2$ | kg·m² | Any axis through center |
| Solid Sphere | $I = (2/5) m R^2$ | kg·m² | Any axis through center |
| Composite Body | $I_{total} = \sum_i I_i$ (about common axis) | kg·m² | All $I_i$ must share same reference axis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Axis Theorem (Scalar Form)

> **Goal:** Show that for any rigid body of mass $m$, the moment of inertia about an axis $A'$ parallel to a COM-axis $A$ (separated by perpendicular distance $d$) satisfies $I_{A'} = I_{A} + m d^2$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**

Choose a 2D plane. Let axis $A$ pass through the COM $C$, and let axis $A'$ be parallel to $A$ at perpendicular distance $d$.

**Step 2 — Express an arbitrary mass element position.**

For any infinitesimal mass element $dm$ at position $\mathbf{r}$ from $C$, the perpendicular distance to axis $A$ is $\rho$, and to axis $A'$ is $\rho'$. By the law of cosines on the triangle formed by $C$, $dm$, and the foot of perpendicular to $A'$:
$$\rho'^2 = \rho^2 + d^2 - 2\rho d \cos\theta$$
where $\theta$ is the angle between the position vector $\boldsymbol{\rho}$ (from $C$ to $dm$) and the displacement vector $\mathbf{d}$ (from $A'$ to $A$).

**Step 3 — Write the moment of inertia integrals.**

$$I_{A'} = \int_V \rho'^2\, dm = \int_V (\rho^2 + d^2 - 2\rho d\cos\theta)\, dm$$

$$I_{A} = \int_V \rho^2\, dm$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral into three parts.**

$$I_{A'} = \underbrace{\int_V \rho^2\, dm}_{I_A} + d^2\underbrace{\int_V dm}_{m} - 2d \int_V \rho\cos\theta\, dm$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the cross term.**

The term $\int_V \rho\cos\theta\, dm$ is the **first moment of mass** about the COM axis $A$. But by the very *definition* of the center of mass, the first moment about $C$ is **zero**:
$$\int_V \rho\cos\theta\, dm = m \cdot 0 = 0$$

> **Why?** The COM is the unique point about which the mass-weighted position vector sums to zero. This is the *defining property* of the COM.

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$I_{A'} = I_{A} + m d^2 + 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{I_{A'} = I_{A} + m d^2}$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Parallel Axis Theorem (Tensor Form)

> **Goal:** Generalize to 3D: $\mathbf{I}_P = \mathbf{I}_c + m[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T]$.

**Step 1 — Position of mass element.**

For $dm$ at $\mathbf{r}_c + \mathbf{s}$ (where $\mathbf{s}$ is the position *relative to the COM*), its position from the displaced origin $P$ is:
$$\mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{d} + \mathbf{s}$$
where $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_P$ (vector from $P$ to $C$).

**Step 2 — Inertia tensor definition.**

The inertia tensor about any point $Q$ for a body is:
$$\mathbf{I}_Q = \int_V \left[(\mathbf{r}_Q \cdot \mathbf{r}_Q)\,\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{r}_Q \mathbf{r}_Q^T\right] dm$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{d} + \mathbf{s}$.**

$$\mathbf{I}_P = \int_V \left[((\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})\cdot(\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s}))\,\mathbf{E} - (\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})(\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})^T\right] dm$$

**Step 4 — Expand the scalar and the dyadic.**

Let $S = (\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})\cdot(\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s}) = \mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d} + 2\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{s} + \mathbf{s}\cdot\mathbf{s}$.

Let $\mathbf{R} = (\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})(\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{s})^T = \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T + \mathbf{d}\mathbf{s}^T + \mathbf{s}\mathbf{d}^T + \mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T$.

**Step 5 — Assemble the integrand.**

$$S\,\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{R} = (\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T + 2(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{s})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{s}^T - \mathbf{s}\mathbf{d}^T + (\mathbf{s}\cdot\mathbf{s})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over the body.**

$$\mathbf{I}_P = \int_V \left[(\mathbf{s}\cdot\mathbf{s})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T\right] dm + \int_V \left[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T\right] dm$$
$$+ \int_V \left[2(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{s})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{s}^T - \mathbf{s}\mathbf{d}^T\right] dm$$

**Step 7 — Identify each term.**

- The first integral is $\mathbf{I}_c$ (inertia about COM).
- The second integral, since $\mathbf{d}$ is constant, factors out: $m[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T]$.
- The third integral involves $\int_V \mathbf{s}\, dm = 0$ (definition of COM) and $\int_V \mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T\, dm$, but the leading factor of $\mathbf{d}$ ensures the first-moment term vanishes:
$$\int_V (\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{s})\mathbf{E}\, dm = (\mathbf{d}\cdot \underbrace{\int_V \mathbf{s}\, dm}_{=0})\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{0}$$
$$\int_V \mathbf{d}\mathbf{s}^T\, dm = \mathbf{d}\underbrace{\int_V \mathbf{s}^T\, dm}_{=0} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 8 — Combine the surviving terms.**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I}_P = \mathbf{I}_c + m\!\left[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\,\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T\right]}$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The cross-term cancellation in Step 7 is the *only* reason the COM is special. If you tried this with an *arbitrary* point, the first-moment term would *not* vanish. This is precisely why the COM is the natural reference for dynamics equations.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Inertia Tensor of a Uniform Rectangular Block

**Problem:** A robot link is modeled as a homogeneous rectangular block of mass $m$ and dimensions $a \times b \times c$, centered at the origin with edges along the coordinate axes. Compute the inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_c$ about the COM.

**Step 1 — Set up the density.**

Since the block is uniform, $\rho = m/(abc)$, and $dm = \rho\, dx\, dy\, dz$.

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{xx}$.**

$$I_{xx} = \int_V (y^2 + z^2)\, dm = \rho \int_{-a/2}^{a/2}\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\int_{-c/2}^{c/2} (y^2 + z^2)\, dz\, dy\, dx$$

Evaluate $\int_{-c/2}^{c/2}(y^2 + z^2)\,dz = y^2 c + \frac{c^3}{12}$.

Then $\int_{-b/2}^{b/2}\left(y^2 c + \frac{c^3}{12}\right)dy = \frac{b^3 c}{12} + \frac{b c^3}{12} = \frac{bc}{12}(b^2 + c^2)$.

Finally, multiply by $\int_{-a/2}^{a/2}dx = a$:
$$I_{xx} = \rho \cdot a \cdot \frac{bc}{12}(b^2 + c^2) = \frac{m}{abc}\cdot \frac{abc}{12}(b^2 + c^2) = \frac{m(b^2 + c^2)}{12}$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry of integration limits (odd functions of single variables integrate to zero):**

$$I_{xy} = \int_V x y\, dm = \rho \int x\, dx \int y\, dy \int dz = 0$$

(Since $\int_{-a/2}^{a/2} x\, dx = 0$.)

**Step 4 — Analogously for $I_{yy}, I_{zz}$:**

$$I_{yy} = \frac{m(a^2 + c^2)}{12}, \qquad I_{zz} = \frac{m(a^2 + b^2)}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Final inertia tensor.**

$$\mathbf{I}_c = \frac{m}{12}\begin{bmatrix} b^2 + c^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a^2 + c^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & a^2 + b^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The off-diagonals are **zero** because the symmetry axes *coincide* with the principal axes for a centered rectangular block. The principal moments are the diagonal entries themselves.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Rod About an End (Application of Steiner's Theorem)

**Problem:** A uniform rod of mass $m$, length $L$. Find $I$ about an axis through one end, perpendicular to the rod.

**Step 1 — Standard formula (perpendicular bisector):**

$$I_{c} = \frac{1}{12} m L^2 \quad \text{(about the center, perpendicular to rod)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply parallel axis theorem with $d = L/2$:**

$$I_{end} = I_{c} + m d^2 = \frac{1}{12} m L^2 + m\!\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{mL^2}{12} + \frac{mL^2}{4} = \frac{mL^2 + 3mL^2}{12} = \frac{1}{3} m L^2$$

$$\boxed{I_{end} = \frac{1}{3} m L^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **4×** difference between the end and center inertias is why a robot link swinging about its joint (typically near one end) requires **4 times** more torque than the same link swinging about its midpoint — a critical design consideration in manipulator sizing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Mass Distribution Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["MASS DISTRIBUTION OF A RIGID BODY"] --> B0["Scalar Mass m"]
    A0 --> C0["Center of Mass rc"]
    A0 --> D0["Inertia Properties"]
    B0 --> B1["Total matter: integral of rho dV"]
    C0 --> C1["First moment of mass"]
    C0 --> C2["Balance point of body"]
    D0 --> E0["Moments of Inertia"]
    D0 --> F0["Products of Inertia"]
    D0 --> G0["Inertia Tensor I"]
    E0 --> E1["Scalar I about an axis"]
    E1 --> E2["I_xx, I_yy, I_zz"]
    F0 --> F1["Mixed second moments"]
    F1 --> F2["I_xy, I_yz, I_xz"]
    G0 --> G1["3x3 symmetric matrix"]
    G0 --> G2["Six independent entries"]
    G0 --> H0["Principal Axes Form"]
    H0 --> H1["Diagonal tensor"]
    H0 --> H2["Eigenvalue decomposition"]
    G0 --> I0["Displaced Frame Form"]
    I0 --> I1["Parallel Axis Theorem"]
    I1 --> I2["Scalar: I_P = I_c + m d squared"]
    I1 --> I3["Tensor: I_P = I_c + m bracket d d E minus d d T bracket"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Mass Distribution to Robot Dynamics Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Link Geometry and Density rho of r"] --> M1["Compute total mass m"]
    IN2["Link Geometry"] --> M2["Compute center of mass rc"]
    IN3["Link Geometry and Density"] --> M3["Compute inertia tensor I_c"]
    M1 --> OUT1["Dynamic Parameters of Link"]
    M2 --> OUT1
    M3 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> J1["Apply Parallel Axis Theorem to shift to joint frame"]
    J1 --> J2["Assemble joint space mass matrix M of q"]
    J2 --> J3["Form Coriolis and gravity vectors"]
    J3 --> OUT2["Equation of Motion: M q double dot + C q dot + G = Tau"]
    M3 --> P1["Identify Principal Axes"]
    P1 --> P2["Diagonalize I via eigenvalue decomposition"]
    P2 --> OUT3["Simplified Inertia: I_diag = diag of I1 I2 I3"]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Inertia Relationships for a Single Robot Link

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Robot Link: Homogeneous Body of Mass m"] --> A1["Standard Formula about COM"]
    L0 --> A2["Standard Formula about Symmetry Axis"]
    A1 --> F1["I_c = integral of r perp squared dm"]
    A2 --> F2["E.g. Rod: m L squared / 12"]
    F1 --> T1["Parallel Axis Theorem"]
    F2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2["Shift to any parallel axis: I_P = I_c + m d squared"]
    T2 --> APP["Application: Joint axis inertia"]
    L0 --> RG1["Radius of Gyration k"]
    RG1 --> RG2["k = sqrt of I over m"]
    RG2 --> RG3["Equivalent point mass distance"]
    L0 --> PR1["Principal Axes via Eigen-decomposition"]
    PR1 --> PR2["Off-diagonal products vanish"]
    PR2 --> PR3["Diagonal Inertia Tensor"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows denote *derivation flow* or *functional dependence*. A node represents a *mathematical object* (a scalar, vector, tensor, or formula). The diagrams are intended as a *revision map* — start at any node and trace forward to discover what concepts feed into the final robot equation of motion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PERAT523]`
**Define the following terms with respect to mass distribution of a rigid body:**
**(i) Radius of Gyration** **(ii) Product of Inertia** **(iii) Principal Axes**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Radius of Gyration ($k$):** It is defined as the distance from the axis of rotation at which, if the *entire mass* of the body were assumed to be concentrated at a single point, the moment of inertia would equal the actual moment of inertia of the body. Mathematically, $I = m k^2 \Rightarrow k = \sqrt{I/m}$. *Unit: metre (m).*
>
> **(ii) Product of Inertia ($I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx}$):** The product of inertia about two perpendicular axes (say $X$ and $Y$) is defined as the integral of the product of the coordinates of each mass element with respect to those axes, taken over the entire body mass. For example, $I_{xy} = \int_V x y\, dm$. It is a measure of the *asymmetry* of mass distribution in the $XY$-plane. *Unit: kg·m².*
>
> **(iii) Principal Axes:** These are the three mutually perpendicular axes of a body about which the **products of inertia vanish**, i.e., the inertia tensor becomes purely diagonal. They are obtained as the *eigenvectors* of the inertia tensor, and the corresponding diagonal entries are the *principal moments of inertia* $I_1, I_2, I_3$. For a body with geometric symmetry (rod, sphere, cylinder, block), the principal axes align with the symmetry axes.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper PERAT523]`
**State the Parallel Axis Theorem. A uniform rod of mass 2 kg and length 1 m rotates about an axis perpendicular to it and passing through one end. Calculate its moment of inertia.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Parallel Axis Theorem (Steiner's Theorem):** The moment of inertia of a rigid body about any axis is equal to the sum of (a) its moment of inertia about a parallel axis passing through the center of mass, and (b) the product of the mass of the body and the square of the perpendicular distance between the two parallel axes.
> $$I_P = I_{c} + m d^2$$
>
> **Calculation:** For a uniform rod about a perpendicular axis through its center: $I_c = \dfrac{mL^2}{12} = \dfrac{2 \times 1^2}{12} = \dfrac{1}{6}$ kg·m².
>
> The end axis is at distance $d = L/2 = 0.5$ m from the center.
>
> $$I_{end} = I_c + m d^2 = \frac{1}{6} + 2 \times (0.5)^2 = \frac{1}{6} + 0.5 = \frac{1 + 3}{6} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ kg·m}^2$$
>
> $$\boxed{I_{end} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) carry 14 marks each, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **one full question** out of a choice of two from the same module.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the **Parallel Axis Theorem** for the moment of inertia of a rigid body. State clearly the assumptions and the significance of the center of mass in the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot link is modeled as a **uniform solid cylinder** of mass $m = 5$ kg, radius $R = 0.1$ m, and length $L = 0.5$ m. The cylinder is mounted such that its symmetry axis is horizontal, and it rotates about a vertical axis passing through a point on its curved surface (i.e., a point at distance $R$ from the central longitudinal axis). Compute the moment of inertia of this link about the rotation axis. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

[Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The expected answer must include:]

1. **Geometric Setup:** Define the COM axis $A$ and the parallel displaced axis $A'$ separated by distance $d$. Draw a labeled diagram (carry 1 mark).
2. **Use the cosine law:** $\rho'^2 = \rho^2 + d^2 - 2\rho d \cos\theta$.
3. **Expand the integral:** $I_{A'} = \int \rho^2 dm + d^2 \int dm - 2d\int \rho\cos\theta\, dm$.
4. **Identify terms:** First integral = $I_A$; second = $m d^2$; third = $0$ (by definition of COM).
5. **Final boxed result:** $I_{A'} = I_A + m d^2$.
6. **Significance of COM:** The COM is the unique point where the first moment of mass vanishes, making the cross term zero. For *any other* reference point, the cross term would not vanish, and the simple additive form of the theorem would be lost.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the standard inertia formula.**

For a solid cylinder of mass $m$, radius $R$, about its **diametral axis** (passing through COM, perpendicular to symmetry axis):
$$I_{diam} = \frac{1}{4} m R^2 + \frac{1}{12} m L^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$I_{diam} = \frac{1}{4}(5)(0.1)^2 + \frac{1}{12}(5)(0.5)^2 = \frac{5 \times 0.01}{4} + \frac{5 \times 0.25}{12}$$
$$= \frac{0.05}{4} + \frac{1.25}{12} = 0.0125 + 0.10417 = 0.11667 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Parallel Axis Theorem.**

The rotation axis is at distance $R = 0.1$ m from the diametral axis through the COM.
$$I_{rot} = I_{diam} + m R^2 = 0.11667 + 5 \times (0.1)^2 = 0.11667 + 0.05 = 0.16667 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_{rot} \approx 0.167 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** What is an **inertia tensor**? Explain its properties. Show that for a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$, the **angular momentum** $\mathbf{H}$ and **kinetic energy** $T$ are given by:
$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}, \qquad T = \frac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}^T \mathbf{I}\, \boldsymbol{\omega}$$
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **inertia tensor** of a uniform rectangular block of mass $m$ and dimensions $a \times b \times c$, with its center of mass at the origin and edges aligned with the coordinate axes. Use the result to compute the inertia tensor of a block with $m = 4$ kg, $a = 0.6$ m, $b = 0.4$ m, $c = 0.2$ m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Inertia Tensor Concept:**

The **inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}$ is a 3×3 symmetric matrix that completely characterizes the *second moments* of mass distribution of a rigid body about a chosen reference point. It unifies the moments and products of inertia into a single tensorial object whose representation depends on the choice of coordinate frame.

**Key Properties (expected to be listed for full 7 marks):**

1. **Symmetry:** $I_{ij} = I_{ji}$, so only 6 of 9 elements are independent.
2. **Positive Semi-Definite:** All eigenvalues (principal moments) are non-negative, since kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}^T \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega} \geq 0$ for all $\boldsymbol{\omega}$.
3. **Coordinate Dependence:** Under a rotation $\mathbf{R}$, it transforms as $\mathbf{I}' = \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{I}\,\mathbf{R}^T$.
4. **Diagonalizability:** There always exists an orthonormal eigenframe (principal axes) in which $\mathbf{I}$ is diagonal.
5. **Trace Invariance:** $I_{xx} + I_{yy} + I_{zz}$ is invariant under rotation (sum of principal moments).

**Derivation of $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$:**

For a mass element $dm$ at position $\mathbf{r}$, its linear momentum is $dm\,\mathbf{v} = dm\,(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})$. Its angular momentum about the origin is $d\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{r}\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})\,dm$. Using the **vector triple product identity** $\mathbf{a}\times(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}) = \mathbf{b}(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{c}) - \mathbf{c}(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b})$:
$$d\mathbf{H} = \left[\boldsymbol{\omega}(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{r}) - \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{r}\cdot\boldsymbol{\omega})\right] dm$$

Integrating:
$$\mathbf{H} = \int_V \left[(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{r})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{r}\mathbf{r}^T\right] dm \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{I}\,\boldsymbol{\omega} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Derivation of $T$:**

Kinetic energy of the element: $dT = \tfrac{1}{2} dm\, \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} dm\, (\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})\cdot(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})$. Using the identity $(\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b})\cdot(\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}) = \|\mathbf{a}\|^2\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 - (\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b})^2$:
$$dT = \tfrac{1}{2} dm\, \boldsymbol{\omega}^T\!\left[(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{r})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{r}\mathbf{r}^T\right]\boldsymbol{\omega}$$

Integrating and using $T = \int dT$:
$$T = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}^T \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Part (b) — Block Inertia Tensor:**

[Refer to **Section 3.3** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The expected result:]

$$\mathbf{I}_c = \frac{m}{12}\begin{bmatrix} b^2 + c^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a^2 + c^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & a^2 + b^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Substitute numerical values** ($m = 4$, $a = 0.6$, $b = 0.4$, $c = 0.2$):

- $b^2 + c^2 = 0.16 + 0.04 = 0.20$
- $a^2 + c^2 = 0.36 + 0.04 = 0.40$
- $a^2 + b^2 = 0.36 + 0.16 = 0.52$

$$\mathbf{I}_c = \frac{4}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 0.20 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.40 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.52 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0.20 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.40 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.52 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{I}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0667 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1333 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1733 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap (3–4 marks lost):** Students often write the inertia tensor with **positive** off-diagonals. The *tensor* form uses **negative** off-diagonals, while the *matrix of second moments* (sometimes called $J$) uses **positive** off-diagonals. Know which one your module's reference (Spong, Craig, or Niku) uses.
>
> 2. **Parallel Axis Misapplication (4–5 marks lost):** The theorem applies **only to parallel axes**. A common error is using it when the second axis is *not parallel* to the first (e.g., rotating from $X$-axis to $Y$-axis). For perpendicular axes, you must use the full 3D tensor form, not the scalar version.
>
> 3. **COM vs. Geometric Center Confusion (2–3 marks lost):** For *non-uniform* or *asymmetric* bodies, the COM is **not** at the geometric center. The Steiner's theorem must always use the *COM*, not the geometric center, as the reference.
>
> 4. **Units Slippage (1 mark):** Always state the units explicitly — moments of inertia are in **kg·m²**, not m² or kg.
>
> 5. **Composite Body Error (3 marks):** When adding inertias of multiple parts, ensure **all** parts are referenced to the **same axis**. If they are not, apply the Parallel Axis Theorem to shift each part's inertia to the common reference axis *before* summing.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Mass distribution** = { mass $m$, COM $\mathbf{r}_c$, inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$ } — the three pillars of rigid-body dynamics.
- ⭐ **COM is the natural reference:** All inertia calculations should start at the COM, then shift to the desired frame via the **Parallel Axis Theorem**.
- ⭐ **Parallel Axis Theorem (scalar):** $I_P = I_c + m d^2$ — works only for **parallel axes**, and only when $I_c$ is the inertia about the **COM** axis.
- ⭐ **Parallel Axis Theorem (tensor):** $\mathbf{I}_P = \mathbf{I}_c + m[(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\mathbf{E} - \mathbf{d}\mathbf{d}^T]$ — use this in 3D problems.
- ⭐ **Inertia tensor is symmetric:** $I_{ij} = I_{ji}$, so only 6 independent components.
- ⭐ **Off-diagonal entries use NEGATIVE signs in the tensor form** (Spong convention) — verify with your reference textbook.
- ⭐ **Principal axes** are eigen-axes of $\mathbf{I}$; for symmetric bodies they coincide with symmetry axes, so off-diagonals are zero.
- ⭐ **Radius of gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I/m}$ — gives an *equivalent* point-mass distance.
- ⭐ **Key standard results to memorize:** Uniform rod ($mL^2/12$ center, $mL^2/3$ end), rectangular block ($m(b^2+c^2)/12$ etc.), solid cylinder ($mR^2/2$ axial), sphere ($2mR^2/5$).
- ⭐ **Composite body rule:** $I_{total} = \sum I_i$ — but all terms must be about the **same axis** (apply Steiner's to align).
- ⭐ **Angular momentum and kinetic energy** in tensor form: $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$, $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}^T\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ — these two equations are the *bridge* from mass distribution to dynamics.
- ⭐ **Engineering context:** In robot dynamics, link inertia must be re-evaluated at **every joint configuration** because the orientation of each link's principal axes changes relative to the joint axis. This is exactly what makes the manipulator inertia matrix $\mathbf{M(q)}$ configuration-dependent.
- ⭐ **Exam mantra:** *"Know the COM, know the principal axes, know the standard formulas, and never apply Steiner's to non-parallel axes."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Rigid Body Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Rigid Body Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In rigid-body robot dynamics, every link is mathematically treated as an infinitely stiff, massless-axis, non-deformable body and every joint as a perfectly rigid torque-transmission element. **Non-rigid body effects** (also called *compliance effects*, *structural flexibility*, or *elasticity effects*) are the set of dynamic phenomena that arise when these idealisations are relaxed — i.e., when the links bend, twist, stretch, and when the joints store and release elastic energy in their gearing, harmonic drives, belts, bearings, or torque sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERAT523 – Module 1):**
> The student is expected to identify the sources of non-rigidity in a serial manipulator, classify them into **joint flexibility** and **link flexibility**, write the governing equations of motion, and analyse the resulting vibration / servo-bandwidth limitations on robot performance.

Mathematically, a non-rigid manipulator has an *infinite-dimensional* configuration space (each point of a flexible link can move transversely, axially, and torsionally), so the dynamic order is theoretically infinite. For control and analysis, the system is reduced to a *finite-dimensional* model through techniques such as the **Assumed Modes Method (AMM)**, **Lumped-Parameter Modelling (LPM)**, or **Finite Element Method (FEM)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fishing Rod vs. The Steel Rod

Imagine a robotic arm built in two extreme ways:

| Analogy | Rigid Robot (Ideal) | Non-Rigid Robot (Real) |
|---|---|---|
| Object | A **steel rod** clamped to a wall | A **fishing rod** clamped to a wall |
| Behaviour under load | The end stays exactly where the kinematics says it should | The end *wobbles and overshoots* when you let go |
| Math | $M(q)\ddot q + C(q,\dot q) + G(q) = \tau$ (n equations) | Same equation **plus** a string of vibration modes |
| Key new concept | None | **Stiffness $K$**, **Natural frequency $\omega_n$**, **Damping $\zeta$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** When you flick the tip of a fishing rod, it oscillates. The frequency of oscillation depends on (a) how stiff the rod is ($EI$) and (b) how massive it is ($\rho A$). The same physics governs flexible robot links — and the *stiffer and lighter* the design, the *higher* the natural frequency, and the *less* the non-rigid effect shows up at typical motion speeds.

## 1.3 Why Non-Rigid Body Effects Cannot Be Ignored in Modern Robots

The trend in modern manufacturing robotics is exactly the direction that makes non-rigidity important:

- **Lighter links** (aluminium, carbon-fibre composites) → lower $EI$, higher tip deflection.
- **Higher speeds** (cycle times < 0.5 s) → inertial forces rival or exceed link stiffness.
- **Higher gear-reduction ratios** (harmonic drives $\geq 100{:}1$) → joint elasticity dominates.
- **Larger workspaces** (anthropomorphic arms > 1.5 m reach) → bending compliance accumulates.
- **Precision tasks** (PCB assembly, deburring, machining) → positioning errors of tens of micrometres matter.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick rule-of-thumb:** If the **lowest natural frequency of the structure is less than 5× the desired closed-loop bandwidth**, you *must* model the flexibility — otherwise the controller will excite the structural modes and the arm will vibrate uncontrollably.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Non-Rigid Effects

Non-rigid effects in robotic manipulators are classified by *where* the compliance is located.

### 2.1.1 Joint Flexibility (Source-Driven Compliance)

The compliance is concentrated at the **actuator–link interface**, typically inside the gearbox (harmonic drives, planetary gears, belt drives, torque-sensor couplings, RV reducers). The motor shaft angle $\theta_m$ and the link-side angle $\theta_l$ are *no longer identical* — they differ by a small elastic twist $\Delta\theta = \theta_m - \theta_l$.

**Why it happens:** every gear tooth, every belt strand, and every flexspline wall has finite torsional stiffness. For a harmonic drive, the no-load torsional stiffness is roughly $K \approx (1 \text{ to } 5)\times 10^5 \text{ N·m/rad}$.

### 2.1.2 Link Flexibility (Distributed Compliance)

The compliance is *spread along* the link. The link behaves like a **beam** that can bend (transverse deflection $w(x,t)$), twist (torsional deflection $\phi(x,t)$), and stretch (axial deflection $u(x,t)$). For slender, lightweight links, bending compliance is usually dominant.

### 2.1.3 Other Compliance Sources (Briefly)

- **Bearing compliance** – radial and axial spring-like behaviour of rolling-element bearings.
- **Encoder / sensor compliance** – mounting brackets that flex.
- **Foundation / pedestal compliance** – for mobile or large gantry robots.
- **Payload compliance** – grasped objects behave as flexible appendages.

## 2.2 Joint Flexibility — Spong's Simplified Model

For a single joint driven by a motor with rotor inertia $B$ (reflected to the link side) and torsional stiffness $K$ connecting motor to link:

$$
\tau_m = B\ddot\theta_m + K(\theta_m - \theta_l)
$$

$$
I\ddot\theta_l + C\dot\theta_l + \tau_g(q) = K(\theta_m - \theta_l)
$$

The **motivating torque** is the elastic restoring torque $\tau_e = K(\theta_m - \theta_l)$. Note that the actuator dynamics are now *part* of the system, so the model is **fourth-order** even for a single revolute joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order-of-magnitude check:** A typical harmonic drive RH-14 with $K \approx 2\times 10^5$ N·m/rad and link inertia $I \approx 5$ kg·m² has natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/I} \approx 200$ rad/s $\approx$ 32 Hz. This is dangerously close to the achievable servo bandwidth of modern drives (often 50–100 Hz), so joint flexibility *must* be considered.

## 2.3 Link Flexibility — Euler–Bernoulli Beam Model

For a prismatic, uniform, slender link undergoing small transverse deflections in the plane, the **Euler–Bernoulli equation** is the standard starting point:

$$
EI\frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A\frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = f(x,t)
$$

where

- $E$ = Young's modulus (N/m²), **typical value for steel ≈ 2.0×10¹¹ N/m²**;
- $I$ = second moment of area of the cross-section (m⁴);
- $\rho$ = material density (kg/m³);
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²);
- $w(x,t)$ = transverse deflection at axial position $x$ and time $t$;
- $f(x,t)$ = distributed transverse load (N/m).

### 2.3.1 Separation of Variables — the Eigenfunction Expansion

Assume $w(x,t) = \phi(x)\,q(t)$. Substituting and dividing through:

$$
\frac{EI\,\phi^{(4)}(x)}{\rho A\,\phi(x)} = -\frac{\ddot q(t)}{q(t)} = \omega^2
$$

This separates into two ODEs:

$$
\phi^{(4)}(x) - \beta^4 \phi(x) = 0, \qquad \ddot q(t) + \omega^2 q(t) = 0
$$

where $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A\,\omega^2}{EI}$. Boundary conditions (clamped-free, pinned-pinned, clamped-pinned, etc.) determine the **eigenfrequencies** $\omega_i$ and **mode shapes** $\phi_i(x)$.

For a **cantilever beam** (clamped at $x=0$, free at $x=L$), the first three are:

$$
\beta_1 L = 1.8751, \quad \beta_2 L = 4.6941, \quad \beta_3 L = 7.8548
$$

and $\omega_i = (\beta_i L)^2\sqrt{EI/(\rho A L^4)}$.

### 2.3.2 Assumed Modes Method (AMM)

For control design, the deflection is truncated to a finite sum of $n$ admissible mode shapes $\phi_i(x)$:

$$
w(x,t) \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_i(x)\,q_i(t)
$$

The $\phi_i$ may be the *true* eigenfunctions (Rayleigh–Ritz) or chosen *ad hoc* (e.g., static deflection shapes from a tip load). Substituting this series into the kinetic and potential energies and applying **Lagrange's equations** gives a finite-dimensional ODE system suitable for state-space control design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AMM is the workhorse of KTU-level analysis:** the true infinite-dimensional PDE is not solvable in closed form once the manipulator is moving (time-varying $q$). AMM reduces it to a tractable ODE set of order $2n$ (where $n$ is the number of retained modes). Typical practice: keep $n=1$ or $n=2$ modes — the *first* mode captures > 90 % of the elastic energy in most motions.

## 2.4 Lumped-Parameter Model (LPM) — Intuitive Beam Approximation

A flexible link is approximated as $n$ rigid sub-links connected by torsional springs of stiffness $K_i$. For a single-mode cantilever the simplest LPM is:

$$
m_t\ddot w + K\,w = F_{tip}
$$

where $m_t$ is the **effective modal mass** at the tip (≈ 33 % of total link mass for a uniform cantilever) and $K$ is the equivalent tip stiffness ($K = 3EI/L^3$ for a cantilever loaded transversely at its tip).

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-relevant formulas — memorise these for Part A and Part B.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint elastic torque | $\tau_e = K(\theta_m - \theta_l)$ | $K$ = torsional stiffness of gearbox | N·m/rad |
| 2 | Motor-side dynamics | $\tau_m = B\ddot\theta_m + \tau_e$ | $B$ = rotor inertia (link side) | kg·m² |
| 3 | Link-side dynamics | $I\ddot\theta_l + \tau_g = \tau_e$ | $I$ = link inertia, $\tau_g$ = gravity | kg·m², N·m |
| 4 | Natural frequency (joint) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/I_{eff}}$ | $I_{eff}$ = effective inertia | rad/s |
| 5 | Euler–Bernoulli PDE | $EI\,w^{(4)} + \rho A\,\ddot w = f$ | $w=w(x,t)$ | N/m |
| 6 | Cantilever 1st mode coeff. | $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$ | (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | Cantilever tip stiffness | $K_{tip} = 3EI/L^3$ | (transverse) | N/m |
| 8 | Cantilever tip natural freq. | $\omega_1 = (1.8751)^2\sqrt{EI/(\rho A L^4)}$ | (1st bending mode) | rad/s |
| 9 | Assumed modes expansion | $w(x,t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\phi_i(x)q_i(t)$ | $\phi_i$ admissible | m |
| 10 | Modal kinetic energy | $T_e = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i M_{ii}\dot q_i^2$ | $M_{ii}=\int_0^L \rho A\,\phi_i^2\,dx$ | J |
| 11 | Modal potential energy | $V_e = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i K_{ii} q_i^2$ | $K_{ii}=\int_0^L EI(\phi_i'')^2 dx$ | J |
| 12 | Damped modal equation | $M_{ii}\ddot q_i + D_{ii}\dot q_i + K_{ii} q_i = Q_i$ | $Q_i$ generalised force | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam pitfall:** Always use $K_{ii}=\int_0^L EI\,(\phi_i''(x))^2 dx$ — *not* $EI\,(d^2\phi_i/dx^2)$ in the integrand without the square. Forgetting the square is one of the most common mistakes in KTU scripts.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Non-Rigid Effects Matter |
|---|---|
| **Space robotics (Canadarm, Dextre)** | Long, slender booms; vibration must be damped before image capture. |
| **Surgical robots (Da Vinci, Raven)** | Long, thin instruments; tip jitter < 0.1 mm is essential. |
| **High-speed pick-and-place (delta, SCARA)** | Belt drives and carbon-fibre arms; vibration limits cycle time. |
| **Collaborative robots (cobots)** | Series-elastic actuators (SEAs) *deliberately* add joint compliance for safety. |
| **Macro-micro manipulators** | Macro arm is flexible, micro stage is rigid; hierarchical control. |
| **Helicopter / UAV rotor blades** | Same Euler–Bernoulli physics, in a different dress. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are written in full, with no algebraic step skipped. Each transition is annotated.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Mode Lumped-Parameter Model of a Cantilever Link

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a uniform cantilever beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, linear density $\rho A$, clamped at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$. A transverse point load $F(t)$ is applied at the tip ($x=L$). We model the tip transverse deflection $w(L,t) = w_t(t)$ as a single degree of freedom, with the link's inertia concentrated as an **effective tip mass** $m_{tip}$ and the elasticity represented as a **tip stiffness** $K_{tip}$.

### 3.1.2 Static Tip Stiffness (3.1.2a — Start from Beam Equation)

For a static load $F$ at the tip of a cantilever, the tip deflection is given by the standard result from strength of materials:

$$
w_{static} = \frac{F L^3}{3EI}
$$

By definition, the static stiffness is $K_{tip} = F / w_{static}$, therefore:

$$
K_{tip} = \frac{F}{F L^3 / (3EI)} = \frac{3EI}{L^3}
$$

This is the *tip stiffness* of a cantilever — it represents how many newtons of force are needed to push the tip down by one metre.

### 3.1.3 Effective Tip Mass (3.1.3a — Kinetic Energy Equivalence)

The kinetic energy of a beam undergoing tip motion of shape $w(x,t)=\phi(x)q(t)$ is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L \rho A \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\right)^2 dx = \frac{1}{2}\dot q^2 \int_0^L \rho A\,\phi^2(x)\,dx
$$

For the *first* bending mode of a uniform cantilever, the normalised mode shape is:

$$
\phi_1(x) = \cosh(\beta_1 x) - \cos(\beta_1 x) - \sigma_1\bigl[\sinh(\beta_1 x) - \sin(\beta_1 x)\bigr]
$$

with $\sigma_1 \approx 0.7341$ and $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$. The modal mass integral evaluates to:

$$
\int_0^L \rho A\,\phi_1^2(x)\,dx = \rho A L \cdot 0.250 \approx 0.25\,m_{link}
$$

This is the **Rayleigh effective mass** for the first cantilever mode: approximately ¼ of the link's total mass. Since $w_t = \phi_1(L) q$, with $\phi_1(L)=2\cosh(1.8751) - 2\cos(1.8751) \approx 2 - 2(-0.2079) \cdot 2 \approx 1.616$ (more precisely $\phi_1(L)=2$ in the unnormalised Rayleigh form), the effective tip mass is:

$$
m_{tip} = \frac{0.25\,m_{link}}{(\phi_1(L))^2} \approx \frac{1}{4}m_{link}
$$

A widely-used engineering approximation is $m_{tip} \approx \dfrac{33}{100}\,m_{link}$.

### 3.1.4 Single-DOF Equation of Motion (3.1.4a — Newton's Law at the Tip)

Equating inertial and elastic forces at the tip:

$$
m_{tip}\,\ddot w_t + K_{tip}\,w_t = F(t)
$$

Substituting the closed forms:

$$
\frac{1}{4}m_{link}\,\ddot w_t + \frac{3EI}{L^3} w_t = F(t)
$$

The natural frequency of this single-mode model is:

$$
\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{K_{tip}}{m_{tip}}} = \sqrt{\frac{3EI/L^3}{m_{link}/4}} = \sqrt{\frac{12\,EI}{m_{link}L^3}} = 2\sqrt{3}\,\sqrt{\frac{EI}{m_{link}L^3}}
$$

which is within 3 % of the exact first cantilever frequency $1.8751^2\sqrt{EI/(\rho A L^4)} = 3.516\sqrt{EI/(m_{link}L^3)}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler–Bernoulli PDE → Modal ODE via Separation of Variables

### 3.2.1 Starting PDE (3.2.1a)

$$
EI\frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A\frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0
$$

(free undamped vibration, $f(x,t)=0$.)

### 3.2.2 Separable Ansatz (3.2.2a)

Assume $w(x,t) = \Phi(x)T(t)$, with $\Phi(x)\neq 0$, $T(t)\neq 0$. Substituting:

$$
EI\,\Phi^{(4)}(x)\,T(t) + \rho A\,\Phi(x)\,\ddot T(t) = 0
$$

### 3.2.3 Separation (3.2.3a)

Divide throughout by $\Phi(x)T(t)$:

$$
\frac{EI\,\Phi^{(4)}(x)}{\Phi(x)} = -\frac{\rho A\,\ddot T(t)}{T(t)}
$$

The left side depends only on $x$, the right only on $t$. For equality for all $x,t$, both must equal a constant $-\rho A\,\omega^2$ (sign chosen to give oscillatory $T$):

$$
EI\,\Phi^{(4)}(x) = -\rho A\,\omega^2\,\Phi(x)
$$

$$
\ddot T(t) + \omega^2 T(t) = 0
$$

### 3.2.4 Spatial ODE (3.2.4a)

Rearrange the spatial ODE:

$$
\Phi^{(4)}(x) - \beta^4 \Phi(x) = 0, \qquad \beta^4 \equiv \frac{\rho A\,\omega^2}{EI}
$$

### 3.2.5 Characteristic Equation (3.2.5a)

The characteristic polynomial is $r^4 - \beta^4 = 0$, with roots $r = \pm \beta, \pm i\beta$. The general solution is:

$$
\Phi(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)
$$

### 3.2.6 Cantilever Boundary Conditions (3.2.6a)

At the clamped end $x=0$: $\Phi(0)=0$ and $\Phi'(0)=0$.
At the free end $x=L$: $\Phi''(L)=0$ and $\Phi'''(L)=0$.

Applying $\Phi(0)=0$:

$$
C_1 + C_3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_3 = -C_1
$$

Applying $\Phi'(0)=0$:

$$
\beta C_2 + \beta C_4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_4 = -C_2
$$

So $\Phi(x) = C_1[\cos\beta x - \cosh\beta x] + C_2[\sin\beta x - \sinh\beta x]$.

Applying $\Phi''(L)=0$ (zero bending moment at the free end):

$$
\Phi''(L) = -C_1 \beta^2[\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L] - C_2 \beta^2[\sin\beta L + \sinh\beta L] = 0
$$

$$
C_1[\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L] + C_2[\sin\beta L + \sinh\beta L] = 0 \quad (i)
$$

Applying $\Phi'''(L)=0$ (zero shear at the free end):

$$
\Phi'''(L) = C_1 \beta^3[\sin\beta L - \sinh\beta L] - C_2 \beta^3[\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L] = 0
$$

$$
C_1[\sin\beta L - \sinh\beta L] - C_2[\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L] = 0 \quad (ii)
$$

### 3.2.7 Frequency Equation (3.2.7a)

For a non-trivial solution, the determinant of (i)–(ii) must vanish:

$$
(\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L)^2 + (\sin\beta L + \sinh\beta L)(\sin\beta L - \sinh\beta L) = 0
$$

Simplify using $(\sin+\sinh)(\sin-\sinh)=\sin^2-\sinh^2$:

$$
(\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L)^2 + \sin^2\beta L - \sinh^2\beta L = 0
$$

Use $\sin^2+\cos^2=1$ and $\cosh^2-\sinh^2=1$:

$$
1 + 2\cos\beta L\,\cosh\beta L + \cosh^2\beta L - 1 - \sinh^2\beta L = 0
$$

$$
2\cos\beta L\,\cosh\beta L + (\cosh^2\beta L - \sinh^2\beta L) = 0
$$

$$
\cos\beta L\,\cosh\beta L + 1 = 0
$$

The transcendental frequency equation is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\cos(\beta L)\,\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0\;}
$$

Its first three roots are $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$, $\beta_2 L = 4.6941$, $\beta_3 L = 7.8548$, yielding:

$$
\omega_i = (\beta_i L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}
$$

### 3.2.8 First Mode Shape (3.2.8a)

For $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$, ratio $C_2/C_1$ from equation (ii):

$$
\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{\sin\beta_1 L - \sinh\beta_1 L}{\cos\beta_1 L + \cosh\beta_1 L}
$$

Numerically $\sin 1.8751 \approx 0.9544$, $\sinh 1.8751 \approx 3.1836$, $\cos 1.8751 \approx -0.2879$, $\cosh 1.8751 \approx 3.4836$. Then:

$$
\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{0.9544 - 3.1836}{-0.2879 + 3.4836} = \frac{-2.2292}{3.1957} = -0.6976
$$

The unnormalised first mode shape is therefore:

$$
\phi_1(x) = \cos(\beta_1 x) - 0.6976\,\sin(\beta_1 x) - \cosh(\beta_1 x) + 0.6976\,\sinh(\beta_1 x)
$$

which is a smooth curve starting at zero with zero slope at $x=0$ and reaching its maximum at the tip $x=L$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lagrangian for a Single Flexible Link with Tip Mass

### 3.3.1 Kinematic Description (3.3.1a)

A planar single-link manipulator rotates in the horizontal plane with rigid-body angle $\theta(t)$. The link is flexible in the direction transverse to its axis. The position of a material point at distance $x$ along the link is:

$$
\mathbf{p}(x,t) = \begin{bmatrix} x\cos\theta(t) - w(x,t)\sin\theta(t) \\[4pt] x\sin\theta(t) + w(x,t)\cos\theta(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3.2 Velocity (3.3.2a)

Differentiate with respect to time:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{bmatrix} -x\dot\theta\sin\theta - \dot w\sin\theta - w\dot\theta\cos\theta \\[4pt] x\dot\theta\cos\theta + \dot w\cos\theta - w\dot\theta\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Its squared magnitude is:

$$
\vert\dot{\mathbf{p}}\vert^2 = (x\dot\theta + \dot w)^2 \cdot \text{(terms cancel to first order in } w\text{)} \approx x^2\dot\theta^2 + 2x\dot\theta\,\dot w + \dot w^2
$$

(We drop the $w^2\dot\theta^2$ term because $w$ is small — geometrically linearised model.)

### 3.3.3 Kinetic Energy (3.3.3a)

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L \rho A \left(x^2\dot\theta^2 + 2x\dot\theta\,\dot w + \dot w^2\right) dx + \frac{1}{2}m_t\left[(L\dot\theta)^2 + 2L\dot\theta\,w(L,t) + w^2(L,t)\right]
$$

With $w(x,t) = \sum_{i=1}^n \phi_i(x) q_i(t)$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}I_{rig}\dot\theta^2 + \sum_i M_{i\theta}\,\dot q_i\dot\theta + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} M_{ij}\dot q_i\dot q_j
$$

where

$$
I_{rig} = \int_0^L \rho A x^2 dx + m_t L^2, \qquad M_{i\theta} = \int_0^L \rho A x\,\phi_i(x)\,dx + m_t L\,\phi_i(L)
$$

$$
M_{ij} = \int_0^L \rho A\,\phi_i(x)\phi_j(x)\,dx + m_t\,\phi_i(L)\phi_j(L)
$$

### 3.3.4 Potential Energy (3.3.4a)

For pure bending (gravity in horizontal plane is irrelevant):

$$
V = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L EI\left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}\right)^2 dx = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} K_{ij}\,q_i q_j
$$

with

$$
K_{ij} = \int_0^L EI\,\phi_i''(x)\phi_j''(x)\,dx
$$

### 3.3.5 Lagrange's Equations (3.3.5a)

For the rigid coordinate $\theta$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot\theta} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta} = \tau
$$

Computing:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot\theta} = I_{rig}\dot\theta + \sum_i M_{i\theta}\dot q_i
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot\theta} = I_{rig}\ddot\theta + \sum_i M_{i\theta}\ddot q_i
$$

For motions in the horizontal plane, $\partial T/\partial\theta = 0$ (no gravity term in $V$ since $V$ is bending only). So:

$$
\boxed{\;I_{rig}\ddot\theta + \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{i\theta}\ddot q_i = \tau\;}
$$

For each elastic coordinate $q_i$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot q_i} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} = 0
$$

This yields:

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot q_j + M_{i\theta}\ddot\theta + \sum_{j=1}^{n} D_{ij}\dot q_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_{ij}\,q_j = 0\;}
$$

where the damping matrix $D_{ij}$ has been added (Rayleigh or modal damping).

### 3.3.6 Compact Matrix Form (3.3.6a)

Stacking the coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [\theta,\, q_1,\, \ldots,\, q_n]^T$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_{rig} & M_{\theta q}^T \\ M_{\theta q} & M_{qq} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \ddot\theta \\ \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_e \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & D_{qq} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot\theta \\ \dot{\mathbf{q}}_e \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & K_{qq} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \theta \\ \mathbf{q}_e \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \tau \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The two **off-diagonal coupling blocks** $M_{\theta q}$ are the source of *dynamic coupling* between rigid motion and elastic vibration — they cause the familiar phenomenon of *rigid motion exciting vibration* and *vibration feeding back into the rigid dynamics*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical / Decision Matrix — When Does Flexibility Matter?

| Question | Indicator | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Is the lowest natural frequency $< 5\times$ desired bandwidth? | $\omega_1 / \omega_{BW} < 5$ | Model as flexible | Rigid approximation OK |
| Is the link slender? | $L/r > 50$ (radius of gyration $r$) | Bending flexibility important | Torsion dominates? |
| Is the gearbox harmonic drive / belt? | Yes | Use Spong joint model | Rigid joint OK |
| Are the cycle times < 1 s? | Yes | Inertial forces can excite modes | Quasi-static analysis OK |
| Is the payload < 1/10 link mass? | No (payload comparable) | Include payload in modal mass | Ignore payload inertia |
| Is the motion in a gravitational field large? | Yes for vertical | Add gravity to $V$ | $V$=bending only |

> [!TIP]
> This table is exam-favourite: a 7-mark question in KTU frequently asks *"For a given manipulator specification, justify whether joint flexibility, link flexibility, or both must be modelled."*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sources of Non-Rigid Effects in a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    actA[Actuator Motor] --> grA[Gearbox Reducer]
    grA --> jA[Joint Compliance K_joint]
    jA --> lnA[Link Material]
    lnA --> endA[End Effector]
    lnA --> bend[Link Bending EI]
    lnA --> tors[Link Torsion GJ]
    lnA --> axSt[Link Axial EA]
    grA --> hD[Harmonic Drive Softness]
    grA --> bD[Belt Drive Elasticity]
    grA --> rv[RV Reducer Torsional Spring]
    endA --> payL[Payload Inertia]
    endA --> envF[Contact / Process Forces]
    bend --> modA[Bending Mode Shapes phi i x]
    tors --> modB[Torsion Mode Shapes psi i x]
    jA --> spong[Spong Joint Model]
    modA --> amm[Assumed Modes Method]
    modB --> amm
    spong --> ctlReq[Control Design Must Consider Flexibility]
    amm --> ctlReq
    payL --> ctlReq
    envF --> ctlReq
    hD --> ctlReq
    bD --> ctlReq
    rv --> ctlReq
    ctlReq --> outP[Output Positioning Error and Vibration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Modelling Hierarchy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Real Flexible Manipulator] --> a[Identify Compliance Source]
    a --> a1[Joint Only]
    a --> a2[Link Only]
    a --> a3[Both]
    a1 --> m1[Lumped Spring at Joint]
    a2 --> m2[Lumped Parameter or Assumed Modes]
    a3 --> m3[Combined Spong and AMM Model]
    m1 --> eom1[Derive EOM Lagrange or Newton Euler]
    m2 --> eom2[Derive EOM with Elastic Coordinates]
    m3 --> eom3[Derive Coupled EOM]
    eom1 --> v1[Compute Natural Frequency]
    eom2 --> v2[Compute Modal Frequencies]
    eom3 --> v3[Compute Coupled Modes]
    v1 --> dec[Compare with Servo Bandwidth]
    v2 --> dec
    v3 --> dec
    dec --> ch1[Ratio less than 5 Model as Flexible]
    dec --> ch2[Ratio greater than 5 Rigid Approximation OK]
    ch1 --> ctrl[Apply Flexible Manipulator Control Strategies]
    ch2 --> ctrl2[Apply Standard Rigid Robot Control]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Cantilever Mode Shapes Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base[Clamped End x equals 0] --> m1[First Mode Shape Smooth Single Curvature]
    m1 --> m1t[Tip Deflection Maximum]
    base --> m2[Second Mode Shape One Internal Node]
    m2 --> m2t[Tip Deflection with Sign Reversal]
    base --> m3[Third Mode Shape Two Internal Nodes]
    m3 --> m3t[Tip Deflection with Two Sign Reversals]
    m1t --> p1[Period T1 equals 2 pi over omega1]
    m2t --> p2[Period T2 equals 2 pi over omega2]
    m3t --> p3[Period T3 equals 2 pi over omega3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** the first mode of a cantilever is a *monotonic* curve; the second has one *internal node*; the third has two. Each higher mode oscillates faster (higher $\omega_i$) and has *more* internal zeros. For control, the *first mode* is by far the most energetic and is the one that must be controlled.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define non-rigid body effects in robot manipulators. List any two sources. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Non-rigid body effects are the deviations from the ideal assumption of perfectly rigid links and joints in a robotic manipulator. They manifest as elastic deformations — joint compliance in gear trains and link flexibility in slender members — leading to additional oscillatory dynamics not captured by the standard rigid-body model $M(q)\ddot q + C(q,\dot q)\dot q + G(q) = \tau$.
>
> Two sources: (i) **joint flexibility** — torsional elasticity in harmonic drives, belt drives, RV reducers, torque sensors; (ii) **link flexibility** — bending, torsion, and axial compliance of long, slender, lightweight links (steel, aluminium, carbon-fibre).

---

### Q2. State the Euler–Bernoulli equation for the transverse vibration of a uniform slender beam and identify each term. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
EI\frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A\frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = f(x,t)
$$

- $E$ = Young's modulus (material stiffness).
- $I$ = second moment of area of cross-section (geometric stiffness).
- $EI$ = flexural rigidity.
- $\rho$ = material density.
- $A$ = cross-sectional area; $\rho A$ = mass per unit length.
- $w(x,t)$ = transverse deflection.
- $f(x,t)$ = external distributed transverse load per unit length.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Two independent alternatives are given below; the student answers *one*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)`** Derive the frequency equation for the transverse vibration of a uniform cantilever beam starting from the Euler–Bernoulli PDE. State the first three natural frequencies. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**` (b)`** A flexible link is modelled as a cantilever of length $L = 1.0$ m, flexural rigidity $EI = 200$ N·m², and total mass $m = 1.5$ kg. Using a single-mode lumped-parameter model with effective tip mass $m_{tip} = m/3$ and tip stiffness $K_{tip} = 3EI/L^3$, compute the first natural frequency and the static tip deflection under a tip load of $F = 5$ N. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Model Solution to (a)

We begin with the **Euler–Bernoulli PDE** for free undamped vibration:

$$
EI\frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A\frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0
$$

(Step 1) Substitute the separable ansatz $w(x,t) = \Phi(x)T(t)$:

$$
EI\,\Phi^{(4)}T + \rho A\,\Phi\,\ddot T = 0
$$

(Step 2) Divide by $\Phi T$:

$$
\frac{EI\,\Phi^{(4)}}{\Phi} = -\frac{\rho A\,\ddot T}{T} = -\rho A\,\omega^2
$$

(Step 3) Spatial ODE:

$$
\Phi^{(4)} - \beta^4 \Phi = 0, \quad \beta^4 = \frac{\rho A\,\omega^2}{EI}
$$

(Step 4) General solution:

$$
\Phi(x) = C_1\cos\beta x + C_2\sin\beta x + C_3\cosh\beta x + C_4\sinh\beta x
$$

(Step 5) Cantilever boundary conditions:

- $x=0$: $\Phi(0)=0 \Rightarrow C_1 + C_3 = 0 \Rightarrow C_3 = -C_1$
- $x=0$: $\Phi'(0)=0 \Rightarrow C_2 + C_4 = 0 \Rightarrow C_4 = -C_2$
- $x=L$: $\Phi''(L)=0 \Rightarrow -C_1(\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L) - C_2(\sin\beta L + \sinh\beta L) = 0$
- $x=L$: $\Phi'''(L)=0 \Rightarrow C_1(\sin\beta L - \sinh\beta L) - C_2(\cos\beta L + \cosh\beta L) = 0$

(Step 6) Setting the determinant of the resulting $2\times 2$ system to zero:

$$
\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0
$$

(Step 7) The first three roots of this transcendental equation are:

$$
\beta_1 L = 1.8751, \quad \beta_2 L = 4.6941, \quad \beta_3 L = 7.8548
$$

(Step 8) Hence the natural frequencies are:

$$
\omega_i = (\beta_i L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} = (\beta_i L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{m L^3}}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $L = 1.0$ m, $EI = 200$ N·m², $m = 1.5$ kg, $m_{tip} = m/3 = 0.5$ kg, $F = 5$ N.

(Step 1) Compute tip stiffness:

$$
K_{tip} = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 200}{1.0^3} = 600 \text{ N/m}
$$

(Step 2) Compute natural frequency:

$$
\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{K_{tip}}{m_{tip}}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{0.5}} = \sqrt{1200} \approx 34.64 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to Hz: $f_1 = \omega_1 / (2\pi) \approx 5.51$ Hz.

(Step 3) Compute static tip deflection under $F = 5$ N:

$$
w_{static} = \frac{F}{K_{tip}} = \frac{5}{600} = 8.33\times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 8.33 \text{ mm}
$$

(Step 4) Cross-check with the direct beam formula:

$$
w_{static} = \frac{FL^3}{3EI} = \frac{5 \times 1.0^3}{3 \times 200} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \;\checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** $\omega_1 \approx 34.64$ rad/s (5.51 Hz) and $w_{static} \approx 8.33$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students confuse $m_{tip} = m/3$ with $m_{tip} = m/4$. The value $m/3$ is a common engineering approximation (33 % of link mass effectively at the tip for a uniform cantilever), while the *Rayleigh* value is exactly $\approx 0.25\,m$. Use the value given in the question; do not substitute your own. **1 mark is lost if the wrong effective mass is used.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`(a)`** Develop Spong's simplified model of a robot with joint flexibility. Write the equations of motion and identify the natural frequency of the joint. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**`(b)`** For a SCARA-type manipulator with a harmonic-drive joint, the link inertia is $I_l = 4$ kg·m², the actuator rotor inertia (reflected to the link side) is $B = 0.5$ kg·m², and the joint torsional stiffness is $K = 1.6 \times 10^4$ N·m/rad. Determine (i) the joint natural frequency, (ii) the natural frequency of the motor alone (i.e., with the link clamped), and (iii) comment on whether joint flexibility will affect the achievable servo bandwidth. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Model Solution to (a)

(Step 1) **Physical setup.** A motor with rotor inertia $B$ (already reflected through the gear ratio to the link side) drives a link of inertia $I_l$ through a torsional spring of stiffness $K$. The motor angle is $\theta_m$ and the link angle is $\theta_l$.

(Step 2) **Free-body at the motor side.** The motor produces torque $\tau_m$, which accelerates the rotor and is opposed by the elastic reaction torque from the spring:

$$
B\ddot\theta_m + K(\theta_m - \theta_l) = \tau_m
$$

(Step 3) **Free-body at the link side.** The link is accelerated by the spring torque, and opposed by gravity $\tau_g$ (and damping, ignored here):

$$
I_l\ddot\theta_l + \tau_g(q) = K(\theta_m - \theta_l)
$$

(Step 4) **Consolidation.** Define elastic twist $\delta = \theta_m - \theta_l$ and the elastic torque $\tau_e = K\delta$. Then:

$$
B\ddot\theta_m = \tau_m - \tau_e
$$

$$
I_l\ddot\theta_l = \tau_e - \tau_g
$$

(Step 5) **Natural frequency.** In the special case of a stationary manipulator with no gravity, $\theta_l = 0$, and the system reduces to a two-inertia torsional system. The natural frequency is:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{K\left(\frac{1}{B} + \frac{1}{I_l}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{K(B+I_l)}{B\,I_l}}
$$

(Step 6) **Limiting cases.**

- If $B \ll I_l$ (light rotor, heavy link): $\omega_n \approx \sqrt{K/I_l}$.
- If $I_l \ll B$ (light link, heavy rotor): $\omega_n \approx \sqrt{K/B}$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $I_l = 4$ kg·m², $B = 0.5$ kg·m², $K = 1.6 \times 10^4$ N·m/rad.

**(i) Joint natural frequency (full system):**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K(B+I_l)}{B\,I_l}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^4 \times (0.5 + 4.0)}{0.5 \times 4.0}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^4 \times 4.5}{2.0}}
$$

$$
