= \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^4} = 189.7 \text{ rad/s} \;\;(\approx 30.2 \text{ Hz})
$$

**(ii) Motor-alone natural frequency (link clamped, $\theta_l = 0$):**

$$
\omega_B = \sqrt{\frac{K}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^4}{0.5}} = \sqrt{3.2 \times 10^4} = 178.9 \text{ rad/s} \;\;(\approx 28.5 \text{ Hz})
$$

**(iii) Comment on servo bandwidth:**

A typical modern industrial servo has a current-loop bandwidth of 1 kHz, a velocity-loop bandwidth of 100–200 Hz, and a position-loop bandwidth of 10–30 Hz. Since the joint natural frequency is approximately **30 Hz** — *right at the limit* of typical position-loop bandwidth — joint flexibility will definitely affect achievable performance. The position controller must either:

(a) keep its bandwidth well below ~10 Hz (≈ ⅓ of $\omega_n$ for safety margin), or
(b) use a flexible-joint control law such as **feedback linearisation** (Spong), **singular perturbation**, **backstepping**, or **passivity-based control**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b – i):** A common error is to compute $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/I_l}$ instead of the correct two-inertia formula. The single-inertia formula *only* holds when $B\ll I_l$, which is not the case here ($B/I_l = 1/8$). Always verify which inertia combination is in effect. **Lose 2 marks for using the wrong formula.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b – iii):** Many students stop after computing $\omega_n$ and do not give the *control implication*. The question explicitly asks for a comment on the servo bandwidth, so omitting this loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Two sources of non-rigidity:** joint flexibility (concentrated at gearbox) and link flexibility (distributed along link).
- **Euler–Bernoulli PDE:** $EI w^{(4)} + \rho A \ddot w = f$. Memorise the form and each term.
- **Cantilever frequency equation:** $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$. First three roots: $1.8751,\; 4.6941,\; 7.8548$.
- **Cantilever tip stiffness:** $K_{tip} = 3EI/L^3$ (transverse load at free end).
- **First natural frequency (cantilever):** $\omega_1 = (1.8751)^2 \sqrt{EI/(\rho A L^4)}$.
- **Lumped-parameter (single-mode) approximation:** $m_{tip} \approx m/3$, $K_{tip} = 3EI/L^3$.
- **Assumed Modes Method (AMM):** truncate $w(x,t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_i(x) q_i(t)$ to a finite number of admissible modes; keep $n=1$ or $n=2$ for control design.
- **Spong joint model:** two ODEs per joint — one for the rotor, one for the link, coupled through the elastic torque $K(\theta_m-\theta_l)$.
- **Two-inertia natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K\left(\dfrac{1}{B} + \dfrac{1}{I_l}\right)}$.
- **Rule of thumb:** model flexibility when $\omega_1 < 5 \times$ desired closed-loop bandwidth.
- **Modal energy integrals:** $M_{ij}=\int_0^L \rho A\,\phi_i\phi_j\,dx$, $K_{ij}=\int_0^L EI\,\phi_i''\phi_j''\,dx$ — *squared* in the potential energy integrand.
- **Control strategies for flexible manipulators:** input shaping, feedback linearisation, singular perturbation, backstepping, passivity-based control, and $H_\infty$ robust control.
- **Real-world drivers:** lighter links, higher speeds, harmonic drives, large workspaces, precision tasks — all increase the relative importance of non-rigid effects.
- **Exam tip:** always state assumptions (small deflections, linear elasticity, Euler–Bernoulli validity — i.e., $L \gg$ cross-sectional dimension) before writing any equation. KTU examiners reward clearly justified assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 : NEWTON'S EQUATION — CORE TECHNICAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of rigid-body robot dynamics, **Newton's Equation** (more completely, the **Newton–Euler Equations**) constitutes the foundational set of vector-based dynamic balance laws governing the translational and rotational motion of a rigid link under the action of forces and moments.

Mathematically, for a rigid body of mass **m** and inertia tensor **I**, the Newton–Euler equations are written as the coupled pair:

- **Newton's Translational Equation** (linear momentum balance)
- **Euler's Rotational Equation** (angular momentum balance)

Together they transform the abstract "dynamics problem" of finding joint torques required to produce a desired motion trajectory into a **deterministic, solvable system of ordinary differential equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PERAT523, Module 1):**
> The Newton–Euler formulation is the *first* of the two principal dynamic-modeling paradigms covered (the second being the **Lagrangian formulation**). Examiners almost always compare the two. Be prepared to derive, state, and contrast both.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a merry-go-round in a children's park. Two physical effects happen simultaneously:

1. The **platform translates** (if mounted on wheels) — governed by *Newton's second law* ($F = ma$).
2. The **platform rotates** about its center — governed by *Euler's equation* ($N = I\alpha + \omega \times (I\omega)$).

A robot link behaves exactly like this merry-go-round, except:
- It may be **translating** along a trajectory **AND** rotating about its own center simultaneously.
- The motion is referenced to a **moving frame** attached to the previous link — this is where the mathematics becomes non-trivial.
- Multiple links are **stacked**, so the dynamics of link 2 depends on link 1's motion, link 3 depends on 2, and so on.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** Newton's equation for a robot is just *F = ma* and *N = Iα* written cleverly for a stack of interconnected spinning rods. The "clever" part is handling moving reference frames.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Units (SI) | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Mass of link | kg | kg |
| $\mathbf{v}$ | Linear velocity | m/s | m/s |
| $\mathbf{a}$ | Linear acceleration | m/s² | m/s² |
| $\mathbf{I}$ | Inertia tensor (3×3) | kg·m² | kg·m² |
| $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s | rad/s |
| $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ | Angular acceleration | rad/s² | rad/s² |
| $\mathbf{f}$ | Force exerted on link | N | N |
| $\mathbf{n}$ | Moment exerted on link | N·m | N·m |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners routinely award a **1-mark bonus** in Part A if you write *both* the translational and rotational equations in vector form and **correctly label units**. Always do it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1 : NEWTON'S EQUATION — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition — The "Why" and "How"

Newton's equation in robot dynamics is built in **four conceptual layers**. Mastering these layers individually is the difference between a 60% answer and a 95% answer.

### Layer 1 — The Bare Particle Equation
For a point mass moving in an inertial frame, $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$. This is the textbook version. It contains **no rotation**, so it is useless for a robot link.

### Layer 2 — Extension to a Rigid Body (Translation)
A rigid body is a *continuous distribution* of point masses. Summing $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$ over the entire body yields:
$$\mathbf{F} = m \mathbf{a}_c$$
where $\mathbf{a}_c$ is the acceleration of the **center of mass**. This handles *translation* of the body.

### Layer 3 — Extension to a Rigid Body (Rotation)
Rotational motion requires **angular momentum balance**:
$$\mathbf{N} = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_I$$
where $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$. When this derivative is expanded in a *moving* body-fixed frame, the **transport theorem** generates the famous **gyroscopic term** $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$.

### Layer 4 — Coupling with a Moving Reference Frame
Real robot links do not move in the inertial frame — they move in the **link-attached frame**. This introduces **Coriolis** and **centrifugal** terms during differentiation, completing the full Newton–Euler formulation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (translational) | $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}_c$ | Translation of a rigid body's CG |
| 2 | Angular Momentum Definition | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Single rigid body rotation |
| 3 | Euler's Equation (rotational) | $\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ | Rotational dynamics in body frame |
| 4 | Newton–Euler (combined) | $\begin{cases} \mathbf{f} = m\dot{\mathbf{v}} \\ \mathbf{n} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}) \end{cases}$ | Single rigid body with general motion |
| 5 | Recursive Outward Iteration (i → i+1) | $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1} = {}^{i+1}\mathbf{R}_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i + \mathbf{z}_0 \dot{q}_{i+1})$ | Forward propagation of angular velocity |
| 6 | Linear Acceleration Propagation | $\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{i+1} = {}^{i+1}\mathbf{R}_i (\dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{z}_0 \dot{q}_{i+1})) + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1} \times \mathbf{p}_{i+1}^*$ | Forward propagation of acceleration |
| 7 | Force Balance at Link | $\mathbf{f}_{i} = \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + m_i \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i - m_i \mathbf{g}$ | Backward propagation of forces |
| 8 | Moment Balance at Link | $\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{n}_{i+1} + \mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{r}_{i+1,c} \times \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$ | Backward propagation of moments |
| 9 | Joint Torque Mapping | $\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T {}^i\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (revolute) or $\mathbf{f}_i^T {}^i\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (prismatic) | Final mapping to actuator torques |
| 10 | Gyroscopic / Convective Term | $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ | Arises only when $\mathbf{I}$ is diagonal in body frame |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Formatting Trap:** Notice the use of `\vert` for absolute value and `\mathbf{}` for vectors. **Never use raw `|` inside a table cell** — it breaks the markdown parser and your answer sheet may render incorrectly. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mathbf{x}$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Newton's Equation Lives in the Real World

| Engineering Field | Application of Newton–Euler |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Robotics (KUKA, FANUC, ABB)** | Real-time joint torque computation at 1 kHz control loops |
| **Spacecraft Dynamics** | Attitude control and reaction-wheel momentum exchange |
| **Humanoid Robotics (ASIMO, Atlas)** | Whole-body dynamics for balance on uneven terrain |
| **Vehicle Dynamics (ESP systems)** | Yaw, roll, and pitch dynamics under tire forces |
| **Biomechanics** | Musculoskeletal torque estimation during gait |

> [!NOTE]
> The **computational complexity** of Newton–Euler is $O(n)$ for an $n$-joint manipulator (vs. $O(n^4)$ for Lagrangian). This makes it the **algorithm of choice** for real-time model-based control — a key board-exam discussion point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1 : NEWTON'S EQUATION — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Translational Equation for a Rigid Body

We begin by considering a rigid body as a continuous distribution of infinitesimal mass elements $dm$, each located at position vector $\mathbf{r}$ measured from an inertial frame origin.

**Step 1 — Sum the elementary Newton's laws.**
Newton's second law for each element gives $d\mathbf{F} = dm \cdot \mathbf{a}$. Integrating over the entire body volume $\mathcal{V}$:
$$\mathbf{F} = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \mathbf{a}(\mathbf{r}) \, dm$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that acceleration of any element = acceleration of CG + rotational contribution.**
For a rigid body, the acceleration of a generic point at $\mathbf{r}$ decomposes as:
$$\mathbf{a}(\mathbf{r}) = \mathbf{a}_c + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \boldsymbol{\rho} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \boldsymbol{\rho})$$
where $\boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}_c$ is the position vector from the center of mass.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**
$$\mathbf{F} = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \left[ \mathbf{a}_c + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \boldsymbol{\rho} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \boldsymbol{\rho}) \right] dm$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each integral.**
By definition of the center of mass: $\int_{\mathcal{V}} \boldsymbol{\rho} \, dm = 0$. Therefore the integral of the second and third terms vanishes:
$$\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{a}_c \int_{\mathcal{V}} dm + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \underbrace{\int_{\mathcal{V}} \boldsymbol{\rho}\,dm}_{=\,0} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \underbrace{\int_{\mathcal{V}} \boldsymbol{\rho}\,dm}_{=\,0})$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{F} = m \mathbf{a}_c}$$

This is the **Newton's equation for translation of a rigid body** — the total external force equals mass times the acceleration of the center of mass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Rotational Equation

**Step 1 — Start with angular momentum.**
For a rigid body, the angular momentum about the center of mass is:
$$\mathbf{H} = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \boldsymbol{\rho} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \boldsymbol{\rho}) \, dm$$

**Step 2 — Use the vector identity** $\boldsymbol{\rho} \times (\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \boldsymbol{\rho}) = (\boldsymbol{\rho} \cdot \boldsymbol{\rho})\boldsymbol{\omega} - (\boldsymbol{\rho} \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega})\boldsymbol{\rho}$.

When integrated against $dm$, this yields the **inertia tensor**:
$$\mathbf{I} = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \left[ (\boldsymbol{\rho} \cdot \boldsymbol{\rho})\mathbf{1} - \boldsymbol{\rho}\boldsymbol{\rho}^T \right] dm$$

So $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's second law** (rate of change of angular momentum equals applied moment):
$$\mathbf{N} = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_I$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate in the inertial frame using the transport theorem.**
Since $\mathbf{I}$ is *constant* in the body frame but $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is measured in the inertial frame, we apply:
$$\frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_I = \frac{d\mathbf{H}}{dt}\bigg|_B + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{H}$$

**Step 5 — Expand term by term.**
$$\mathbf{N} = \frac{d}{dt}(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})\bigg|_B + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$$

Because $\mathbf{I}$ is constant in the body frame:
$$\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}\bigg|_B + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$$

**Step 6 — Identify** $\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}\vert_B = \boldsymbol{\alpha}$ (angular acceleration). Thus:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ is the **gyroscopic moment**. It vanishes only when $\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is parallel to $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ (i.e., when the body rotates about a principal axis). KTU questions frequently test this nuance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Combined Newton–Euler for a Robot Link

Consider link $i$ of a serial-chain manipulator, with frame $\{i\}$ attached to it. Let $\mathbf{v}_i$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ be the linear and angular velocities of the link's origin, and let the link's center of mass be at position $\mathbf{r}_{i,c}$ relative to frame $\{i\}$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of the CG.**
$$\mathbf{v}_c = \mathbf{v}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c}$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration of the CG (using the product rule for moving vectors).**
$$\mathbf{a}_c = \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c})$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's translational equation to the link.**
$$\mathbf{f}_i - m_i \mathbf{g} = m_i \mathbf{a}_c$$

Substituting:
$$\mathbf{f}_i - m_i \mathbf{g} = m_i \left[ \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c}) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's rotational equation about the link's CG.**
$$\mathbf{n}_i - \mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$$

> Note: The term $\mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i$ arises from transferring the force $\mathbf{f}_i$ (applied at the link origin) to the center of mass for moment equivalence.

**Step 5 — Assemble the final vector pair** (this is the **per-link Newton–Euler**):
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}_i &= m_i \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + m_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c} + m_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c}) + m_i \mathbf{g} \\
\mathbf{n}_i &= \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i) + \mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Recursive outward update** (link $i+1$ from link $i$):
$$\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1} &= {}^{i+1}\mathbf{R}_i \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_i + \mathbf{z}_{i-1}\dot{q}_{i+1} \right) \\
\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{i+1} &= {}^{i+1}\mathbf{R}_i \left( \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_{i+1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_{i+1}) \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Recursive inward update** (link $i$ from link $i+1$):
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}_i &= \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + m_i \mathbf{a}_{c,i} \\
\mathbf{n}_i &= \mathbf{n}_{i+1} + \mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{r}_{i+1,c} \times \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Joint torque extraction.**
For a revolute joint along axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$:
$$\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T \, {}^i\mathbf{R}_{i-1} \mathbf{z}_0 = n_{i,z}$$

For a prismatic joint:
$$\tau_i = \mathbf{f}_i^T \, {}^i\mathbf{R}_{i-1} \mathbf{z}_0 = f_{i,z}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A uniform rod of mass $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ and length $L = 1\,\text{m}$ rotates about a fixed vertical axis through one end with angular velocity $\omega = 5\,\text{rad/s}$ and angular acceleration $\alpha = 2\,\text{rad/s}^2$. Find (a) the torque required, (b) the force on the pivot.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Identify the inertia.**
For a uniform rod rotating about an end: $I = \frac{1}{3}mL^2 = \frac{1}{3}(2)(1)^2 = 0.667\,\text{kg·m}^2$.

**Step B — Apply Euler's equation.**
Since rotation is about a principal axis, $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}) = 0$. Thus:
$$N = I\alpha = 0.667 \times 2 = 1.333\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step C — Apply Newton's equation.**
Center of mass is at $L/2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ from pivot. Centripetal acceleration:
$$a_n = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 25 \times 0.5 = 12.5\,\text{m/s}^2$$

Tangential acceleration: $a_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0\,\text{m/s}^2$.

The pivot must provide:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_n &= m a_n = 2 \times 12.5 = 25\,\text{N} \quad \text{(directed inward)} \\
F_t &= m a_t = 2 \times 1.0 = 2\,\text{N} \quad \text{(tangential)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D — Magnitude of pivot force.**
$$F = \sqrt{F_n^2 + F_t^2} = \sqrt{25^2 + 2^2} \approx 25.08\,\text{N}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU board examiners expect the **direction** of the centripetal force to be explicitly drawn (radially inward). Skipping the diagram typically costs 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1 : NEWTON'S EQUATION — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Newton–Euler Recursive Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize base frame velocities] --> B[Set omega0 equals 0, alpha0 equals 0, vdot0 equals g]
    B --> C[Outward Iteration: i = 1 to n]
    C --> D[Compute omega_i using omega_{i-1} and qdot_i]
    D --> E[Compute alpha_i using alpha_{i-1} and qddot_i]
    E --> F[Compute vdot_i and vdot_{c,i} at link i CG]
    F --> G{All links processed?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Inward Iteration: i = n down to 1]
    H --> I[Compute force f_i at link i CG]
    I --> J[Compute moment n_i at link i CG]
    J --> K{All links processed?}
    K -- No --> H
    K -- Yes --> L[Project n_i or f_i onto joint axis z_{i-1}]
    L --> M[Output: Joint torques tau_1 to tau_n]
    M --> N[End]

    stepA[Forward Kinematics Input: q, qdot, qddot] --> C
    stepB[Backward Force Pass: f_{i+1}, n_{i+1}] --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Free-Body Decomposition of a Single Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINKi["Rigid Link i"]
        m1[Mass m_i at CG] --> m2[Inertia Tensor I_i]
    end

    A1[Force from joint i-1: f_{i-1}] --> LINKi
    LINKi --> A2[Force to joint i+1: f_i]
    B1[Moment from joint i-1: n_{i-1}] --> LINKi
    LINKi --> B2[Moment to joint i+1: n_i]
    G[Gravity vector m_i times g] --> LINKi

    LINKi -. Newton Translational Equation .-> EQ1[F = m_i times a_c]
    LINKi -. Euler Rotational Equation .-> EQ2[N = I_i alpha + omega cross I omega]
    EQ1 --> OUT1[Tau_i Extraction]
    EQ2 --> OUT1
```

## 4.3 Vector Relationship Diagram: Force and Moment Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Link i-1 origin] -->|p_{i-1,i} vector| B[Link i origin]
    B -->|r_{i,c} vector| C[Center of mass of link i]
    C -->|r_{i+1,c} vector| D[Link i+1 origin]
    B -->|Force f_i| E[Force balance node]
    C -->|Force m_i a_c| E
    E --> F[Newton equation f_i = m_i a_c]
    B -->|Moment n_i| G[Moment balance node]
    C -->|Gyroscopic I alpha + omega cross I omega| G
    G --> H[Euler equation n_i = I_i alpha + omega cross I omega + r cross f]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are **abstract functional representations**, not physical geometry. KTU examiners accept logical block diagrams as long as every node and arrow is **labeled** with the corresponding physical quantity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 1 : NEWTON'S EQUATION — KTU 2024 EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Newton's equation for the translational and rotational motion of a rigid body. Clearly define each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a rigid body of mass $m$ with center of mass acceleration $\mathbf{a}_c$ and inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$:

**Translational (Newton's) Equation:**
$$\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}_c$$
where $\mathbf{F}$ = sum of all external forces acting on the body (N), $m$ = mass (kg), $\mathbf{a}_c$ = linear acceleration of the CG (m/s²).

**Rotational (Euler's) Equation:**
$$\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$$
where $\mathbf{N}$ = sum of external moments about CG (N·m), $\mathbf{I}$ = inertia tensor about CG (kg·m²), $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ = angular acceleration (rad/s²). The last term is the **gyroscopic moment**, vanishing only for principal-axis rotation.

> *Award 1 mark for each equation and 1 mark for correct term definitions.*

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of the gyroscopic term $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ in Euler's equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The gyroscopic term arises from differentiating angular momentum in a **moving body-fixed frame**. Its physical significance:

1. **(1 mark)** It represents the **coupling** between the angular velocity vector and the inertia distribution of the body. Even when $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = 0$ (constant angular velocity), a non-zero moment may be required to maintain that motion if the rotation is **not about a principal axis**.

2. **(1 mark)** It is responsible for phenomena such as the **precession of a spinning top** and the **stability of bicycle wheels** while in motion.

3. **(1 mark)** In robotics, neglecting this term leads to inaccurate torque predictions especially in **high-speed manipulators** with off-axis inertia coupling — a common modelling error in industrial robot controllers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Newton–Euler equations of motion for a single rigid link in a serial manipulator, clearly stating all assumptions.** (7 Marks)

**(b) A uniform slender rod of length $L = 0.8\,\text{m}$ and mass $m = 3\,\text{kg}$ rotates about a fixed pivot at one end in a vertical plane. At a given instant, the angular velocity is $\omega = 4\,\text{rad/s}$ and angular acceleration is $\alpha = 6\,\text{rad/s}^2$. Calculate the magnitude of the reaction force at the pivot and the torque applied at the pivot.** (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions** *(1 mark)*:
- Rigid body, constant mass
- Inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$ constant in body frame
- Gravity acts uniformly

**Translational derivation** *(3 marks)*:
As shown in Section 3.3 above, applying Newton's law at the center of mass of link $i$:
$$\mathbf{f}_i - m_i\mathbf{g} = m_i \dot{\mathbf{v}}_c = m_i\left[ \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c}) \right]$$

**Rotational derivation** *(3 marks)*:
Applying Euler's equation about the CG and transferring moments to the link origin:
$$\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{I}_i\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i) + \mathbf{r}_{i,c} \times \mathbf{f}_i$$

> *(The framework shown in Section 3.3, Steps 1–5, must be reproduced with all five sub-steps for full marks.)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute inertia** *(1 mark)*:
$$I_O = \frac{1}{3}mL^2 = \frac{1}{3}(3)(0.8)^2 = 0.64\,\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the pivot force components** *(2 marks)*:
- Centripetal component (radially inward): $F_c = m\omega^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 3 \times 16 \times 0.4 = 19.2\,\text{N}$
- Tangential component: $F_t = m\alpha \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 3 \times 6 \times 0.4 = 7.2\,\text{N}$
- Weight component (downward): $W = mg = 3 \times 9.81 = 29.43\,\text{N}$

**Step 3 — Compute the total reaction magnitude** *(2 marks)*:
$$F_R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2 + W^2} = \sqrt{(19.2)^2 + (7.2)^2 + (29.43)^2}$$
$$F_R = \sqrt{368.64 + 51.84 + 866.12} = \sqrt{1286.6} \approx 35.87\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pivot torque** *(2 marks)*:
Taking moments about the pivot:
$$T_O = I_O \alpha = 0.64 \times 6 = 3.84\,\text{N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Reaction force at pivot: $\mathbf{F}_R \approx 35.87\,\text{N}$
- Torque at pivot: $T_O = 3.84\,\text{N·m}$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the recursive Newton–Euler algorithm for computing joint torques of an $n$-joint serial manipulator. Differentiate between the forward (outward) and backward (inward) iterations.** (7 Marks)

**(b) For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with links of length $l_1 = l_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and equal masses $m_1 = m_2 = 2\,\text{kg}$ at the end of each link, with the manipulator currently at configuration $q_1 = 60°, q_2 = 90°, \dot{q}_1 = \dot{q}_2 = 1\,\text{rad/s}, \ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 0.5\,\text{rad/s}^2$, write the Newton–Euler equations of motion and identify the inertia, Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravity terms.** (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Recursive Newton–Euler Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Block diagram explanation** *(3 marks)*: The algorithm has two passes (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Outward Iteration (i = 1 → n):** Starting from the base, propagate angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$, angular acceleration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i$, and linear acceleration $\dot{\mathbf{v}}_i$ outward through each link.
2. **Inward Iteration (i = n → 1):** Starting from the end-effector (where the force is known or zero), propagate forces $\mathbf{f}_i$ and moments $\mathbf{n}_i$ inward, and project onto the joint axis to obtain $\tau_i$.

**Equations** *(3 marks)*:
- Outward: as in Section 3.3, Step 6.
- Inward: as in Section 3.3, Step 7.
- Torque extraction: $\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (revolute).

**Computational advantage** *(1 mark)*: Complexity $O(n)$ vs. $O(n^4)$ for Lagrangian, suitable for real-time control.

---

### Part (b) — 2-DOF Newton–Euler Setup (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Frame assignment and DH parameters** *(1 mark)*:
Standard planar DH table:
| Link | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_1$ | 0 | 0 | $q_1$ |
| 2 | $l_2$ | 0 | 0 | $q_2$ |

**Step 2 — Outward pass for link 1** *(2 marks)*:
$$\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 = \dot{q}_1 \hat{z}_0, \quad \boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 = \ddot{q}_1 \hat{z}_0$$
$$\dot{\mathbf{v}}_1 = \ddot{q}_1 \hat{x}_1 \times l_1 + \mathbf{g} \text{ components}$$

**Step 3 — Outward pass for link 2** *(1 mark)*:
$$\boldsymbol{\omega}_2 = (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)\hat{z}_0, \quad \boldsymbol{\alpha}_2 = (\ddot{q}_1 + \ddot{q}_2)\hat{z}_0$$

**Step 4 — Inward pass and identification of terms** *(3 marks)*:
The full Newton–Euler equation yields:
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + h_{121}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + g_1 \\
\tau_2 &= M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_1 + g_2
\end{aligned}$$

Term identification:
- $M_{11} = m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2)$ → **Inertia** *(0.75 mark)*
- $M_{12} = m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2)$ → **Coupling inertia** *(0.75 mark)*
- $h_{121}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$ → **Coriolis/centrifugal** *(0.75 mark)*
- $g_1, g_2$ → **Gravity** *(0.75 mark)*

> *Plugging numerical values yields numeric coefficients — full credit requires the symbolic identification above, with numerical substitution as a bonus step.*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Newton's Equation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the gyroscopic term** — Writing only $\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ without the $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ term. Costs **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Wrong reference point for moments** — Moments must be taken about the **center of mass**, not the joint origin. If you write the moment balance at the joint without transferring, expect **3 marks deducted**.
> 3. **Sign errors in the recursion** — The cross products $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}$ have a fixed sign convention. Mismatched signs in the inward/outward recursion lose **4 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Missing gravity vector** — Forgetting to include $-m_i\mathbf{g}$ in the translational balance for every link. **2 marks deducted**.
> 5. **Wrong axis projection for joint torque** — Extracting $\tau_i$ from the **wrong component** of $\mathbf{n}_i$ or $\mathbf{f}_i$ (must project onto the joint axis $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$). **2 marks deducted**.
> 6. **Units omission** — Always write SI units in the final numerical answer. **½ to 1 mark** is at stake.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's equation** for a rigid body has **two parts**: translational ($\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}_c$) and rotational ($\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$).
- The **gyroscopic term** $\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ is **non-zero** whenever the body rotates about a **non-principal axis**. Drop it only if explicitly justified.
- For a **robot manipulator**, Newton's equations must be written in a **body-fixed moving frame**, leading to additional **Coriolis** and **centrifugal** contributions.
- The **recursive Newton–Euler** algorithm has **outward** (kinematics) and **inward** (force/torque) passes. Computational complexity is $O(n)$ — far superior to Lagrangian's $O(n^4)$.
- **Joint torque** for a revolute joint is the projection of the moment $\mathbf{n}_i$ onto the joint axis; for a prismatic joint, it is the projection of the force $\mathbf{f}_i$.
- Always include **gravity** ($m_i\mathbf{g}$) explicitly in the translational balance.
- Always **define every symbol** the first time it appears in your answer — examiners award **definite marks** for notation discipline.
- The **inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}$ is a **symmetric 3×3 matrix** in the body frame; only its **principal-axis components** are non-zero when diagonalized.
- KTU questions **favor numerical substitution** in Part (b); always carry **three significant figures** minimum and box the final answer.
- Finally: **practice deriving from first principles** — KTU examiners frequently award partial credit (up to 4 marks) even if your final number is wrong, **provided** the derivation steps are visible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Euler's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, **Euler's Equations of Motion** (also called **Euler's Dynamic Equations** or **Euler's Rotational Equations**) constitute a set of three coupled, non-linear, first-order ordinary differential equations that describe the **rotational dynamics of a rigid body** about its **center of mass** when expressed in a **body-fixed principal axis coordinate frame**.

Mathematically, for a rigid body with **principal moments of inertia** $I_1$, $I_2$, and $I_3$ along the body-fixed principal axes (often labeled $x_b$, $y_b$, $z_b$ or 1, 2, 3), and with **angular velocity components** $\omega_1$, $\omega_2$, $\omega_3$ resolved along these same principal axes, the equations are stated as:

$$
I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 = \tau_1
$$

$$
I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 - (I_3 - I_1)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 = \tau_2
$$

$$
I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 = \tau_3
$$

where $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ are the components of the **resultant external moment** (torque) applied to the body about its center of mass, expressed in the body-fixed frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Euler's equations are the rotational analogue of Newton's second law ($\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$) and form the foundation for the **Newton-Euler formulation of robot manipulator dynamics**. They are essential for deriving the **Inverse Dynamics** and **Forward Dynamics** equations of multi-link robotic manipulators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** These equations were first derived by **Leonhard Euler** in **1758** as a generalization of Newton's laws to rotating rigid bodies. The form taught in modern robotics is specifically the **principal-axis form**, where the inertia tensor reduces to a diagonal matrix.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **spinning top** (a *chakram* or a bicycle wheel) in your hand. When you try to tilt the spinning top sideways, instead of falling over, it **precesses** — the axis of rotation itself begins to rotate. This seemingly magical behavior is governed entirely by Euler's equations.

Think of it this way:
- **Newton's law for translation** says: *Force = mass × linear acceleration.*
- **Euler's law for rotation** says: *Torque = (how hard it is to rotate) × angular acceleration* + **extra mysterious cross-coupling terms** that arise because the body's own rotation creates gyroscopic effects.

The **cross-product terms** $-(I_2 - I_3)\omega_2\omega_3$, etc., represent these **gyroscopic coupling effects**. Even if you apply **zero torque**, the body can still accelerate angularly because the existing spin about one axis "leaks" into rotation about another axis.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition check:** A **gyroscope** stays upright not because of magic, but because Euler's equations predict that the gravitational torque (which would topple a non-spinning object) is converted into precession rather than tipping — a direct consequence of the $\omega_2\omega_3$ cross-coupling terms.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Units (SI):** Moments of inertia are measured in **kg·m²**, angular velocity in **rad/s**, angular acceleration in **rad/s²**, and torque in **N·m**.
- **Key physical principle:** Conservation of **angular momentum** $\vec{H} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\tau}$ when $\vec{\tau} = 0$ leads to **torque-free motion** solutions that are the basis for spacecraft attitude dynamics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Euler's Equations

For the principal-axis form of Euler's equations to be **valid**, the following assumptions must be satisfied:

1. **Rigid Body Constraint:** The body is perfectly rigid (no deformation).
2. **Body-Fixed Principal Axes:** The reference frame is attached to the body and aligned with the **principal axes of inertia**. This diagonalizes the inertia tensor.
3. **Origin at Center of Mass:** The origin of the body-fixed frame coincides with the body's center of mass (otherwise the translation of the center of mass and the rotation about the center of mass must be coupled via the **parallel axis theorem**).
4. **Newtonian Mechanics:** The body is in a **non-relativistic** regime (angular velocities well below relativistic scales).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The derivation flows through this logical chain (we will expand each step in Section 3):

- **Step A:** Start from the **angular momentum** $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}$ where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor.
- **Step B:** Apply **Newton's second law for rotation** in the inertial frame: $\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}}$.
- **Step C:** Use the **transport theorem** (coriolis theorem) to convert the inertial derivative to a body-fixed derivative: $\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}$.
- **Step D:** Substitute $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ and apply the **diagonalization condition** (principal axes → only diagonal elements of $\mathbf{I}$ are non-zero).
- **Step E:** Expand the cross product to obtain the three scalar equations (the famous Euler's equations).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Physical Meaning** | **Formula / Expression** | **SI Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vec{\omega}$ | Angular velocity vector in body frame | $\vec{\omega} = \omega_1 \hat{e}_1 + \omega_2 \hat{e}_2 + \omega_3 \hat{e}_3$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $\vec{L}$ | Angular momentum about CoM | $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $\mathbf{I}$ | Inertia tensor (diagonal in principal axes) | $\mathbf{I} = \text{diag}(I_1, I_2, I_3)$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| $\vec{\tau}$ | Net external torque about CoM | $\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| $\dot{\vec{\omega}}$ | Angular acceleration in body frame | $\dot{\vec{\omega}} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}}$ | $\text{rad/s}^2$ |
| Euler 1 | Rotational equation about axis 1 | $I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2 \omega_3 = \tau_1$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| Euler 2 | Rotational equation about axis 2 | $I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 - (I_3 - I_1)\omega_3 \omega_1 = \tau_2$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| Euler 3 | Rotational equation about axis 3 | $I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\omega_1 \omega_2 = \tau_3$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| Inertia matrix | $3 \times 3$ symmetric tensor | $\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & -I_{xy} & -I_{xz} \\ -I_{yx} & I_{yy} & -I_{yz} \\ -I_{zx} & I_{zy} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| Inertia components | Element formula | $I_{xy} = \int_V \rho\, x\, y\, dV$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| Inertia components | Diagonal formula | $I_{xx} = \int_V \rho\, (y^2 + z^2)\, dV$ | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| Torque-free motion | $\tau_i = 0$ case | $I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 = (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2 \omega_3$, etc. | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU exams, students frequently confuse the **sign convention** of the cross-product term. The standard form has a **negative sign** in front of the product-of-inertia-difference term. Memorize the pattern: $I_i \dot{\omega}_i - (I_j - I_k)\omega_j \omega_k = \tau_i$ with cyclic indices $(i,j,k) \to (1,2,3) \to (2,3,1) \to (3,1,2)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Euler's equations are not just textbook mathematics — they are deployed in:

- **Spacecraft attitude control:** Satellites use reaction wheels; the dynamics are governed by Euler's equations.
- **Robotic manipulator design:** The **Newton-Euler recursive algorithm** (covered later in the KTU syllabus) is the industrial standard for real-time robot control (e.g., KUKA, ABB, FANUC arms).
- **Drone/UAV control:** Quadrotor rotational dynamics are a simplified version of Euler's equations (assuming symmetric airframe → $I_1 = I_2 \neq I_3$).
- **Gyroscopic instruments:** Heading indicators, inertial measurement units (IMUs).
- **Sports engineering:** Analysis of figure skater spin dynamics, bicycle stability, and frisbee flight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Equations of Motion (Exhaustive)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Newton's Second Law for Rotation

In an **inertial (world) reference frame**, the rate of change of angular momentum equals the net external torque:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}}
$$

where $\vec{L}$ is the angular momentum of the rigid body about its center of mass.

### 3.1.2 Expressing Angular Momentum via the Inertia Tensor

The angular momentum of a rigid body is related to its angular velocity through the **inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}$:

$$
\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}
$$

In the most general form, the inertia tensor is a $3 \times 3$ **symmetric** matrix:

$$
\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix}
I_{xx} & I_{xy} & I_{xz} \\
I_{yx} & I_{yy} & I_{yz} \\
I_{zx} & I_{zy} & I_{zz}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where, for a body of density $\rho$ occupying volume $V$:

$$
I_{xx} = \int_V \rho\, (y^2 + z^2)\, dV, \quad I_{xy} = -\int_V \rho\, x\, y\, dV
$$

(analogous expressions for the other five components; the off-diagonal terms are **products of inertia**).

### 3.1.3 Transforming the Inertial Derivative to the Body Frame

We need to evaluate $\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}}$, but it is far more convenient to compute it in a **body-fixed rotating frame**. Using the **Coriolis transport theorem** (also called the **derivative transport formula** or **kinematic transport theorem**):

$$
\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}} = \frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{A}
$$

Applied to $\vec{L}$:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}
$$

### 3.1.4 Differentiating the Angular Momentum in the Body Frame

Substituting $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ and noting that $\mathbf{I}$ is **constant in the body frame** (since the body is rigid and the frame is attached to it):

$$
\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}} = \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}}
$$

because the elements $I_{ij}$ do not change with time when measured in the body-fixed frame. Therefore:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})
$$

### 3.1.5 Choosing Principal Axes to Diagonalize the Inertia Tensor

By the **spectral theorem** for real symmetric matrices, there always exists a body-fixed coordinate system — the **principal axis frame** — in which the inertia tensor becomes **diagonal**:

$$
\mathbf{I}_{\text{principal}} = \begin{bmatrix}
I_1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & I_2 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & I_3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The eigenvalues $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are the **principal moments of inertia**, and the corresponding eigenvectors are the **principal axes**. With this choice, $\vec{\omega} = (\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3)^T$ and $\vec{L} = (I_1\omega_1, I_2\omega_2, I_3\omega_3)^T$.

### 3.1.6 Computing the Cross Product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}$

The cross product of two vectors in component form is:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{L} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{e}_1 & \hat{e}_2 & \hat{e}_3 \\ \omega_1 & \omega_2 & \omega_3 \\ I_1\omega_1 & I_2\omega_2 & I_3\omega_3 \end{vmatrix}
$$

Evaluating the determinant:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{L} = \hat{e}_1 (\omega_2 I_3 \omega_3 - \omega_3 I_2 \omega_2) + \hat{e}_2 (\omega_3 I_1 \omega_1 - \omega_1 I_3 \omega_3) + \hat{e}_3 (\omega_1 I_2 \omega_2 - \omega_2 I_1 \omega_1)
$$

Simplifying each component:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{L} = \begin{bmatrix} (I_3 - I_2)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 \\ (I_1 - I_3)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 \\ (I_2 - I_1)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.7 Writing the Component Equations

Substituting everything into $\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega}\times\vec{L}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \\ \tau_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 \\ I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 \\ I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} (I_3 - I_2)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 \\ (I_1 - I_3)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 \\ (I_2 - I_1)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Rearranging to the canonical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 + (I_3 - I_2)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 &= \tau_1 \\
I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 + (I_1 - I_3)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 &= \tau_2 \\
I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 + (I_2 - I_1)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 &= \tau_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying the signs through to obtain the **most commonly written form**:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 &= \tau_1 \\
I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 - (I_3 - I_1)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 &= \tau_2 \\
I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 &= \tau_3
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is the celebrated **Euler's Equations of Motion** for a rigid body rotating about a fixed point (its center of mass), expressed in body-fixed principal axes.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Symmetric Top (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A uniform solid cylinder of mass $m = 2$ kg, radius $R = 0.1$ m, length $L = 0.4$ m spins about its symmetry axis with $\omega_3 = 100$ rad/s. Determine the principal moments of inertia and write the Euler equations.

**Step 1: Principal moments of a uniform solid cylinder**

For a uniform solid cylinder with the $z$-axis as the symmetry axis:

$$
I_1 = I_2 = \frac{1}{12} m L^2 + \frac{1}{4} m R^2
$$

$$
I_3 = \frac{1}{2} m R^2
$$

**Step 2: Numerical evaluation**

$$
I_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times (0.1)^2 = 0.01 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2
$$

$$
I_1 = I_2 = \frac{1}{12} \times 2 \times (0.4)^2 + \frac{1}{4} \times 2 \times (0.1)^2 = 0.02667 + 0.005 = 0.03167 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Write Euler's equations (torque-free case $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau_3 = 0$)**

Since $I_1 = I_2$, the first equation gives $\dot{\omega}_1 = 0$ and the second gives $\dot{\omega}_2 = 0$, while $\dot{\omega}_3 = 0$ trivially. Thus a symmetric top spinning about its axis of symmetry remains stable — a result of enormous practical importance in **gyroscope design**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Inertia Tensor Component Formula

The inertia tensor components are derived from the fundamental definition of angular momentum of a collection of point masses. For a continuous body:

$$
\vec{L} = \int_V \vec{r} \times (\rho\, \vec{v})\, dV
$$

For a rigid body, $\vec{v} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$. Substituting and using the vector triple product identity:

$$
\vec{r} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) = \vec{\omega}(\vec{r}\cdot\vec{r}) - \vec{r}(\vec{r}\cdot\vec{\omega})
$$

Carrying out the cross product component-wise yields the **inertia tensor element formulas**:

$$
I_{xx} = \int_V \rho\, (y^2 + z^2)\, dV
$$

$$
I_{yy} = \int_V \rho\, (x^2 + z^2)\, dV
$$

$$
I_{zz} = \int_V \rho\, (x^2 + y^2)\, dV
$$

$$
I_{xy} = I_{yx} = -\int_V \rho\, x\, y\, dV
$$

$$
I_{xz} = I_{zx} = -\int_V \rho\, x\, z\, dV
$$

$$
I_{yz} = I_{zy} = -\int_V \rho\, y\, z\, dV
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention pitfall:** Some textbooks define the products of inertia with a **positive** sign and incorporate it in the inertia matrix as $-I_{xy}$. Both notations are equivalent — the key is to be **consistent** within a derivation. The KTU 2024 Scheme follows the sign convention shown above (negative products in the matrix), which matches **Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar's** standard robotics textbook treatment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture flow** of how Euler's equations integrate into the broader robot dynamics pipeline. This is the analytical mapping most relevant to the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Body Link] --> B[Compute Inertia Tensor I about CoM]
    B --> C[Diagonalize via Principal Axis Transform]
    C --> D[Identify Principal Moments I1 I2 I3]
    D --> E[Express Angular Velocity in Body Frame omega1 omega2 omega3]
    E --> F[Apply Transport Theorem dL/dt inertial equals dL/dt body plus omega cross L]
    F --> G[Compute Cross Product omega cross I omega]
    G --> H[Assemble Three Scalar Euler Equations]
    H --> I{Torque Applied?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Solve for Angular Accelerations omega dot 1 omega dot 2 omega dot 3]
    I -- No --> K[Torque Free Motion: Conservation of Angular Momentum]
    J --> L[Integrate to Get omega of t]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Integrate to Get Orientation of t via Euler Angles or Quaternions]
    M --> N[Forward Dynamics Output: Joint Accelerations or End Effector Pose]
```

## 4.2 Process Map: Derivation of Euler's Equations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation] --> B[Transport Theorem Application]
    B --> C[Substitute L equals I omega]
    C --> D[Choose Principal Axes]
    D --> E[Evaluate Cross Product]
    E --> F[Expand into 3 Scalar Equations]
    F --> G[Canonical Euler Equations of Motion]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Physical Interpretation of Each Term

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Euler Equation I1 omega dot 1 minus I2 minus I3 omega2 omega3 equals tau1] --> B[Term 1: I1 omega dot 1]
    A --> C[Term 2: minus I2 minus I3 times omega2 omega3]
    A --> D[Term 3: tau1]
    B --> E[Inertial Resistance to Angular Acceleration about Axis 1]
    C --> F[Gyroscopic Coupling from Rotation about Axes 2 and 3]
    D --> G[External Torque Applied about Axis 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Euler's equations of motion for a rigid body rotating about a fixed point. What is the significance of the principal axes in this formulation?**

**Model Answer:**

Euler's equations of motion for a rigid body rotating about a fixed point (its center of mass), expressed in body-fixed principal axes, are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\,\omega_2\,\omega_3 &= \tau_1 \\
I_2 \dot{\omega}_2 - (I_3 - I_1)\,\omega_3\,\omega_1 &= \tau_2 \\
I_3 \dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\,\omega_1\,\omega_2 &= \tau_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance of principal axes:** The principal axes are the eigenvectors of the inertia tensor. When the body-fixed reference frame is aligned with these axes, the inertia tensor becomes **diagonal** ($\mathbf{I} = \text{diag}(I_1, I_2, I_3)$). This eliminates the products of inertia from the equations, **decoupling the inertia matrix** and yielding the compact form above. Without this choice, the equations contain additional cross-coupling terms involving $I_{xy}$, $I_{yz}$, $I_{xz}$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Euler's angles and Euler's equations. Why are Euler's equations non-linear?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Euler's Angles** | **Euler's Equations** |
|---|---|---|
| **What** | Three angles (precession $\phi$, nutation $\theta$, spin $\psi$) used to **describe the orientation** of a rigid body. | Three scalar **differential equations** describing the **rotational dynamics** of a rigid body. |
| **Purpose** | Kinematic parameterization. | Dynamic equations of motion. |
| **Mathematical Nature** | Geometric transformation. | Ordinary differential equations. |
| **Example Formula** | $\omega_1 = \dot{\phi}\sin\theta\sin\psi + \dot{\theta}\cos\psi$ | $I_1\dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2-I_3)\omega_2\omega_3 = \tau_1$ |

**Why Euler's equations are non-linear:** Euler's equations contain the **product terms** $\omega_2 \omega_3$, $\omega_3 \omega_1$, and $\omega_1 \omega_2$. Since each of these is a product of two state variables ($\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$), the equations are **quadratic** (and hence non-linear) in the state variables. This non-linearity arises physically from the **gyroscopic coupling** between rotations about different body-fixed axes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** carry **14 marks** and provide an **internal choice**. Below are two complete alternative questions (Question A and Question B) that could appear on a Module 1 question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a step-by-step derivation, obtain **Euler's equations of motion** for a rigid body rotating about a fixed point, expressed in a body-fixed principal axis frame. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin uniform disk of mass $m = 5$ kg and radius $R = 0.2$ m spins about its symmetry axis with $\omega_3 = 50$ rad/s, while precessing about a vertical axis at $\Omega = 5$ rad/s. The disk is inclined at a constant nutation angle $\theta = 30°$. Compute the gyroscopic torque required to maintain this motion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Full Derivation:** (Award 7 marks)

Follow the **complete derivation** given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The examiner expects the student to:

1. State Newton's second law for rotation: $\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}}$ — **1 mark**.
2. Apply the transport theorem: $\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{inertial}} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\Big|_{\text{body}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}$ — **1 mark**.
3. Substitute $\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ and note that $\mathbf{I}$ is constant in the body frame — **1 mark**.
4. Choose principal axes to diagonalize $\mathbf{I}$ — **1 mark**.
5. Expand the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ — **1 mark**.
6. Write out the three scalar equations — **1 mark**.
7. State the assumptions (rigid body, principal axes, CoM as origin) — **1 mark**.

**Part (b) — Gyroscopic Torque Calculation:** (Award 7 marks)

**Step 1: Principal moments of a thin uniform disk** (with symmetry axis as axis 3, in-plane axes as 1 and 2):

$$
I_1 = I_2 = \frac{1}{4} m R^2, \quad I_3 = \frac{1}{2} m R^2
$$

**Step 2: Numerical evaluation:**

$$
I_1 = I_2 = \frac{1}{4} \times 5 \times (0.2)^2 = 0.05 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2
$$

$$
I_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times (0.2)^2 = 0.1 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Components of angular velocity in the body frame** (using the standard 3-1-3 Euler angle convention with precession $\Omega$ about space-fixed $Z$, nutation $\theta$, and spin $\omega_3$):

$$
\omega_1 = \Omega \sin\theta \sin\psi, \quad \omega_2 = \Omega \sin\theta \cos\psi, \quad \omega_3 = \Omega \cos\theta + \dot{\psi}
$$

For a **steady precession** with $\dot{\psi} = \omega_3 - \Omega\cos\theta$ and $\dot{\theta} = 0$, averaging over one spin cycle (or evaluating at $\psi = 0$ for maximum effect):

$$
\omega_1 \approx 0, \quad \omega_2 \approx \Omega \sin\theta, \quad \omega_3 \approx \omega_3
$$

**Step 4: Apply Euler's first equation (for the cross-coupling torque):**

$$
\tau_1 = I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2 \omega_3
$$

For steady motion, $\dot{\omega}_1 = 0$:

$$
\tau_1 = -(I_2 - I_3)\omega_2 \omega_3 = -(0.05 - 0.1)(5 \sin 30°)(50)
$$

$$
\tau_1 = -(-0.05)(5 \times 0.5)(50) = -(-0.05)(125) = 6.25 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Magnitude of gyroscopic torque:**

$$
\boxed{\vert\tau_g\vert = 6.25 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the **inertia tensor** of a rigid body. Derive expressions for its components in terms of the body's mass distribution. Explain why the inertia tensor is symmetric and positive definite. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rigid body has the following inertia tensor (in body-fixed frame, units of kg·m²):

$$
\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The body is rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega} = (2, 3, 4)^T$ rad/s. Compute the **angular momentum vector** $\vec{L}$, the **kinetic energy** $T$ of rotation, and the **magnitude of the gyroscopic torque** if the angular velocity vector rotates in the body frame at $\dot{\vec{\omega}} = (0, 0, 0)^T$ rad/s² (steady spin). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Inertia Tensor Definition and Derivation:** (Award 7 marks)

**Definition:** The inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$ is a $3\times 3$ symmetric matrix that maps the angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}$ to the angular momentum vector $\vec{L}$:

$$
\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}
$$

**Derivation:** Consider a rigid body of density $\rho(\vec{r})$ occupying volume $V$. The angular momentum about the center of mass is:

$$
\vec{L} = \int_V \vec{r} \times (\rho\, \vec{v})\, dV = \int_V \vec{r} \times \rho\, (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})\, dV
$$

Using the vector triple product identity and matching coefficients of $\omega_i$, we obtain:

$$
L_i = \sum_{j=1}^{3} I_{ij}\,\omega_j
$$

where the components are:

$$
I_{ij} = \int_V \rho\, (\delta_{ij}\, r^2 - x_i x_j)\, dV
$$

In expanded form:

$$
I_{xx} = \int_V \rho\, (y^2 + z^2)\, dV, \quad I_{xy} = -\int_V \rho\, x\, y\, dV, \text{ etc.}
$$

**Symmetry:** $I_{ij} = I_{ji}$ because the integrand $\rho\, x_i x_j$ is symmetric in $i$ and $j$. — **1 mark**

**Positive definiteness:** For any non-zero vector $\vec{\omega}$:

$$
\vec{\omega}^T \mathbf{I} \vec{\omega} = \int_V \rho\, \vert\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}\vert^2\, dV > 0
$$

since the cross product is non-zero for non-zero $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{r}$, and $\rho > 0$. — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** (Award 7 marks)

**Step 1: Angular momentum $\vec{L}$:**

$$
\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 16 \\ 18 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Rotational kinetic energy:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\,\vec{\omega}^T \mathbf{I} \vec{\omega} = \frac{1}{2}\,\vec{\omega}\cdot\vec{L}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} (2 \times 16 + 3 \times 18 + 4 \times 16) = \frac{1}{2} (32 + 54 + 64) = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Gyroscopic torque (with $\dot{\vec{\omega}} = 0$):**

$$
\vec{\tau} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}
$$

Computing the cross product:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{e}_1 & \hat{e}_2 & \hat{e}_3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 16 & 18 & 16 \end{vmatrix}
$$

$$
\tau_1 = (3)(16) - (4)(18) = 48 - 72 = -24
$$

$$
\tau_2 = (4)(16) - (2)(16) = 64 - 32 = 32
$$

$$
\tau_3 = (2)(18) - (3)(16) = 36 - 48 = -12
$$

$$
\vec{\tau} = (-24, 32, -12)^T \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 4: Magnitude of gyroscopic torque:**

$$
\vert\vec{\tau}\vert = \sqrt{(-24)^2 + (32)^2 + (-12)^2} = \sqrt{576 + 1024 + 144} = \sqrt{1744} \approx 41.76 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Summary of results:**

$$
\boxed{\vec{L} = (16, 18, 16)^T \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad T = 75 \text{ J}, \quad \vert\vec{\tau}\vert \approx 41.76 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in cross-product expansion:** A large fraction of students lose 2-3 marks by incorrectly expanding $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$. Always verify with the determinant formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the transport theorem:** Many students directly write $\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\vec{\omega}}$ and miss the cross-product term entirely. This loses up to **3 marks** in derivation questions.
> 3. **Confusing $I_{xy}$ signs:** The product of inertia has a **negative** sign in the inertia matrix per the KTU convention. Writing it positive without justification costs a mark.
> 4. **Units omission:** Always state units (kg·m², rad/s, N·m). Examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units.
> 5. **Mixing reference frames:** Never resolve $\vec{\omega}$ in the inertial frame when applying Euler's equations — they **must** be in the body-fixed frame.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Euler's Equations**

- **Core formula (memorize verbatim):**
  $I_1 \dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2\omega_3 = \tau_1$, and cyclically.
- **Euler's equations are the rotational analogue of $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$** — commit this analogy to memory.
- They are valid **only** in the body-fixed **principal axis** frame.
- The **transport theorem** is the key step that introduces the gyroscopic term $\vec{\omega}\times\vec{L}$.
- The cross-product terms cause **non-linearity** — important for stability analysis.
- The **inertia tensor is symmetric** ($I_{ij} = I_{ji}$) and **positive definite**.
- Principal moments satisfy the **triangle inequality**: $I_1 + I_2 \geq I_3$, etc.
- For a **symmetric body** ($I_1 = I_2 \neq I_3$), rotation about the symmetry axis is **stable**.
- For a **torque-free symmetric body**, the angular velocity vector **precesses** around the symmetry axis (this is the basis of gyroscope physics).
- **Kinetic energy** in body frame: $T = \frac{1}{2}\vec{\omega}^T \mathbf{I} \vec{\omega} = \frac{1}{2}(I_1\omega_1^2 + I_2\omega_2^2 + I_3\omega_3^2)$ (in principal axes).
- **Angular momentum magnitude** is conserved in torque-free motion: $\vert\vec{L}\vert = $ constant.
- **Kinetic energy is conserved** in torque-free motion: $T = $ constant.
- Connection to robot dynamics: Euler's equations generalize to the **Newton-Euler formulation** for multi-link manipulators (covered in later modules).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrange's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lagrange's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lagrange's Equation** is a second-order differential equation of motion derived from the principle of *stationary action* (Hamilton's principle). In the context of **robot dynamics**, it provides a systematic, scalar-energy-based method to derive the governing equations of motion of a multi-body robotic manipulator directly from its **kinetic energy (K)** and **potential energy (P)**, eliminating the need to compute constraint forces explicitly.

The standard form of **Lagrange's Equation of the second kind** for the *i*-th generalized coordinate $q_i$ is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

where:
- $L = K - P$ is the **Lagrangian function** (scalar),
- $q_i$ = the *i*-th **generalized coordinate**,
- $\dot{q}_i$ = the *i*-th **generalized velocity**,
- $Q_i$ = the *i*-th **generalized (non-conservative) force** (e.g., joint actuator torque $\tau_i$),
- $n$ = the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the robot.

For a robotic manipulator, this expands to the celebrated **manipulator dynamic equation**:

$$
\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\,\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})\,\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})$ | Symmetric positive-definite **Inertia (mass) matrix** | $n \times n$ kg |
| $\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})$ | **Coriolis and centripetal** matrix | $n \times n$ kg/s |
| $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q})$ | **Gravity** vector | $n \times 1$ N·m |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | **Joint torque** vector | $n \times 1$ N·m |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Bank Account"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for B.Tech Students:** Imagine your robot as a **savings account holding two kinds of wealth — *motion wealth* (Kinetic Energy K) and *height wealth* (Potential Energy P)**. The Lagrangian $L = K - P$ is simply the *net wealth*. Lagrange's equation is the **bank's rule book**: it tells you exactly how the *rate of change of wealth growth* must balance the *external deposits and withdrawals* (joint torques). You never need to look at the *internal ledger* (constraint/reaction forces) — the energy account is sufficient.

### Why Use Lagrange's Equation in Robotics?

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Scalar formulation:** Only energy terms (K and P) are needed — no free-body diagrams or vector force balances.
> 2. **Constraint elimination:** Internal joint reaction forces are *automatically cancelled*.
> 3. **Compact symbolic form:** Yields the standard structured $\boldsymbol{M}\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ form essential for **computed-torque control** and **adaptive control**.
> 4. **Systematic for high DOF:** Scales well to 6-DOF and 7-DOF serial manipulators.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

- **Generalized coordinates ($q_i$):** Minimum set of independent variables that completely specify the configuration of the robot. For revolute joints: $q_i = \theta_i$ (radians).
- **Generalized forces ($Q_i$):** The work-conjugate of $q_i$. For $\theta_i$ it is torque (N·m); for $d_i$ (prismatic) it is force (N).
- **Holonomic constraints:** Constraints expressible as $f(\boldsymbol{q}) = 0$. A serial chain with $n$ revolute joints and a fixed base has zero such constraints.
- **Conservative vs Non-conservative:** Gravity is *conservative* (absorbed in $P$); joint friction and actuator torques are *non-conservative* (appear as $Q_i$).
- **Configuration space $\mathcal{C}$:** The $n$-dimensional manifold spanned by $\boldsymbol{q} = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Lagrangian Recipe

The application of Lagrange's equation to a robotic manipulator follows a strict, repeatable **6-step recipe** that examiners love to test:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6-Step Lagrangian Recipe (KTU Board Favourite)**
> 1. Choose **generalized coordinates** $\boldsymbol{q}$ (joint variables).
> 2. Build the **forward kinematics Jacobian** $\boldsymbol{J}(\boldsymbol{q})$ to express each link's velocity $\boldsymbol{v}_i$ in terms of $\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$.
> 3. Compute the **kinetic energy** $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T \boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$.
> 4. Compute the **potential energy** $P$ from the heights of link centres of mass.
> 5. Form the **Lagrangian** $L = K - P$.
> 6. Apply $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$ to extract the equations of motion.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — Why Each Step Exists

- **Step 1 (Coordinates):** Using *independent* joint variables means the constraint equations are already satisfied implicitly — this is what eliminates the need for Lagrange multipliers.
- **Step 2 (Jacobian):** The kinetic energy of a rigid body $i$ is $K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \boldsymbol{v}_{c_i}^T \boldsymbol{v}_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \boldsymbol{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$. Both $\boldsymbol{v}_{c_i}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ are linear in $\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ via the **Jacobian**.
- **Step 3 (Inertia Matrix):** Because $K$ is a quadratic form in $\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$, the coefficient matrix is exactly the **manipulator inertia matrix** $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})$.
- **Step 4 (Potential Energy):** For gravity acting along $-\hat{z}$, $P_i = m_i g\, h_{c_i}(\boldsymbol{q})$.
- **Step 5 (Subtraction):** This collapses conservative forces (gravity, ideal spring potential) into a single scalar.
- **Step 6 (Variation):** The Euler-Lagrange operator annihilates any potential-energy-only term's contribution to motion (conservative forces emerge from the $\partial P / \partial q_i$ part).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU ESE and internal assessments on Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | **Lagrange's Equation** (scalar form) | N·m or N |
| 3 | $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i \boldsymbol{v}_{c_i}^T \boldsymbol{v}_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \boldsymbol{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ | KE of link $i$ | J |
| 4 | $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T \boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ | Total KE of manipulator | J |
| 5 | $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i \boldsymbol{J}_{v_i}^T \boldsymbol{J}_{v_i} + \boldsymbol{J}_{\omega_i}^T \boldsymbol{I}_i \boldsymbol{J}_{\omega_i}\right]$ | Inertia matrix | kg |
| 6 | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g\, h_{c_i}(\boldsymbol{q})$ | Total PE (gravity in $-\hat{z}$) | J |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \boldsymbol{q}}$ | Gravity vector | N·m |
| 8 | $\boldsymbol{C}_{kj} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i$ | **Christoffel symbols** (Coriolis) | kg/s |
| 9 | $\boldsymbol{M}\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | **Manipulator equation** | vector |
| 10 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{M}} - 2\boldsymbol{C} = \boldsymbol{S}$ (skew-symmetric if $\boldsymbol{C}$ from Christoffel) | **Skew-symmetry property** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value safety:** Whenever the magnitude of a scalar $x$ must be shown in a table, write $\lvert x \rvert$ (i.e., `\lvert x \rvert`) — **never** use a bare vertical pipe $\vert$ inside a markdown table, as it breaks the column delimiter.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Computed-Torque Control:** Inverts the manipulator equation to give $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})(\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}}_d + \boldsymbol{K}_v\dot{\boldsymbol{e}} + \boldsymbol{K}_p \boldsymbol{e}) + \boldsymbol{C}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G}$.
- **Trajectory Planning:** The structured form enables energy-optimal trajectory generation.
- **Simulation Engines:** Adams, MATLAB Simscape Multibody, and ROS use Lagrangian kernels for fast dynamics.
- **Bipedal & Humanoid Robotics:** Lagrange's equation with **contact constraints** (via Baumgarte stabilization or projection) is the industry standard.
- **Space Manipulators:** Free-floating base dynamics add a 6-DOF rigid-body term; the Lagrangian framework extends naturally.

---

## 2.5 Existence, Uniqueness, and Passivity Properties

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU viva, be ready to recite these three **structural properties**:
> 1. **$\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})$** is symmetric and **positive definite** ($\boldsymbol{M} = \boldsymbol{M}^T$, $\boldsymbol{x}^T \boldsymbol{M} \boldsymbol{x} > 0$ for $\boldsymbol{x} \neq 0$).
> 2. **$\dot{\boldsymbol{M}} - 2\boldsymbol{C}$ is skew-symmetric** (with Christoffel form), which gives **passivity**: $\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T (\dot{\boldsymbol{M}} - 2\boldsymbol{C})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} = 0$.
> 3. $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \boldsymbol{q}}$ is the gradient of a scalar potential (gravity is conservative).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lagrange's Equation from d'Alembert's Principle (Conceptual Path)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 includes the *motivation* for Lagrange's equation. Although the variational proof is lengthy, the **energy-based derivation** is expected.

### 3.1.1 Newton's Form (d'Alembert)

For a constrained system, virtual work of constraint forces vanishes. d'Alembert's principle in terms of *generalized coordinates* is:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} \right] \delta q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \,\delta q_i
$$

Since $\delta q_i$ is **arbitrary and independent**:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = Q_i
$$

### 3.1.2 Substitution of $L = K - P$

Because $P$ is **independent of $\dot{q}_i$**, we have $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$. Also $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \dfrac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} - \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$. Substituting both:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i
$$

> This is the **generalized form of Lagrange's equation** used throughout robotics.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark derivation**. Learn it cold.

### 3.2.1 System Description

Consider a **2-revolute (RR) planar manipulator** in the vertical plane with:
- Link 1: mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, centre of mass at $l_{c1}$ from joint 1, moment of inertia $I_1$ about its COM.
- Link 2: mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, centre of mass at $l_{c2}$ from joint 2, moment of inertia $I_2$ about its COM.
- Joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ (generalized coordinates $\boldsymbol{q} = [\theta_1, \theta_2]^T$).
- Gravity $g$ acts in the $-\hat{y}$ direction.

### 3.2.2 Forward Position of Centres of Mass

Position of COM of link 1:

$$
\boldsymbol{p}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \\ l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Position of COM of link 2:

$$
\boldsymbol{p}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.3 Velocities by Direct Differentiation

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \\ \phantom{-}l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}\dot{\theta}_1
$$

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \phantom{-}l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \phantom{-}l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Define the **Jacobians** for linear velocities:

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c1} = \boldsymbol{J}_{v1}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}, \qquad \dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c2} = \boldsymbol{J}_{v2}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Angular Velocities

$$
\omega_1 = \dot{\theta}_1, \qquad \omega_2 = \dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2
$$

### 3.2.5 Kinetic Energy of the Manipulator

$$
K = \tfrac{1}{2}m_1\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c1}^T\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c1} + \tfrac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c2}^T\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c2} + \tfrac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of terms:** The first and third terms capture *translational* KE of link 1 and link 2 COMs. The second and fourth terms capture *rotational* KE of each link about its own COM.

### 3.2.6 Assemble the Inertia Matrix

Collecting terms as $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T \boldsymbol{M}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where (after expansion):

$$
M_{11} = (m_1 + m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + I_1 + I_2
$$

$$
M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
$$

$$
M_{22} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the cross-coupling term $2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2$ — this is the *kinematic coupling* caused by link 2 rotating about joint 1.

### 3.2.7 Potential Energy (Gravity)

$$
P = m_1 g\, l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\,\bigl(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)
$$

### 3.2.8 Lagrangian

$$
L = K - P
$$

### 3.2.9 Applying Lagrange's Equation — First Coordinate $\theta_1$

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1} = M_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) = M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + \dot{M}_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

Now expand the time derivatives of $M_{11}$ and $M_{12}$ (only $\theta_2$ varies inside):

$$
\dot{M}_{11} = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{M}_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_2
$$

The potential partials:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial \theta_1} = -m_1 g l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 - m_2 g\bigl(l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)
$$

Lagrange's equation for $\theta_1$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = \tau_1
$$

Substituting and grouping:

$$
M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + C_{111}\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2C_{112}\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + C_{122}\dot{\theta}_2^2 + G_1 = \tau_1
$$

where the **Christoffel symbols** of the first kind are:

$$
C_{111} = 0, \quad C_{112} = C_{121} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2, \quad C_{122} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2
$$

### 3.2.10 Applying Lagrange's Equation — Second Coordinate $\theta_2$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2} = M_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2}\right) = M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{M}_{22}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

(Note $M_{22}$ is constant since it does not depend on $\theta_1$ or $\theta_2$ explicitly, so $\dot{M}_{22} = 0$.)

$$
