\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_1\,\text{(from KE coupling term)}\; - m_2 g l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch the algebra:** the $\partial K / \partial \theta_2$ piece produces a *velocity-squared* term $m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1^2$, while the $\partial P / \partial \theta_2$ piece produces the gravity term.

Substituting:

$$
M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + C_{211}\dot{\theta}_1^2 + C_{222}\dot{\theta}_2^2 + G_2 = \tau_2
$$

with $C_{211} = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2$ and $C_{222} = 0$, and $G_2 = m_2 g l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.

### 3.2.11 Final Manipulator Equation in Standard Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) &= \boldsymbol{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

with the explicit **Coriolis matrix** (computed via Christoffel symbols):

$$
\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and the **gravity vector**:

$$
\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete 2-DOF manipulator dynamic model** — the expected final answer for a 14-mark KTU derivation question.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Christoffel / Coriolis Matrix (General n-DOF)

The Christoffel symbol of the **first kind** is:

$$
c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)
$$

The $kj$-th entry of the Coriolis matrix is then:

$$
C_{kj} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\,\dot{q}_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This form guarantees the **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{\boldsymbol{M}} - 2\boldsymbol{C}$ is skew-symmetric, which is essential for proving the passivity of robotic systems in advanced control theory.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Lagrangian Formulation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams the *pipeline* of deriving robot dynamics via Lagrange's equation. The **subgraph** keyword is preserved as a structural wrapper; the **node IDs** are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB`) — no reserved keywords are used as bare node names.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[STEP 1: Define generalized coordinates q]
    nodeB[STEP 2: Build forward kinematics Jacobians Jv and Jw]
    nodeC[STEP 3: Assemble mass inertia matrix M of q]
    nodeD[STEP 4: Compute total kinetic energy K]
    nodeE[STEP 5: Compute total potential energy P]
    nodeF[STEP 6: Form Lagrangian L equals K minus P]
    nodeG[STEP 7: Apply Lagrange operator to each qi]
    nodeH[STEP 8: Extract Christoffel symbols and build C]
    nodeI[STEP 9: Form gravity vector G equals dP by dq]
    nodeJ[FINAL: Manipulator equation M q double dot plus C q dot plus G equals tau]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Energy-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT ENERGY STREAMS]
        nodeK[KINETIC ENERGY K]
        nodeP[POTENTIAL ENERGY P]
    end

    nodeK --> nodeL[LAGRANGIAN L equals K minus P]
    nodeP --> nodeL

    subgraph OPERATOR[EULER LAGRANGE OPERATOR]
        nodeE1[partial L by partial q dot]
        nodeE2[d by dt of partial L by partial q dot]
        nodeE3[partial L by partial q]
    end

    nodeL --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2
    nodeL --> nodeE3
    nodeE2 --> nodeS[SUBTRACT]
    nodeE3 --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT[OUTPUT GENERALIZED TORQUE TAU]
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Use of the Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQd[Desired Trajectory qd]
    nodeE[Error e equals qd minus q]
    nodeK1[Gain Kp]
    nodeK2[Gain Kv]
    nodeACC[Reference Acceleration q double dot ref]
    nodeMM[Inertia Matrix M of q]
    nodeCC[Coriolis C of q q dot]
    nodeGG[Gravity G of q]
    nodeSUM[Sum Node: tau equals M q double dot ref plus C q dot plus G]
    nodeROBO[ROBOT PLANT]
    nodeQ[Actual Joint Position q]

    nodeQd --> nodeE
    nodeQ --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeK1 --> nodeACC
    nodeE --> nodeK2 --> nodeACC
    nodeACC --> nodeSUM
    nodeMM --> nodeSUM
    nodeCC --> nodeSUM
    nodeGG --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeROBO
    nodeROBO --> nodeQ
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
State **Lagrange's equation** of motion for a robotic manipulator. Define the **Lagrangian** and list the four standard terms that appear in the resulting manipulator dynamic equation.

**Model Answer:**

Lagrange's equation for the $i$-th generalized coordinate $q_i$ of an $n$-DOF manipulator is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

The **Lagrangian** is the scalar:

$$
L = K - P
$$

where $K$ is the total kinetic energy and $P$ is the total potential energy of the manipulator.

The four standard terms in the resulting manipulator equation are:

1. $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ — Inertial torque,
2. $\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ — Coriolis and centripetal torque,
3. $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q})$ — Gravitational torque,
4. $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ — Generalized actuator torque.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
What are **generalized coordinates** in a robotic manipulator? Why is it advantageous to use **joint variables** as generalized coordinates when applying Lagrange's equation?

**Model Answer:**

**Generalized coordinates** are the *minimum set of independent scalar variables* $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ that uniquely and completely specify the configuration of a mechanical system. For an $n$-DOF serial manipulator, the natural choice is the **set of joint displacements** — angles $\theta_i$ for revolute joints and displacements $d_i$ for prismatic joints.

**Advantages of using joint variables:**

- The number of generalized coordinates equals the number of DOF, making the system *unconstrained* in these coordinates.
- Constraint forces at the joints are *automatically eliminated* from the equations.
- The resulting torques $\tau_i$ are directly the **actuator inputs**, ready for control design.
- The forward kinematics $\boldsymbol{x} = f(\boldsymbol{q})$ and Jacobian $\boldsymbol{J} = \partial f / \partial \boldsymbol{q}$ are well-known and easily computed.
- The structured form $\boldsymbol{M}\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ is obtained naturally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting $\partial L / \partial q_i$ contains BOTH $\partial K / \partial q_i$ AND $-\partial P / \partial q_i$.** Many students write only the gravity term and lose 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in Coriolis terms.** Velocity-coupling terms are $\dot{\theta}_i \dot{\theta}_j$ products — write them out explicitly, do not combine prematurely.
> 3. **Mixing up Christoffel indices.** $c_{ijk}$ is symmetric in the *last two* indices: $c_{ijk} = c_{ikj}$. Examiners check this.
> 4. **Losing the $2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2$ cross-coupling** in $M_{11}$ — this is the most common derivation slip.
> 5. **Forgetting the rotational KE** of link 2 about its own COM: $I_2(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 / 2$ contributes to $K$.
> 6. **Not simplifying $\boldsymbol{G}$** into a column vector at the end — always present the final answer in matrix form.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
Consider a **2-DOF planar manipulator (RR type)** with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, link masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, centre-of-mass distances $l_{c1}$ and $l_{c2}$, and link moments of inertia $I_1$ and $I_2$ about their own COMs. The manipulator moves in a vertical plane under gravity $g$.

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **kinetic energy** $K$ and the **potential energy** $P$ of the manipulator. Show the inertia matrix $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})$ explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply **Lagrange's equation** to obtain the equations of motion in the standard form $\boldsymbol{M}\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, and write down the **Coriolis matrix** $\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})$ and **gravity vector** $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q})$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a) — Kinetic and Potential Energy + Inertia Matrix**

The generalized coordinates are $\boldsymbol{q} = [\theta_1, \theta_2]^T$.

The position of COM of link 1:

$$
\boldsymbol{p}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \\ l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The position of COM of link 2:

$$
\boldsymbol{p}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \\ \phantom{-}l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}\dot{\theta}_1
$$

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{p}}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} -\bigl(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr) & -l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \phantom{-}\bigl(l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr) & \phantom{-}l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2\end{bmatrix}
$$

Angular velocities: $\omega_1 = \dot{\theta}_1$, $\omega_2 = \dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2$.

Total kinetic energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \tfrac{1}{2}m_1 l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2\Bigl[\bigl(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)^2\dot{\theta}_1^2 \\
&\qquad\quad + 2\bigl(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\qquad\quad + l_{c2}^2\sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)\dot{\theta}_2^2 \Bigr. \\
&\qquad\quad + \bigl(l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)^2\dot{\theta}_1^2 \\
&\qquad\quad + 2\bigl(l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\qquad\quad \bigl.+ l_{c2}^2\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)\dot{\theta}_2^2\Bigr] + \tfrac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$, the squared terms simplify to:

$$
K = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(m_1l_{c1}^2 + m_2l_1^2 + I_1 + I_2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2(m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + I_2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + (m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2)\dot{\theta}_2^2\bigr]
$$

Writing $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T \boldsymbol{M} \dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + I_1 + I_2) + 2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 & m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + I_2 \\
m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplification note:** Some texts use $I_1$ to denote the moment about the joint axis (i.e., $I_1 = I_{1,\text{com}} + m_1 l_{c1}^2$). If that convention is used, the term $m_1 l_{c1}^2$ merges into $I_1$.

Potential energy (gravity in $-\hat{y}$):

$$
P = m_1 g\, l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\bigl(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)
$$

---

**Part (b) — Equations of Motion**

Compute the partial derivatives of $L = K - P$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1} = M_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2} = M_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

Time derivatives (only $\theta_2$ varies inside $M_{11}$ and $M_{12}$):

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) = M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 - 2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2^2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2\dot{\theta}_1
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2}\right) = M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2
$$

Partial derivatives with respect to $q_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2\dot{\theta}_1 - m_1 g l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 - m_2 g l_1\cos\theta_1 - m_2 g l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1^2 - m_2 g l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

Applying Lagrange's equation $\dfrac{d}{dt}(\partial L/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial L/\partial q_i = \tau_i$ and collecting terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2^2 - 2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad + (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 &= M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compactly in **matrix form**:

$$
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}}_{\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})} \begin{bmatrix}\ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{\theta}_2\end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})} \begin{bmatrix}\dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2\end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1}+m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}}_{\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q})} = \begin{bmatrix}\tau_1 \\ \tau_2\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **standard manipulator dynamic equation** for the 2-DOF RR planar arm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Define **generalized coordinates** and **generalized forces**. With the aid of **d'Alembert's principle**, derive **Lagrange's equation** of motion in the form $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$ for a system of particles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple **1-DOF pendulum** of mass $m$, length $l$, swinging in a vertical plane, has generalized coordinate $\theta$ (angle from the vertical). Use Lagrange's equation to derive its equation of motion. State the **kinetic energy**, **potential energy**, and the **restoring torque**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Lagrange's Equation**

**Generalized coordinates** $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ are the minimum set of independent variables that fully describe the configuration of a constrained mechanical system. The corresponding **generalized forces** $Q_i$ are defined via the *virtual work* principle:

$$
\delta W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i\, \delta q_i
$$

For a system of $N$ particles with $3N$ Cartesian coordinates $x_j$ subject to $m$ holonomic constraints, we can switch from $x_j$ to $q_i$:

$$
\boldsymbol{x}_j = \boldsymbol{x}_j(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n, t), \quad j = 1, \dots, 3N
$$

The **generalized force** corresponding to $q_i$ is:

$$
Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{3N} F_j \frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i}
$$

where $F_j$ are the applied (non-constraint) Cartesian forces.

**d'Alembert's Principle** states that the *inertial forces* $(m_j \ddot{x}_j)$ together with applied forces do no virtual work on a system in equilibrium:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{3N} \left( F_j - m_j \ddot{x}_j \right) \frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i} = 0
$$

Substituting the definition of $Q_i$ and using $\partial x_j / \partial q_i$:

$$
Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{3N} F_j \frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i}
$$

and the inertial term:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{3N} m_j \ddot{x}_j \frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{3N} m_j \dot{x}_j \frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i}\right) - \sum_{j=1}^{3N} m_j \dot{x}_j \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial x_j}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$

Using the identity $\partial \dot{x}_j / \partial \dot{q}_i = \partial x_j / \partial q_i$, the first term becomes $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$ and the second becomes $\dfrac{\partial K}{\partial q_i}$, where $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum m_j \dot{x}_j^2$ is the kinetic energy. Hence:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} = Q_i
$$

If the applied forces are **derivable from a potential** $P$ such that $Q_i^{\text{cons}} = -\partial P / \partial q_i$, then substituting $L = K - P$ yields:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i^{\text{nc}}
$$

where $Q_i^{\text{nc}}$ are any *remaining* non-conservative generalized forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** This derivation assumes holonomic, scleronomic (time-invariant) constraints and smooth potentials. For robotic manipulators with revolute joints, these conditions are met.

---

**Part (b) — 1-DOF Pendulum**

Generalized coordinate: $\theta$ (angle from vertical downward).

Position of the bob (treating it as a point mass at the end):

$$
x = l\sin\theta, \quad y = -l\cos\theta
$$

Velocities:

$$
\dot{x} = l\cos\theta\,\dot{\theta}, \quad \dot{y} = l\sin\theta\,\dot{\theta}
$$

Squared speed:

$$
\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 = l^2\dot{\theta}^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = l^2\dot{\theta}^2
$$

**Kinetic energy:**

$$
K = \tfrac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2
$$

**Potential energy** (taking $y = 0$ at the pivot, gravity in $-\hat{y}$):

$$
P = m g y = -m g l\cos\theta
$$

**Lagrangian:**

$$
L = K - P = \tfrac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + m g l\cos\theta
$$

**Apply Lagrange's equation:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m l^2 \dot{\theta} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}\right) = m l^2 \ddot{\theta}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = -m g l\sin\theta
$$

Lagrange's equation:

$$
m l^2 \ddot{\theta} - (-m g l\sin\theta) = \tau
$$

$$
m l^2 \ddot{\theta} + m g l\sin\theta = \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the applied joint torque at the pivot. In the **free** case ($\tau = 0$):

$$
\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{l}\sin\theta = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **nonlinear pendulum equation**, valid for arbitrary amplitude. The **small-angle** approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ gives the linearized form $\ddot{\theta} + (g/l)\theta = 0$ with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/l}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lagrange's Equation for Module 1**

- **Lagrangian definition:** $L = K - P$ (kinetic minus potential energy) — always write this first.
- **Lagrange's equation (scalar form):** $\dfrac{d}{dt}(\partial L/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial L/\partial q_i = \tau_i$.
- **Lagrange's equation (vector form):** $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})\ddot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} + \boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- **Inertia matrix $\boldsymbol{M}(\boldsymbol{q})$:** Symmetric and **positive definite**; obtained by collecting $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}^T\boldsymbol{M}\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ from the kinetic energy.
- **Coriolis matrix $\boldsymbol{C}(\boldsymbol{q},\dot{\boldsymbol{q}})$:** Built from **Christoffel symbols**; $\boldsymbol{C}_{kj} = \sum_i c_{ijk}\dot{q}_i$ where $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial M_{kj}/\partial q_i + \partial M_{ki}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k)$.
- **Gravity vector $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q})$:** Simply $\boldsymbol{G}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \partial P / \partial \boldsymbol{q}$.
- **Structural properties:** (i) $\boldsymbol{M} = \boldsymbol{M}^T > 0$, (ii) $\dot{\boldsymbol{M}} - 2\boldsymbol{C}$ is skew-symmetric (Christoffel form), (iii) $\boldsymbol{G}$ is the gradient of a scalar potential.
- **2-DOF RR manipulator key expressions:**
  - $M_{11} = (m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + I_1 + I_2) + 2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2$,
  - $M_{12} = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 + I_2$,
  - $M_{22} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$.
- **Potential energy of 2-DOF RR:** $P = m_1 g l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g(l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))$.
- **Pendulum sanity check:** $m l^2 \ddot{\theta} + m g l\sin\theta = \tau$ (free: $\tau = 0$).
- **Why use $L$?** Constraint forces are eliminated automatically; the resulting torque vector is the actuator input ready for control.
- **Common exam pitfalls:** Missing rotational KE $I_i\omega_i^2/2$, sign errors in $\partial L/\partial q_i$, combining velocity terms before simplification, omitting the $2m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2$ coupling, mixing indices in Christoffel symbols.
- **Real-world deployment:** Computed-torque control, adaptive control, energy-efficient trajectory planning, multi-body simulation (Adams, MATLAB Simscape, Gazebo with ODE physics).
- **Pedagogical tip:** Always start a derivation with **(i) coordinates → (ii) velocities → (iii) K → (iv) P → (v) L → (vi) Euler-Lagrange** — never jump straight to the matrix form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Dynamics: General Expressions for Kinetic and Potential Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Dynamics: General Expressions for Kinetic and Potential Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robot Dynamics** is the branch of manipulator mechanics that relates the motion of a serial-chain robotic arm (positions, velocities, and accelerations of the generalized coordinates $q_i$) to the generalized forces (joint torques $\tau_i$) required to produce that motion, while rigorously accounting for the mass distribution, inertia, gravity loading, Coriolis, and centrifugal effects of the rigid links.

For an $n$-degree-of-freedom (DOF) serial manipulator with generalized joint coordinates $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the **total kinetic energy** $K(q, \dot{q})$ and the **total potential energy** $P(q)$ are scalar functions of the configuration. They are formally defined as:

$$K(q,\dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q) \dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j \equiv \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$$

$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i(q) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

where $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **symmetric positive-definite manipulator inertia matrix** (also called the mass matrix), $m_i$ is the mass of link $i$, $g$ is the gravity vector in the base frame, and $\bar{r}_{ci}(q)$ is the position vector to the center of mass of link $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Lagrangian formulation $L = K - P$ is the foundational route by which these two scalar energies are converted into the **Euler-Lagrange equations of motion** for the manipulator. Mastering the *general* closed-form expressions for $K$ and $P$ is a mandatory pre-requisite for writing the manipulator dynamic equation $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long fishing rod horizontally in your hand and rotating your wrist. Even though only the *wrist* (the joint) is being driven, the heavy tip of the rod accelerates violently. Your shoulder muscle must therefore apply a force not only to hold the rod's weight (this is the **potential energy** contribution) but also to *push* the mass of the rod through space (this is the **kinetic energy** contribution).

**Key Intuitive Points:**
- **Kinetic energy ($K$):** The "energy of motion" stored in *every* infinitesimal mass element of every link, *including* the speed contributions produced by the *motion of every other joint*. This cross-coupling is why $K$ contains the *off-diagonal* terms $M_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$). If joint 2 moves, it drags joint 3's link through space, contributing to $K$.
- **Potential energy ($P$):** The "stored energy of elevation" in the gravity field. As the robot's configuration $q$ changes, the heights of each link's center of mass change, changing $P$. Only gravity does work, so $P$ depends *only* on $q$, not $\dot{q}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are $K$ and $P$ so important for control?**
> They are the two *scalar* building blocks that, when fed into the Lagrange operator $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \tau$, automatically produce a *complete* set of $n$ coupled second-order differential equations describing the robot. This is far more systematic than writing Newton's laws for each link.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **$\mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$** (magnitude, vector form in base frame) |
| $m_i$ | Mass of link $i$ | kilograms (**kg**) |
| $I_i$ | Inertia tensor of link $i$ about its CoM | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| $\dot{q}_i$ | Generalized joint velocity | rad/s (revolute) or m/s (prismatic) |
| $K, P$ | Energy | Joules (**J**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Stage 1 — Decompose the Rigid Body into Distributed Mass

A rigid link $i$ is **not** a point mass. It is a continuous distribution of differential mass elements $dm$. The kinetic energy of *any* rigid body is therefore the integral of the kinetic energy of each of its $dm$ elements, taken over the volume of the link.

For link $i$, with total mass $m_i$, the kinetic energy is the sum of:
1. The translational kinetic energy of its **center of mass** (moving at linear velocity $v_{ci}$), and
2. The rotational kinetic energy of the mass elements **about** the center of mass (rotating at angular velocity $\omega_i$).

### Stage 2 — Express the Velocities in Terms of Joint Rates $\dot{q}$

Using the **Jacobian** of each link's center of mass and the angular Jacobian of the link:
- Linear velocity of CoM of link $i$: $v_{ci} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$
- Angular velocity of link $i$: $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$

Substituting these into the rigid-body kinetic energy integral and collecting terms yields a *quadratic form* in $\dot{q}$:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i} \right] \dot{q}$$

### Stage 3 — Sum Over All Links

The manipulator's total kinetic energy is the sum over all $n$ links. By linearity, the result is again a quadratic form in $\dot{q}$:
$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i} \right) \right] \dot{q}$$

The bracketed $n \times n$ matrix is the **manipulator inertia matrix** $M(q)$.

### Stage 4 — Express Potential Energy

A **rigid** body's potential energy in a uniform gravity field is purely translational — internal rigid-body motion does not change $P$. Thus:
$$P_i = m_i g h_{ci} = -m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

where $h_{ci}$ is the height of the CoM of link $i$ above the reference (datum) plane. Summing over all links:
$$P = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

The *only* configuration variable is $q$, confirming that $P$ is independent of $\dot{q}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 questions on Robot Dynamics.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Symmetry |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ | Total kinetic energy (quadratic form) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $K = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$ | Component-wise expansion of $K$ | J |
| 3 | $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i} \right]$ | Inertia matrix (symmetric, positive-definite) | $n \times n$ matrix |
| 4 | $K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{ci}^T v_{ci} + \frac{1}{2}\omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$ | KE of a single rigid link | J |
| 5 | $v_{ci} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}$ | Linear velocity of CoM of link $i$ | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\dot{q}$ | Angular velocity of link $i$ | rad/s |
| 7 | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \text{Trace}\left[ U_{kj} I_k U_{ki}^T \right] + m_k \delta_{ij}$ (Khatib-style) | Elemental form of $M$ | kg or kg·m² |
| 8 | $P = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$ | Total potential energy | J |
| 9 | $P_i = m_i g h_{ci}(q)$ | PE of link $i$ using scalar height | J |
| 10 | $G_i(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = -\sum_{k=i}^{n} m_k g^T \frac{\partial \bar{r}_{ck}}{\partial q_i}$ | Gravity term (partial derivative) | N·m |
| 11 | $L = K - P$ | Lagrangian (scalar) | J |
| 12 | $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler-Lagrange equation | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Rule for Off-Diagonal Coupling:** $M_{ij} \neq 0$ whenever moving joint $j$ alone causes motion at joint $i$ (or vice-versa). For an open-chain serial arm with all revolute joints, the *upper triangle* of $M$ is generally non-zero.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The general expressions for $K$ and $P$ are the **theoretical backbone** for:

1. **Model-Based Control** — Computed Torque Control requires $M(q)$ to be evaluated online.
2. **Energy-Efficient Trajectory Planning** — Minimum-energy paths minimise $\int K + P \, dt$.
3. **Dynamic Simulation Software** — Tools like MATLAB Robotics Toolbox, ADAMS, and Gazebo internally evaluate $K$ and $P$ to integrate the equations of motion.
4. **Payload Estimation** — Observing changes in $K$ and $P$ allows observers to identify unknown carried loads.
5. **Bipedal & Humanoid Robotics** — Walking gaits are formulated as an optimization of kinetic + potential energy exchange (the *inverted pendulum* analogy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Expression for Total Kinetic Energy $K$

### Starting Point: Kinetic Energy of a Single Differential Mass Element

A rigid link $i$ contains a continuous distribution of mass. Consider an infinitesimal mass element $dm$ located at position $r$ within the link. Its linear velocity is $v(r)$ and its speed squared is $v^T v$. The kinetic energy of this $dm$ is $\frac{1}{2} v^T v \, dm$.

The total kinetic energy of link $i$ is therefore the volume integral:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \int_{\text{link } i} v(r)^T v(r) \, dm$$

### Rigid-Body Kinematics: Decompose into Translation + Rotation

For any point on a rigid body, the velocity is the sum of (a) the velocity of the CoM, and (b) the velocity due to rotation about the CoM:
$$v(r) = v_{ci} + \omega_i \times (r - r_{ci})$$

Squaring (dotting with itself):
$$v^T v = v_{ci}^T v_{ci} + 2 v_{ci}^T (\omega_i \times (r - r_{ci})) + (\omega_i \times (r - r_{ci}))^T (\omega_i \times (r - r_{ci}))$$

### Integrating Over the Link

The second cross term vanishes because $\int (r - r_{ci}) \, dm = 0$ (definition of center of mass). Using the rigid-body inertia tensor definition $I_i = \int (r - r_{ci})(r - r_{ci})^T dm$ (more precisely, the inertia tensor about the CoM with the mass-weighted form), we obtain:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{ci}^T v_{ci} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$$

This is the **standard König-Shafranov theorem** for rigid body kinetic energy.

### Expressing Velocities in Terms of Joint Rates

The position of the CoM of link $i$ in the base frame is a function of the joint coordinates only:
$$\bar{r}_{ci} = \bar{r}_{ci}(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$v_{ci} = \frac{d\bar{r}_{ci}}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \bar{r}_{ci}}{\partial q_j} \dot{q}_j \equiv J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$$

where the **linear Jacobian** of the CoM of link $i$ is the $3 \times n$ matrix whose $j$-th column is $\frac{\partial \bar{r}_{ci}}{\partial q_j}$. Similarly, the angular velocity of link $i$ in the base frame is:
$$\omega_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \omega_i}{\partial \dot{q}_j} \dot{q}_j \equiv J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$$

> Note: For revolute joint $j$, $\frac{\partial \omega_i}{\partial \dot{q}_j} = z_{j-1}$ (the previous joint's axis), which is *constant* w.r.t. $\dot{q}_j$. For prismatic joint $j$, it is zero.

### Substituting Into $K_i$ and Collecting Into Matrix Form

Inserting the Jacobian expressions:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q}$$

The middle matrix is a $n \times n$ symmetric matrix. Denote it $M_i(q)$:
$$M_i(q) = m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}$$

### Summing Over All Links

Since kinetic energy is a scalar, the total kinetic energy is the sum:
$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \underbrace{\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i(q) \right]}_{M(q)} \dot{q}$$

Hence the **general closed-form expression for total kinetic energy**:
$$\boxed{K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j}$$

### Key Properties of $M(q)$ (The Manipulator Inertia Matrix)

1. **Symmetric:** $M(q) = M^T(q)$ because the sum of symmetric matrices is symmetric.
2. **Positive-definite:** $x^T M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ (kinetic energy is strictly positive for any non-zero velocity).
3. **Configuration-dependent:** $M(q)$ is *not* constant; it changes with robot pose.
4. **Bounded:** $\mu_1 I \preceq M(q) \preceq \mu_2 I$ for some positive scalars $\mu_1, \mu_2$ for all $q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence of Positive-Definiteness:** $M(q)$ is always *invertible*, which is what makes the manipulator equation of motion solvable for the accelerations: $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(q)[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)]$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General Expression for Total Potential Energy $P$

### Physical Foundation: Conservative Gravity Field

In a *uniform* gravitational field (the standard Earth-surface assumption), the gravitational potential energy of a point mass $m$ at height $h$ is $P = mgh$. The minus-sign convention is for the *datum* (reference) being at $h = 0$ and energy increasing *upward*.

For a *rigid body* with distributed mass, the PE is:
$$P_i = \int_{\text{link } i} g h(r) \, dm$$

Since gravity is uniform, $g$ is constant. Using the definition of center of mass ($\int r \, dm = m_i \bar{r}_{ci}$), the integral reduces to:
$$P_i = m_i g h_{ci} = -m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

The *sign* convention is set so that $P$ is the *work* gravity does when the CoM is displaced *downward*; the dot product $g^T \bar{r}_{ci}$ gives the scalar height component of $\bar{r}_{ci}$ along the gravity vector.

### Summing Over All Links

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

### Key Property of $P(q)$

$P$ depends *only* on the joint positions $q$ (configuration), not on $\dot{q}$. This is because:
- Gravity is a *conservative* force — it depends only on position.
- A rigid body has *internal* degrees of freedom that do not change its gravitational PE.

### The Gravity Term $G(q)$

The gradient of $P$ with respect to $q$ yields the gravity loading vector:
$$G_i(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = -\sum_{k=i}^{n} m_k g^T \frac{\partial \bar{r}_{ck}(q)}{\partial q_i}$$

(The summation starts at $k = i$ because link $i$ and *all* subsequent links' CoMs are affected when joint $i$ moves.)

In compact form:
$$G(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Out-of-Plane Check:** For a 2-DOF planar arm with both links of mass $m$ and length $L$ rotating in a vertical plane, $P = mg\left[ \frac{L}{2}\sin q_1 + L\sin q_1 + \frac{L}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$. Differentiating yields the joint torques needed to *hold* the arm against gravity — directly used in the static case $\tau = G(q)$.

## 3.3 Final Generalized Energy Expressions — Summary Box

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} K(q,\dot{q}) &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Kinetic Energy)} \\[8pt] P(q) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q) \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Potential Energy)} \end{aligned} }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Units:** All energies must be in **Joules**. If $q_i$ is in radians and $\dot{q}_i$ in rad/s, the resulting $K$ is in J automatically. If mixed revolute-prismatic joints are present, $M_{ij}$ carries the correct kg or kg·m² scaling to make units consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Robot Dynamics Pipeline

The following **Mermaid Block-Level Flow Diagram** shows how the distributed-mass model of a serial manipulator is processed through the two scalar energy functions $K$ and $P$, and how they feed into the Lagrangian $L = K - P$ and ultimately the Euler-Lagrange manipulator equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Joint Variables q and dq/dt"]:::input
    B["For Each Link i in 1 to n"]:::proc
    C["Compute Linear Jacobian Jvi of CoM"]:::calc
    D["Compute Angular Jacobian Jwi of Link i"]:::calc
    E["Form Mi q = m_i Jvi^T Jvi + Jwi^T Ii Jwi"]:::calc
    F["Form vci = Jvi dq/dt and wi = Jwi dq/dt"]:::calc
    G["Compute Ki = 0.5 dq/dt^T Mi dq/dt"]:::calc
    H["Accumulate K = K plus Ki and M = M plus Mi"]:::proc
    I["Compute rci q = Forward Kinematics of CoM"]:::calc
    J["Compute Pi = minus m_i g^T rci q"]:::calc
    K["Accumulate P = P plus Pi"]:::proc
    L["Form Lagrangian L = K minus P"]:::combine
    M["Apply Euler Lagrange Operator to Each qi"]:::combine
    N["Output: Manipulator Equation tau = M q d2q/dt2 plus C plus G"]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B
    A --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> B
    H --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M
    M --> N

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0d6efd
    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#664d03
    classDef calc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0f5132
    classDef combine fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#842029
    classDef output fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#3d1d6e
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Energy Domain Mapping for a 2R Planar Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConfigurationSpace["Configuration Space q1, q2"]
        q1["q1: Shoulder Angle"]
        q2["q2: Elbow Angle"]
    end

    subgraph EnergyScalar["Scalar Energy Outputs"]
        KE["K = 0.5 dq/dt^T M q dq/dt"]
        PE["P = minus sum m_i g^T rci q"]
        LAG["L = K minus P"]
    end

    subgraph EquationOfMotion["Euler Lagrange Step"]
        EL["d/dt partial L by partial dq/dt minus partial L by partial q = tau"]
    end

    q1 --> KE
    q2 --> PE
    q1 --> PE
    q2 --> KE
    KE --> LAG
    PE --> LAG
    LAG --> EL

    classDef config fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0d6efd
    classDef energy fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#664d03
    classDef eom fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0f5132
    class q1 config
    class q2 config
    class KE energy
    class PE energy
    class LAG energy
    class EL eom
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decomposition of $M(q)$ into Per-Link Contributions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["M q = Manipulator Inertia Matrix n by n"]:::out
    M1["M1 from Link 1"]:::in
    M2["M2 from Link 2"]:::in
    MN["Mn from Link n"]:::in
    Msum["M = M1 plus M2 plus ... plus Mn"]:::proc
    M1trans["m1 Jv1^T Jv1"]:::calc
    M1rot["Jw1^T I1 Jw1"]:::calc
    M1 --> M1trans
    M1 --> M1rot
    M1trans --> Msum
    M1rot --> Msum
    M2 --> Msum
    MN --> Msum
    Msum --> M

    classDef out fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#3d1d6e
    classDef in fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0d6efd
    classDef calc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0f5132
    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#664d03
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PERAT523, May 2024]`
**State the general expression for the total kinetic energy of an $n$-DOF serial rigid-link manipulator in terms of the joint rates $\dot{q}$ and the manipulator inertia matrix $M(q)$. Comment on the symmetry and positive-definiteness of $M(q)$.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The total kinetic energy of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator is the sum of the kinetic energies of all its $n$ links:
$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i v_{ci}^T v_{ci} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$$

Using the Jacobians $v_{ci} = J_{v_i}\dot{q}$ and $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}\dot{q}$, this reduces to the *quadratic form*:
$$K = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$$

where $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$.

**Properties of $M(q)$:** It is **symmetric**, i.e., $M(q) = M^T(q)$, and **positive-definite**, i.e., $x^T M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$. Positive-definiteness follows from the fact that $K > 0$ for any non-zero velocity. **[3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for symmetry, 1 for positive-definiteness]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PERAT523, Dec 2023]`
**Write the general closed-form expression for the total potential energy of an $n$-DOF rigid-link manipulator. Why does it depend only on $q$ and not on $\dot{q}$?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The total potential energy is the sum of the gravitational potential energies of all links, each evaluated at its center of mass:
$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of link $i$, $g$ is the gravity vector in the base frame, and $\bar{r}_{ci}(q)$ is the position vector of the center of mass of link $i$.

$P$ depends *only* on $q$ because:
1. Gravity is a *conservative* force — the work done by it depends only on the endpoints (positions), not the path or the rate.
2. For a *rigid* body, the internal mass distribution does not change with time, so only the *configuration* of the CoM matters, not the internal spin. **[3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for conservative force reason, 1 for rigid-body reason]**

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module-2 questions carry 14 marks, are split into two 7-mark sub-parts, and offer an internal choice. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PERAT523, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** *For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$, link lengths $L_1, L_2$, and center of masses located at the midpoints of the links, derive the general expression for the kinetic energy $K$ in terms of the joint variables $q_1, q_2$ and their velocities $\dot{q}_1, \dot{q}_2$.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Step 1: Forward Kinematics of Centers of Mass**

Link 1's CoM is at $\bar{r}_{c1} = \left[ \frac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1, \; \frac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 \right]^T$.

Link 2's CoM is at $\bar{r}_{c2} = \left[ L_1\cos q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \; L_1\sin q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \right]^T$.

**Step 2: Compute Velocities by Time-Differentiation**

$$v_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \\[4pt] \frac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow v_{c1}^T v_{c1} = \left(\frac{L_1}{2}\right)^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

For link 2, the two components (using the chain rule):
$$\dot{x}_{c2} = -L_1 \sin q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 - \frac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\cdot(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_{c2} = L_1 \cos q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\cdot(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

Squaring and adding (after algebraic simplification using $\sin^2+\cos^2 = 1$):
$$v_{c2}^T v_{c2} = L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \left(\frac{L_2}{2}\right)^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

**Step 3: Sum Translational KE of Both Links**

$$K_{\text{trans}} = \frac{1}{2}m_1 \left(\frac{L_1}{2}\right)^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2\left[L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \left(\frac{L_2}{2}\right)^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\right]$$

**Step 4: Add Rotational KE of Links** (using $I_1 = \frac{m_1 L_1^2}{12}$, $I_2 = \frac{m_2 L_2^2}{12}$ about their CoMs; $\omega_1 = \dot{q}_1$, $\omega_2 = \dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2$):
$$K_{\text{rot}} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{m_1 L_1^2}{12}\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{m_2 L_2^2}{12}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$$

**Step 5: Combine and Collect Into $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$**

After collecting $\dot{q}_1^2$, $\dot{q}_2^2$, and $\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$ terms:
$$K = \frac{1}{2}\left[M_{11}\dot{q}_1^2 + 2M_{12}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + M_{22}\dot{q}_2^2\right]$$

where:
$$M_{11} = \frac{m_1 L_1^2}{3} + m_2 L_1^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 L_2^2}{3}$$
$$M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 L_2^2}{3}$$
$$M_{22} = \frac{m_2 L_2^2}{3}$$

This is the required general kinetic energy expression. **[7 marks distributed across the 5 steps]**

---

**(b)** *For the same 2-DOF planar manipulator, derive the total potential energy $P(q)$ and compute the gravity vector $G(q) = \partial P / \partial q$.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1: Identify the heights of the two CoMs**

Taking the datum at $y = 0$ and gravity acting in the $-y$ direction, the height of CoM of link 1 is $h_{c1} = \frac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1$. The height of CoM of link 2 is $h_{c2} = L_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$.

**Step 2: Apply the General PE Formula**

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{2} m_i g h_{ci} = m_1 g \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[L_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]$$

**Step 3: Compute $G_1 = \partial P / \partial q_1$**

$$G_1 = m_1 g \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1 + m_2 g\left[L_1 \cos q_1 + \frac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\right]$$

**Step 4: Compute $G_2 = \partial P / \partial q_2$**

$$G_2 = m_2 g \cdot \frac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 5: Express in Vector Form**

$$G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \left(\frac{m_1 L_1}{2} + m_2 L_1\right) g \cos q_1 + \frac{m_2 L_2 g}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt] \frac{m_2 L_2 g}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

These torques are the static torques required to hold the arm at any configuration $q$ against gravity. **[7 marks: 1 for heights, 1 for PE formula, 1 each for G1, G2, 1 for collection, 1 for engineering interpretation]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PERAT523, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** *Starting from the definition of kinetic energy of a differential mass element $dm$, derive the general expression for the total kinetic energy of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Step 1 — Differential Mass Element Approach**

For a differential mass $dm$ on link $i$, moving with linear velocity $v(r)$, the KE is:
$$dK_i = \frac{1}{2} v(r)^T v(r)\, dm$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Over the Link**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2}\int_{\text{link }i} v(r)^T v(r)\, dm$$

**Step 3 — König Decomposition**

Decompose $v(r) = v_{ci} + \omega_i \times (r - r_{ci})$. After squaring and integrating, the cross-term vanishes (CoM definition):
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{ci}^T v_{ci} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$$

**Step 4 — Velocity-Jacobian Relation**

$v_{ci} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}$, $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\dot{q}$.

**Step 5 — Quadratic Form in $\dot{q}$**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T\left[m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}\right]\dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M_i(q)\dot{q}$$

**Step 6 — Sum Over All Links**

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \underbrace{\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i(q)\right]}_{M(q)} \dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$$

This is the required general expression. **[7 marks: 1 each for the 6 steps + final boxed result]**

---

**(b)** *For a 3-DOF RRR planar manipulator (three revolute joints in a vertical plane) with link masses $m_1, m_2, m_3$ concentrated at the link endpoints, derive the inertia matrix $M(q)$ and explain the physical meaning of the off-diagonal terms $M_{12}$, $M_{13}$, $M_{23}$.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1 — CoM Positions**

Link 1 CoM at endpoint of link 1: $\bar{r}_{c1} = [L_1 \cos q_1, L_1 \sin q_1]^T$. Link 2 CoM at endpoint of link 2 (relative to joint 2): $\bar{r}_{c2} = [L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2), \, L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)]^T$. Link 3 CoM: $\bar{r}_{c3} = \bar{r}_{c2} + [L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3), L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3)]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute Linear Velocities**

$v_{c1} = L_1 \dot{q}_1 [-\sin q_1, \cos q_1]^T \Rightarrow v_{c1}^T v_{c1} = L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$.

$v_{c2} = [-L_1\dot{q}_1 \sin q_1 - L_2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin(q_1+q_2), \; L_1\dot{q}_1\cos q_1 + L_2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos(q_1+q_2)]^T$.

$v_{c2}^Tv_{c2} = L_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + L_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$.

$v_{c3}^Tv_{c3}$ is more complex and is left as a structured sum.

**Step 3 — Mass Matrix Elements (rotational KE neglected as masses are point masses at endpoints)**

$$M_{11} = m_1 L_1^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + m_3 L_1^2 + m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 + m_3[L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 + L_1 L_3 \cos(q_2+q_3)]$$

$$M_{22} = m_2 L_2^2 + m_3 L_2^2 + m_3 L_2 L_3 \cos q_3$$

$$M_{33} = m_3 L_3^2$$

$$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 + m_3 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 + \frac{m_3 L_1 L_3}{2}\cos(q_2+q_3)$$

$$M_{13} = M_{31} = \frac{m_3 L_1 L_3}{2}\cos(q_2+q_3)$$

$$M_{23} = M_{32} = m_3 L_2 L_3 \cos q_3 + \frac{m_3 L_2 L_3}{2}\cos q_3 = \frac{3}{2} m_3 L_2 L_3 \cos q_3$$

*(Note: the exact coefficients depend on the placement convention; the *form* of cosine-coupling is the key learning point.)*

**Step 4 — Physical Meaning of Off-Diagonal Terms**
- $M_{12} \neq 0$: When joint 2 moves alone ($\dot{q}_2 \neq 0, \dot{q}_1 = 0$), link 2 and link 3 are displaced, contributing to $K$. Conversely, moving joint 1 drags the rest of the arm. $M_{12}$ represents this **mutual inertial coupling** between joints 1 and 2.
- $M_{13}$: Coupling between shoulder (joint 1) and wrist (joint 3) — non-zero because the geometry of links 2 and 3 propagates motion.
- $M_{23}$: Coupling between elbow and wrist.

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight**

Off-diagonal terms are *configuration-dependent* (functions of $q$), so the inertia "felt" by a joint changes as the arm reconfigures. This is why a single robot can have very different dynamic response in different poses. **[7 marks: 2 for the matrix structure, 2 for the off-diagonal elements, 3 for the physical meaning and engineering insight]**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost in ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting rotational KE** — many students write only the translational $K_{\text{trans}}$ for a link. If the link is a distributed mass, **you must add** $\frac{1}{2}\omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$ as well. Lost marks: **2–3 marks per link**.
>
> 2. **Sign of potential energy** — writing $P = +m g h$ instead of $P = -mg^T \bar{r}_{ci}$. While both are valid if the *convention* is stated, failing to declare the convention loses 1 mark. Standard KTU convention: $P$ is *positive* when raised *upward*.
>
> 3. **Asymmetric inertia matrix** — writing $M_{12} \neq M_{21}$. The matrix is **always symmetric** by construction; any asymmetry in a student's answer is an automatic red flag for the examiner.
>
> 4. **Mixing unit conventions** — using $\dot{q}$ in deg/s and forgetting to convert to rad/s, or treating gravity as a *scalar* $g$ when the vector form $g^T$ is required for a 3-D arm. KTU examiners do deduct for this.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Lagrangian link** — a common error is jumping from $K$ and $P$ directly to $\tau$ without explicitly showing the Euler-Lagrange operator step. Always write the Lagrangian $L = K - P$ as an intermediate step.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Revision Box — Memorize These Before You Enter The Hall.**

- ✅ **Kinetic Energy** is a **quadratic form** in $\dot{q}$: $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$.
- ✅ **Inertia matrix** $M(q)$ is **symmetric** and **positive-definite**, $n \times n$, configuration-dependent.
- ✅ The element $M_{ij}$ is the **effective inertia** coupling joints $i$ and $j$.
- ✅ $M(q)$ is computed by **summing per-link contributions** $M_i = m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}$.
- ✅ **Potential Energy** $P(q) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T \bar{r}_{ci}(q)$ — depends on $q$ **only**.
- ✅ $P_i$ is evaluated at the **center of mass** of link $i$, **not** at the joint or the link endpoint.
- ✅ For a **point mass** at a joint, rotational KE contribution is **zero**; for a **distributed link**, both translational and rotational KE are required.
- ✅ The **gravity term** $G(q) = \partial P / \partial q$ is an $n \times 1$ vector; it gives the static joint torques needed to hold the robot in configuration $q$ against gravity.
- ✅ The **Lagrangian** $L = K - P$ is the bridge that connects $K$ and $P$ to the equations of motion via $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \tau$.
- ✅ In **MATLAB** (Robotics Toolbox), one uses `robot.inertia(q)` to evaluate $M(q)$ and `robot.gravload(q)` to evaluate $G(q)$ — these internally evaluate the general expressions we derived.
- ✅ The kinetic energy expression **always** reduces to a positive-definite quadratic form regardless of joint type (revolute / prismatic).
- ✅ If the manipulator is in a **gravity-free** environment (e.g., space), $P \equiv 0$ and $G \equiv 0$.
- ✅ The **Christoffel symbols** used to derive $C(q,\dot{q})$ depend on $\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k$ — so the elements of $M(q)$ you compute here are the *first step* in getting the Coriolis/centrifugal matrix too.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetic Energy for an n-Link Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetic Energy for an n-Link Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Kinetic Energy of an n-Link Robot** is defined as the scalar sum of the translational and rotational kinetic energies of every rigid link comprising the manipulator, expressed entirely in joint-space coordinates. For a serial-chain manipulator with $n$ revolute and/or prismatic joints, the total kinetic energy assumes the canonical **quadratic-in-velocity form**:

$$K(q, \dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$$

where $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of generalized joint coordinates, $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint velocity vector, and $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the symmetric, positive-definite **Manipulator Inertia Matrix** (also called the Mass Matrix or Pseudo-Inertia Matrix in joint space).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The kinetic-energy formulation is the **gateway equation** to deriving the Euler-Lagrange equations of motion for a robot manipulator. The mass matrix $M(q)$ obtained here directly enters the dynamics equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-segment gymnastics ribbon** held by a performer. Each segment has its own mass and tumbles independently, yet the motion of every segment is *coupled* — when the wrist flicks, the elbow follows, the shoulder rotates, and the ribbon tip whips violently. Now, instead of measuring the kinetic energy of each segment in 3D space (which would require knowing the position and velocity of every particle), the performer only controls the **joint angles** at the shoulder, elbow, and wrist. The kinetic energy expression $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ is precisely the mathematical tool that:

- Takes the *raw* kinetic energy of every link in 3D Cartesian space.
- Re-expresses it in terms of the *controllable* joint variables $(q, \dot{q})$.
- Captures the coupling between joints automatically inside $M(q)$.

The matrix $M(q)$ is, in essence, a **"configuration-dependent effective mass"** that the robot *feels* at its joints for any given pose $q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Joint Space?** Robots are actuated at the joints, not in task space. Therefore, for controller design, energy must be expressed using the same coordinates in which the actuators deliver power — i.e., joint coordinates.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

- The total kinetic energy $K$ has SI units of **Joules (J)** or equivalently **N·m**.
- The mass matrix $M(q)$ is **symmetric** ($M = M^T$) and **positive-definite** ($x^T M x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$).
- Diagonal terms $M_{ii}$ represent the *effective inertia* seen at joint $i$ when only joint $i$ moves.
- Off-diagonal terms $M_{ij}$ represent the *coupling inertia* between joints $i$ and $j$.
- The dimension of $M(q)$ is $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ where $n$ is the number of joints (typically **6** for a full 6-DOF manipulator, **7** for collaborative robots like Franka Panda).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

The derivation of the kinetic-energy expression proceeds through **five rigorously ordered stages**:

1. **Link Decomposition:** Decompose the $n$-link robot into $n$ individual rigid bodies. For each link $i$, identify its mass $m_i$ and its rotational inertia tensor $I_i$ (about the link's center of mass, expressed in the link's own frame).

2. **Velocity Extraction:** Express the linear velocity $v_{c_i}$ of the center of mass of link $i$ and the angular velocity $\omega_i$ of link $i$ in terms of the joint velocity vector $\dot{q}$:
$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\, \dot{q}, \qquad \omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\, \dot{q}$$
   Here $J_{v_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **linear Jacobian** and $J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ is the **angular Jacobian**, both evaluated at the center of mass of link $i$.

3. **Per-Link Kinetic Energy:** Write the kinetic energy of link $i$ as the sum of its translational and rotational parts:
$$K_i = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\, m_i\, v_{c_i}^{T} v_{c_i}}_{\text{translational}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\, \omega_i^{T}\, I_i\, \omega_i}_{\text{rotational}}$$

4. **Global Summation:** Sum over all $n$ links:
$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, \dot{q}^{T} J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i}\, \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{q}^{T} J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i}\, \dot{q}$$

5. **Canonical Form Factorization:** Factor out the common $\dot{q}^{T}$ and $\dot{q}$ to obtain:
$$K = \frac{1}{2}\, \dot{q}^{T}\, M(q)\, \dot{q}, \quad \text{where} \quad M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - Steps 1–2 convert an *intricate 3D mechanics* problem into a *linear algebraic* problem via Jacobians.
> - Steps 3–4 exploit the **superposition principle** of energy (energy is additive over non-interpenetrating bodies).
> - Step 5 is the *defining moment* — it produces the symmetric positive-definite matrix that governs the manipulator's inertial response. This matrix is the cornerstone of model-based control (computed torque, adaptive control, impedance control).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$ | KE of a single rigid link $i$ | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Linear velocity of COM of link $i$ in terms of $\dot{q}$ | m/s; $J_{v_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ |
| 3 | $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Angular velocity of link $i$ in terms of $\dot{q}$ | rad/s; $J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ |
| 4 | $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$ | Total KE = sum of per-link KE | J |
| 5 | $K = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ | Canonical quadratic form | J |
| 6 | $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$ | Manipulator mass matrix | $M \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, symmetric, PD |
| 7 | $M_{ii}(q) > 0$ | Diagonal entries are strictly positive | kg·m² equivalent |
| 8 | $M_{ij}(q) = M_{ji}(q)$ | Symmetry property | $\forall i,j$ |
| 9 | $\frac{d}{dt}K = \dot{q}^T \tau$ (for conservative $G(q)$) | Power = generalized force × velocity | W (Watts) |
| 10 | $I_i = R_i\, {}^{i}I_i\, R_i^T$ | Inertia tensor transformation rule | where $R_i$ is link $i$ orientation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** When $q$ is a mix of revolute ($\theta$, in rad) and prismatic ($d$, in m) joints, the mass matrix $M(q)$ acquires **mixed units** in its entries. This is acceptable because the quadratic form $\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q$ yields Joules regardless.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotics:** The mass matrix is essential for **computed-torque control**, where the inverse dynamics $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ is solved online to linearize and decouple the manipulator.
- **Humanoid Robotics:** The $M(q)$ matrix of a humanoid (e.g., 30+ DOF) determines its **balance, push-recovery capability, and locomotion energy efficiency**.
- **Space Manipulators:** For free-flying space robots, the kinetic energy generalizes to include the **base spacecraft**, leading to the **symmetric inertia matrix** that couples arm motion with base reaction.
- **Surgical Robots:** The mass matrix determines the **dynamic manipulability ellipsoid** — a measure of how the robot can accelerate in various task-space directions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Canonical Kinetic Energy Form

**Starting Point:** The $n$-link robot is decomposed into $n$ rigid links, each with mass $m_i$ and inertia tensor $I_i$ (about its own center of mass, expressed in a body-fixed frame).

**Step 1 — Express the kinetic energy of link $i$.**

A rigid body in 3D space possesses kinetic energy split into translational (motion of the center of mass) and rotational (spin about the center of mass) contributions.

$$K_i = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i}}_{\text{translational}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i}_{\text{rotational}}$$

Here $v_{c_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the linear velocity of the center of mass of link $i$, $\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the angular velocity of link $i$, and $I_i \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is the inertia tensor of link $i$ about its center of mass.

**Step 2 — Relate link velocities to joint velocities using Jacobians.**

From the velocity kinematics of serial chains, the velocity of any point rigidly attached to link $i$ is linearly related to the joint velocity vector. For the center of mass point:

$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\, \dot{q}$$

For the angular velocity of link $i$:

$$\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\, \dot{q}$$

where $J_{v_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ and $J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ are the linear and angular Jacobian sub-matrices *corresponding to link $i$*.

**Step 3 — Substitute the velocity expressions into the kinetic energy of link $i$.**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \left( J_{v_i} \dot{q} \right)^T \left( J_{v_i} \dot{q} \right) + \frac{1}{2} \left( J_{\omega_i} \dot{q} \right)^T I_i \left( J_{\omega_i} \dot{q} \right)$$

Using the identity $(A\dot{q})^T (A\dot{q}) = \dot{q}^T A^T A \dot{q}$:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i\, \dot{q}^T J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i}\, \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}\, \dot{q}$$

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all $n$ links.**

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \right] \dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — Define the manipulator mass matrix.**

$$\boxed{M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i\, J_{v_i}^T(q)\, J_{v_i}(q) + J_{\omega_i}^T(q)\, I_i\, J_{\omega_i}(q) \right]}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{K(q, \dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\, \dot{q}^T\, M(q)\, \dot{q}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — 2-Link Planar Manipulator

Consider a **2-link planar RR manipulator** moving in the horizontal plane (so gravity does no work — this is a free-floating example purely for kinetic energy illustration).

**Given data:**
- Link 1: length $l_1$, mass $m_1$ concentrated at its tip (point mass), inertia $I_1 = 0$.
- Link 2: length $l_2$, mass $m_2$ concentrated at its tip, inertia $I_2 = 0$.
- Joint angles: $q = [\theta_1, \theta_2]^T$.

**Step A — Positions of centers of mass.**

Position of COM of link 1:
$$x_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos\theta_1, \qquad y_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\sin\theta_1$$

Position of COM of link 2:
$$x_{c_2} = l_1\cos\theta_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y_{c_2} = l_1\sin\theta_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step B — Velocities (time derivatives).**

For link 1:
$$\dot{x}_{c_1} = -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin\theta_1\, \dot{\theta}_1, \qquad \dot{y}_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos\theta_1\, \dot{\theta}_1$$

$$v_{c_1}^T v_{c_1} = \dot{x}_{c_1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c_1}^2 = \left(\frac{l_1}{2}\right)^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2$$

For link 2:
$$\dot{x}_{c_2} = -l_1\sin\theta_1\, \dot{\theta}_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_{c_2} = l_1\cos\theta_1\, \dot{\theta}_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$

**Step C — Kinetic energy of link 1.**

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_1 \left(\frac{l_1}{2}\right)^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 = \frac{1}{8} m_1 l_1^2\, \dot{\theta}_1^2$$

**Step D — Kinetic energy of link 2.**

After expanding and simplifying the algebra (squaring the velocities and summing), we obtain:

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[ l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_1 l_2 \dot{\theta}_1 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2 + \frac{l_2^2}{4}(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 \right]$$

**Step E — Total kinetic energy and identification of $M(q)$.**

$$K = K_1 + K_2 = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$$

Expanding $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M \dot{q}$ with $\dot{q} = [\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2]^T$ and matching coefficients:

$$M_{11} = \frac{1}{4} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + \frac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2$$

$$M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + \frac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2$$

$$M_{22} = \frac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2$$

In matrix form:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{4} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + \tfrac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2 & \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + \tfrac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2 \\[6pt] \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + \tfrac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2 & \tfrac{1}{4} m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The mass matrix is **configuration-dependent** (notice the $\cos\theta_2$ terms). It is symmetric ($M = M^T$) and positive-definite (since all eigenvalues are strictly positive for any $q$). The off-diagonal $M_{12} = M_{21}$ encodes the *centrifugal coupling* between the two joints.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Properties of the Mass Matrix

For the KTU exam, students must articulate the following **theoretical properties** of $M(q)$:

- **Symmetry:** $M_{ij}(q) = M_{ji}(q)$ for all $i,j$. This follows from the summation of symmetric terms $J^T A J$ where $A$ is symmetric.
- **Positive Definiteness:** $\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} > 0$ for all $\dot{q} \neq 0$. This follows from the physical fact that $K > 0$ for any non-zero motion.
- **Boundedness:** There exist positive scalars $\mu_1, \mu_2$ such that $\mu_1 I \leq M(q) \leq \mu_2 I$ for all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (this holds when joint limits do not cause singularities).
- **Skew-Symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$:** For a properly defined Coriolis matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$, the matrix $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric. This property is essential for **passivity-based control** and **Lyapunov stability proofs**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kinetic Energy Computation

The following block diagram maps the **data flow** from raw link parameters to the final canonical kinetic energy expression. This is a functional flow rather than a physical drawing, as the underlying mathematics operates on multi-dimensional tensors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Geometric and Inertial Parameters] --> B[Link Decomposition Module]
    B --> C1[Link 1: m1, I1, COM1]
    B --> C2[Link 2: m2, I2, COM2]
    B --> C3[Link n: mn, In, COMn]
    C1 --> D1[Jacobian Computation Jv1 Jw1]
    C2 --> D2[Jacobian Computation Jv2 Jw2]
    C3 --> D3[Jacobian Computation Jvn Jwn]
    D1 --> E1[Per Link Kinetic Energy K1]
    D2 --> E2[Per Link Kinetic Energy K2]
    D3 --> E3[Per Link Kinetic Energy Kn]
    E1 --> F[Global Summation Stage]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Factorization into Quadratic Form]
    G --> H[Mass Matrix M of q]
    G --> I[Canonical KE K equals one half q dot transpose M q dot]
    H --> J[Symmetric PD Verification]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output: Ready for Euler Lagrange Derivation]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        P1[Joint Variables q and q dot]
        P2[Link Parameters mi, Ii]
    end
    subgraph KIN[Kinematics Layer]
        P1 --> Q1[Forward Position Kinematics]
        P1 --> Q2[Velocity Kinematics Jacobians]
        Q2 --> Q3[Jv sub i and Jw sub i]
    end
    subgraph EN[Energy Layer]
        Q3 --> R1[Translational KE: one half mi vci transpose vci]
        P2 --> R2[Rotational KE: one half wi transpose Ii wi]
        R1 --> S[Per Link Ki]
        R2 --> S
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        S --> T[Sum over i: K]
        T --> U[Canonical Form: one half q dot transpose M of q q dot]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow: From Physical Motion to Quadratic Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Physical n Link Robot in Motion] --> DECOMP[Decompose into n Rigid Links]
    DECOMP --> COMVEL[Compute COM Linear Velocity vci]
    DECOMP --> ANGVEL[Compute Angular Velocity wi]
    COMVEL --> JACV[Map to Joint Space via Jv sub i]
    ANGVEL --> JACW[Map to Joint Space via Jw sub i]
    JACV --> KE_TRANS[Translational KE Term]
    JACW --> KE_ROT[Rotational KE Term]
    KE_TRANS --> SUM[Sum All Contributions]
    KE_ROT --> SUM
    SUM --> MASS_MAT[Extract Mass Matrix M of q]
    MASS_MAT --> CANON[Canonical Form K = half qdot transpose M qdot]
    CANON --> USE[Used in Euler Lagrange Dynamics]
    USE --> CTRL[Controller Design Computed Torque]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above use **strictly alphanumeric node identifiers** and **plain uppercase text labels** to ensure clean compilation. Each block represents a mathematical or computational stage, not a physical component.
