## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the canonical expression for the total kinetic energy of an n-link robot manipulator in joint-space coordinates. Mention the physical significance of the mass matrix $M(q)$ and list any two of its mathematical properties.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The total kinetic energy of an $n$-link robot in joint space is given by:

$$K(q,\dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\, \dot{q}^{T}\, M(q)\, \dot{q}$$

where $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **manipulator inertia (mass) matrix**.

**Physical significance:** $M(q)$ represents the *configuration-dependent effective inertia* of the robot as perceived at its joints. Diagonal entries $M_{ii}$ are the effective inertia about joint $i$, while off-diagonal entries $M_{ij}$ represent the inertial coupling between joints $i$ and $j$.

**Two mathematical properties:**
1. $M(q)$ is **symmetric**: $M(q) = M^T(q)$.
2. $M(q)$ is **positive definite**: $\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} > 0$ for all $\dot{q} \neq 0$.

*(Award 1 mark for canonical expression, 1 mark for physical significance, 1 mark for the two properties.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*For a single rigid link of mass $m$ and rotational inertia $I$ (about its center of mass), write the general expression for the kinetic energy and show how it can be expressed entirely in terms of joint velocities $\dot{q}$ using Jacobian matrices.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

General kinetic energy of a single rigid link:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^{T} v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^{T} I_i \omega_i$$

Using the velocity Jacobian sub-matrices for the $i$-th link:

$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}, \quad \omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$$

Substituting and factoring:

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^{T} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q}$$

*(1 mark for general expression, 1 mark for Jacobian substitution, 1 mark for the final factored form.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *Starting from first principles, derive the canonical expression for the total kinetic energy of an $n$-link robot manipulator in terms of the joint velocity vector $\dot{q}$ and the manipulator mass matrix $M(q)$. Clearly define every symbol used. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *For a 2-link planar RR manipulator with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, link masses $m_1, m_2$ (each concentrated at the distal end of its link), and zero rotational inertia, derive the explicit $2 \times 2$ manipulator mass matrix $M(q)$. Verify its symmetry and discuss its configuration dependence. [7 marks]*

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Link Decomposition [1 mark]:**
Decompose the $n$-link robot into $n$ rigid links. For each link $i$, define:
- $m_i$ = mass of link $i$
- $I_i \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ = inertia tensor of link $i$ about its center of mass
- $v_{c_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ = linear velocity of the COM of link $i$
- $\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ = angular velocity of link $i$

**Step 2 — Per-link kinetic energy [1 mark]:**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^{T} v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^{T} I_i \omega_i$$

**Step 3 — Velocity-Jacobian relationship [2 marks]:**

$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}, \quad \omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$$

where $J_{v_i}, J_{\omega_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$.

**Step 4 — Substitution and factorization [2 marks]:**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^{T} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — Summation and definition of $M(q)$ [1 mark]:**

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$$

$$\boxed{M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]}$$

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Position of COMs [2 marks]:**
With point-mass assumption and COMs at distal ends (i.e., $r_{c_1} = l_1$, $r_{c_2} = l_2$ from each joint):

$$x_{c_1} = l_1 \cos\theta_1, \quad y_{c_1} = l_1 \sin\theta_1$$

$$x_{c_2} = l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$y_{c_2} = l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Velocities and squared speed of each COM [2 marks]:**

$$v_{c_1}^T v_{c_1} = l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2$$

$$v_{c_2}^T v_{c_2} = l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2$$

**Step 3 — Total kinetic energy and identification of $M(q)$ [2 marks]:**

$$K = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 + m_2)l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{\theta}_1 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2) \cos\theta_2$$

Expanding and matching $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M \dot{q}$:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 \\[4pt] m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of symmetry and discussion [1 mark]:**
$M_{12} = M_{21}$ is explicitly satisfied. The matrix is configuration-dependent via $\cos\theta_2$: when the arm is fully stretched ($\theta_2 = 0$), coupling is maximum; when folded ($\theta_2 = \pi$), the cross-terms decrease.

---

**Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** *For an n-link serial manipulator, the kinetic energy is $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$. Show that $M(q)$ is symmetric and positive definite. State the physical reason for positive definiteness. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *A 2-DOF manipulator has the following Jacobian matrices for its two links (in the horizontal plane):*
$$J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 & 0 \\ l_1 c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\ l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & l_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
*where $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. Assuming point masses $m_1, m_2$ and zero rotational inertia, compute $M(q)$. [7 marks]*

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Symmetry [3 marks]:**
The mass matrix is given by:
$$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^{T} J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^{T} I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$$

Each term $m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i}$ is symmetric since $(J^T J)^T = J^T (J^T)^T = J^T J$.
Each term $J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}$ is symmetric because $I_i$ is symmetric and the same transpose argument applies.
Sum of symmetric matrices is symmetric. Therefore $M(q) = M^T(q)$.

**Positive Definiteness [2 marks]:**
Take any $\dot{q} \neq 0$:
$$\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i \Vert J_{v_i} \dot{q} \Vert^2 + \dot{q}^T J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \dot{q} \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i \Vert v_{c_i} \Vert^2 + \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i \right] = 2K \geq 0$$

Since $K > 0$ whenever the robot is in motion ($\dot{q} \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ at least one link moves), we conclude $\dot{q}^T M \dot{q} > 0$ for all $\dot{q} \neq 0$.

**Physical reason [2 marks]:**
The kinetic energy of any physically moving body is *strictly positive* (it can never be zero for a non-trivial motion, since kinetic energy is a non-negative scalar vanishing only when all velocities vanish). The mass matrix inherits this positivity because the quadratic form $\dot{q}^T M \dot{q}$ is identically equal to $2K$, which is positive for any non-zero velocity.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Form $J_{v_1}^T J_{v_1}$ [2 marks]:**
$$J_{v_1}^T J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1^2 s_1^2 + l_1^2 c_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$m_1 J_{v_1}^T J_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Form $J_{v_2}^T J_{v_2}$ [3 marks]:**

$$J_{v_2}^T J_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1^2 s_1^2 + l_1^2 c_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2(s_1 s_{12} + c_1 c_{12}) + l_2^2(s_{12}^2 + c_{12}^2) & l_2^2(s_{12}^2 + c_{12}^2) + l_1 l_2(s_1 s_{12} + c_1 c_{12}) \\ \text{symmetric} & l_2^2(s_{12}^2 + c_{12}^2) \end{bmatrix}$$

Using $s_1 s_{12} + c_1 c_{12} = \cos\theta_2$ and $s_{12}^2 + c_{12}^2 = 1$:

$$m_2 J_{v_2}^T J_{v_2} = m_2 \begin{bmatrix} l_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + l_2^2 & l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 \\[4pt] l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 & l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Sum to obtain $M(q)$ [2 marks]:**

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + l_2^2) & m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2) \\[4pt] m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2) & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + m_2 l_2^2 & m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + m_2 l_2^2 \\[4pt] m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2 + m_2 l_2^2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rotational KE term:** Many students write only the translational part. Always include $\tfrac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$.
> 2. **Sign errors in $J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i}$:** The matrix $J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i}$ is always symmetric positive semi-definite; a non-symmetric answer indicates an algebraic error.
> 3. **Mixing Jacobian frames:** The angular velocity Jacobian $J_{\omega_i}$ and inertia $I_i$ must be expressed in **consistent frames** (typically the link's body frame).
> 4. **Ignoring units in mixed-joint manipulators:** A 1-DOF revolute + 1-DOF prismatic robot has $M(q)$ with units kg·m² on some entries and kg on others. Do not normalize blindly.
> 5. **Skipping the verification of positive definiteness:** A common KTU follow-up question is *"Is your $M(q)$ positive definite? Justify."* Students should compute $\det(M) > 0$ and trace$(M) > 0$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The kinetic energy of an $n$-link robot is **always quadratic in joint velocities**: $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$.
- ✅ The mass matrix $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is **symmetric** and **positive definite** for all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- ✅ Diagonal entries of $M(q)$ = effective inertia at each joint; off-diagonal entries = coupling inertia between joints.
- ✅ The formula $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$ is the **only** canonical way to construct $M(q)$ from link Jacobians.
- ✅ $M(q)$ is **configuration-dependent** for all manipulators with revolute joints; for pure prismatic chains, $M$ becomes constant.
- ✅ The matrix $M(q)$ is the **cornerstone** of Euler-Lagrange dynamics, computed-torque control, and adaptive control.
- ✅ For the 2-link planar example, the cross-coupling term is proportional to $\cos\theta_2$ — a result that recurs in many KTU problems.
- ✅ Units of $K$ are **Joules (J)**, units of $M(q)$ are mixed (kg·m² for revolute–revolute, kg for prismatic–prismatic, kg·m for mixed).
- ✅ The property $\dot{M} - 2C$ skew-symmetric is essential for **passivity-based stability proofs** (frequently asked as a 3-mark or 7-mark question).
- ✅ Remember the relationship $\frac{dK}{dt} = \dot{q}^T \tau$ for conservative systems (no gravity or for horizontal motion) — this leads directly to the Euler-Lagrange equations in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Energy for an n-Link Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Potential Energy for an n-Link Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Potential Energy for an n-Link Robot

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potential Energy (P)** in the context of an n-link robotic manipulator is defined as the total stored energy of the manipulator due to its position in a conservative force field, primarily the **gravitational field**. Mathematically, it is a scalar function of the generalized joint coordinates, expressed as $P(q)$, which represents the work done against gravity to move the manipulator from a zero reference configuration to the current configuration.

For a rigid body in a uniform gravitational field, the potential energy is given by the dot product of the gravity vector with the position vector of the body's center of mass, weighted by its mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT523 – Module 2):** The potential energy of a serial n-link rigid-body chain is the sum of the potential energies of the individual links. Each link's contribution depends on its **mass**, the **location of its center of mass**, and the **gravity vector** acting at that center of mass.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chain of heavy metal bars connected by joints, hanging from a ceiling. The lowest bar has the most "stored falling energy" (highest potential energy if measured positively), while the top bar has less because it's already higher up. The total "stored falling energy" of the whole chain is the sum of what each bar would release if the chain were to fall.

In robotics terms:
- Each **link** is like a metal bar.
- The **joint angle** is like the angle at which the bar hangs.
- The **center of mass** of each link is the average location of all its weight.
- The **gravity vector** ($\mathbf{g}$) is the "pulling force" we work against.

The total potential energy is essentially the **total "anti-gravity work"** stored in the manipulator's pose.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Vectors

- **Gravity Vector ($\mathbf{g}$):** Standard value on Earth surface is $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T \text{ m/s}^2$ (with $\mathbf{z}$ pointing upward). Its magnitude is **$\vert \mathbf{g} \vert = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**.
- **Mass ($m_i$):** Scalar mass of link $i$ in **kg**.
- **Position Vector of Center of Mass ($\mathbf{r}_{ci}$):** Position of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the **base frame**, measured in **meters (m)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Potential Energy Matters in Dynamics:**
> The generalized gravitational force vector $\mathbf{g}(q)$ required to write the Euler-Lagrange equations of motion is obtained as the **gradient** of the potential energy:
> $$\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$$
> Without $P(q)$, we cannot compute gravity compensation torques — critical for industrial robots carrying heavy payloads.
> 
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Potential Energy for a Rigid Body

A rigid body in a uniform gravitational field behaves as if all its mass is concentrated at a single point — the **center of mass (CoM)**. The potential energy depends only on:
1. The total mass of the body.
2. The height of its center of mass.
3. The magnitude of gravity.

Since the gravitational field is **conservative** (work done is path-independent), we can define a scalar potential energy function that is a function of position alone.

### 2.2 Potential Energy of a Single Rigid Link

For the $i^{\text{th}}$ link of the manipulator:
- Let $m_i$ be the mass of link $i$.
- Let $\mathbf{r}_{ci} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ be the position vector of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the base frame.
- Let $\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ be the gravity vector expressed in the base frame.

The potential energy of link $i$ is:
$$P_i = -m_i \, \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$$

The negative sign convention ensures that as the height of the link's center of mass increases (work done against gravity), the potential energy **increases** (consistent with the physics convention that $P = mgh$).

### 2.3 Total Potential Energy of an n-Link Robot

The total potential energy is the sum of potential energies of all $n$ links:
$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ -m_i \, \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q) \right]$$

This is a **scalar function** that depends only on the joint configuration $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

### 2.4 Expressing Center of Mass Position

The position of the center of mass of link $i$ in the base frame is obtained through the forward kinematics:
$$\mathbf{r}_{ci}(q) = \mathbf{T}_i^0(q) \, \mathbf{r}_{ci}^i$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{T}_i^0(q) \in SE(3)$ is the homogeneous transformation matrix from link $i$'s frame to the base frame.
- $\mathbf{r}_{ci}^i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the position of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in link $i$'s own coordinate frame (a constant vector).

So the total potential energy becomes:
$$P(q) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \mathbf{g}^T \left[ \mathbf{T}_i^0(q) \, \mathbf{r}_{ci}^i \right]$$

### 2.5 Alternative Formulation Using Inertia Tensors

In some advanced derivations (used when considering distributed mass and rotational kinetic energy simultaneously), the potential energy is also written using the inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_i$ and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$. However, **for potential energy alone**, the simple scalar form $P = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$ suffices.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | PE of single link | $P_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$ | Gravitational PE of link $i$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Total PE of n-link robot | $P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q)$ | Sum over all links | Joules (J) |
| 3 | CoM position (base frame) | $\mathbf{r}_{ci}(q) = \mathbf{T}_i^0(q) \mathbf{r}_{ci}^i$ | Forward kinematics of CoM | meters (m) |
| 4 | Gravity vector (base frame, $\mathbf{z}$-up) | $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ | Earth gravity | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 5 | Gravity vector (base frame, $\mathbf{z}$-down) | $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, +9.81]^T$ | Alternate convention | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 6 | Gravitational force vector | $\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$ | Generalized force for Lagrange eq. | N·m or N |
| 7 | Gravity compensation torque | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_g = \mathbf{g}(q)$ | Torque to cancel gravity | N·m |
| 8 | For 2-D (planar) case | $\mathbf{g} = [0, -g, 0]^T$ | Gravity in plane | $\text{m/s}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Tip:** In the formula $P_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$, if $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ (i.e., gravity points downward in the negative $\mathbf{z}$ direction), and the CoM has positive $\mathbf{z}$ coordinate, then $P_i$ becomes **positive**, which is physically correct. Many KTU problems use this convention.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Robotics (e.g., KUKA, ABB, FANUC arms):** The gravity term $\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q}$ is computed in real-time to provide **feedforward gravity compensation**, reducing the burden on feedback controllers.
- **Humanoid Robots (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas):** The potential energy of each limb is used in **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP)** calculations for balance control.
- **Space Manipulators:** When $\mathbf{g} \approx 0$, the entire potential energy term vanishes, simplifying dynamics.
- **Cable-Driven Robots:** Gravity directly affects cable tension calculations through potential energy.

> 
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Potential Energy from Work-Energy Principle

### Step 1: Define Work Done Against Gravity
For a particle of mass $m$ at position $\mathbf{r}$, the infinitesimal work done by gravity as it moves by $d\mathbf{r}$ is:
$$dW = \mathbf{F}_g \cdot d\mathbf{r} = m\mathbf{g} \cdot d\mathbf{r}$$

### Step 2: Recognize Conservative Field
Since gravity is conservative, there exists a scalar potential $P$ such that:
$$\mathbf{F}_g = -\nabla P$$

### Step 3: Integrate to Find P
For uniform gravity $\mathbf{g} = \text{constant}$:
$$P = -m\mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r} + C$$

where $C$ is an arbitrary reference constant (often set to zero at a reference configuration).

### Step 4: Apply to a Rigid Body
For a rigid body, the total mass acts effectively at the center of mass. So:
$$P_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$$

### Step 5: Sum Over All Links
$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Explicit Form for a 2-Link Planar Robot

Consider a 2-link planar manipulator in the $\mathbf{x}$-$\mathbf{y}$ plane with revolute joints, link lengths $l_1, l_2$, and joint angles $q_1, q_2$.

### Step 1: Set Up Gravity Vector
Gravity acts in the $-\mathbf{y}$ direction:
$$\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Center of Mass Positions
Let the center of mass of link 1 be at distance $l_{c1}$ from joint 1, and similarly for link 2.

Position of CoM of link 1:
$$\mathbf{r}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1} \cos q_1 \\ l_{c1} \sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Position of CoM of link 2:
$$\mathbf{r}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos q_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Compute Each Term $m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$

For link 1:
$$m_1 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c1} = m_1 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1} \cos q_1 \\ l_{c1} \sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -m_1 g \, l_{c1} \sin q_1$$

For link 2:
$$m_2 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c2} = m_2 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos q_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= -m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$$

### Step 4: Sum to Get Total Potential Energy
$$\begin{aligned} P(q) &= -\left[ -m_1 g \, l_{c1} \sin q_1 \right] - \left[ -m_2 g \left( l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right) \right] \\
P(q) &= m_1 g \, l_{c1} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right] \end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Simplify
$$\boxed{P(q) = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \sin q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \sin(q_1+q_2)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Generalized Gravity Force Vector from Potential Energy

Given $P(q)$, the gravity term in the Euler-Lagrange equations is:
$$\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$$

For the 2-link example above:
$$\begin{aligned} g_1(q) &= \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\
g_2(q) &= \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} = m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{aligned}$$

These are the **gravity torques** that must be applied at the joints to hold the robot stationary against gravity — a cornerstone result in KTU Module 2.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: General n-Link Compact Form

Using the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{r}_{ci})$ that maps joint velocities to the linear velocity of link $i$'s CoM:
$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{ci} = \mathbf{J}_i \dot{q}$$

But for potential energy, we don't need the Jacobian. The compact form is:
$$P(q) = -\mathbf{g}^T \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q)$$

This shows that the total gravity force on the manipulator is the same as if all link masses were concentrated at their respective centers of mass — a beautiful consequence of the **superposition principle** for conservative forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The potential energy of a serial n-link robot depends only on the joint positions (not velocities or accelerations), making it a **configuration-dependent scalar** in the configuration space $\mathbb{R}^n$.

## 3.5 Worked Example: 2-Link Robot with Point Masses

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar robot has link masses concentrated at the end of each link. Link 1 has mass $m_1$ at distance $l_1$ from the base, and link 2 has mass $m_2$ at distance $l_1 + l_2$ (the end-effector). Find $P(q)$ and $\mathbf{g}(q)$.

**Solution:**

The CoM positions are simply the joint positions:
$$\mathbf{r}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos q_1 \\ l_1 \sin q_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

With $\mathbf{g} = [0, -g]^T$:
$$\begin{aligned} P_1 &= m_1 g \, l_1 \sin q_1 \\
P_2 &= m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \right] \\
P(q) &= (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \sin q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \end{aligned}$$

Gravity torques:
$$\begin{aligned} g_1(q) &= (m_1+m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\
g_2(q) &= m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{aligned}$$

> 
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Potential Energy Computation Pipeline

The following diagram maps the data flow involved in computing the potential energy of an n-link robot, from joint variables through forward kinematics to the scalar output $P(q)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Joint Variable Vector q"] --> B["FORWARD KINEMATICS MODULE T_i^0 q"]
    B --> C["TRANSFORM MATRIX T_1^0 q for Link 1"]
    B --> D["TRANSFORM MATRIX T_2^0 q for Link 2"]
    B --> E["TRANSFORM MATRIX T_n^0 q for Link n"]
    C --> F["COM PUTE r_c1 = T_1^0 times r_c1^i"]
    D --> G["COM PUTE r_c2 = T_2^0 times r_c2^i"]
    E --> H["COM PUTE r_cn = T_n^0 times r_cn^i"]
    F --> I["GRAVITY VECTOR g = 0, 0, minus 9.81 T"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["DOT PRODUCT STEP: m_i g TRANSCRIPT r_ci"]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K["SUM OVER ALL LINKS: P q equals minus sum m_i g TRANSCRIPT r_ci"]
    K --> L["OUTPUT: SCALAR POTENTIAL ENERGY P q IN JOULES"]
    L --> M["GRADIENT STEP: g q equals partial P divided by partial q"]
    M --> N["OUTPUT: GRAVITY TORQUE VECTOR g q IN NEWTON METERS"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mass Distribution and Center of Mass Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RIGID LINK i WITH MASS m_i"] --> B["DISTRIBUTED MASS ELEMENTS dm"]
    B --> C["INTEGRATE: r_ci equals 1 over m_i times integral of r dm"]
    C --> D["CENTER OF MASS POINT: r_ci"]
    D --> E["CONCENTRATE ALL MASS AT r_ci"]
    E --> F["COMPUTE P_i equals minus m_i g TRANSCRIPT r_ci"]
    F --> G["SUM OVER ALL LINKS"]
    G --> H["TOTAL P q FOR n-LINK ROBOT"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Gravity Vector and CoM Height Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BASE FRAME ORIGIN O_0"] --> B["z AXIS VERTICAL UPWARD"]
    A --> C["y AXIS HORIZONTAL"]
    A --> D["x AXIS HORIZONTAL"]
    B --> E["GRAVITY VECTOR g POINTS DOWNWARD: g equals 0, 0, minus 9.81"]
    E --> F["CENTER OF MASS AT HEIGHT h above ORIGIN"]
    F --> G["POTENTIAL ENERGY P equals m g h"]
    G --> H["IF h INCREASES THEN P INCREASES"]
    G --> I["IF h DECREASES THEN P DECREASES"]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: From Forward Kinematics to Gravity Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: SPECIFY DH PARAMETERS"] --> B["STEP 2: COMPUTE T_i^0 q FOR EACH LINK"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: EXTRACT POSITION PART OF T_i^0"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: MULTIPLY BY CONSTANT CoM VECTOR r_ci^i"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: FORM SCALAR m_i g TRANSCRIPT r_ci q"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: SUM ALL LINKS: P q"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: COMPUTE GRADIENT g q equals partial P divided by partial q"]
    G --> H["STEP 8: APPLY TORQUE tau equals g q TO COMPENSATE GRAVITY"]
    H --> I["ROBOT HOLDS POSITION WITHOUT FEEDBACK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** When drawing or interpreting diagrams related to potential energy, always identify the **base frame orientation** and the **gravity vector direction** first. The sign of each term in $P(q)$ depends on this convention.

> 
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q:** Define the potential energy of a rigid link in a gravitational field. Write the expression for the total potential energy of an n-link robot.

**Model Answer:**

The potential energy of a rigid link of mass $m_i$ in a uniform gravitational field is the work done against gravity to position its center of mass at a given location. It is given by:
$$P_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$$

where $\mathbf{r}_{ci}$ is the position vector of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the base frame, and $\mathbf{g}$ is the gravity vector.

For an n-link robot, the total potential energy is the sum of the potential energies of all links:
$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q)$$

It is a **scalar function** of the joint configuration $q$.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q:** State the role of the potential energy function in deriving the equations of motion of a robotic manipulator using the Lagrangian formulation.

**Model Answer:**

In the Lagrangian formulation, the potential energy function $P(q)$ is used to compute the **generalized gravitational force vector** $\mathbf{g}(q)$ acting on the manipulator:
$$\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$$

This term appears in the Euler-Lagrange equation:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

where $L = K - P$ is the Lagrangian ($K$ = kinetic energy). The gradient $\frac{\partial P}{\partial q}$ provides the gravity-induced joint torques, enabling the controller to apply feedforward gravity compensation torques equal to $\mathbf{g}(q)$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Derive the potential energy function for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with revolute joints. Hence, compute the gravity torque vector. Given: link lengths $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, link masses $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, CoM distances $l_{c1} = l_{c2} = 0.5$ m, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

#### Part (a) Derive $P(q)$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the geometry.**
The robot lies in the $\mathbf{x}$-$\mathbf{y}$ plane with revolute joints. Joint angles are $q_1$ and $q_2$.

**Step 2: Gravity vector.**
$$\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Position vectors of CoM.**
$$\mathbf{r}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1} \cos q_1 \\ l_{c1} \sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{r}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos q_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute each term $m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$.**
$$m_1 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c1} = -m_1 g \, l_{c1} \sin q_1$$
$$m_2 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c2} = -m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$$

**Step 5: Sum to obtain total potential energy.**
$$P(q) = m_1 g l_{c1} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$$

Substituting the given values:
$$P(q) = (2)(9.81)(0.5)\sin q_1 + 2(9.81)\left[ (1)\sin q_1 + (0.5)\sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$$
$$P(q) = 9.81 \sin q_1 + 19.62 \sin q_1 + 9.81 \sin(q_1+q_2)$$
$$\boxed{P(q) = 29.43 \sin q_1 + 9.81 \sin(q_1+q_2) \text{ Joules}}$$

#### Part (b) Compute the gravity torque vector $\mathbf{g}(q)$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute $\frac{\partial P}{\partial q_1}$.**
$$g_1(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} = 29.43 \cos q_1 + 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 2: Compute $\frac{\partial P}{\partial q_2}$.**
$$g_2(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} = 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 3: Assemble the gravity torque vector.**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{g}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 29.43 \cos q_1 + 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}}$$

These torques, when applied at joints 1 and 2, hold the manipulator stationary against gravity at any configuration $(q_1, q_2)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**A 2-link planar PR (Prismatic-Revolute) manipulator operates in a vertical plane. The first joint is prismatic with extension $q_1$ along the horizontal direction, and the second joint is revolute with angle $q_2$ from the vertical. The prismatic link has mass $m_1 = 5$ kg with CoM at $q_1/2$. The revolute link has mass $m_2 = 3$ kg with CoM at distance $l_{c2} = 0.4$ m from joint 2. Find the potential energy $P(q)$ and gravity force/torque vector.**

#### Part (a) Derive $P(q)$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Coordinate setup.**
- Prismatic joint extends horizontally: $x$-axis.
- Revolute joint rotates from the vertical $y$-axis.

**Step 2: Position of CoM of link 1 (prismatic).**
The CoM is at $q_1/2$ from the base along the horizontal:
$$\mathbf{r}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1/2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Position of CoM of link 2 (revolute).**
The base of link 2 is at $(q_1, 0)$. The CoM of link 2 is at angle $q_2$ from vertical:
$$\mathbf{r}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 + l_{c2} \sin q_2 \\ l_{c2} \cos q_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Gravity vector.**
$$\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute potential energies.**
$$P_1 = -m_1 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c1} = -5 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -9.81 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} q_1/2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$P_2 = -m_2 \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{c2} = -3 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -9.81 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} q_1 + l_{c2} \sin q_2 \\ l_{c2} \cos q_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 3(9.81)(0.4)\cos q_2$$

**Step 6: Total potential energy.**
$$P(q) = 0 + 11.772 \cos q_2 = 11.772 \cos q_2 \text{ J}$$
$$\boxed{P(q) = 11.772 \cos q_2 \text{ Joules}}$$

#### Part (b) Compute the gravity force/torque vector (7 Marks)

For a prismatic joint, the generalized force is a **force** (Newtons). For a revolute joint, it is a **torque** (N·m).

**Step 1: Force at prismatic joint 1.**
$$f_1 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Torque at revolute joint 2.**
$$\tau_2 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} = -11.772 \sin q_2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Final result.**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{g}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -11.772 \sin q_2 \end{bmatrix}}$$

where the first entry is a **force in N** and the second is a **torque in N·m**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Forgetting the negative sign in $P = -m \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_c$, leading to $P$ being **negative** when it should be positive. Always check by considering a specific configuration (e.g., arm horizontal: $q_1 = 0$).
> 2. **Wrong Gravity Vector:** Using $\mathbf{g} = [0, -9.81, 0]^T$ for a 3-D problem where $\mathbf{z}$ is vertical. The correct form is $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ for $\mathbf{z}$-up convention.
> 3. **CoM vs. Joint Position:** Confusing the position of the **center of mass** $\mathbf{r}_{ci}$ with the position of the **end-effector** or **next joint**. Always identify which point the mass is attached to.
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** Mixing force units (N) and torque units (N·m) in the gravity vector for hybrid prismatic-revolute manipulators.
> 5. **Forgetting to Sum All Links:** Computing PE for only one link and presenting it as the total — a classic 2-to-3 mark deduction.
> 6. **Wrong Reference Frame:** Writing the gravity vector in the wrong frame (e.g., link frame instead of base frame) without applying the rotation transformation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The potential energy of a robotic manipulator is the scalar function $P(q)$ obtained by summing the gravitational potential energies of all links.
- **Fundamental Formula:** $P(q) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}(q)$
- **Each link's PE:** $P_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{ci}$
- **CoM Position via Forward Kinematics:** $\mathbf{r}_{ci}(q) = \mathbf{T}_i^0(q) \, \mathbf{r}_{ci}^i$
- **Gravity Vector (Earth, z-up convention):** $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Gravity Torque Vector:** $\mathbf{g}(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$ — this is the input to the Euler-Lagrange equations.
- **Gravity Compensation:** Applying $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{g}(q)$ at the joints holds the manipulator stationary at any configuration without feedback.
- **Path Independence:** Since gravity is conservative, $P(q)$ depends only on the **final configuration**, not the path taken.
- **Superposition Principle:** Total PE = Sum of individual link PEs, because conservative forces add linearly.
- **Independence from Velocities:** $P(q)$ is **only a function of position** $q$ — it does not depend on $\dot{q}$ or $\ddot{q}$.
- **Planar Robot Tip:** For 2-D problems, only the component of gravity perpendicular to the support surface matters. Use $\mathbf{g} = [0, -g, 0]^T$ for vertical-plane manipulators.
- **Hybrid Joints:** For PR or RP robots, the first component of $\mathbf{g}(q)$ is a **force** (N) and the second is a **torque** (N·m) — handle units carefully.
- **Real-World Application:** Industrial robot controllers (KUKA, ABB) compute $P(q)$ and $\mathbf{g}(q)$ in real-time at every control cycle (typically 1 kHz) for gravity compensation.
- **Exam Favorite:** KTU frequently asks for $P(q)$ and $\mathbf{g}(q)$ of a 2-link planar manipulator — be ready to derive both from scratch in under 10 minutes.
> 
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equations of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations of Motion (EoM) for a Robotic Manipulator** are a set of coupled, second-order, non-linear ordinary differential equations that mathematically describe the dynamic behaviour of an n-degrees-of-freedom (n-DOF) serial-chain rigid-body robot in terms of its joint positions **q**, joint velocities **q̇**, and joint accelerations **q̈**, under the influence of applied joint torques **τ** and external forces.

The **generalized/standard manipulator dynamic model** is universally written in the matrix-vector form:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **symmetric, positive-definite joint-space inertia matrix** (also called mass matrix).
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **Coriolis and centrifugal matrix** containing centripetal and Coriolis terms.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **gravitational torque vector** arising from potential energy.
- $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **friction vector** (viscous + Coulomb).
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **generalized joint torque/force vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two classical derivations expected are **(a) Newton–Euler Formulation** (force-balance, recursive) and **(b) Euler–Lagrange Formulation** (energy-based, closed-form). The closed-form Lagrangian form is the most frequently asked derivation in University exams.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Acrobat on a Trampoline"

Imagine an acrobat standing on a trampoline holding several ropes attached to her arms and legs. Each rope is being pulled with a different force by an assistant. The acrobat's body accelerates, spins, and swings — but her motion is not just a function of the rope forces. It also depends on:

- **How heavy her limbs are** (and how that mass is distributed) → this is the **Inertia Matrix M(q)**. When she folds her arms, her rotational inertia changes, just as the inertia matrix of a robot changes with joint configuration.
- **How fast she is currently spinning** (the centripetal/Coriolis effects) → the **C(q,q̇)q̇** matrix. Even if no new force is applied, a spinning arm "wants" to fly outward and drags neighbouring joints along.
- **The pull of Earth's gravity on each of her body parts** → the **G(q)** vector. This changes depending on her posture (upright vs. upside-down).
- **Air resistance and joint friction** → the **F(q̇)** term.

The **Equation of Motion** simply says: *"The total torque/force you must apply at the joints = the torque needed to overcome inertia + the torque needed to overcome Coriolis/centrifugal effects + the torque needed to hold up against gravity + the torque lost to friction."* That single sentence is the entire content of the manipulator dynamic model.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit (SI) | Magnitude Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **g** | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** | Standard Earth gravity |
| **ρ** | Density of structural material | ~7850 kg/m³ (steel) | Used in link inertia |
| **n** | Degrees of freedom | dimensionless | 2–7 for industrial arms |
| **μᵥ** | Viscous friction coefficient | N·m·s/rad | ~0.1 – 10 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lagrangian Approach — Conceptual Walk-Through

The **Euler–Lagrange formulation** is built on **D'Alembert's principle of virtual work** and **Hamilton's principle of stationary action**. The procedure is:

1. **Choose generalized coordinates** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$ — these are the joint variables.
2. **Compute the Kinetic Energy T(q, q̇)** of the entire manipulator as a function of q and q̇.
3. **Compute the Potential Energy V(q)** of the entire manipulator (mainly due to gravity, sometimes elastic).
4. **Form the Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})$.
5. **Apply the Euler–Lagrange operator** to each generalized coordinate:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

6. **Expand and group** the resulting terms into $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

---

## 2.2 Why the Equation Has the Structure It Does — The "Why & How"

- **Inertia term M(q)q̈**: Arises because $\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ is a quadratic form in $\dot{q}_j$; differentiating it with respect to time brings out a $\ddot{q}_j$ term multiplied by a configuration-dependent coefficient — this coefficient is $M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})$.
- **Coriolis/centrifugal term C(q,q̇)q̇**: Arises from the **time-derivative of $M_{ij}$ multiplied by $\dot{q}_j$**, plus cross-product velocity terms. They represent fictitious forces observed in a rotating reference frame.
- **Gravity term G(q)**: Comes from $-\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$. Since V depends only on q (not q̇), no time derivative appears.
- **Why not a $\dot{M}$ term?**: It is hidden inside $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. A standard identity (Khalil's notation) lets us write:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{M}}(\mathbf{q}) - 2\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \mathbf{S}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})
$$

where $\mathbf{S}$ is a **skew-symmetric matrix** — this is the famous **passivity property** exploited in adaptive/robust control design.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — every KTU question on Module 2 reduces to one of them.**

| # | Equation / Identity | Meaning | Where It Appears |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ | Lagrangian definition | Start of derivation |
| 2 | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Scalar KE of the whole arm | Energy step |
| 3 | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \text{trace}\!\left(\frac{\partial {}^{k}\mathbf{T}_{0}}{\partial q_i} \mathbf{I}_k \frac{\partial ({}^{k}\mathbf{T}_{0})^{T}}{\partial q_j}\right)$ | Inertia matrix from Jacobians | Element-wise inertia |
| 4 | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big[ m_k (\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)})^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + (\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)})^{T} {}^{k}\mathbf{I}_{k} \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)} \big]$ | Jacobian-based inertia | Most useful form |
| 5 | $G_i = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (-m_k \mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)})$ | Gravity vector element | Gravity step |
| 6 | $c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Christoffel symbols | Coriolis derivation |
| 7 | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ | Coriolis matrix elements | C-matrix step |
| 8 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Final manipulator EoM | Final answer |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{S}$ (skew-symmetric) | Passivity property | Control-theory follow-up |
| 10 | $\mathbf{v}_{k} = \mathbf{J}_{v_k}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \boldsymbol{\omega}_{k} = \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Link velocities via Jacobians | KE computation |

> **Note on notation:** The symbol $m_k$ is the mass of link $k$; ${}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k$ is the inertia tensor of link $k$ about its own COM; $\mathbf{J}_{v_k}$ and $\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}$ are the linear and angular velocity Jacobians of the COM of link $k$; $\mathbf{g}$ is the gravity vector expressed in the base frame.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Trajectory tracking control** (Computed-Torque Control, Sliding-Mode Control) requires the *inverse dynamics* — i.e., given $\mathbf{q}(t)$, solve for $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ using the EoM.
- **Feed-forward + feedback** in industrial robots (ABB, KUKA, FANUC) uses the inverse dynamics block running at 1–8 kHz.
- **Simulation engines** (MATLAB Simscape, Gazebo, MuJoCo, Adams) integrate the forward dynamics $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G})$.
- **Structural design** of arms: bounds on $\mathbf{M}$ determine peak motor torques and structural loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Euler–Lagrange Equation of Motion (Scalar Form → Matrix Form)

We derive the manipulator equation from first principles. Let the manipulator have **n rigid links** in 3-D space, with generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, \dots, q_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

### Step 1 — Express the kinetic energy of link k

The COM of link $k$ has linear velocity $\mathbf{v}_k$ and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_k$. Using the Jacobian formulation:

$$
\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{J}_{v_k}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_k = \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}
$$

The total kinetic energy of link $k$ is:

$$
T_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m_k\,\mathbf{v}_k^{T}\mathbf{v}_k \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_k^{T}\,{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_k
$$

Substitute the Jacobian expressions:

$$
T_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m_k\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}\,{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Factor out $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ on both sides:

$$
T_k = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\Big[\,m_k\,\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k} \;+\; \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}\,{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\,\Big]\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

### Step 2 — Total kinetic energy of the manipulator

Summing over all n links:

$$
T = \sum_{k=1}^{n} T_k = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\!\left[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(m_k\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}\,{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\big)\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Define the **manipulator inertia matrix**:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n}\Big(m_k\,\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{J}_{v_k}(\mathbf{q}) \;+\; \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}(\mathbf{q})\Big)\;}
$$

Then the kinetic energy compactly is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

### Step 3 — Potential energy due to gravity

Let $\mathbf{p}_k(\mathbf{q})$ be the position of the COM of link $k$ expressed in the base frame. Then:

$$
V_k = m_k\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_k(\mathbf{q})
$$

Total:

$$
V = \sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_k(\mathbf{q})
$$

Note that V depends **only on q** (not on q̇). Differentiate with respect to $q_i$ to get the gravity term.

### Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \;=\; T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} - V(\mathbf{q})
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange operator

For each coordinate $q_i$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

**Compute the first term** — derivative with respect to $\dot{q}_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{q}_j
$$

Now differentiate with respect to time:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{q}_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j
$$

**Compute the second term** — derivative of $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $q_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k \;-\; \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}
$$

**Substitute** the two expressions into the Euler–Lagrange equation:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

### Step 6 — Isolate the Coriolis/centrifugal coefficient

Combine the two double-summation terms. By symmetry of $M_{jk}$ under exchange of $j \leftrightarrow k$, the two triple-indexed sums can be rewritten using **Christoffel symbols of the first kind**:

$$
c_{ijk} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$

After algebraic rearrangement (using the symmetry of M to combine terms), the i-th equation becomes:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{q}_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k \;+\; \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} \;=\; \tau_i
$$

Define the **Coriolis matrix element** $C_{ij}$:

$$
C_{ij}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{q}_k
$$

Define the **gravity vector element**:

$$
G_i(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}
$$

### Step 7 — Write in compact matrix form

Stacking all $i = 1, \dots, n$ equations into a single vector equation:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \boldsymbol{\tau}\;}
$$

This is the **standard manipulator equation of motion** — the central result of Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — 2-Revolute (RR) Planar Arm Numerical Worked Example

A 2-DOF planar RR arm with both links of length $L$, point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends, gravity $g$ acting downward. Find $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $\mathbf{G}$.

### Step 1 — Positions of the two masses

$$
\mathbf{p}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} L\cos q_1 \\ L\sin q_1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{p}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} L\cos q_1 + L\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ L\sin q_1 + L\sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Velocities (Jacobians)

$$
\dot{\mathbf{p}}_1 = L\dot{q}_1\begin{bmatrix} -\sin q_1 \\ \cos q_1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \dot{\mathbf{p}}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -L\dot{q}_1\sin q_1 - L(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L\dot{q}_1\cos q_1 + L(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The squared speed of mass 1:

$$
v_1^{2} = L^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2}
$$

The squared speed of mass 2 (after expansion and use of $\sin^{2}+\cos^{2}=1$):

$$
v_2^{2} = L^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + L^{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^{2} + 2L^{2}\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2
$$

### Step 3 — Kinetic energy

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}m_1 v_1^{2} + \frac{1}{2}m_2 v_2^{2}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}m_1 L^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + \frac{1}{2}m_2\big[L^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + L^{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^{2} + 2L^{2}\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2\big]
$$

Expand the bracket:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)L^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + \frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}(\dot{q}_1^{2}+2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2+\dot{q}_2^{2}) + m_2 L^{2}\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2
$$

Group terms in $\dot{q}_1^{2}, \dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2, \dot{q}_2^{2}$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\Big[(2m_2+m_1)L^{2} + 2m_2 L^{2}\cos q_2\Big]\dot{q}_1^{2} + m_2 L^{2}\cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}\dot{q}_2^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Read off the inertia matrix

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (2m_2+m_1)L^{2} + 2m_2 L^{2}\cos q_2 & m_2 L^{2}\cos q_2 \\ m_2 L^{2}\cos q_2 & m_2 L^{2} \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

Symmetric and positive definite ✓.

### Step 5 — Coriolis matrix via Christoffel symbols

Compute the partial derivatives of $M_{ij}$ with respect to $q_2$ (only $q_2$-dependent entries):

$$
\frac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2} = -2m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2, \quad \frac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2, \quad \frac{\partial M_{22}}{\partial q_2} = 0
$$

Now compute the four non-zero Christoffel symbols (with $q_k = q_2$, since other q's are absent):

$$
c_{112} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2
$$

$$
c_{122} = c_{212} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2} = -\frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2
$$

$$
C_{11} = c_{112}\dot{q}_2 = -m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2
$$

$$
C_{12} = c_{122}\dot{q}_2 = -\frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2
$$

$$
C_{21} = c_{212}\dot{q}_2 = -\frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{21}}{\partial q_1}\dot{q}_1 + \dots
$$

Since $M_{ij}$ depends only on $q_2$, all terms with $\partial/\partial q_1$ vanish. Final:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 & -m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 \\ m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

(after using $c_{211} = -c_{121} = \frac{1}{2}m_2 L^{2}\sin q_2$ and $C_{21} = c_{211}\dot{q}_1 + c_{212}\dot{q}_2$).

### Step 6 — Potential energy and gravity vector

$$
V = m_1 g L \sin q_1 + m_2 g \big[L\sin q_1 + L\sin(q_1+q_2)\big]
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_1} = (m_1+m_2) g L \cos q_1 + m_2 g L \cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_2} = m_2 g L \cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2) g L \cos q_1 + m_2 g L \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 g L \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Final assembled equation

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **complete equation of motion** for the 2R planar arm — a standard KTU problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Forward Dynamics (for Simulation)

To simulate the arm given the applied torques, rearrange the EoM:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\big[\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation integrated at every time step $\Delta t$ (RK4, Adams–Bashforth, etc.) in every robotics simulator. For the 2R arm above, $\mathbf{M}^{-1}$ can be computed analytically:

$$
\mathbf{M}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det \mathbf{M}} \begin{bmatrix} M_{22} & -M_{12} \\ -M_{21} & M_{11} \end{bmatrix}
$$

with $\det \mathbf{M} = L^{4}m_2\big[(2m_2+m_1) + 2m_2\cos^{2} q_2 - m_2\cos^{2} q_2\big] = L^{4}m_2\big[(2m_2+m_1) + m_2\cos^{2} q_2\big]$ — strictly positive, confirming positive-definiteness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Energies to Torques

This Mermaid diagram traces the **information flow** in the Euler–Lagrange derivation pipeline — what examiners often call the "**energy-to-torque block diagram**".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Generalized Coordinates q] --> B[Forward Kinematics + Jacobians]
    B --> C[Linear Velocity Jacobian Jv_k]
    B --> D[Angular Velocity Jacobian Jw_k]
    C --> E[Kinetic Energy T]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Inertia Matrix M of q]
    A --> G[Potential Energy V of q]
    G --> H[Gravity Vector G of q]
    F --> I[Coriolis Matrix C of q, q-dot]
    F --> J[Equation Assembly Block]
    I --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Generalized Torque tau]
    K --> L[Actuator Command to Joint i]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff

    style F fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style H fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style I fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style J fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[tau applied] --> SUM[Vector Summation Junction]
    MBLK[M of q times q-double-dot] --> SUM
    SUM --> QDD[Resolved q-double-dot]
    QDD --> INT[Integration Operator]
    INT --> QD[q-dot]
    QD --> INT2[Second Integration]
    INT2 --> Q[q]
    Q --> MBLK
    Q --> CBLK[C of q, q-dot times q-dot]
    Q --> GBLK[G of q]
    CBLK --> SUM
    GBLK --> SUM
    style IN1 fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff
    style SUM fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style MBLK fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff

    style GBLK fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff

    style INT fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style INT2 fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000

    style Q fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Pipeline Diagram — Lagrangian Derivation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Select generalized coordinates q] --> P2[Step 2: Write Jacobians Jv and Jw]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute KE of each link]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Sum for total T]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute total V of q]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Lagrangian L = T minus V]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Apply Euler-Lagrange operator]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Isolate Christoffel symbols]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Assemble M, C, G matrices]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Final EoM: M q-double-dot + C q-dot + G = tau]
    style P1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **The #1 reason students lose marks** is forgetting the **factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$** in the kinetic energy expression. A second common error is **mixing up $\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k$ indices** when computing Christoffel symbols. Always write out the indices explicitly on the exam paper.
> - **Do not skip the Jacobian step.** Examiners allocate **3 out of 14 marks** for correctly expressing $\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{J}_{v_k}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> - **State the symmetry and positive-definiteness of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$** at the end of the inertia matrix derivation — this is a "free 1-mark" that most students miss.
> - **Units matter.** Joint torque must be in N·m (revolute) or N (prismatic). Forgetting units loses 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the generalized coordinates of a robotic manipulator and write the standard form of its equation of motion. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Generalized coordinates of a robotic manipulator are the **minimum set of independent variables $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$** that completely specify the configuration of the manipulator with respect to the base frame, where n is the number of degrees of freedom. For a serial-chain rigid-body manipulator, these are the joint displacements (rotary or prismatic).

The standard form of the equation of motion is:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

where $\mathbf{M}$ is the symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix, $\mathbf{C}$ contains Coriolis and centripetal terms, $\mathbf{G}$ is the gravity vector, $\mathbf{F}$ is the friction vector, and $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the generalized joint torque vector.

---

### Q2. State and explain the physical significance of the inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ of a robotic manipulator. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ of a robotic manipulator is defined as the matrix that, when multiplied by the joint acceleration vector $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$, gives the inertial (Newtonian) joint torque required to accelerate the manipulator:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{inertial}} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Physical significance:**
1. **Configuration-dependent mass distribution**: $M_{ii}$ represents the effective inertia seen at joint $i$ when all other joints are locked. It varies with $\mathbf{q}$ because the spatial arrangement of the links changes.
2. **Coupling inertia**: $M_{ij}$ (for $i \neq j$) represents the coupling — accelerating joint $j$ alone produces a reaction torque on joint $i$.
3. **Properties**: $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric** ($M_{ij} = M_{ji}$) and **positive-definite** ($\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$). The positive-definiteness guarantees that the kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} > 0$ for any non-zero velocity.
4. **Boundedness**: For a rigid manipulator, there exist positive scalars $\mu_1, \mu_2$ such that $\mu_1 \mathbf{I} \leq \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \leq \mu_2 \mathbf{I}$ for all $\mathbf{q}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Derive the manipulator equation of motion using the Euler–Lagrange formulation, clearly identifying M(q), C(q,q̇), and G(q). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Starting from the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = T - V$, apply the Euler–Lagrange operator to each generalized coordinate and derive the **general matrix form** of the equation of motion. State clearly all assumptions and the **symmetry/positive-definiteness property** of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **2-DOF planar RR manipulator** with both links of length $L$, with point masses $m_1$ at the end of link 1 and $m_2$ at the end of link 2, operating in a vertical plane under gravity $g$, derive the explicit expressions for $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Express the KE of each link via Jacobians:**

For the $k$-th link with mass $m_k$ and inertia tensor ${}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k$ about its COM:

$$
T_k = \frac{1}{2}m_k\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total KE and definition of M(q):**

$$
T = \sum_{k=1}^{n}T_k = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\!\left[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(m_k\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\big)\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Defining $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ as the bracketed sum, we have $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

**Step 3 — Potential energy:**

$$
V(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{p}_k(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 4 — Lagrangian and Euler–Lagrange operator:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = T - V, \quad \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the partial derivatives explicitly:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j}M_{ij}\dot{q}_j \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j}M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j}\sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j}\sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k - \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine, introduce Christoffel symbols, isolate M, C, G:**

Substituting and using the identity that the double-sums collapse to $\sum_{j}\sum_{k}c_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$ with the Christoffel symbol:

$$
c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$

we get the final equation:

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{j=1}^{n}M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}C_{ij}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{q}_j + G_i(\mathbf{q}) = \tau_i\;}
$$

where $C_{ij} = \sum_{k}c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ and $G_i = \partial V/\partial q_i$.

**Assumptions:** (i) Rigid links, (ii) ideal joint geometry (no backlash), (iii) frictionless joints, (iv) no external load.

**Property of M(q):** Since $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \geq 0$ with equality only at $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{M}$ is **positive-definite**; symmetry follows from the Jacobian-based definition with $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 2R Planar Arm

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the full derivation. Final answers:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (2m_2+m_1)L^{2}+2m_2L^{2}\cos q_2 & m_2L^{2}\cos q_2 \\ m_2L^{2}\cos q_2 & m_2L^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 & -m_2L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 \\ m_2L^{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)gL\cos q_1 + m_2gL\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2gL\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Final assembled equation: $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

---

### Question B — (Alternative choice) — Newton–Euler formulation & dynamic properties. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** State the **Newton–Euler equations** for a single rigid body and explain how they are applied recursively (outward and inward iterations) to obtain the joint torques of a serial n-DOF manipulator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that for the manipulator equation $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, the matrix $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric** for the choice of $C_{ij}$ defined via the Christoffel symbols. State the **physical/control significance** of this property. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Newton–Euler equations for a single rigid body** (link $i$ with COM acceleration $\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{c_i}$ and angular acceleration $\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i$):

$$
\mathbf{f}_i = m_i\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{c_i}
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_i = {}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times ({}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)
$$

where $\mathbf{f}_i$ is the net force on the COM and $\boldsymbol{\tau}_i$ is the net moment about the COM.

**Recursive application to a serial manipulator:**

1. **Outward iteration (i = 1 to n):** Compute, for each link, the linear/angular velocity and acceleration propagated from the base. Use the joint variable $q_i, \dot{q}_i, \ddot{q}_i$ and the previous link's motion:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1})
$$

$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}\big[\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}\big]
$$

$$
\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{c_i} = \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i,c_i} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times \mathbf{r}_{i,c_i}) + \dot{\mathbf{v}}_i
$$

2. **Inward iteration (i = n to 1):** Compute forces and moments on each link starting from the end-effector. At the end-effector, an external wrench $\mathbf{f}_{n+1}, \boldsymbol{\tau}_{n+1}$ may be applied. Propagate inward:

$$
\mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i+1}\mathbf{f}_{i+1} + m_i\dot{\mathbf{v}}_{c_i}
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i+1}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{i+1} + \mathbf{r}_{i,c_i}\times\mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{r}_{i+1,c_i}\times(\mathbf{R}_i^{i+1}\mathbf{f}_{i+1}) + {}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times({}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)
$$

3. **Project onto the joint axis** to extract the scalar joint torque:

$$
\tau_i = \boldsymbol{\tau}_i^{T}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i-1}
$$

**Final result** is the same equation $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, but computed **recursively in O(n)** instead of symbolically in O(n⁴).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Skew-Symmetry of $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$

**Statement to prove:** For $C_{ij} = \sum_{k}c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ with $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\big(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i\big)$, the matrix $\mathbf{S} = \dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ satisfies $S_{ij} = -S_{ji}$, i.e., $\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{S}^{T} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Proof:**

Compute the (i,j) entry of $\dot{\mathbf{M}}$:

$$
\dot{M}_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k
$$

Compute the (i,j) entry of $2\mathbf{C}$:

$$
2C_{ij} = 2\sum_{k=1}^{n}c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)\dot{q}_k
$$

Therefore, the (i,j) entry of $\mathbf{S} = \dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is:

$$
S_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k - \sum_{k=1}^{n}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)\dot{q}_k
$$

Cancel the common first term:

$$
S_{ij} = -\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_k + \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_k
$$

Swap the dummy indices in the second sum: rename $k \to k$ in the first, $k \to k$ in the second — they already match. Now compute $S_{ji}$:

$$
S_{ji} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ji}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k - \sum_{k=1}^{n}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ji}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i} - \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\right)\dot{q}_k
$$

Cancel again:

$$
S_{ji} = -\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_k + \sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_k
$$

Comparing $S_{ij}$ and $S_{ji}$:

$$
S_{ij} = -\sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_k + \sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_k
$$

$$
S_{ji} = +\sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_k - \sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_k = -S_{ij}
$$

Therefore $S_{ij} + S_{ji} = 0$ for all $i, j$, proving $\mathbf{S}$ is **skew-symmetric**. ∎

**Control significance:**
- The skew-symmetry implies $\mathbf{x}^{T}(\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C})\mathbf{x} = 0$ for any $\mathbf{x}$, which means $\frac{d}{dt}T = \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ — i.e., the rate of change of kinetic energy equals the **input mechanical power** (no internal generation/dissipation of energy by the Coriolis terms).
- This is the **passivity property** of the manipulator, and it is exploited in the **Passivity-Based Control**, **Computed-Torque Control** stability proofs, and **adaptive control** Lyapunov design (the energy function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \text{PE term}$ is a valid Lyapunov candidate).
- It guarantees the absence of energy "leaks" through the Coriolis channel, which is crucial for proving closed-loop stability of model-based controllers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it on your desk during revision.**

- [ ] The **manipulator equation of motion** has exactly four terms: $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{F} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- [ ] $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric positive-definite**; element $M_{ij}$ is the inertia coupling between joints $i$ and $j$.
- [ ] $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\big(m_k\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{T}{}^{k}\mathbf{I}_k\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}\big)$ — **Jacobian form**, the most useful for derivation.
- [ ] The **Coriolis matrix elements** are obtained from Christoffel symbols: $C_{ij} = \sum_{k} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ with $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i)$.
- [ ] The **gravity vector** is $G_i = \partial V/\partial q_i = \sum_{k} m_k \mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}$.
- [ ] The skew-symmetry $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{S}$ with $\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{S}^{T} = \mathbf{0}$ holds **only for the Christoffel-based choice of C** — this is the *passivity property*.
- [ ] For a **2R planar arm** with point masses, the inertia matrix is a 2×2 with $(1,1)$-entry $=(2m_2+m_1)L^2 + 2m_2L^2\cos q_2$ and $(1,2)$-entry $= m_2L^2\cos q_2$.
- [ ] **Newton–Euler** is recursive (O(n)) and used in real-time control; **Euler–Lagrange** is symbolic (closed-form) and used for analysis and design.
- [ ] **Forward dynamics** (for simulation): $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})[\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}]$.
- [ ] **Inverse dynamics** (for control): $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ — feed-forward term in computed-torque control.
- [ ] Always state units: joint torques in **N·m**, joint forces in **N**, masses in **kg**, lengths in **m**, gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] **KTU exam tip**: Show all 10 steps of the Euler–Lagrange pipeline — examiners award marks per step, not just for the final boxed equation.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
