<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, *Lagrangian Multipliers* (often denoted by the Greek letter **λ**, lambda) are auxiliary scalar variables that are introduced to convert a **constrained optimization problem** into an equivalent **unconstrained problem**, or to incorporate **holonomic kinematic constraints** into the **Euler–Lagrange equations of motion** of a robotic manipulator.

Mathematically, given an objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ to be extremized subject to a vector of constraints $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$, the **augmented Lagrangian function** (also called the *Lagrangian functional* in this sense) is constructed as:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})
$$

Here, each component of the vector $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ is a **Lagrange multiplier** whose physical interpretation in robotics is the **generalized constraint force (or torque)** required to maintain the geometric constraint during motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The term *Lagrangian* in this topic refers to the **method of Lagrange multipliers** (a constrained optimization technique). This is **distinct from the Lagrangian function** $L = T - V$ used in the Euler–Lagrange formulation, although both share the same name due to the historical contributions of Joseph-Louis Lagrange.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a book across a **frictionless inclined table** with a specific slope. You want the book to reach a target point at a *minimum effort*. The unconstrained problem is simple — just push straight. But what if the book is **glued to a circular track** on the table (a constraint)? The book *must* remain on the circle. The force you apply must now do two jobs simultaneously:

1. Move the book along the circular path (the *useful* work).
2. Press the book radially inward to keep it on the curve (the *constraint force*).

The **Lagrange multiplier λ** is a mathematical placeholder that quantifies exactly **how hard the constraint is "pushing back"** on the system. It tells you, numerically, the magnitude of that invisible radial force keeping the book on the curve.

In a **robot arm with a closed-loop constraint** (such as a parallel manipulator, a four-bar linkage, or a hand grasping a rigid object), the same idea applies: the joint motors must provide motion-generating torques *plus* internal forces that maintain the kinematic closure. Those internal forces are precisely what **λ** represents.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional Footprint of λ**
> - In a translational mechanical system: $[\lambda] = \text{Newton (N)}$
> - In a rotational mechanical system: $[\lambda] = \text{Newton–meter (N·m)}$
> - In a general optimization problem: $\lambda$ carries the units of $\dfrac{[f]}{[g]}$, i.e., whatever units make the product $\lambda \cdot g$ dimensionally consistent with $f$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Constrained Optimization Problem — Logical Decomposition

The method of Lagrange multipliers is built on **four foundational steps**:

1. **Identify the Objective:** Define the scalar function $f(\mathbf{x})$ that must be maximized or minimized. In robotics, this might be joint torque norm, energy expenditure, path length, or tracking error.

2. **Identify the Constraints:** Express all equality constraints as $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$. In robot dynamics, these are typically:
   - Loop-closure equations of parallel mechanisms.
   - Fixed contact-point conditions during grasping.
   - Prescribed end-effector trajectory constraints (e.g., $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q}, t) = \mathbf{0}$).

3. **Form the Augmented Lagrangian:** Construct $\mathcal{L} = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$, treating both $\mathbf{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ as independent variables.

4. **Apply Stationarity Conditions:** The first-order necessary condition for an extremum is:
   $$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}$$
   The second condition merely recovers the original constraint (a self-consistency check).

## 2.2 The Underlying Geometric "Why"

Geometrically, at the extremum, the **gradient of the objective function** must be **parallel (anti-parallel)** to the **gradient of the constraint surface**. If they were not parallel, you could slide along the constraint surface to improve the objective. The multiplier $\lambda$ is the *scalar proportionality factor* that enforces this parallelism:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -\lambda \, \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works in Robot Dynamics:** The constraint forces in a holonomic system perform **zero virtual work** (D'Alembert's principle). Therefore, when we write the Euler–Lagrange equations for a constrained system, the constraint forces do not appear in the scalar Lagrangian $L = T - V$, but they do appear in the generalized force vector. Multiplying each constraint by a multiplier $\lambda_j$ and adding it to the Lagrangian is the mathematical trick that lets us treat constraint forces as if they were *derivable from a potential*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Name / Context** | **Units** |
|:-----:|:-----------------------|:-------------------|:---------:|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$ | Augmented Lagrangian (scalar form) | Same as $f$ |
| 2 | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L} = \nabla f + \mathbf{J}_{g}^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}$ | Primal stationarity | Variable |
| 3 | $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Dual stationarity (constraint recovery) | Constraint |
| 4 | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = -\lambda \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^{*})$ | Geometric parallelism condition | Variable |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}} = \tau_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_{j} a_{ij}$ | Constrained Euler–Lagrange (robot form) | N·m |
| 6 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ | Manipulator equation with constraints | N·m |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = \left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\right)^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right)$ | Explicit solution for $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ | Variable |
| 8 | $L^{*} = f(\mathbf{x}^{*})$ | Optimal objective value | Same as $f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q}) = \partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{q}$ is the **constraint Jacobian**, and $a_{ij}$ denotes the $(i,j)$ element of this matrix.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Parallel Manipulators** (Stewart platform, Delta robot): The closed-loop kinematics yield constraint equations; $\lambda$ gives the leg compression/tension forces.
- **Multi-finger Grasping:** Contact constraints between fingertips and object are enforced via $\lambda$, yielding feasible grasp forces.
- **Bipedal Walking Robots:** The foot–ground no-slip and no-lift conditions are holonomic constraints enforced with multipliers.
- **Trajectory Optimization** (e.g., minimum-snap quadrotor paths): Boundary and waypoint constraints are written as $\mathbf{g}=\mathbf{0}$ with $\lambda$ as KKT multipliers.
- **Cable-Driven Robots:** Cable-length constraints and unilateral tension limits are handled through complementarity conditions that generalize the multiplier idea.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Standard Method of Lagrange Multipliers (Scalar Case)

**Statement:** Find the extremum of $f(x, y)$ subject to $g(x, y) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the augmented Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = f(x, y) + \lambda\, g(x, y)
$$

*Logic:* We adjoin the constraint to the objective, weighted by the unknown scalar $\lambda$. This is purely a *bookkeeping* device; the value of $\lambda$ will be solved for alongside $x$ and $y$.

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives with respect to each variable.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial y} = 0
$$

*Logic:* At a stationary point, infinitesimal changes in $x$ or $y$ (while obeying the constraint) must not change $f$ to first order.

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint itself (dual stationarity).**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = g(x, y) = 0
$$

*Logic:* Recovering the constraint verifies that the solution lies on the feasible surface.

**Step 4 — Solve the resulting 3 × 3 nonlinear system.**

The system $\{\partial f/\partial x + \lambda \partial g/\partial x = 0,\ \partial f/\partial y + \lambda \partial g/\partial y = 0,\ g(x,y) = 0\}$ yields the candidate extremum $(x^*, y^*, \lambda^*)$.

**Geometric Interpretation Step 5 — Show parallelism.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{*} + \lambda^{*} \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg|_{*} &= 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{*} = -\lambda^{*} \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg|_{*} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg|_{*} + \lambda^{*} \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\bigg|_{*} &= 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg|_{*} = -\lambda^{*} \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\bigg|_{*}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stacking the two scalar equations into a single vector identity:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = -\lambda^{*} \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^{*})
$$

This is the **parallel-gradient theorem** of Lagrange.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Lagrange Multipliers in the Euler–Lagrange Robot Dynamics Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider an $n$-DOF manipulator with generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, subject to $m$ holonomic constraints:

$$
\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{h}: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}, \quad m < n
$$

**Step 1 — Write the unconstrained Lagrangian and the principle of virtual work.**

The unconstrained dynamics is

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}} = \tau_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_{j} a_{ij}
$$

where $a_{ij} = \partial h_{j}/\partial q_{i}$ and $\tau_i$ is the applied generalized force.

*Logic:* D'Alembert's principle states that the virtual work of the constraint forces vanishes for all virtual displacements $\delta q_i$ that respect the constraints. The multipliers $\lambda_j$ are introduced precisely so that the constraint forces **vanish from the scalar variational principle** but are recovered as explicit unknowns.

**Step 2 — Differentiate the constraint to obtain a velocity relation.**

Differentiating $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$ with respect to time:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{h}}{dt} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Differentiating once more (for acceleration-level constraint):

$$
\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{A}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}
$$

*Logic:* This is the **acceleration constraint** that the joint accelerations must obey.

**Step 3 — Stack all $n$ Euler–Lagrange equations into matrix form.**

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\boldsymbol{\lambda}
$$

*Logic:* The right-hand side separates the **actuator torques** $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ (which we control) from the **constraint reaction forces** $\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ (which we usually wish to compute or limit).

**Step 4 — Couple with the acceleration constraint to solve for $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$.**

We have two coupled equations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} &= \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda} \qquad \text{(dynamic eq.)} \\
\mathbf{A}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} &= \mathbf{0} \qquad\qquad\qquad\quad\ \text{(acceleration constraint)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solve the dynamic equation for $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$:
$$
\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}\right)
$$

Substitute into the acceleration constraint:
$$
\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}\right) + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$.**

Expand and collect the $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ terms on the left:
$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}\right) + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Move all non-$\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ terms to the right:
$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda} = -\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G}\right) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Therefore the **closed-form expression for the Lagrange multipliers** is:
$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\lambda} = \left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\right)^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} - \boldsymbol{\tau}\right) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right)\;}
$$

*Logic:* The matrix $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}$ is an $m \times m$ symmetric positive-definite matrix (assuming independent constraints and an invertible mass matrix), so its inverse always exists for a well-posed constrained system.

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation of $\lambda_k$.**

The $k$-th component $\lambda_k$ equals the **generalized force of constraint** associated with the $k$-th constraint equation. For a parallel manipulator with $m$ legs closing $m$ loop equations, $\lambda_k$ is the **axial force in the $k$-th leg**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Pedagogical)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$ subject to $x + y = 4$.

**Step 1 — Identify constraint:** $g(x,y) = x + y - 4 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Augmented Lagrangian:**
$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + \lambda(x + y - 4)
$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivatives:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} &= 2x + \lambda = 0 \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} &= 2y + \lambda = 0 \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} &= x + y - 4 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve.** From the first two equations, $2x + \lambda = 2y + \lambda \Rightarrow x = y$. Combined with the constraint, $2x = 4 \Rightarrow x = y = 2$. Then $\lambda = -2(2) = -4$.

**Step 5 — Verify:** $f(2, 2) = 4 + 4 = 8$. Geometrically, the point $(2,2)$ is on the line $x+y=4$ closest to the origin, distance $\sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$, so the minimum of the squared distance is $8$. The negative sign of $\lambda$ indicates the constraint "pulls" the optimum toward the line from inside the parabola.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Lagrange Multiplier Method

The diagram below traces the complete logical pipeline from problem statement to the recovered physical interpretation of the multipliers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define objective function f of x] --> B[Identify constraints g of x equals zero]
    B --> C[Form augmented Lagrangian L equals f plus lambda transpose g]
    C --> D[Compute gradient of L with respect to x equals zero]
    C --> E[Compute gradient of L with respect to lambda equals zero]
    D --> F[Solve coupled nonlinear system for x star and lambda star]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Verify second order sufficiency}
    G -->|Yes| H[Report optimum x star and physical meaning of lambda star]
    G -->|No| I[Search alternative stationary candidates]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Interpret lambda as generalized constraint force or KKT shadow price]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Lagrange Multipliers in Constrained Robot Dynamics

The diagram below illustrates how the multipliers enter the standard manipulator dynamic equation and how they are computed in real time within a control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inputs]
        Q1[Generalized coordinates q]
        Q2[Generalized velocities q dot]
        Q3[Actuator torques tau]
    end

    subgraph MASS[Mass Matrix Block]
        M1[Compute M of q]
        M2[Compute C of q and q dot]
        M3[Compute G of q]
    end

    subgraph CONSTR[Constraint Block]
        C1[Constraint function h of q]
        C2[Constraint Jacobian A of q]
        C3[Time derivative A dot]
    end

    subgraph LAMBDA[Lagrange Multiplier Solver]
        L1[Form matrix A M inverse A transpose]
        L2[Compute lambda via explicit formula]
        L3[Output lambda vector]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs]
        O1[Constraint forces A transpose lambda]
        O2[Free dynamics residual tau minus A transpose lambda]
    end

    Q1 --> M1
    Q1 --> M2
    Q1 --> M3
    Q1 --> C1
    Q1 --> C2
    Q2 --> C3
    Q3 --> O2
    M1 --> L1
    C2 --> L1
    M2 --> L2
    M3 --> L2
    Q3 --> L2
    C3 --> L2
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    L3 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Constrained Euler–Lagrange Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Robot joint motion q of t] --> P2[Compute Lagrangian L equals T minus V]
    P2 --> P3[Form Euler Lagrange operator d by dt of partial L by partial q dot minus partial L by partial q]
    P3 --> P4[Add applied torques tau]
    P3 --> P5[Add constraint reaction term A transpose lambda]
    P4 --> P6[Equate to zero for closed form]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Solve coupled system for q double dot and lambda]
    P7 --> P8[Integrate to update q and q dot]
    P8 --> P1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the method of Lagrange multipliers. What is the physical significance of the multiplier $\lambda$ in the context of constrained robot dynamics?**

**Model Answer:**

The method of Lagrange multipliers is a strategy for finding the extremum of a scalar objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to equality constraints $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$. The constrained problem is converted into an unconstrained one by augmenting the objective:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})
$$

Stationarity requires $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Physical significance in robot dynamics:** Each multiplier $\lambda_j$ represents the **generalized constraint force (or torque)** that maintains the $j$-th holonomic constraint. In a closed-loop manipulator it is the internal axial force in the connecting leg; in grasping it is the contact normal force; in trajectory optimization it acts as a *KKT shadow price* measuring sensitivity of the optimum to constraint relaxation. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the "Lagrangian function" $L = T - V$ and the "augmented Lagrangian" $\mathcal{L} = f + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{g}$ used with Lagrange multipliers. Why do they share the same name?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lagrangian $L = T - V$ | Augmented Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = f + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{g}$ |
|:-------|:-----------------------|:----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Domain | Energy-based formulation of dynamics | Constrained optimization of any objective |
| Variables | $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, t$ | $\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}$ |
| Purpose | Derive equations of motion via calculus of variations | Find stationary points of $f$ on the constraint surface |
| Resulting unknowns | Trajectories $\mathbf{q}(t)$ | Decision vector $\mathbf{x}^*$ and multipliers $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$ |

Both are named after **Joseph-Louis Lagrange**, who independently developed the calculus-of-variations framework (1760s) and the multiplier method (1788, *Mécanique Analytique*). The shared nomenclature is a historical artefact, not a mathematical equivalence. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Module 2 carries internal choice. **Attempt either Question A or Question B in full.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of the Euler–Lagrange formulation, derive the equation of motion of a robotic manipulator subjected to $m$ holonomic constraints. Clearly bring out the role of Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_j$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator (links of length $l_1, l_2$ and point masses $m_1, m_2$) with the end-effector constrained to move along a horizontal line $x = d$ (a holonomic constraint), obtain the constraint equation, the constraint Jacobian $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})$, and the expression for the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ in terms of joint torques, joint angles, and link inertias. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Constrained Euler–Lagrange Derivation

**Step 1 — Assume the unconstrained Lagrangian** $L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})$ for an $n$-DOF system.

**Step 2 — Introduce $m$ holonomic constraints** in Pfaffian form: $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

**Step 3 — Apply D'Alembert's principle** (principle of virtual work for inertial forces):
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left[\tau_{i} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right) + \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_{i}} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_{i}}\right]\delta q_{i} = 0
$$
subject to $\delta \mathbf{q}$ being consistent with the constraints: $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{q})\delta\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{0}$, where $a_{ij} = \partial h_j/\partial q_i$.

**Step 4 — Introduce the multipliers** $\lambda_j$ to relax the consistency requirement, allowing all $\delta q_i$ to be independent. This yields:
$$
\tau_{i} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right) + \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_{j} a_{ij} = 0
$$
or equivalently
$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_{i}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_{i}} = \tau_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_{j} a_{ij} \quad (i = 1, \dots, n)
$$

**Step 5 — Stack in matrix form:**
$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\boldsymbol{\lambda}
$$
Coupled with the acceleration constraint $\mathbf{A}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$, the system of $(n+m)$ equations in $(n+m)$ unknowns $(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}, \boldsymbol{\lambda})$ is solved uniquely. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 2-DOF Manipulator with Horizontal-Line Constraint

**Step 1 — Forward kinematics of the end-effector:**

$$
x = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1} + q_{2}), \quad y = l_{1}\sin q_{1} + l_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2})
$$

**Step 2 — Impose the constraint** $x = d$:

$$
h(q_1, q_2) = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1} + q_{2}) - d = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the constraint Jacobian** $\mathbf{A} = \partial h/\partial \mathbf{q}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{11} &= \frac{\partial h}{\partial q_{1}} = -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2}) \\
a_{12} &= \frac{\partial h}{\partial q_{2}} = -l_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2}) & -l_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2}) \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 4 — Mass matrix** (point masses at link ends):

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} (m_{1}+m_{2})l_{1}^{2} + m_{2}l_{2}^{2} + 2m_{2}l_{1}l_{2}\cos q_{2} & m_{2}l_{2}^{2} + m_{2}l_{1}l_{2}\cos q_{2} \\ m_{2}l_{2}^{2} + m_{2}l_{1}l_{2}\cos q_{2} & m_{2}l_{2}^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Centrifugal/Coriolis vector** $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ (G = 0 for horizontal motion):

$$
\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -m_{2}l_{1}l_{2}(2\dot{q}_{1}\dot{q}_{2} + \dot{q}_{2}^{2})\sin q_{2} \\ m_{2}l_{1}l_{2}\dot{q}_{1}^{2}\sin q_{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the multiplier** (with $m = 1$ scalar constraint):

$$
\lambda = \frac{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \boldsymbol{\tau}) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}}{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}}
$$

Here $\lambda$ is the **horizontal contact force** the wall must exert on the end-effector to keep it on the line $x = d$ — a single scalar with units of Newtons. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Using the method of Lagrange multipliers, find the minimum value of $f(x, y, z) = x^{2} + 2y^{2} + 3z^{2}$ subject to the constraint $2x - y + 3z = 10$. Interpret the sign and magnitude of the multiplier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF RR planar manipulator, derive the explicit formula for the Lagrange multiplier when a closed kinematic loop is enforced by the constraint $l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) - l_3 = 0$, where $l_3$ is a fixed coupler length. Show that $\lambda$ corresponds to the tension/compression in the coupler. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Constrained Quadratic Minimization

**Step 1 — Form the augmented Lagrangian:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, z, \lambda) = x^{2} + 2y^{2} + 3z^{2} + \lambda(2x - y + 3z - 10)
$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} &= 2x + 2\lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = -\lambda \\[2pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} &= 4y - \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \lambda/4 \\[2pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} &= 6z + 3\lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = -\lambda/2 \\[2pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} &= 2x - y + 3z - 10 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the constraint:**

$$
2(-\lambda) - \frac{\lambda}{4} + 3\!\left(-\frac{\lambda}{2}\right) = 10
$$

$$
-\lambda\!\left(2 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{2}\right) = 10
$$

$$
-\lambda\!\left(\frac{8 + 1 + 6}{4}\right) = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; -\frac{15\lambda}{4} = 10
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = -\frac{8}{3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Recover the optimal point and objective:**

$$
x = \frac{8}{3},\quad y = -\frac{2}{3},\quad z = \frac{4}{3}
$$

$$
f_{\min} = \left(\frac{8}{3}\right)^{2} + 2\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} + 3\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{64}{9} + \frac{8}{9} + \frac{48}{9} = \frac{120}{9} = \frac{40}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of $\lambda = -8/3$:** The negative sign indicates that the constraint $2x - y + 3z = 10$ is *active* in the direction opposite to its normal vector at the optimum; the magnitude $8/3$ measures the rate at which $f_{\min}$ would decrease if the right-hand side of the constraint were relaxed by one unit (KKT shadow price). **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — RR Manipulator with Fixed Coupler

**Step 1 — Constraint equation:**

$$
h(q_1, q_2) = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1} + q_{2}) - l_{3} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Constraint Jacobian:**

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Time derivative of $\mathbf{A}$:**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{A}} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{1}\dot{q}_{1}\cos q_{1} - l_{2}(\dot{q}_{1}+\dot{q}_{2})\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -l_{2}(\dot{q}_{1}+\dot{q}_{2})\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard multiplier formula:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} - \boldsymbol{\tau}) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}}{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:** The virtual work principle shows that the constraint force along the coupler direction has magnitude $\vert \lambda \vert$. A positive $\lambda$ corresponds to **tension** (the coupler pulls the joints inward), a negative $\lambda$ to **compression** (the coupler pushes the joints outward). In a four-bar mechanism, this is exactly the axial force the coupler link must transmit. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Lagrange Multiplier Problems**
> 1. **Confusing the two Lagrangians:** Students frequently write $L = T - V$ and $\mathcal{L} = f + \lambda g$ interchangeably. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if the distinction is not made explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting the dual stationarity condition:** Many students write only $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x = 0$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial y = 0$, omitting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \lambda = g(x,y) = 0$. This is a complete-answer requirement.
> 3. **Sign errors in the constraint Jacobian:** The Jacobian is $a_{ij} = \partial h_j/\partial q_i$. A sign slip here propagates into the wrong sign of $\lambda$, which examiners check explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting $\dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ in the explicit formula:** The acceleration constraint is $\mathbf{A}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$, not $\mathbf{A}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$. Dropping the $\dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ term is a frequent error worth 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Dimensionally inconsistent $\lambda$:** Always verify $[\lambda] \cdot [g] = [f]$. If the units do not match, the augmented Lagrangian is wrong.
> 6. **No second-order check:** KTU examiners occasionally award a bonus mark for a brief mention of the bordered-Hessian or KKT second-order sufficiency condition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lagrange multipliers convert constrained problems into unconstrained ones** by adjoining each constraint $g_j = 0$ to the objective $f$ with an unknown scalar $\lambda_j$.
- **Stationarity requires three conditions:** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$, $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}$, and (in dynamics) the *coupled* acceleration constraint $\mathbf{A}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Geometric meaning:** $\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla g$ — gradients of objective and active constraint are parallel (or anti-parallel) at the optimum.
- **Physical meaning in robotics:** $\lambda_j$ is the **generalized constraint force/torque** maintaining the $j$-th holonomic constraint; in a closed-chain mechanism it is the axial force in the connecting leg; in grasping it is the contact normal force; in optimization it is the **KKT shadow price**.
- **Standard manipulator dynamic equation with constraints:**
  $$\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{\lambda}$$
- **Explicit solution for $\lambda$:**
  $$\boldsymbol{\lambda} = \left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}\right)^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\!\left(\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} - \boldsymbol{\tau}\right) - \dot{\mathbf{A}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right)$$
- **Two different "Lagrangians":** $L = T - V$ (dynamics) versus $\mathcal{L} = f + \lambda g$ (constrained optimization). Both are named after Lagrange but serve different purposes.
- **Units of $\lambda$:** Determined by dimensional consistency — $\lambda g$ must share units with $f$ (or $T - V$).
- **Existence of $\lambda$:** The matrix $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}$ must be non-singular, which holds when the $m$ constraints are *independent* and the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}$ is positive definite.
- **Distinguish holonomic vs non-holonomic:** The multiplier technique applies *directly* to holonomic (position-level) constraints; non-holonomic (velocity-level) constraints are usually treated via **Maggi's formulation** or the **Appell–Hamel approach**, which KTU Module 2 may briefly contrast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrange's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lagrange's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lagrange's Equation (Euler-Lagrange Equation):** A scalar, energy-based formulation used to derive the equations of motion of a multi-degree-of-freedom mechanical system directly in terms of a chosen set of **generalized coordinates** $q_i$, the system's **kinetic energy** $T$, and its **potential energy** $V$.

For an $n$-DOF robotic manipulator, the scalar form of Lagrange's equation is written as:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

where:
- $L = T - V$ is the **Lagrangian** of the system.
- $q_i$ is the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate (joint variable).
- $\dot{q}_i$ is its time derivative (joint velocity).
- $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force** (joint torque/force) associated with $q_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In robot dynamics, the vector-matrix form of Lagrange's equation directly yields the **manipulator dynamic model**:
> $$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$
> This is a **board-exam favorite** (worth 7-14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a roller-coaster track. You do not need Newton's laws and contact forces at every wheel to predict the car's motion — you only need its **total kinetic energy** (motion) and **total potential energy** (height). If you subtract them and apply a simple "rule of least action," the entire motion of the coaster pops out automatically.

Lagrange's equation does **exactly that for robots**:
- Forget every internal joint force, gear friction, or link interaction.
- Just compute **how much energy the robot stores** (kinetic) and **how much gravitational energy it has** (potential).
- Feed that into one elegant equation, and the joint accelerations $\ddot{q}_i$ — and hence the torques required to drive the robot — fall out automatically.

It is, in essence, the **"energy bookkeeping" approach** to robot dynamics.

## 1.3 Why Lagrange's Formulation Over Newton-Euler?

> [!TIP]
> - **Newton-Euler**: Force/torque balance at every link → recursive, fast for simulation, but messy symbolically.
> - **Lagrange-Euler**: Energy-based, scalar-by-scalar → clean symbolic form, ideal for deriving compact dynamic models and designing model-based controllers (e.g., computed-torque control).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Prerequisites (KTU 2024 Expected Background)

Before applying Lagrange's equation, the student **must** be able to:
1. Express the **position and velocity of every link's center of mass** in terms of joint variables $q_i$.
2. Compute the **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}_i$ of each link's center of mass.
3. Recall the scalar kinetic energy of a rigid body:
   $$T_i = \frac{1}{2}\,m_i\, \mathbf{v}_{c_i}^T \mathbf{v}_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\, \boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$$
4. Recall that potential energy for gravity is $V_i = m_i\, g\, h_{c_i}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure (The "Lagrange Recipe")

The KTU 2024 examiner rewards students who follow a **structured, repeating procedure** rather than ad-hoc derivations. Use this 5-step recipe for every problem:

1. **Select generalized coordinates** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ (joint variables for serial manipulators).
2. **Write the total kinetic energy** $T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ and put it in **quadratic form**:
   $$T = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$
3. **Write the total potential energy** $V(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$ as a function of $\mathbf{q}$ only (no velocities).
4. **Form the Lagrangian** $L = T - V$.
5. **Apply the Euler-Lagrange operator** to each $q_i$:
   $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

## 2.3 Identification of the Dynamic Sub-Matrices

After expanding the scalar equations, the result is **always** rearranged into canonical robot form. The three KTU-mandatory matrices are:

- **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — Mass/Inertia Matrix** (symmetric, positive definite, $n \times n$):
  $$M_{ij}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left[ m_k\, \left(\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}\right)^T \mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + \left(\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)}\right)^T \mathbf{I}_k\, \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)} \right]$$
- **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — Coriolis & Centrifugal Matrix** (not unique; $n \times n$):
  $$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i}\right)\dot{q}_k$$
  (This is the **Christoffel symbol** form, the KTU-preferred representation.)
- **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — Gravity Vector** ($n \times 1$):
  $$G_i(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Pitfall:** Many students write $G_i = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$. The sign convention is **$+ \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$** when $V$ is the gravitational potential energy measured **upward** from a reference. Always check your derivation direction by testing a simple 1-DOF pendulum.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Remarks / Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Lagrangian definition | $L = T - V$ | Scalar energy difference (Joules) |
| 2 | Euler-Lagrange equation (scalar) | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | $i = 1,\dots,n$ (one per DOF) |
| 3 | Euler-Lagrange (vector) | $\frac{d}{dt}\nabla_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}}L - \nabla_{\mathbf{q}}L = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Compact vector form |
| 4 | Quadratic KE form | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Defines M-matrix |
| 5 | Mass matrix element | $M_{ij} = \sum_k \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{T}_k}{\partial q_i} \mathbf{I}_k \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}_k^T}{\partial q_j}\right)$ | Alternative trace form |
| 6 | Coriolis (Christoffel) | $C_{ij} = \sum_k \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial_k M_{ij} + \partial_j M_{ik} - \partial_i M_{kj}\right)\dot{q}_k$ | Where $\partial_k \equiv \frac{\partial}{\partial q_k}$ |
| 7 | Gravity vector | $G_i = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | $V = \sum m_i g h_{c_i}$ |
| 8 | Manipulator equation | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Canonical robot form |
| 9 | Skew-symmetry property | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric | Used in passivity proofs |
| 10 | Generalized force | $\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Equivalent expanded form |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Lagrange's Formulation |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Industrial Robotics** | Model-based control (computed-torque, adaptive control) of 6-DOF arms in assembly lines. |
| **Space Robotics** | Dynamic modeling of free-floating manipulators for satellite servicing. |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Equations of motion for 20+ DOF bipeds (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas). |
| **Vehicle Dynamics** | Suspension modeling, car chassis analysis. |
| **Biomechanics** | Human gait analysis, prosthesis design. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The General Vector-Matrix Expansion (Rigorous Derivation)

We now expand the scalar Euler-Lagrange equation in full to show **exactly** how the manipulator equation emerges. This is the derivation that earns full 14-mark credit in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Start with the Lagrangian

We begin with $L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})$, where $T$ is in quadratic form:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{q}_i\, \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k}$

Since $V$ does not depend on $\dot{q}_k$, only $T$ contributes. Differentiating the quadratic form:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\left(\delta_{ik}\dot{q}_j + \dot{q}_i \delta_{jk}\right)
$$

Using Kronecker-delta substitution $\delta_{ik}$ (which is 1 when $i=k$, else 0):

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{ik}\dot{q}_i
$$

Because $M_{kj} = M_{jk}$ (symmetry of mass matrix), the two sums are equal:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the total time derivative

Applying the chain rule $\frac{d}{dt} = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\ddot{q}_j$, we get:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j
$$

The first term is $\sum_j M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j$ (the linear-in-acceleration term). The second term contains products of velocities.

### Step 4 — Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k}$

Since $\dot{q}_i$ are independent of $q_k$ (generalized coordinates and their velocities are independent variables), and $V$ is independent of $\dot{q}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} - \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k}
$$

The potential term gives the gravity vector directly:
$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k} = G_k(\mathbf{q})
$$

The kinetic term is:
$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Euler-Lagrange equation

Substituting Steps 3 and 4 into $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k} = \tau_k$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j + G_k = \tau_k
$$

### Step 6 — Index gymnastics to extract the Coriolis matrix

The two double-sum velocity terms must be combined and re-indexed. Use the symmetry trick: relabel $i \leftrightarrow m$ in the first double sum:

$$
\sum_{j}\sum_{m} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

Now subtract the third term. The coefficient of $\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}
$$

By cyclic index relabeling (swap $i \leftrightarrow j$ in half the term) and using $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$, this can be written as:

$$
\sum_{i,j} \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 7 — Define the Christoffel symbol $C_{kj}$

$$
C_{kj}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

Notice that $C_{kj}$ is **linear in $\dot{q}$** (since one $\dot{q}$ remains free per double sum). Therefore the entire velocity-product term is $\sum_j C_{kj}\dot{q}_j$.

### Step 8 — Final canonical form

The complete vector-matrix manipulator equation is:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}}
$$

This is the **master equation** that every KTU board question on this topic ultimately reduces to.

## 3.2 Worked Mini-Derivation — 2R Planar Manipulator (Scaffolding)

To give you a complete worked template (which the examiner expects on a 14-mark problem), here is the **skeleton** for a planar 2R arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, and inertias $I_1, I_2$.

**Position of link 1's center of mass:**
$$
x_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1, \qquad y_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1
$$

**Position of link 2's center of mass:**
$$
x_{c_2} = l_1 \cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \qquad y_{c_2} = l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Velocities** are obtained by time-differentiation (e.g., $\dot{x}_{c_1} = -\tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1$, etc.). The **Jacobians** $\mathbf{J}_{v_1}, \mathbf{J}_{v_2}$ of the two centers of mass are then:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 & 0 \\[4pt] \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) & -\frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt] l_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) & \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Mass matrix** (assembled by $M_{ij} = \sum_k \left[m_k \mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)\,T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)\,T}\mathbf{I}_k \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)}\right]$):

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where

$$
M_{11} = \left(\frac{m_1}{4} + m_2\right)l_1^2 + m_2\,l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_1
$$

$$
M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + \frac{m_2\,l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2
$$

$$
M_{22} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_2
$$

**Gravity vector** (with gravity $g$ acting downward, $V = m_1 g \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g\left[l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]$):

$$
G_1 = \left(\frac{m_1 l_1}{2} + m_2 l_1\right) g \cos q_1 + \frac{m_2 l_2}{2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
G_2 = \frac{m_2 l_2}{2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Coriolis matrix** (from Christoffel symbols):

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 & -\frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 \\[4pt] \frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark question, the examiner will give you a specific manipulator and expect you to **explicitly show** all five steps: choose coordinates, write $T$, write $V$, form $L$, then take derivatives. Do not skip the Lagrangian stage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concept to Operation

| KTU Concept | Mathematical Tool | Output Symbol | Matrix/Vector Size |
|-------------|-------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| Generalized coordinates | Independent variables | $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n \times 1$ |
| Lagrangian | Scalar function | $L = T - V$ | $1 \times 1$ (scalar) |
| Euler-Lagrange operator | Differential operator | $\mathcal{L}_i \equiv \frac{d}{dt}\partial_{\dot{q}_i} - \partial_{q_i}$ | Scalar per DOF |
| Generalized force | Energy conjugate | $\tau_i$ | $n \times 1$ |
| Inertia effect | Quadratic form in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times n$ |
| Velocity coupling | Bilinear in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | $n \times n$ |
| Gravity loading | Gradient of $V$ | $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Applying Lagrange's Equation to a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Define generalized coordinates q1 q2 ... qn] --> B[STEP 2: Locate every link center of mass in terms of q]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Compute linear and angular Jacobians Jv Jw]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Assemble total kinetic energy T half qdotT M q qdot]
    C --> E[STEP 4b: Assemble total potential energy V from m g h]
    D --> F[STEP 5: Form Lagrangian L equals T minus V]
    E --> F
    F --> G[STEP 6: Apply Euler Lagrange operator to each qi]
    G --> H[STEP 7: Expand and identify M q C q qdot G q]
    H --> I[STEP 8: Write canonical manipulator equation M q qddot plus C q qdot qdot plus G q equals tau]
    I --> J[OUTPUT: Dynamic model ready for control synthesis]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style I fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Lagrange-Based Controller Pipeline

flowchart LR

        QD[Desired Trajectory qd qdotd qdddotd]
        Q[Measured State q qdot]
    end

        SENSOR[Encoder / IMU Feedback]
    end

    QD --> CALC
    MBLOCK --> CALC

    GBLOCK --> CALC
    CALC --> ROBOT
    ROBOT --> SENSOR
    SENSOR --> Q
    Q --> MBLOCK
    Q --> CBLOCK
    Q --> GBLOCK

    D --> G[G q]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the **Lagrangian function** $L$ used in deriving the equations of motion of a robotic manipulator. Why is it preferred over force-balance methods for symbolic dynamic modeling?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Lagrangian** is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy and the total potential energy of the system:

$$
L(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})
$$

It is preferred over Newton-Euler force-balance methods because:

1. It is a **scalar** formulation → no vector cross-products or frame transformations are needed at intermediate steps.
2. It automatically accounts for **constraint forces** (no need to enumerate reaction forces at each joint).
3. The final result is a **closed-form symbolic dynamic model** $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, ideal for control design.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: scalar property; 1 mark: control-design utility.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State the scalar form of **Euler-Lagrange's equation** and clearly identify the meaning of every symbol. What are **generalized coordinates**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The scalar form is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

- $L = T - V$ — Lagrangian (scalar, in Joules).
- $q_i$ — $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate (independent variable, in rad or m).
- $\dot{q}_i$ — generalized velocity (rad/s or m/s).
- $\tau_i$ — generalized force conjugate to $q_i$ (N·m or N).

**Generalized coordinates** are a **minimum set of independent variables** that completely specify the configuration of a multi-DOF system. For a serial robot, the natural choice is the **set of joint variables** $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$. Their number equals the number of degrees of freedom.

*(1 mark: equation; 1 mark: symbol meaning; 1 mark: generalized coordinates concept.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Module-2 question for 14 marks carries an **internal choice**. The two questions below are independent alternatives — answer **either one in full**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — 2R Planar Manipulator via Lagrange

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Choice Q1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Consider a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, link masses $m_1, m_2$ concentrated at the distal ends, joint variables $q_1, q_2$, and gravity $g$ acting downward. Using **Lagrange's formulation**, derive the **kinetic energy**, **potential energy**, and the **Lagrangian** of the system.

**(b) [7 marks]** From the Lagrangian obtained in part (a), derive the **complete manipulator equation** in canonical form $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, identifying each matrix element explicitly.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Energies and Lagrangian**

Since masses are at the distal ends, the position of mass 1 is $(x_1, y_1) = (l_1\cos q_1,\, l_1\sin q_1)$. The position of mass 2 is:

$$
x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2), \qquad y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

Velocities (differentiating w.r.t. time):

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -l_1\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1, \qquad \dot{y}_1 = l_1\cos q_1\, \dot{q}_1
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -l_1\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y}_2 = l_1\cos q_1\, \dot{q}_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Squared speeds:

$$
\dot{x}_1^2 + \dot{y}_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Total kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Total potential energy (heights above base, gravity $g$ downward):

$$
V = m_1 g\, l_1\sin q_1 + m_2 g\left[l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

Lagrangian $L = T - V$:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2 l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) - (m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\sin q_1 - m_2 g\, l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of the Canonical Equation**

Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_2
$$

Its time derivative is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = \left[(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right]\ddot{q}_1 + \left[m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right]\ddot{q}_2 - 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2^2
$$

Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1} = -(m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\cos q_1 - m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

Subtracting per the Euler-Lagrange equation, the **first joint equation** becomes:

$$
\boxed{M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{11}\dot{q}_1 + C_{12}\dot{q}_2 + G_1 = \tau_1}
$$

Similarly, the **second joint equation** is:

$$
\boxed{M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{21}\dot{q}_1 + C_{22}\dot{q}_2 + G_2 = \tau_2}
$$

The identified matrices are:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \\ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 \\[4pt] m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\cos q_1 + m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The final compact form is:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Pendulum-Cart System

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Q2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A **point mass** $m$ is mounted on a **massless cart** of mass $M$ that slides on a horizontal frictionless track. The pendulum has length $l$ and angle $\theta$ measured from the vertical. Using **Lagrange's equation**, derive expressions for the **kinetic energy, potential energy, and Lagrangian** in generalized coordinates $(x, \theta)$, where $x$ is the cart displacement.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Lagrangian, derive the **two coupled equations of motion** and express them in the matrix form $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. Show the explicit form of $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ for this system.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Energies and Lagrangian**

Position of the bob:
$$
x_b = x + l\sin\theta, \qquad y_b = -l\cos\theta
$$

Velocities (time-differentiated):
$$
\dot{x}_b = \dot{x} + l\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta}, \qquad \dot{y}_b = l\sin\theta\, \dot{\theta}
$$

Total kinetic energy (cart + bob):

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}M\dot{x}^2 + \frac{1}{2}m\!\left[(\dot{x}+l\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta})^2 + (l\sin\theta\, \dot{\theta})^2\right]
$$

Expanding $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}(M+m)\dot{x}^2 + m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + \frac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2
$$

Potential energy (taking the pivot as reference, gravity downward):

$$
V = -mgl\cos\theta
$$

Lagrangian $L = T - V$:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{1}{2}(M+m)\dot{x}^2 + m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + \frac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + mgl\cos\theta}
$$

**Part (b) — Equations of Motion**

**Equation for $x$:** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = (M+m)\dot{x} + ml\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 0$. Hence:

$$
(M+m)\ddot{x} + m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{\theta} - m l \sin\theta\, \dot{\theta}^2 = F
$$

**Equation for $\theta$:** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x} + m l^2 \dot{\theta}$. Time derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}\right) = m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{x} - m l \sin\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + m l^2 \ddot{\theta}
$$

And $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = -m l \sin\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} - mgl\sin\theta$. Hence:

$$
m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{x} + m l^2 \ddot{\theta} + mgl\sin\theta = \tau
$$

Stacking in matrix form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ with $\mathbf{q} = [x, \theta]^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = [F, \tau]^T$:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M+m & m l \cos\theta \\ m l \cos\theta & m l^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -m l \sin\theta\, \dot{\theta} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -mgl\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign of $G$:** Always check with a **static case** ($\dot{q} = \ddot{q} = 0$). For the pendulum hanging vertically ($\theta = 0$), $G_2 = 0$ — torque needed is zero. ✓
> 2. **Symmetry of $\mathbf{M}$:** If you ever find $M_{12} \neq M_{21}$, you have an algebra error. The mass matrix is **always symmetric**.
> 3. **Units:** Joint torque $\tau$ is in **N·m** for revolute joints and **N** for prismatic joints. Mixed systems (like the cart-pendulum) are common exam topics.
> 4. **Skipping $L$:** Do not jump from $T, V$ directly to the final equation. The Lagrangian $L = T - V$ **must be written explicitly** as a 1-mark reward in ESE valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lagrangian:** $L = T - V$ (kinetic minus potential energy). Always in Joules.
- **Scalar form:** $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$.
- **Vector form:** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ — the **canonical manipulator equation**.
- **Generalized coordinates $q_i$:** Independent, minimum-set variables (joint angles/displacements for serial robots).
- **Mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$:** Symmetric, positive-definite, $n \times n$, depends only on configuration $\mathbf{q}$.
- **Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}$:** Linear in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, captures Coriolis + centripetal effects; not unique.
- **Gravity vector $\mathbf{G}$:** $G_i = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$; depends only on $\mathbf{q}$ (and gravity $g$).
- **Christoffel symbol form (KTU-preferred):** $C_{ij} = \sum_k \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial_k M_{ij} + \partial_j M_{ik} - \partial_i M_{kj}\right)\dot{q}_k$.
- **Skew-symmetry property:** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric → used in stability/passivity proofs.
- **Procedure to remember:** (1) choose $q$, (2) write $T$ in quadratic form, (3) write $V$, (4) form $L = T - V$, (5) apply Euler-Lagrange, (6) identify $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$.
- **Static check:** With $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$, the equation must reduce to $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{static}}$.
- **Exam mantra:** Always show **all five steps explicitly**; do not skip the Lagrangian; verify $\mathbf{M}$ symmetry before writing the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hamilton's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hamilton's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hamilton's Equations (Hamiltonian Formulation)** are a set of **first-order differential equations** that describe the time evolution of a holonomic, conservative (or generalized) mechanical system in terms of generalized coordinates $q_i$ and their conjugate generalized momenta $p_i$. They replace the second-order **Lagrange's equations** with a symmetric, canonical pair of **2n first-order equations**, providing the natural framework for **state-space representation** and **optimal control** of robotic systems.

For a system with $n$ generalized coordinates, the canonical Hamilton's equations are:

$$\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \quad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $H(q_i, p_i, t)$ is the **Hamiltonian function** (often equal to the **total mechanical energy**) and $(q_i, p_i)$ are coordinates of the **phase space** of dimension $2n$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car. The **Lagrangian** view tracks your **acceleration** at every instant (how fast your speed is changing) — it is a *rate-of-change-of-rate* viewpoint. **Hamilton's view**, in contrast, tracks two separate variables simultaneously: your **position** $q$ *and* your **momentum** $p$ (which is mass × velocity). 

Think of a musical duet: instead of one musician playing the rhythm (Lagrangian's second-order), two musicians play synchronized melodies — one handling position, the other handling momentum. Both melodies evolve independently but are coupled through the shared musical score (the Hamiltonian $H$). This **symmetry** is what makes Hamilton's formulation so powerful for **optimal control**, because we can independently specify desired position and momentum trajectories.

## 1.3 Why Hamilton's Equations Matter in Robot Dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Significance:**
> - **State-Space Compatibility:** The pair $(q, p)$ forms a natural **state vector** $x \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ suitable for modern **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** and **LQR** formulations used in robotic manipulators.
> - **Energy Conservation Insight:** For autonomous robotic systems, $H$ equals the **total mechanical energy** $T + V$, which is conserved along trajectories — a powerful invariant for trajectory planning.
> - **Optimal Control Foundation:** Forms the mathematical backbone of the **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle**, used to derive time-optimal and energy-optimal trajectories for robot arms.
> - **Symplectic Structure:** Preserves phase-space volume (Liouville's theorem), making numerical integration more stable for long-duration simulations.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Configuration space dimension:** $n$ (number of independent generalized coordinates / DOF of robot)
- **Phase space dimension:** $2n$ (joint positions + joint momenta)
- **Conjugate momentum units:** $[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$ (angular momentum for revolute joints)
- **Hamiltonian units:** $[\text{Joules}]$ (= energy for conservative systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Step-by-Step Concept Breakdown

### Step 1 — Starting Point: The Lagrangian
We begin with the **Lagrangian function** $L(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t) = T - V$, where:
- $T = T(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t)$ is the **kinetic energy**
- $V = V(q_i, t)$ is the **potential energy**
- $q_i$ are the generalized coordinates
- $\dot{q}_i$ are the generalized velocities

Lagrange's equations are **second-order**:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Define Generalized (Conjugate) Momenta
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **conjugate (generalized) momentum** $p_i$ corresponding to coordinate $q_i$ is defined as the partial derivative of the Lagrangian with respect to the generalized velocity:
> $$p_i \triangleq \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$$

For a robotic manipulator where $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$:
$$p_i = \sum_{j=1}^n M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_j$$

This shows that **conjugate momenta are linear combinations of velocities**, weighted by the inertia matrix $M(q)$.

### Step 3 — The Legendre Transformation
The Lagrangian is a function of $(q, \dot{q}, t)$. Hamilton's key insight was to **replace** velocities $\dot{q}$ with **momenta** $p$ as independent variables using the **Legendre transformation**:

$$H(q_i, p_i, t) = \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \dot{q}_i - L(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t)$$

The mapping is invertible when $L$ is convex in $\dot{q}$ (true for standard robotic manipulators since $M(q)$ is positive definite).

### Step 4 — Hamilton's Canonical Equations (Derivation)
Taking the total differential of $H$:
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i + \sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} dq_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} d\dot{q}_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

Substituting $p_i = \partial L/\partial \dot{q}_i$ and using Lagrange's equation $\frac{d}{dt}p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$:

$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i - \sum_i \dot{p}_i \, dq_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

Equating coefficients of the independent differentials $dq_i$, $dp_i$, $dt$ yields **Hamilton's canonical equations**.

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation
For **natural systems** (kinetic energy quadratic in $\dot{q}$, potential $V(q)$ time-independent):
- $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ is **homogeneous degree 2** in velocities
- By **Euler's homogeneous theorem**: $\sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i = 2T$
- Therefore: $H = 2T - (T - V) = T + V$ = **Total Energy**

> [!NOTE]
> For natural robotic systems with time-independent constraints, **$H$ is conserved** ($dH/dt = 0$). This is the famous **conservation of energy** expressed in Hamiltonian form.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Generalized Momentum** | $p_i = \partial L / \partial \dot{q}_i$ | $[\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$ for revolute joints |
| 2 | **Hamiltonian Definition** | $H = \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i - L$ | Legendre transform of $L$ |
| 3 | **Hamilton's Eq. (Position)** | $\dot{q}_i = \partial H / \partial p_i$ | First canonical equation |
| 4 | **Hamilton's Eq. (Momentum)** | $\dot{p}_i = -\partial H / \partial q_i$ | Second canonical equation |
| 5 | **Time Derivative of H** | $dH/dt = -\partial L/\partial t = \partial H/\partial t$ | Energy conserved if $H$ has no explicit $t$-dependence |
| 6 | **Hamiltonian = Total Energy** | $H = T + V$ (natural systems) | $T$ quadratic in $\dot{q}$; $V$ independent of $\dot{q}$ |
| 7 | **Phase-Space Dimension** | $\dim = 2n$ | For $n$-DOF robotic manipulator |
| 8 | **Momentum for Robotic Manipulator** | $p = M(q)\,\dot{q}$ | $M(q)$ is the $n \times n$ inertia matrix |
| 9 | **Hamilton–Jacobi Equation** | $\partial S/\partial t + H(q, \partial S/\partial q, t) = 0$ | $S$ = Hamilton's principal function |
| 10 | **Poisson Bracket** | $\{f, g\} = \sum_i \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial q_i}\frac{\partial g}{\partial p_i} - \frac{\partial f}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial g}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Used to check conservation laws |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Applications in Robotics:**
> 1. **Trajectory Optimization:** NASA's Mars rover path planners use **indirect methods** based on Hamilton's equations + Pontryagin's principle to compute fuel-optimal trajectories.
> 2. **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Modern robotic arms (e.g., Franka Emika Panda) embed Hamilton's state-space form in their MPC controllers for predictive motion.
> 3. **Energy-Based Control:** Passivity-based controllers leverage the fact that $H$ is a storage function, ensuring **Lyapunov stability**.
> 4. **Reinforcement Learning:** Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) parameterize robot dynamics preserving energy, enabling physically consistent policies from data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Hamilton's Equations from Lagrangian

### **Derivation A: From Lagrangian to Hamiltonian via Legendre Transform**

**Step 1 — Recall the Lagrangian framework.**
For a holonomic mechanical system with $n$ generalized coordinates $q_i$, the Lagrangian is:
$$L(q_1, \ldots, q_n, \dot{q}_1, \ldots, \dot{q}_n, t)$$

Lagrange's equation of motion (Euler–Lagrange):
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i^{(\text{nc})}$$
where $Q_i^{(\text{nc})}$ denotes non-conservative generalized forces.

**Step 2 — Define the generalized (conjugate) momentum.**
$$p_i \triangleq \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$$

This definition has a **physical meaning**: $p_i$ represents the quantity canonically conjugate to coordinate $q_i$. For a Cartesian coordinate $x$, $p_x = m\dot{x}$ is linear momentum. For an angle $\theta$, $p_\theta = I\dot{\theta}$ is angular momentum.

**Step 3 — Verify with Euler–Lagrange.**
Substituting $p_i$ into the Euler–Lagrange equation:
$$\dot{p}_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} + Q_i^{(\text{nc})}$$

For a conservative system ($Q_i^{(\text{nc})} = 0$):
$$\dot{p}_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Legendre Transformation.**
The Legendre transformation is a change of variables from $(q, \dot{q})$ to $(q, p)$. Define:
$$H(q, p, t) \triangleq \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \dot{q}_i - L(q, \dot{q}, t)$$

The variables $\dot{q}_i$ on the right-hand side are understood to be expressed in terms of $(q, p)$ using $p_i = \partial L / \partial \dot{q}_i$.

**Step 5 — Take the total differential of $H$.**
$$dH = \sum_i \frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i} dq_i + \sum_i \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i} dp_i + \frac{\partial H}{\partial t} dt$$

From the definition:
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i + \sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} dq_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} d\dot{q}_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $p_i = \partial L / \partial \dot{q}_i$.**
The $d\dot{q}_i$ terms cancel:
$$\sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} d\dot{q}_i = \sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i - \sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i = 0$$

Therefore:
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} dq_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 7 — Use Euler–Lagrange to simplify.**
For a conservative system, $\partial L / \partial q_i = \dot{p}_i$. Substituting:
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i - \sum_i \dot{p}_i \, dq_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 8 — Compare with the chain-rule expansion of $dH$.**
Since $(q_i, p_i, t)$ are now the **independent** variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i} &= -\dot{p}_i \\
\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i} &= \dot{q}_i \\
\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} &= -\frac{\partial L}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange into canonical form.**
This produces Hamilton's canonical equations of motion:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}_i &= \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i} \\
\dot{p}_i &= -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

These are **$2n$ first-order ODEs** (compared to $n$ second-order Lagrange equations), and they are the foundation of Hamiltonian mechanics. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Derivation B: Hamiltonian for a Natural Robotic Manipulator**

For a serial-chain robotic manipulator with $n$ revolute joints, the kinetic energy is:
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$$

where $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **positive-definite inertia matrix**.

**Step 1 — Compute the generalized momenta.**
$$p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^n M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_j$$

In matrix form:
$$p = M(q)\,\dot{q} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{q} = M^{-1}(q)\,p$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's Homogeneous Function Theorem.**
Since $T$ is homogeneous of degree 2 in $\dot{q}$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^n \dot{q}_i \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = 2T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i = 2T$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Hamiltonian.**
With $L = T - V(q)$:
$$H = \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i - L = 2T - (T - V) = T + V$$

Thus for a natural robotic system, **$H$ is the total mechanical energy** — independent of whether the inertia matrix $M(q)$ varies with $q$.

**Step 4 — Verify Hamilton's equations yield the manipulator equations of motion.**

Computing $\partial H / \partial p_i$:
$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial p_i} = \sum_j \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\frac{\partial \dot{q}_j}{\partial p_i} = \sum_j M_{ij}(q) \cdot [M^{-1}(q)]_{ji} = \delta_{ii} = \dot{q}_i \quad \checkmark$$

Computing $\partial H / \partial q_i$:
$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k} \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j \dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i} = -\dot{p}_i$$

Solving for $\dot{p}_i$:
$$\dot{p}_i = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k} \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j \dot{q}_k - \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$$

Expanding $\dot{p}_i = \frac{d}{dt}\sum_j M_{ij}\dot{q}_j = \sum_j M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k\dot{q}_j$ and equating with $-\partial H/\partial q_i$ recovers the standard manipulator equation:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$
where $C$ contains Christoffel symbols and $G = \partial V/\partial q$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Derivation C: Conservation of $H$ for Autonomous Systems**

If $H$ has no explicit time dependence (i.e., $\partial H/\partial t = 0$):
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \sum_i \frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_i + \sum_i \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\dot{p}_i + \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}$$

Substituting Hamilton's equations:
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \sum_i \left(-\dot{p}_i\right)\dot{q}_i + \sum_i \dot{q}_i \dot{p}_i + 0 = 0$$

Therefore $H = \text{constant} = T + V$ along any trajectory — the **law of conservation of mechanical energy** is recovered directly from Hamilton's formulation. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Lagrangian to Hamiltonian Formulation

The following block diagram illustrates the logical transformation chain from the variational principle to Hamilton's canonical equations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Variational Principle\ndelta integral of L dt equals 0] --> B[Define Lagrangian\nL equals T minus V]
    B --> C[Euler Lagrange Equation\n2nd order ODE in q]
    B --> D[Define Conjugate Momentum\np equals partial L by partial q dot]
    D --> E[Legendre Transformation\nH equals sum p q dot minus L]
    E --> F[Hamiltonian Function\nH equals q, p, t]
    F --> G1[Canonical Equation 1\nq dot equals partial H by partial p]
    F --> G2[Canonical Equation 2\np dot equals negative partial H by partial q]
    F --> H[Phase Space Trajectory\n2n dimensional state]
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Conservation Law\nH constant if autonomous]
    H --> J[Optimal Control\nPontryagin Principle]
```

## 4.2 Phase-Space Schematic for a 1-DOF Pendulum

The following schematic illustrates the phase-space portrait of a simple pendulum using Hamilton's formulation. The state $(q, p) = (\theta, p_\theta)$ traces closed orbits around the stable equilibrium.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inverted Position\ntheta equals pi, p equals 0\nUnstable equilibrium] -.->|Separatrix| P2[Stable Region\nClosed orbits]
    P2 --> P3[Low Energy\nSmall oscillations\nNearly elliptical]
    P2 --> P4[Higher Energy\nAnharmonic orbits]
    P4 -.->|Approaching separatrix| P1
    P2 --> P5[Hanging Down\ntheta equals 0, p equals 0\nStable equilibrium]
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow for Robot Control via Hamiltonian Formulation

This diagram maps how Hamilton's equations integrate into a complete robotic manipulator control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Trajectory\nqd, qd dot, qd double dot] --> B[Trajectory Optimizer\nUses Hamilton Pontryagin]
    B --> C[Optimal Control Law\ntau star]
    C --> D[Robot Manipulator\nn DOF]
    D --> E[Sensor Feedback\nq actual, q dot actual]
    E --> F[State Estimator\nComputes p equals M q dot]
    F --> G[Hamiltonian Evaluator\nH equals T plus V]
    G --> H[Lyapunov Stability Check\nH decreasing]
    H -->|Stable| I[Continue Operation]
    H -->|Unstable| J[Re plan Trajectory]
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Mapping: Lagrangian Variables ↔ Hamiltonian Variables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L[LAGRANGIAN DOMAIN]
        L1[Generalized Coordinate q]
        L2[Generalized Velocity q dot]
        L3[Lagrangian L q q dot t]
    end
    subgraph H[HAMILTONIAN DOMAIN]
        H1[Generalized Coordinate q]
        H2[Generalized Momentum p]
        H3[Hamiltonian H q p t]
    end
    L1 --> H1
    L2 -->|Legendre Transform| H2
    L3 -->|Legendre Transform| H3
    H2 -->|Inverse Transform| L2
    H3 -->|Inverse Transform| L3
```
