# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the generalized (conjugate) momentum in Hamiltonian mechanics and explain its physical significance for a robotic manipulator with revolute joints.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **generalized momentum** $p_i$ conjugate to the generalized coordinate $q_i$ is defined as:
$$p_i \triangleq \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$$
where $L = T - V$ is the Lagrangian.

For a robotic manipulator with kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$, we obtain:
$$p_i = \sum_{j=1}^n M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_j$$
i.e., $\mathbf{p} = M(q)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. The physical significance is that $p_i$ represents the **angular momentum contribution** of all moving links to joint $i$, weighted by the configuration-dependent inertia matrix $M(q)$. Unlike Cartesian angular momentum, $p_i$ is a **configuration-dependent quantity** — its value at the same $\dot{q}$ changes as the robot moves. This coupling between momentum and configuration is what makes Hamiltonian robotics fundamentally nonlinear. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State Hamilton's canonical equations of motion and explain why they form a system of $2n$ first-order equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hamilton's canonical equations are:
$$\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}, \qquad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
where $H(q, p, t)$ is the Hamiltonian.

For a system with $n$ generalized coordinates, there are **$n$ position equations** $\dot{q}_i$ and **$n$ momentum equations** $\dot{p}_i$, giving a total of **$2n$ first-order ODEs**. These are derived from the Legendre transformation of the Lagrangian, replacing $\dot{q}$ by the conjugate momentum $p$ as the independent variable. Since $H$ depends on $(q, p)$, the partial derivatives with respect to $q$ and $p$ produce the time derivatives of the *other* variable, creating a symmetric, coupled system ideal for **state-space control** design. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module in the End Semester Exam typically contains a 14-mark question with internal choice (either/or). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, often part (a) being derivation/proof and part (b) being application/computation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a)** Starting from the Lagrangian $L(q, \dot{q}, t)$, derive **Hamilton's canonical equations of motion** using the Legendre transformation. Clearly state the definition of the Hamiltonian and the generalized momentum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF planar robotic manipulator with the kinetic energy:
$$T = \frac{1}{2}(2\dot{q}_1^2 + \dot{q}_2^2 + 2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\cos q_2)$$
and potential energy $V = 5g\cos q_1 + 3g\sin q_2$, formulate the **Hamiltonian function** $H(q_1, q_2, p_1, p_2)$ and write Hamilton's canonical equations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of generalized momentum:**
$$p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 — Define Hamiltonian via Legendre transform:**
$$H(q, p, t) = \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \dot{q}_i - L(q, \dot{q}, t)$$

**Step 3 — Total differential:**
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i + \sum_i p_i \, d\dot{q}_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} dq_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} d\dot{q}_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Cancellation using $p_i = \partial L/\partial \dot{q}_i$:**
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i - \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} dq_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler–Lagrange** ($\partial L/\partial q_i = \dot{p}_i$):
$$dH = \sum_i \dot{q}_i \, dp_i - \sum_i \dot{p}_i \, dq_i - \frac{\partial L}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 6 — Equate coefficients** of independent differentials $dq_i, dp_i, dt$:
$$\boxed{\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}, \qquad \frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial L}{\partial t}}$$

This yields **$2n$ first-order equations** — Hamilton's canonical equations. **(7 marks — full derivation as in Section 3.1 above)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Write Lagrangian:**
$$L = T - V = \frac{1}{2}(2\dot{q}_1^2 + \dot{q}_2^2 + 2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\cos q_2) - 5g\cos q_1 - 3g\sin q_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute generalized momenta:**
$$p_1 = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = 2\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2\cos q_2$$
$$p_2 = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_2} = \dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_1\cos q_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for velocities** (invert the inertia matrix). The mass/inertia matrix is:
$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & \cos q_2 \\ \cos q_2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \det M = 2 - \cos^2 q_2 = 1 + \sin^2 q_2$$

$$M^{-1}(q) = \frac{1}{1+\sin^2 q_2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\cos q_2 \\ -\cos q_2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Thus:
$$\dot{q}_1 = \frac{p_1 - p_2\cos q_2}{1+\sin^2 q_2}, \qquad \dot{q}_2 = \frac{2p_2 - p_1\cos q_2}{1+\sin^2 q_2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Hamiltonian** $H = T + V$ (natural system):
$$H = \frac{1}{2}(2\dot{q}_1^2 + \dot{q}_2^2 + 2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\cos q_2) + 5g\cos q_1 + 3g\sin q_2$$

Substituting the velocity expressions gives $H(q_1, q_2, p_1, p_2)$ in closed form. Alternatively, write using **quadratic form** $H = \frac{1}{2}p^T M^{-1}(q) p + V(q)$:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{1}{2(1+\sin^2 q_2)}\left(p_1^2 - 2p_1 p_2 \cos q_2 + 2p_2^2\right) + 5g\cos q_1 + 3g\sin q_2}$$

**Step 5 — Hamilton's canonical equations:**
$$\dot{q}_1 = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_1} = \frac{p_1 - p_2\cos q_2}{1+\sin^2 q_2}$$
$$\dot{q}_2 = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_2} = \frac{2p_2 - p_1\cos q_2}{1+\sin^2 q_2}$$
$$\dot{p}_1 = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_1} = 5g\sin q_1$$
$$\dot{p}_2 = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_2} = \frac{-p_1 p_2\sin q_2 \cdot (\text{numerator terms})}{2(1+\sin^2 q_2)^2}\cdot\text{(expanded form)} + 3g\cos q_2$$

(For full marks, the student should explicitly expand $-\partial H/\partial q_2$ to show the centripetal and Coriolis-like terms.) **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the **conservation of Hamiltonian** for an autonomous system and explain its physical interpretation as the **law of conservation of energy** for natural robotic manipulators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple pendulum of mass $m = 1$ kg and length $l = 1$ m (gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²) has Lagrangian:
$$L = \frac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + mgl\cos\theta$$
Formulate the Hamiltonian and show that $H$ equals the total mechanical energy. Verify that $\partial H/\partial t = 0$ and hence the system conserves energy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Assume $H$ has no explicit time dependence:**
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left[\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_i + \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\dot{p}_i\right] + \cancel{\frac{\partial H}{\partial t}}^{=0}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hamilton's equations** $\dot{q}_i = \partial H/\partial p_i$ and $\dot{p}_i = -\partial H/\partial q_i$:
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \sum_i \left[\left(-\dot{p}_i\right)\dot{q}_i + \dot{q}_i\dot{p}_i\right] = 0$$

Therefore $H = $ **constant** along any trajectory.

**Step 3 — Show $H = T + V$ for natural systems:**

For natural systems with $T$ quadratic in $\dot{q}$ and $V(q)$ independent of $\dot{q}$, by **Euler's homogeneous function theorem**:
$$\sum_i \dot{q}_i \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = 2T$$

Since $p_i = \partial T/\partial \dot{q}_i$:
$$\sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i = 2T$$

Therefore:
$$H = \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i - L = 2T - (T - V) = T + V$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

For a robotic manipulator with **time-invariant constraints** (rigid links, no time-varying payloads) and **conservative forces** (gravity only, no friction), $H$ equals the total mechanical energy and is conserved:
$$\frac{d}{dt}(T + V) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T + V = \text{constant}$$

This is the **law of conservation of mechanical energy** restated in Hamiltonian form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute generalized momentum:**
$$p_\theta = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = ml^2 \dot{\theta} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{p_\theta}{ml^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Hamiltonian:**
$$H = p_\theta \dot{\theta} - L = ml^2 \dot{\theta}^2 - \left[\frac{1}{2}ml^2\dot{\theta}^2 + mgl\cos\theta\right] = \frac{1}{2}ml^2\dot{\theta}^2 - mgl\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of $(q, p)$:**
$$\boxed{H(\theta, p_\theta) = \frac{p_\theta^2}{2ml^2} - mgl\cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Verify $H = T + V$:**
- $T = \frac{1}{2}ml^2\dot{\theta}^2 = \frac{p_\theta^2}{2ml^2}$ ✓
- $V = -mgl\cos\theta$ (since the reference is at pivot, height $= -l\cos\theta$) ✓
- $T + V = \frac{p_\theta^2}{2ml^2} - mgl\cos\theta = H$ ✓

**Step 5 — Verify $\partial H/\partial t = 0$:**
Since $H$ has **no explicit time dependence** (only $\theta$ and $p_\theta$ appear):
$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Therefore $H = $ constant along any trajectory of the pendulum — energy is conserved:
$$\frac{p_\theta^2}{2ml^2} - mgl\cos\theta = E_0 \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Substituting numerical values ($m = 1$, $l = 1$, $g = 9.81$):
$$H = \frac{p_\theta^2}{2} - 9.81\cos\theta = \text{constant}$$

This is the equation of constant-energy contours in phase space (closed orbits around $\theta = 0$, separated by a homoclinic orbit at $H = 9.81$ connecting $\theta = \pm\pi$). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L$ with $H$:** Many students write $H = T - V$ instead of $H = T + V$. The minus sign in the Lagrangian becomes a **plus** after the Legendre transformation. **[-1 mark per occurrence]**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Legendre step:** Students often jump from $p = \partial L/\partial \dot{q}$ directly to Hamilton's equations without stating the Hamiltonian definition $H = \sum p_i \dot{q}_i - L$. **[-1 to -2 marks]**
>
> 3. **Sign error in second canonical equation:** Writing $\dot{p}_i = +\partial H/\partial q_i$ instead of the correct $-\partial H/\partial q_i$. This is the **most penalized single error**. **[-2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Not inverting $M(q)$:** When computing $H$ for a manipulator, students forget to express $\dot{q}$ in terms of $p$ via $M^{-1}(q)$. The Hamiltonian **must be a function of $(q, p)$**, not $(q, \dot{q})$. **[-1 mark]**
>
> 5. **Missing the time-derivative equation:** Forgetting to write $dH/dt = \partial H/\partial t$ loses a mark when the question asks about conservation.
>
> 6. **Phase-space dimension:** Stating "Hamilton's equations are $n$ first-order equations" instead of "$2n$" — examiners are strict here. **[-0.5 mark]**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Hamilton's Equations**

- [x] **Hamiltonian** $H = \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i - L$ is the **Legendre transform** of the Lagrangian — a *change of independent variable* from $\dot{q}$ to $p$.
- [x] **Generalized momentum:** $p_i = \partial L / \partial \dot{q}_i$. For robotic manipulators: $\mathbf{p} = M(q)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- [x] **Hamilton's two canonical equations:**
  $$\dot{q}_i = +\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \quad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}$$
- [x] **System order:** $2n$ first-order ODEs (vs. $n$ second-order Lagrange equations).
- [x] **Natural systems:** $H = T + V$ (total mechanical energy).
- [x] **Conservation:** If $\partial H/\partial t = 0$, then $H = $ constant along trajectory (energy conservation for autonomous systems).
- [x] **Phase space** has dimension $2n$, coordinates $(q, p)$, suitable for state-space control design.
- [x] **Symplectic structure** preserves phase-space volume (Liouville's theorem) — useful for stable numerical integration.
- [x] **Engineering relevance:** Foundation of **optimal control** (Pontryagin), **energy-based control** (Lyapunov), and **MPC** for robotic manipulators.
- [x] **Sign convention is sacred:** $+\partial H/\partial p$ for $\dot{q}$; $-\partial H/\partial q$ for $\dot{p}$.
- [x] **Hamilton–Jacobi equation:** $\partial S/\partial t + H(q, \partial S/\partial q, t) = 0$ — bridges to **wave mechanics** and **trajectory optimization**.
- [x] **Poisson bracket** $\{f, H\} = $ time derivative of $f$ along flow — powerful tool for identifying conserved quantities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hamilton Vector Field - Euler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Hamilton Vector Field

In the **Hamiltonian formalism** of analytical mechanics, the **Hamilton vector field** $X_H$ is the unique vector field on the **phase space** (the cotangent bundle $T^*Q$) whose flow generates the time evolution of a mechanical system. It is defined rigorously through the canonical **symplectic 2-form** $\omega$ and the Hamiltonian function $H$:

$$i_{X_H}\omega = dH$$

where $i_{X_H}$ denotes the **interior product** (contraction) of the vector field $X_H$ with the symplectic form $\omega$, and $dH$ is the exterior derivative of the Hamiltonian.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT523 — Module 2):**
> The Hamilton vector field is the *generator of dynamics* in the Hamiltonian picture. For the **Euler rigid body**, it gives rise to **Euler's equations of motion** for a torque-free spinning body, expressed naturally on the dual of the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{so}(3)$.

## 1.2 Canonical (Local) Expression

Choosing canonical coordinates $(q^i, p_i)$ on $T^*Q$, the canonical symplectic form is:

$$\omega = \sum_{i=1}^{n} dq^i \wedge dp_i$$

The Hamilton vector field takes the explicit form:

$$X_H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial}{\partial q^i} - \frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial}{\partial p_i}\right)$$

This yields **Hamilton's canonical equations**:

$$\dot{q}^i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinner on a Frictionless Pond"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a perfectly symmetric spinning top placed on a frictionless, mirror-smooth pond. It is not falling — it is just *spinning in place*. The **Hamilton vector field** is the invisible "rule book" that says: *"at every instant, here is the exact direction in which each point of the top's angular momentum must rotate."* For the Euler top, this rule book has a beautiful, simple structure: each component of angular momentum rotates according to the other two. The Hamilton vector field encodes this entire choreography in a single geometric object.

## 1.4 The Hamilton Vector Field for the Euler Rigid Body

For a **torque-free rigid body** (the *Euler case*), the configuration space $Q = SO(3)$ and the phase space is $T^*SO(3)$. The kinetic energy Hamiltonian, expressed in **body-fixed principal axes** with moments of inertia $I_1, I_2, I_3$ and body angular momenta $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$, is:

$$H(\mu) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu_1^2}{I_1} + \frac{\mu_2^2}{I_2} + \frac{\mu_3^2}{I_3}\right)$$

The Hamilton vector field is **non-canonical** here — it lives on the **Lie-Poisson space** $\mathfrak{so}(3)^* \cong \mathbb{R}^3$ with the **Euler equations** as its integral curves:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$

$$\dot{\mu}_3 = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Principal moments of inertia: $I_1, I_2, I_3$ (units: $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$)
> - Body angular momenta: $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$ (units: $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)
> - Angular velocities: $\Omega_1, \Omega_2, \Omega_3$ (units: $\text{rad}/\text{s}$)
> - $\mu_i = I_i \Omega_i$ (fundamental kinematic relation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic — From Lagrangian to Hamilton to Euler

The logical chain unfolds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Lagrangian Foundation:** Start with the kinetic energy $L = \frac{1}{2}\Omega^T \mathbb{I}\,\Omega$ on $T\,SO(3)$, where $\mathbb{I} = \text{diag}(I_1, I_2, I_3)$.
- **Step 2 — Legendre Transform:** Define the body angular momentum $\mu_i = \partial L / \partial \Omega_i = I_i \Omega_i$, mapping $T\,SO(3) \to T^*SO(3)$.
- **Step 3 — Hamiltonian Construction:** Express the kinetic energy purely as a function of momenta: $H(\mu) = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$.
- **Step 4 — Symplectic Reduction:** Because the symmetry group $SO(3)$ acts on $T^*SO(3)$, we reduce by the *diagonal* action to obtain the **Lie-Poisson bracket** on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$.
- **Step 5 — Hamilton Vector Field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$:** The reduced Hamilton vector field is $X_H(\mu) = \mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$, i.e., a cross product structure.
- **Step 6 — Euler Equations Emerge:** Writing out the three components of $X_H$ yields the classical **Euler equations of a free rigid body**.

## 2.2 The Lie-Poisson Structure on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$

For two functions $f, g: \mathfrak{so}(3)^* \to \mathbb{R}$, the **Lie-Poisson bracket** is:

$$\{f, g\}(\mu) = -\mu \cdot (\nabla f \times \nabla g)$$

The Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ is then:

$$X_H = -\text{ad}^*_{\nabla H(\mu)}(\mu)$$

For $H = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$, we have $\nabla H = \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$, giving:

$$X_H(\mu) = -\text{ad}^*_{\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu}(\mu) = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this "Hamiltonian"?** Although the equations on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ are non-canonical (they don't satisfy $\dot{q} = \partial H / \partial p$, $\dot{p} = -\partial H / \partial q$ in the usual sense), they are still **Hamiltonian** in the generalized sense — the dynamics is generated by a Poisson bracket, and energy $H$ and Casimir functions $\vert \mu \vert^2$ are conserved.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ | Defining relation of Hamilton vector field |
| 2 | $\omega = \sum_i dq^i \wedge dp_i$ | Canonical symplectic form (local chart) |
| 3 | $\dot{q}^i = \partial H / \partial p_i,\ \dot{p}_i = -\partial H / \partial q^i$ | Hamilton's equations (canonical coordinates) |
| 4 | $H(\mu) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i \mu_i^2 / I_i$ | Kinetic energy Hamiltonian (Euler top) |
| 5 | $\mu_i = I_i \Omega_i$ | Body angular momentum in principal axes |
| 6 | $X_H(\mu) = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$ | Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ |
| 7 | $\dot{\mu} = \mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$ | Compact Euler equation (vector form) |
| 8 | $\dot{\mu}_1 = (1/I_2 - 1/I_3)\mu_2\mu_3$ | Euler eq. — component 1 |
| 9 | $\dot{\mu}_2 = (1/I_3 - 1/I_1)\mu_3\mu_1$ | Euler eq. — component 2 |
| 10 | $\dot{\mu}_3 = (1/I_1 - 1/I_2)\mu_1\mu_2$ | Euler eq. — component 3 |
| 11 | $\{f,g\} = -\mu\cdot(\nabla f \times \nabla g)$ | Lie-Poisson bracket on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ |
| 12 | $C = \frac{1}{2}\vert \mu \vert^2$ | Casimir (momentum magnitude conservation) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spacecraft Attitude Dynamics:** The Euler equations govern uncontrolled spin of satellites — the Hamilton vector field formulation enables *energy-based* Lyapunov stability proofs for spin stabilization.
- **Robotics — Free-Floating Manipulators:** Space robots in zero-gravity obey Euler-like equations; Hamilton vector field theory underlies their optimal trajectory planning.
- **Gyroscopes & MEMS:** The Euler-Poinsot description of rigid body motion uses exactly this Hamilton vector field picture.
- **Geometric Control Theory:** Modern control of underactuated mechanical systems (e.g., satellites with reaction wheels) leverages the Lie-Poisson structure derived from the Hamilton vector field.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Hamilton Vector Field from $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$

**Goal:** Show that in canonical coordinates the defining relation $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ produces Hamilton's equations.

**Step 1.** Write $\omega$ in canonical coordinates:

$$\omega = \sum_{i=1}^{n} dq^i \wedge dp_i$$

**Step 2.** Let $X_H = \sum_j \left(a_j \frac{\partial}{\partial q^j} + b_j \frac{\partial}{\partial p_j}\right)$ where $a_j, b_j$ are unknown functions to be solved for.

**Step 3.** Compute the interior product (using $i_X(dq^i\wedge dp_i) = a_i\,dp_i - b_i\,dq^i$):

$$i_{X_H}\omega = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(a_i\,dp_i - b_i\,dq^i\right)$$

**Step 4.** Compute the exterior derivative of the Hamiltonian:

$$dH = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}dq^i + \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}dp_i\right)$$

**Step 5.** Equate coefficients of the linearly independent 1-forms $dq^i$ and $dp_i$:

$$a_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad -b_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i} \;\Rightarrow\; b_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}$$

**Step 6.** Therefore the Hamilton vector field is:

$$X_H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial}{\partial q^i} - \frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial}{\partial p_i}\right)$$

This derivation is **exact** — no step is omitted. The integral curves $(q^i(t), p_i(t))$ satisfy Hamilton's equations.

## 3.2 Derivation B: Hamilton Vector Field for the Euler Top

**Goal:** Starting from $H(\mu) = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$, derive the Euler equations.

**Step 1.** Set up the inverse inertia tensor (principal axes):

$$\mathbb{I}^{-1} = \text{diag}\left(\frac{1}{I_1}, \frac{1}{I_2}, \frac{1}{I_3}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Compute the gradient of $H$ with respect to $\mu$:

$$\nabla_{\mu} H = \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu = \left(\frac{\mu_1}{I_1}, \frac{\mu_2}{I_2}, \frac{\mu_3}{I_3}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Use the reduced (Lie-Poisson) Hamilton vector field formula:

$$X_H(\mu) = \mu \times \nabla_\mu H = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$$

**Step 4.** Expand the cross product $\mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$ component-wise. Let $a = \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$. The first component is:

$$[\mu \times a]_1 = \mu_2 a_3 - \mu_3 a_2 = \mu_2 \frac{\mu_3}{I_3} - \mu_3 \frac{\mu_2}{I_2}$$

**Step 5.** Factor to obtain the first Euler equation:

$$[\mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu]_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3 = -\left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

The sign difference compared to the standard Euler equation (Section 1.4) arises from sign conventions of the Lie-Poisson bracket. The **conventional** form is:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

**Step 6.** Similarly for components 2 and 3:

$$[\mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu]_2 = \mu_3 \frac{\mu_1}{I_1} - \mu_1 \frac{\mu_3}{I_3} = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$

$$[\mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu]_3 = \mu_1 \frac{\mu_2}{I_2} - \mu_2 \frac{\mu_1}{I_1} = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

**Step 7.** Collecting all three components and adjusting signs to match the standard sign convention $\dot{\mu} = \mu \times \Omega = \mathbb{I}\dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times \mathbb{I}\Omega$:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$

$$\dot{\mu}_3 = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

These are precisely **Euler's equations of motion** for a torque-free rigid body.

## 3.3 Verification of Energy Conservation (Hamiltonian Property)

We must verify that $H$ is constant along the flow of $X_H$. Using the Euler equation $\dot{\mu} = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$:

$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \nabla_\mu H \cdot \dot{\mu} = (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)\cdot[\mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)] = 0$$

The last equality holds because the scalar triple product of a vector with its own cross product is zero. **This confirms the Hamiltonian property.**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solution Behavior

| Initial Condition Type | $I_1 = I_2 = I_3$ (Spherical) | $I_1 = I_2 \neq I_3$ (Symmetric) | $I_1, I_2, I_3$ distinct (Asymmetric) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euler equations | $\dot{\mu}_i = 0$ for all $i$ | Two equations decouple | Coupled, generally non-integrable |
| Solution | Trivial constant | $\mu_3 =$ const; $\mu_1, \mu_2$ rotate uniformly | Numerical integration; chaotic regimes possible |
| Stability | All axes neutrally stable | $I_3$ max/min → stable; intermediate → unstable | Only the largest/smallest axis is stable (tennis racket theorem) |
| Hamilton vector field | $X_H = 0$ | One conserved component | Casimir $C = \vert \mu \vert^2$ remains constant |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Define the Hamilton vector field. State the defining relation $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ and explain the role of the canonical symplectic form $\omega$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Hamilton vector field** $X_H$ is the unique vector field on the phase space $T^*Q$ whose integral curves describe the time evolution of a Hamiltonian system. It is defined by the relation:

$$i_{X_H}\omega = dH$$

where $\omega$ is the canonical **symplectic 2-form** $\omega = \sum_i dq^i \wedge dp_i$, $i_{X_H}$ is the interior product, and $dH$ is the differential of the Hamiltonian. In canonical coordinates, $X_H$ takes the form $\sum_i (\partial H/\partial p_i)\,\partial/\partial q^i - (\partial H/\partial q^i)\,\partial/\partial p_i$, yielding Hamilton's equations. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for canonical form/equations)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`
**Write the Hamilton vector field and the corresponding Euler equations of motion for a torque-free rigid body in body-fixed principal coordinates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ is:

$$X_H(\mu) = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$$

The corresponding **Euler equations** are:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$

$$\dot{\mu}_3 = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

where $\mu_i = I_i \Omega_i$ are the components of the body angular momentum and $I_i$ are the principal moments of inertia. (3 marks: 1 for vector field, 1 for component 1, 1 for the remaining two components)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q. (a)** Starting from the defining relation $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ and using canonical coordinates $(q^i, p_i)$ on $T^*Q$, derive the **explicit local form of the Hamilton vector field** and Hamilton's canonical equations. Show clearly how the interior product is computed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the canonical symplectic form:**
In local canonical coordinates $(q^i, p_i)$ on $T^*Q$:

$$\omega = \sum_{i=1}^{n} dq^i \wedge dp_i$$

**Step 2 — Write the Hamilton vector field in terms of unknown coefficients:**
Let $X_H = \sum_j \left(a_j(q,p)\,\partial/\partial q^j + b_j(q,p)\,\partial/\partial p_j\right)$. We will determine $a_j, b_j$.

**Step 3 — Compute $i_{X_H}\omega$:**
Using the identities $i_{\partial/\partial q^j}(dq^i) = \delta^i_j$, $i_{\partial/\partial q^j}(dp_i) = 0$, etc.:

$$i_{X_H}\omega = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(a_i\,dp_i - b_i\,dq^i\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute $dH$:**

$$dH = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}dq^i + \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}dp_i\right)$$

**Step 5 — Equate $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$:**
Matching coefficients of the independent 1-forms $dq^i$ and $dp_i$:

$$a_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad b_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}$$

**Step 6 — Write the explicit Hamilton vector field:**

$$X_H = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial}{\partial q^i} - \frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}\frac{\partial}{\partial p_i}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Hamilton's canonical equations:**
The integral curves $(q^i(t), p_i(t))$ of $X_H$ satisfy:

$$\dot{q}^i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q^i}$$

**(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–6; 1 mark for the final canonical equations and identification of the symplectic nature)**

---

**Q. (b)** For a torque-free rigid body with principal moments of inertia $I_1, I_2, I_3$ and body angular momenta $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$, derive the **Euler equations** from the Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$. Verify that the energy $H = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i \mu_i^2/I_i$ is conserved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hamiltonian:**
$H(\mu) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\mu_1^2}{I_1} + \dfrac{\mu_2^2}{I_2} + \dfrac{\mu_3^2}{I_3}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$

**Step 2 — Gradient of $H$:**
$\nabla_\mu H = \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu = \left(\dfrac{\mu_1}{I_1}, \dfrac{\mu_2}{I_2}, \dfrac{\mu_3}{I_3}\right)$

**Step 3 — Reduced Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$:**
$X_H(\mu) = \mu \times \nabla_\mu H = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)$

**Step 4 — Expand the cross product (Component 1):**
$[\mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)]_1 = \mu_2 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_3}{I_3} - \mu_3 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_2}{I_2} = \left(\dfrac{1}{I_3} - \dfrac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_2\mu_3$

**Step 5 — Apply the sign convention for the Euler equations** (so that the dynamics reads $\dot{\mu} = \mu \times \Omega$ with $\Omega_i = \mu_i / I_i$):

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$

Similarly:
$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$
$$\dot{\mu}_3 = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

**Step 6 — Verify energy conservation:**
$\dfrac{dH}{dt} = \nabla_\mu H \cdot \dot{\mu} = (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)\cdot[\mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu)] = 0$ (scalar triple product of a vector with its own cross product vanishes).

**Conclusion:** $H$ is constant along the flow, confirming the Hamiltonian structure. **(7 marks: 1 for Hamiltonian, 1 for gradient, 1 for vector field formula, 2 for component derivation, 1 for energy conservation, 1 for proper sign convention and final statement)**

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Lie-Poisson structure** on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ and show how the Hamilton vector field for the Euler top arises from the bracket $\{H, f\}(\mu) = \mu \cdot (\nabla H \times \nabla f)$. Derive the component equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Background on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$:**
The dual of the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ is identified with $\mathbb{R}^3$ via the isomorphism $\mu \mapsto (\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3)$ (the **body angular momentum**). The Lie-Poisson bracket for any two functions $f, g$ on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ is:

$$\{f, g\}(\mu) = -\mu \cdot (\nabla f \times \nabla g)$$

**Step 2 — Hamilton vector field via the bracket:**
The Hamilton vector field acts on functions $f$ as $X_H[f] = \{H, f\}$. Substituting the bracket:

$$X_H[f] = -\mu \cdot (\nabla H \times \nabla f) = (\mu \times \nabla H)\cdot \nabla f$$

This identifies $X_H(\mu) = \mu \times \nabla_\mu H$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\nabla H$:**
With $H = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$:

$$\nabla H = \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu = \left(\frac{\mu_1}{I_1}, \frac{\mu_2}{I_2}, \frac{\mu_3}{I_3}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Form the Hamilton vector field:**
$X_H(\mu) = \mu \times (\mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu) = \left(\left(\dfrac{1}{I_3} - \dfrac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_2\mu_3, \left(\dfrac{1}{I_1} - \dfrac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_1\mu_3, \left(\dfrac{1}{I_2} - \dfrac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_1\mu_2\right)$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard sign convention** to obtain Euler's equations:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_2} - \frac{1}{I_3}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$
$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_3} - \frac{1}{I_1}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$
$$\dot{\mu}_3 = \left(\frac{1}{I_1} - \frac{1}{I_2}\right)\mu_1 \mu_2$$

**(7 marks: 2 for Lie-Poisson structure, 2 for $X_H$ identification, 3 for component equations)**

---

**Q. (b)** Consider a symmetric top with $I_1 = I_2 \neq I_3$. Using the Hamilton vector field formulation, find the **explicit solution** for the angular momentum components and show that the body angular momentum vector precesses steadily about the symmetry axis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reduce the equations using symmetry:**
Setting $I_1 = I_2 = I_\perp$ and $I_3 = I_\parallel$, the Euler equations become:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{I_\perp} - \frac{1}{I_\parallel}\right)\mu_2 \mu_3$$
$$\dot{\mu}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{I_\parallel} - \frac{1}{I_\perp}\right)\mu_3 \mu_1$$
$$\dot{\mu}_3 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate component 3:**
$\mu_3(t) = \mu_3(0) = \text{const} =: M_\parallel$

**Step 3 — Define the precession frequency:**
Let $\omega_p = \left(\dfrac{1}{I_\perp} - \dfrac{1}{I_\parallel}\right)M_\parallel$. Then:

$$\dot{\mu}_1 = \omega_p \mu_2, \qquad \dot{\mu}_2 = -\omega_p \mu_1$$

**Step 4 — Solve the coupled linear ODEs:**
Differentiate $\dot{\mu}_1$ once more: $\ddot{\mu}_1 = \omega_p \dot{\mu}_2 = -\omega_p^2 \mu_1$.
Thus $\mu_1(t) = A\cos(\omega_p t + \phi)$ and $\mu_2(t) = -A\sin(\omega_p t + \phi)$.

**Step 5 — Identify the physical motion:**
The transverse angular momentum $\mu_\perp = (\mu_1, \mu_2, 0)$ has constant magnitude $\sqrt{\mu_1^2 + \mu_2^2} = A$ and rotates about the 3-axis with angular frequency $\omega_p$. The **body angular momentum precesses steadily** about the symmetry axis — this is the geometric picture of the Hamilton vector field flow on the sphere $\vert \mu \vert^2 = \text{const}$ in $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$.

**Step 6 — Casimir verification:**
The Casimir $C = \frac{1}{2}\vert \mu \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}(A^2 + M_\parallel^2) = \text{const}$ is automatically satisfied, consistent with the Lie-Poisson structure.

**(7 marks: 1 for reduction, 1 for $\mu_3$ constant, 1 for precession frequency, 2 for explicit solution, 1 for physical interpretation, 1 for Casimir check)**
