## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** The Hamilton vector field on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^*$ gives $X_H = \mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$, but Euler's equations are conventionally written as $\dot{\mu} = \mu \times \Omega$. **Always verify the sign** — a sign flip on any component will cost full marks on that part.
> 2. **Confusing $\Omega_i$ with $\mu_i$:** Students frequently interchange angular velocity and angular momentum. Remember: $\mu_i = I_i \Omega_i$, not $\mu_i = \Omega_i$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Legendre Transform Step:** Skipping the transition from Lagrangian to Hamiltonian formulation loses method marks. Always state: $p_i = \partial L / \partial \dot{q}^i$, then $H = \sum p_i \dot{q}^i - L$.
> 4. **Missing the Symplectic 2-Form:** In canonical derivations, the explicit form of $\omega = \sum dq^i \wedge dp_i$ must be written out. Writing only $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ without specifying $\omega$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping Energy Conservation Proof:** The verification that $dH/dt = 0$ is a **mandatory** concluding step in any Euler-equation derivation. Examiners allocate 1 full mark for this alone.
> 6. **Component Order in Euler Equations:** Always write components in the order $(1, 2, 3)$ cyclically consistent with $(I_2 - I_3)$, $(I_3 - I_1)$, $(I_1 - I_2)$ — not in any other permutation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hamilton vector field definition:** $i_{X_H}\omega = dH$ is the master equation; everything flows from it.
- **Canonical form:** $X_H = \sum_i (\partial H/\partial p_i)\,\partial_{q^i} - (\partial H/\partial q^i)\,\partial_{p_i}$.
- **Euler top Hamiltonian:** $H = \frac{1}{2}\mu^T \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$ in principal body axes.
- **Reduced vector field:** $X_H(\mu) = \mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$ on $\mathfrak{so}(3)^* \cong \mathbb{R}^3$.
- **Euler equations:** Three nonlinear coupled ODEs in $(\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3)$ with coupling coefficients $(1/I_i - 1/I_j)$.
- **Lie-Poisson bracket:** $\{f,g\} = -\mu \cdot (\nabla f \times \nabla g)$ is the geometric origin of the cross-product structure.
- **Conserved quantities:** Energy $H$ and Casimir $C = \frac{1}{2}\vert \mu \vert^2$ are both constants of motion.
- **Symmetric top simplification:** $\mu_3 = $ const; transverse components execute uniform precession with frequency $\omega_p = (1/I_\perp - 1/I_\parallel)\mu_3$.
- **Physical meaning:** $X_H$ generates the *flow of rigid body rotation* in phase space — geometrically, it is the infinitesimal rotation $\mu \to \mu + \epsilon\,\mu \times \mathbb{I}^{-1}\mu$.
- **Engineering relevance:** Spacecraft attitude dynamics, free-floating robots, gyroscopes, and geometric control all rely on this exact Hamilton vector field structure.
- **Exam mantra:** "Symplectic form, interior product, Hamilton field, Euler equations — in that order."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrange's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lagrange's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lagrange's Equation (Euler-Lagrange Equation):** A scalar, energy-based formulation used to derive the equations of motion of a multi-degree-of-freedom mechanical system directly in terms of a chosen set of **generalized coordinates** $q_i$, the system's **kinetic energy** $T$, and its **potential energy** $V$.

For an $n$-DOF robotic manipulator, the scalar form of Lagrange's equation is written as:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

where:
- $L = T - V$ is the **Lagrangian** of the system.
- $q_i$ is the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate (joint variable).
- $\dot{q}_i$ is its time derivative (joint velocity).
- $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force** (joint torque/force) associated with $q_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In robot dynamics, the vector-matrix form of Lagrange's equation directly yields the **manipulator dynamic model**:
> $$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$
> This is a **board-exam favorite** (worth 7-14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a roller-coaster track. You do not need Newton's laws and contact forces at every wheel to predict the car's motion — you only need its **total kinetic energy** (motion) and **total potential energy** (height). If you subtract them and apply a simple "rule of least action," the entire motion of the coaster pops out automatically.

Lagrange's equation does **exactly that for robots**:
- Forget every internal joint force, gear friction, or link interaction.
- Just compute **how much energy the robot stores** (kinetic) and **how much gravitational energy it has** (potential).
- Feed that into one elegant equation, and the joint accelerations $\ddot{q}_i$ — and hence the torques required to drive the robot — fall out automatically.

It is, in essence, the **"energy bookkeeping" approach** to robot dynamics.

## 1.3 Why Lagrange's Formulation Over Newton-Euler?

> [!TIP]
> - **Newton-Euler**: Force/torque balance at every link → recursive, fast for simulation, but messy symbolically.
> - **Lagrange-Euler**: Energy-based, scalar-by-scalar → clean symbolic form, ideal for deriving compact dynamic models and designing model-based controllers (e.g., computed-torque control).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Prerequisites (KTU 2024 Expected Background)

Before applying Lagrange's equation, the student **must** be able to:
1. Express the **position and velocity of every link's center of mass** in terms of joint variables $q_i$.
2. Compute the **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}_i$ of each link's center of mass.
3. Recall the scalar kinetic energy of a rigid body:
   $$T_i = \frac{1}{2}\,m_i\, \mathbf{v}_{c_i}^T \mathbf{v}_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\, \boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$$
4. Recall that potential energy for gravity is $V_i = m_i\, g\, h_{c_i}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure (The "Lagrange Recipe")

The KTU 2024 examiner rewards students who follow a **structured, repeating procedure** rather than ad-hoc derivations. Use this 5-step recipe for every problem:

1. **Select generalized coordinates** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ (joint variables for serial manipulators).
2. **Write the total kinetic energy** $T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ and put it in **quadratic form**:
   $$T = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$
3. **Write the total potential energy** $V(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$ as a function of $\mathbf{q}$ only (no velocities).
4. **Form the Lagrangian** $L = T - V$.
5. **Apply the Euler-Lagrange operator** to each $q_i$:
   $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

## 2.3 Identification of the Dynamic Sub-Matrices

After expanding the scalar equations, the result is **always** rearranged into canonical robot form. The three KTU-mandatory matrices are:

- **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — Mass/Inertia Matrix** (symmetric, positive definite, $n \times n$):
  $$M_{ij}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left[ m_k\, \left(\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)}\right)^T \mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + \left(\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)}\right)^T \mathbf{I}_k\, \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)} \right]$$
- **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — Coriolis & Centrifugal Matrix** (not unique; $n \times n$):
  $$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i}\right)\dot{q}_k$$
  (This is the **Christoffel symbol** form, the KTU-preferred representation.)
- **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — Gravity Vector** ($n \times 1$):
  $$G_i(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks Pitfall:** Many students write $G_i = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$. The sign convention is **$+ \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$** when $V$ is the gravitational potential energy measured **upward** from a reference. Always check your derivation direction by testing a simple 1-DOF pendulum.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Remarks / Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Lagrangian definition | $L = T - V$ | Scalar energy difference (Joules) |
| 2 | Euler-Lagrange equation (scalar) | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | $i = 1,\dots,n$ (one per DOF) |
| 3 | Euler-Lagrange (vector) | $\frac{d}{dt}\nabla_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}}L - \nabla_{\mathbf{q}}L = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Compact vector form |
| 4 | Quadratic KE form | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Defines M-matrix |
| 5 | Mass matrix element | $M_{ij} = \sum_k \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{T}_k}{\partial q_i} \mathbf{I}_k \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}_k^T}{\partial q_j}\right)$ | Alternative trace form |
| 6 | Coriolis (Christoffel) | $C_{ij} = \sum_k \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial_k M_{ij} + \partial_j M_{ik} - \partial_i M_{kj}\right)\dot{q}_k$ | Where $\partial_k \equiv \frac{\partial}{\partial q_k}$ |
| 7 | Gravity vector | $G_i = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | $V = \sum m_i g h_{c_i}$ |
| 8 | Manipulator equation | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Canonical robot form |
| 9 | Skew-symmetry property | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric | Used in passivity proofs |
| 10 | Generalized force | $\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Equivalent expanded form |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Lagrange's Formulation |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Industrial Robotics** | Model-based control (computed-torque, adaptive control) of 6-DOF arms in assembly lines. |
| **Space Robotics** | Dynamic modeling of free-floating manipulators for satellite servicing. |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Equations of motion for 20+ DOF bipeds (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas). |
| **Vehicle Dynamics** | Suspension modeling, car chassis analysis. |
| **Biomechanics** | Human gait analysis, prosthesis design. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The General Vector-Matrix Expansion (Rigorous Derivation)

We now expand the scalar Euler-Lagrange equation in full to show **exactly** how the manipulator equation emerges. This is the derivation that earns full 14-mark credit in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Start with the Lagrangian

We begin with $L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})$, where $T$ is in quadratic form:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\, \dot{q}_i\, \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k}$

Since $V$ does not depend on $\dot{q}_k$, only $T$ contributes. Differentiating the quadratic form:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\left(\delta_{ik}\dot{q}_j + \dot{q}_i \delta_{jk}\right)
$$

Using Kronecker-delta substitution $\delta_{ik}$ (which is 1 when $i=k$, else 0):

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{ik}\dot{q}_i
$$

Because $M_{kj} = M_{jk}$ (symmetry of mass matrix), the two sums are equal:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the total time derivative

Applying the chain rule $\frac{d}{dt} = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\ddot{q}_j$, we get:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j
$$

The first term is $\sum_j M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j$ (the linear-in-acceleration term). The second term contains products of velocities.

### Step 4 — Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k}$

Since $\dot{q}_i$ are independent of $q_k$ (generalized coordinates and their velocities are independent variables), and $V$ is independent of $\dot{q}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} - \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k}
$$

The potential term gives the gravity vector directly:
$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k} = G_k(\mathbf{q})
$$

The kinetic term is:
$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Euler-Lagrange equation

Substituting Steps 3 and 4 into $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_k} - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_k} = \tau_k$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j + G_k = \tau_k
$$

### Step 6 — Index gymnastics to extract the Coriolis matrix

The two double-sum velocity terms must be combined and re-indexed. Use the symmetry trick: relabel $i \leftrightarrow m$ in the first double sum:

$$
\sum_{j}\sum_{m} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

Now subtract the third term. The coefficient of $\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}
$$

By cyclic index relabeling (swap $i \leftrightarrow j$ in half the term) and using $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$, this can be written as:

$$
\sum_{i,j} \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 7 — Define the Christoffel symbol $C_{kj}$

$$
C_{kj}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial M_{ki}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

Notice that $C_{kj}$ is **linear in $\dot{q}$** (since one $\dot{q}$ remains free per double sum). Therefore the entire velocity-product term is $\sum_j C_{kj}\dot{q}_j$.

### Step 8 — Final canonical form

The complete vector-matrix manipulator equation is:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}}
$$

This is the **master equation** that every KTU board question on this topic ultimately reduces to.

## 3.2 Worked Mini-Derivation — 2R Planar Manipulator (Scaffolding)

To give you a complete worked template (which the examiner expects on a 14-mark problem), here is the **skeleton** for a planar 2R arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, and inertias $I_1, I_2$.

**Position of link 1's center of mass:**
$$
x_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1, \qquad y_{c_1} = \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1
$$

**Position of link 2's center of mass:**
$$
x_{c_2} = l_1 \cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \qquad y_{c_2} = l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Velocities** are obtained by time-differentiation (e.g., $\dot{x}_{c_1} = -\tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1$, etc.). The **Jacobians** $\mathbf{J}_{v_1}, \mathbf{J}_{v_2}$ of the two centers of mass are then:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{v_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 & 0 \\[4pt] \frac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{J}_{v_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) & -\frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \\[4pt] l_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) & \frac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Mass matrix** (assembled by $M_{ij} = \sum_k \left[m_k \mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(i)\,T}\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{(j)} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(i)\,T}\mathbf{I}_k \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{(j)}\right]$):

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where

$$
M_{11} = \left(\frac{m_1}{4} + m_2\right)l_1^2 + m_2\,l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 + \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_1
$$

$$
M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + \frac{m_2\,l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2
$$

$$
M_{22} = \frac{m_2 l_2^2}{4} + I_2
$$

**Gravity vector** (with gravity $g$ acting downward, $V = m_1 g \frac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g\left[l_1 \sin q_1 + \frac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]$):

$$
G_1 = \left(\frac{m_1 l_1}{2} + m_2 l_1\right) g \cos q_1 + \frac{m_2 l_2}{2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
G_2 = \frac{m_2 l_2}{2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Coriolis matrix** (from Christoffel symbols):

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 & -\frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 \\[4pt] \frac{m_2 l_1 l_2}{2}\sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark question, the examiner will give you a specific manipulator and expect you to **explicitly show** all five steps: choose coordinates, write $T$, write $V$, form $L$, then take derivatives. Do not skip the Lagrangian stage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concept to Operation

| KTU Concept | Mathematical Tool | Output Symbol | Matrix/Vector Size |
|-------------|-------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| Generalized coordinates | Independent variables | $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n \times 1$ |
| Lagrangian | Scalar function | $L = T - V$ | $1 \times 1$ (scalar) |
| Euler-Lagrange operator | Differential operator | $\mathcal{L}_i \equiv \frac{d}{dt}\partial_{\dot{q}_i} - \partial_{q_i}$ | Scalar per DOF |
| Generalized force | Energy conjugate | $\tau_i$ | $n \times 1$ |
| Inertia effect | Quadratic form in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times n$ |
| Velocity coupling | Bilinear in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | $n \times n$ |
| Gravity loading | Gradient of $V$ | $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | $n \times 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Applying Lagrange's Equation to a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Define generalized coordinates q1 q2 ... qn] --> B[STEP 2: Locate every link center of mass in terms of q]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Compute linear and angular Jacobians Jv Jw]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Assemble total kinetic energy T half qdotT M q qdot]
    C --> E[STEP 4b: Assemble total potential energy V from m g h]
    D --> F[STEP 5: Form Lagrangian L equals T minus V]
    E --> F
    F --> G[STEP 6: Apply Euler Lagrange operator to each qi]
    G --> H[STEP 7: Expand and identify M q C q qdot G q]
    H --> I[STEP 8: Write canonical manipulator equation M q qddot plus C q qdot qdot plus G q equals tau]
    I --> J[OUTPUT: Dynamic model ready for control synthesis]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style I fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Lagrange-Based Controller Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        QD[Desired Trajectory qd qdotd qdddotd]
        Q[Measured State q qdot]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[DYNAMIC MODEL]
        MBLOCK[M q matrix Inertia]
        CBLOCK[C q qdot matrix Coriolis]
        GBLOCK[G q vector Gravity]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[INVERSE DYNAMICS]
        CALC[tau equals M qddot plus C qdot plus G]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[ROBOT RESPONSE]
        ROBOT[Robot Manipulator]
        SENSOR[Encoder / IMU Feedback]
    end

    QD --> CALC
    MBLOCK --> CALC
    CBLOCK --> CALC
    GBLOCK --> CALC
    CALC --> ROBOT
    ROBOT --> SENSOR
    SENSOR --> Q
    Q --> MBLOCK
    Q --> CBLOCK
    Q --> GBLOCK

    style CALC fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#ff6f00
    style ROBOT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style MODEL fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
```

## 4.3 Concept-to-Equation Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Functions T and V] --> B[Lagrangian L equals T minus V]
    B --> C[Scalar Euler Lagrange eqn]
    C --> D[Vector Matrix Form]
    D --> E[M q qddot]
    D --> F[C q qdot qdot]
    D --> G[G q]
    E --> H[Canonical Manipulator Equation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Computed Torque Control tau equals M v plus C qdot plus G]
    H --> J[Simulation via numerical integration]

    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style I fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the **Lagrangian function** $L$ used in deriving the equations of motion of a robotic manipulator. Why is it preferred over force-balance methods for symbolic dynamic modeling?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Lagrangian** is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy and the total potential energy of the system:

$$
L(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})
$$

It is preferred over Newton-Euler force-balance methods because:

1. It is a **scalar** formulation → no vector cross-products or frame transformations are needed at intermediate steps.
2. It automatically accounts for **constraint forces** (no need to enumerate reaction forces at each joint).
3. The final result is a **closed-form symbolic dynamic model** $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, ideal for control design.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: scalar property; 1 mark: control-design utility.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State the scalar form of **Euler-Lagrange's equation** and clearly identify the meaning of every symbol. What are **generalized coordinates**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The scalar form is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

- $L = T - V$ — Lagrangian (scalar, in Joules).
- $q_i$ — $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate (independent variable, in rad or m).
- $\dot{q}_i$ — generalized velocity (rad/s or m/s).
- $\tau_i$ — generalized force conjugate to $q_i$ (N·m or N).

**Generalized coordinates** are a **minimum set of independent variables** that completely specify the configuration of a multi-DOF system. For a serial robot, the natural choice is the **set of joint variables** $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$. Their number equals the number of degrees of freedom.

*(1 mark: equation; 1 mark: symbol meaning; 1 mark: generalized coordinates concept.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Module-2 question for 14 marks carries an **internal choice**. The two questions below are independent alternatives — answer **either one in full**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — 2R Planar Manipulator via Lagrange

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Choice Q1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Consider a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, link masses $m_1, m_2$ concentrated at the distal ends, joint variables $q_1, q_2$, and gravity $g$ acting downward. Using **Lagrange's formulation**, derive the **kinetic energy**, **potential energy**, and the **Lagrangian** of the system.

**(b) [7 marks]** From the Lagrangian obtained in part (a), derive the **complete manipulator equation** in canonical form $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, identifying each matrix element explicitly.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Energies and Lagrangian**

Since masses are at the distal ends, the position of mass 1 is $(x_1, y_1) = (l_1\cos q_1,\, l_1\sin q_1)$. The position of mass 2 is:

$$
x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2), \qquad y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

Velocities (differentiating w.r.t. time):

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -l_1\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1, \qquad \dot{y}_1 = l_1\cos q_1\, \dot{q}_1
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -l_1\sin q_1\, \dot{q}_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y}_2 = l_1\cos q_1\, \dot{q}_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Squared speeds:

$$
\dot{x}_1^2 + \dot{y}_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Total kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
$$

Total potential energy (heights above base, gravity $g$ downward):

$$
V = m_1 g\, l_1\sin q_1 + m_2 g\left[l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

Lagrangian $L = T - V$:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2 l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) - (m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\sin q_1 - m_2 g\, l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of the Canonical Equation**

Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\, \dot{q}_2
$$

Its time derivative is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = \left[(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right]\ddot{q}_1 + \left[m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right]\ddot{q}_2 - 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2^2
$$

Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1} = -(m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\cos q_1 - m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

Subtracting per the Euler-Lagrange equation, the **first joint equation** becomes:

$$
\boxed{M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{11}\dot{q}_1 + C_{12}\dot{q}_2 + G_1 = \tau_1}
$$

Similarly, the **second joint equation** is:

$$
\boxed{M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{21}\dot{q}_1 + C_{22}\dot{q}_2 + G_2 = \tau_2}
$$

The identified matrices are:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \\ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} -2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_2 \\[4pt] m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2\, \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)g\,l_1\cos q_1 + m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 g\, l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The final compact form is:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Pendulum-Cart System

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Q2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A **point mass** $m$ is mounted on a **massless cart** of mass $M$ that slides on a horizontal frictionless track. The pendulum has length $l$ and angle $\theta$ measured from the vertical. Using **Lagrange's equation**, derive expressions for the **kinetic energy, potential energy, and Lagrangian** in generalized coordinates $(x, \theta)$, where $x$ is the cart displacement.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Lagrangian, derive the **two coupled equations of motion** and express them in the matrix form $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. Show the explicit form of $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ for this system.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Energies and Lagrangian**

Position of the bob:
$$
x_b = x + l\sin\theta, \qquad y_b = -l\cos\theta
$$

Velocities (time-differentiated):
$$
\dot{x}_b = \dot{x} + l\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta}, \qquad \dot{y}_b = l\sin\theta\, \dot{\theta}
$$

Total kinetic energy (cart + bob):

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}M\dot{x}^2 + \frac{1}{2}m\!\left[(\dot{x}+l\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta})^2 + (l\sin\theta\, \dot{\theta})^2\right]
$$

Expanding $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}(M+m)\dot{x}^2 + m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + \frac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2
$$

Potential energy (taking the pivot as reference, gravity downward):

$$
V = -mgl\cos\theta
$$

Lagrangian $L = T - V$:

$$
\boxed{L = \frac{1}{2}(M+m)\dot{x}^2 + m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + \frac{1}{2}m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + mgl\cos\theta}
$$

**Part (b) — Equations of Motion**

**Equation for $x$:** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = (M+m)\dot{x} + ml\cos\theta\, \dot{\theta}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 0$. Hence:

$$
(M+m)\ddot{x} + m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{\theta} - m l \sin\theta\, \dot{\theta}^2 = F
$$

**Equation for $\theta$:** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m l \cos\theta\, \dot{x} + m l^2 \dot{\theta}$. Time derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}\right) = m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{x} - m l \sin\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} + m l^2 \ddot{\theta}
$$

And $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = -m l \sin\theta\, \dot{x}\dot{\theta} - mgl\sin\theta$. Hence:

$$
m l \cos\theta\, \ddot{x} + m l^2 \ddot{\theta} + mgl\sin\theta = \tau
$$

Stacking in matrix form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ with $\mathbf{q} = [x, \theta]^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = [F, \tau]^T$:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} M+m & m l \cos\theta \\ m l \cos\theta & m l^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -m l \sin\theta\, \dot{\theta} \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -mgl\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign of $G$:** Always check with a **static case** ($\dot{q} = \ddot{q} = 0$). For the pendulum hanging vertically ($\theta = 0$), $G_2 = 0$ — torque needed is zero. ✓
> 2. **Symmetry of $\mathbf{M}$:** If you ever find $M_{12} \neq M_{21}$, you have an algebra error. The mass matrix is **always symmetric**.
> 3. **Units:** Joint torque $\tau$ is in **N·m** for revolute joints and **N** for prismatic joints. Mixed systems (like the cart-pendulum) are common exam topics.
> 4. **Skipping $L$:** Do not jump from $T, V$ directly to the final equation. The Lagrangian $L = T - V$ **must be written explicitly** as a 1-mark reward in ESE valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lagrangian:** $L = T - V$ (kinetic minus potential energy). Always in Joules.
- **Scalar form:** $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$.
- **Vector form:** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ — the **canonical manipulator equation**.
- **Generalized coordinates $q_i$:** Independent, minimum-set variables (joint angles/displacements for serial robots).
- **Mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$:** Symmetric, positive-definite, $n \times n$, depends only on configuration $\mathbf{q}$.
- **Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}$:** Linear in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, captures Coriolis + centripetal effects; not unique.
- **Gravity vector $\mathbf{G}$:** $G_i = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$; depends only on $\mathbf{q}$ (and gravity $g$).
- **Christoffel symbol form (KTU-preferred):** $C_{ij} = \sum_k \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial_k M_{ij} + \partial_j M_{ik} - \partial_i M_{kj}\right)\dot{q}_k$.
- **Skew-symmetry property:** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric → used in stability/passivity proofs.
- **Procedure to remember:** (1) choose $q$, (2) write $T$ in quadratic form, (3) write $V$, (4) form $L = T - V$, (5) apply Euler-Lagrange, (6) identify $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$.
- **Static check:** With $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$, the equation must reduce to $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{static}}$.
- **Exam mantra:** Always show **all five steps explicitly**; do not skip the Lagrangian; verify $\mathbf{M}$ symmetry before writing the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Vector and Equation Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State Vector and Equation Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, a **State Vector** is the minimum set of independent variables (generalized coordinates and their time derivatives) that completely describe the dynamic condition of a robotic manipulator at any given instant of time. For an **n-DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** serial-chain manipulator, the state vector is conventionally defined as:

$$x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} q(t) \\ \dot{q}(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$$

where $q(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of **joint position variables** and $\dot{q}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of **joint velocity variables**.

**State Equation Formulation** is the systematic mathematical procedure of converting the second-order **Lagrangian/Euler-Lagrange equations of motion** of a manipulator into an equivalent system of first-order ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of the canonical form $\dot{x} = f(x, u, t)$, augmented by an output equation $y = h(x, u, t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The state vector is NOT just "all variables you can measure." It is a *minimal* set, meaning it must satisfy two properties — **completeness** (future behavior is uniquely determined given the present state and future inputs) and **minimality** (no component can be removed without losing completeness). The **Markov Property** governs this.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. To predict where the car will be in the next 5 seconds, what information do you *absolutely need*?

- **Position** (where you are on the road) 
- **Velocity** (how fast you are going)
- **Acceleration** would be derivable from current forces, so it's redundant
- The color of the car? Irrelevant — it doesn't affect motion

So the *state* of the car is $[x, \dot{x}]$ — the position and velocity. Knowing only position (without velocity) is insufficient because you don't know whether the car is parked or zooming past. Knowing velocity alone doesn't tell you *where* the car is.

For a robot arm, the same logic applies: knowing the **joint angles** alone does not tell us where the end-effector is heading; we also need **joint velocities**. Together, they form the state. The **state equation** is then the rule book that says: "Given where you are now and what torques you apply, here is how the state will evolve in the next infinitesimal time step."

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $\mathbf{g = 9.81 \, m/s^2}$
> - Inertia matrix $M(q)$ is symmetric positive-definite: $M(q) = M(q)^T \succ 0$
> - Degrees of Freedom $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, typically $n = 2, 3, 6$ for academic manipulators (planar 2R, 3R, or full 6-DOF PUMA-like arms)

## 1.3 Why Do We Convert to State-Space Form?

The Euler-Lagrange equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$ is a **second-order, nonlinear, coupled** ODE. Classical control techniques (PID, pole-placement, LQR, observers) are designed for **first-order state-space systems**. The state-space conversion is the universal "translator" that lets us apply modern control theory to robotic manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Starting Point — The Manipulator Equation

The closed-form dynamic model of an n-DOF rigid serial manipulator is:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) = \tau$$

where the terms are rigorously defined below.

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension | Physical Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $q$ | Vector of joint positions | $n \times 1$ | Generalized coordinates |
| $\dot{q}$ | Vector of joint velocities | $n \times 1$ | Rate of change of configuration |
| $\ddot{q}$ | Vector of joint accelerations | $n \times 1$ | Required for forward dynamics |
| $M(q)$ | Inertia matrix (symmetric, PD) | $n \times n$ | Captures kinetic energy coupling |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ | Coriolis & centrifugal vector | $n \times 1$ | Velocity-dependent inertial forces |
| $G(q)$ | Gravity loading vector | $n \times 1$ | Potential energy gradient |
| $F(\dot{q})$ | Friction vector (viscous/Coulomb) | $n \times 1$ | Energy dissipation |
| $\tau$ | Generalized force/torque vector | $n \times 1$ | Control input |

The aggregated term $h(q,\dot{q}) = C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$ groups all non-inertial effects.

## 2.2 Decomposition into Two First-Order Systems — The Canonical Procedure

We introduce the **auxiliary variable** $x_2 = \dot{q}$ to reduce order. The system is then split into two coupled first-order ODEs:

**Step 1 — Kinematic Subsystem (Trivial):** The definition of velocity is its own time derivative.

$$\dot{q} = \dot{q} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Subsystem (From the Manipulator Equation):** Solve $M(q)\ddot{q} = \tau - h(q,\dot{q})$ for $\ddot{q}$, then substitute $x_1, x_2$.

$$\ddot{q} = M(q)^{-1}\left[\tau - h(q,\dot{q})\right]$$

Since $M(q)$ is symmetric positive-definite, $M(q)^{-1}$ always exists (a non-negotiable property of physical systems). Hence:

$$\dot{x}_2 = M(x_1)^{-1}\left[\tau - h(x_1, x_2)\right]$$

## 2.3 The Compact State-Space Representation

Stacking the two subsystems gives the **nonlinear autonomous-affine state equation**:

$$\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u$$

with the explicit identification:

- State vector: $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \dot{q} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$
- Control input: $u = \tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$
- Drift vector field: $f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -M(x_1)^{-1} h(x_1, x_2) \end{bmatrix}$
- Input vector field: $g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{n \times n} \\ M(x_1)^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$

And the **output equation** (typically choosing joint positions as outputs):

$$y = h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} I_{n \times n} & 0_{n \times n} \end{bmatrix} x = x_1 = q$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these expressions verbatim — they are high-yield for KTU ESE.**

| # | Equation | Name | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $x = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \dot{q} \end{bmatrix}$ | State vector definition | Starting point of all state-space derivations |
| 2 | $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$ | Velocity subsystem | Always true by definition |
| 3 | $\dot{x}_2 = M^{-1}(\tau - h)$ | Acceleration subsystem | Solved from manipulator equation |
| 4 | $M(q) = M^T(q) \succ 0$ | Inertia matrix property | Guarantees invertibility |
| 5 | $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$ | Nonlinear affine form | Standard control-design form |
| 6 | $y = Cx$ | Output equation (linear) | $C = \begin{bmatrix} I \mid 0 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 7 | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $\,y = Cx + Du$ | LTI form (after linearization) | Enables pole-placement, LQR |
| 8 | $A = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x^*,u^*}$ | Jacobian linearization | For local stability/stabilization |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The state-space representation is the **universal language of modern control**. In industry, this formulation enables:

- **Computed-Torque Control:** Inverting $M(q)$ in real-time at every control cycle (1 kHz typical).
- **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator):** Designing optimal feedback gains $K$ for trajectory tracking.
- **State Observers (Luenberger / EKF):** Estimating unmeasured states like joint velocities from position encoders.
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Predicting state evolution over a horizon.
- **Robust $H_{\infty}$ Control:** Used in surgical robots (da Vinci) and space manipulators (Canadarm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: 2R Planar Manipulator in State-Space Form

This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** in Module 2. We derive the full state-space form of a 2R planar manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$, link lengths $l_1, l_2$, and joint variables $q_1, q_2$.

### 3.1.1 The Known Inertia Matrix and Bias Vector

From Module 1 (Lagrangian formulation), the entries of the dynamic model for the 2R planar arm are:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha + 2\beta\cos(q_2) & \delta + \beta\cos(q_2) \\ \delta + \beta\cos(q_2) & \delta \end{bmatrix}$$

with the auxiliary constants:

- $\alpha = (m_1 + m_2)\,l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + I_1 + I_2$
- $\beta = m_2 \, l_1 l_2$
- $\delta = m_2 l_2^2 + I_2$

And the Coriolis + Gravity vector:

$$h(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -\beta(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2)\sin(q_2) \\ \beta\dot{q}_1^2\sin(q_2) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)g l_1 \cos(q_1) + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.2 Defining the State Vector

By the convention established:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$$

### 3.1.3 Splitting into Two First-Order Systems

**Subsystem A (Kinematic):**

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x}_1 &= x_3 \\ \dot{x}_2 &= x_4 \end{aligned}$$

These are obtained by the *definition* of velocity: $\dot{q}_i = x_{i+2}$.

**Subsystem B (Dynamic):** We must compute $M(q)^{-1}$ explicitly.

$$\det M(q) = \alpha\delta - (\delta + \beta\cos q_2)^2$$

The adjugate matrix:

$$\text{adj}(M) = \begin{bmatrix} \delta & -(\delta + \beta\cos q_2) \\ -(\delta + \beta\cos q_2) & \alpha + 2\beta\cos q_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Hence:

$$M^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det M}\begin{bmatrix} \delta & -(\delta + \beta\cos q_2) \\ -(\delta + \beta\cos q_2) & \alpha + 2\beta\cos q_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now apply the acceleration formula $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(\tau - h)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x}_3 &= \ddot{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\det M}\Big[\delta(\tau_1 - h_1) - (\delta + \beta\cos x_2)(h_2 - \tau_2)\Big] \\ \dot{x}_4 &= \ddot{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\det M}\Big[-(\delta + \beta\cos x_2)(\tau_1 - h_1) + (\alpha + 2\beta\cos x_2)(h_2 - \tau_2)\Big] \end{aligned}$$

where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the first and second components of the bias vector $h(q,\dot{q})$ written in terms of the state $x$.

### 3.1.4 The Complete Nonlinear State-Space Form

The full nonlinear state equation is:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \\ \dot{x}_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_3 \\ x_4 \\ f_3(x) + g_{31}(x)\tau_1 + g_{32}(x)\tau_2 \\ f_4(x) + g_{41}(x)\tau_1 + g_{42}(x)\tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the four scalar nonlinear functions are:

$$\begin{aligned} f_3(x) &= \frac{1}{\det M}\Big[-\delta\, h_1(x) + (\delta + \beta\cos x_2)\, h_2(x)\Big] \\ f_4(x) &= \frac{1}{\det M}\Big[(\delta + \beta\cos x_2)\, h_1(x) - (\alpha + 2\beta\cos x_2)\, h_2(x)\Big] \\ g_{31}(x) &= \frac{\delta}{\det M}, \quad g_{32}(x) = \frac{-(\delta + \beta\cos x_2)}{\det M} \\ g_{41}(x) &= \frac{-(\delta + \beta\cos x_2)}{\det M}, \quad g_{42}(x) = \frac{\alpha + 2\beta\cos x_2}{\det M} \end{aligned}$$

This is the **exam-ready form**. For full marks, students must show the determinant $\det M$ and the substituted $h_1, h_2$ expressions.

## 3.2 Linearization About an Operating Point

For LQR or pole-placement design, we linearize the nonlinear system about an equilibrium point $(x^*, u^*)$ using the **Jacobian**.

$$A = \left.\frac{\partial f(x,u)}{\partial x}\right|_{(x^*,u^*)}, \quad B = \left.\frac{\partial f(x,u)}{\partial u}\right|_{(x^*,u^*)}$$

For a general n-DOF manipulator at equilibrium $x^* = (q^*, 0)$, the linearized matrices have the form:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{n \times n} & I_{n \times n} \\ -M^{-1}\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\Big\vert_{q^*} & 0_{n \times n} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{n \times n} \\ M(q^*)^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

The $\dot{x}_2$ block expands as:

$$M(q)\delta\ddot{q} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial q}\bigg\vert_{q^*}\delta q + \frac{\partial h}{\partial \dot{q}}\bigg\vert_{q^*}\delta\dot{q} = \delta\tau$$

At equilibrium $\dot{q}^* = 0$, the Coriolis term $C(q^*,0)\cdot 0 = 0$ vanishes, leaving only the gravity-gradient matrix $\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Mini-Example (for clarity)

Consider a **single-link manipulator** with $q$ the only joint, mass $m$, length $l$, inertia $I$, under gravity $g$. The scalar dynamic model is:

$$(I + m l^2)\ddot{q} + m g l \sin(q) = \tau$$

Define state $x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \dot{q} \end{bmatrix}$. The state-space form is:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ \frac{1}{I + m l^2}\big(\tau - m g l \sin(x_1)\big) \end{bmatrix}$$

Linearize about the downward equilibrium $x^* = (\pi, 0)$ (using $\sin(\pi) = 0$ and $\cos(\pi) = -1$):

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ \frac{m g l}{I + m l^2} & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{I + m l^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the classic **inverted-pendulum-on-cart** style model, foundational to robotic balance and pendulum control problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification tip:** At the *downward* equilibrium $q^* = 0$, $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\cos(0) = +1$, giving $A_{21} = -\frac{mgl}{I+ml^2}$ (stable — pendulum hanging). At the *upward* equilibrium $q^* = \pi$, the sign flips, giving a positive $A_{21}$ (unstable — inverted pendulum). Always check signs in the gravity-gradient term.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of State-Space Formulation

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete information flow** from the physical manipulator to the linearized control-ready state-space model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical n-DOF Manipulator] --> B[Euler-Lagrange or Newton-Euler Formulation]
    B --> C[Second-Order Manipulator Equation: Mq_ddot + h = tau]
    C --> D{State Vector Selection}
    D -->|Define x1 = q, x2 = q_dot| E[First Kinematic Subsystem: x1_dot = x2]
    C --> F[Matrix Inversion: M_inverse]
    F --> G[Acceleration Subsystem: x2_dot = M_inv times tau_minus_h]
    E --> H[Nonlinear State Equation: x_dot = f_x + g_x times u]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Equation: y = C x = q]
    H --> J{Jacobian Linearization at x_star, u_star}
    I --> J
    J --> K[Linear Time-Invariant Form: x_dot = A x + B u]
    K --> L[Modern Control Design: LQR / Pole-Placement / MPC]
    L --> M[Computed-Torque Control Law: tau = M v + h]
    M --> N[Torque Commands to Actuators]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Detailed Decomposition Diagram — Kinematic vs Dynamic Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KinematicBlock [Kinematic Subsystem — Order n]
        K1[State x1 = q] --> K2[x1_dot = x2]
        K2 --> K3[Position Update via Integration]
    end
    subgraph DynamicBlock [Dynamic Subsystem — Order n]
        D1[State x2 = q_dot] --> D2[Manipulator Equation Mq_ddot + h = tau]
        D2 --> D3[Solve for q_ddot: q_ddot = M_inv times tau_minus_h]
        D3 --> D4[x2_dot = q_ddot]
        D4 --> D5[Velocity Update via Integration]
    end
    K1 -.feeds q into.-> D2
    D1 -.feeds q_dot into.-> D2
    K3 --> StateOut[Augmented State x in R_two_n]
    D5 --> StateOut
```

## 4.3 State-Space Block Architecture for Control Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Trajectory q_d] --> B[Trajectory Generator]
    B --> C[Error Computation: e = q_d - q]
    C --> D[State Feedback Gain Matrix K]
    D --> E[Control Law: u = -Kx + Feedforward]
    E --> F[Manipulator M q_ddot + h = tau]
    F --> G[Sensors: Encoders / Tachometers]
    G --> H[State Estimator / Observer]
    H --> I[Estimated State x_hat]
    I --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams for exams:** Block diagrams in KTU papers usually expect you to label the **dimensions** of each signal (e.g., $x \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$, $u \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $y \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$). Always include the signal-dimension annotations on the arrows for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term *state vector* in the context of robotic manipulator dynamics. Why is $x = [q, \dot{q}]^T$ the appropriate choice?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A *state vector* is the minimal set of variables that completely specifies the dynamic configuration of a system at time $t$, such that the future evolution is uniquely determined by the present state and future inputs (Markov property).
- **(1 mark)** For an n-DOF rigid manipulator, the Euler-Lagrange equation is second-order: $M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) = \tau$. To convert it to a first-order state-space system, we need both $q$ (to evaluate $M$ and $G$) and $\dot{q}$ (to evaluate $C\dot{q}$ and $F\dot{q}$). Hence $x = [q, \dot{q}]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
- **(1 mark)** Minimality is satisfied because the accelerations $\ddot{q}$ are *outputs* of the differential equation, not independent state variables — they are functions of $q, \dot{q}, \tau$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the property $M(q) = M(q)^T \succ 0$ in state-space formulation of manipulators?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** $M(q)$ being symmetric ($M^T = M$) follows from the energy conservation and the fact that the kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ is a quadratic form in $\dot{q}$.
- **(1 mark)** Positive-definiteness ($\succ 0$) means $v^T M v > 0$ for all $v \neq 0$, which physically guarantees that kinetic energy is always positive for any nonzero velocity.
- **(1 mark)** Mathematically, positive-definiteness guarantees that $M^{-1}(q)$ exists for all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$. This is what permits the crucial algebraic step $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(\tau - h)$ needed to construct the state equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Starting from the Euler-Lagrange manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$, derive the canonical nonlinear state-space form $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$ for a general n-DOF manipulator. Clearly define the state vector, drift field, and input field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2R planar manipulator with $m_1 = m_2 = m$, $l_1 = l_2 = l$, $I_1 = I_2 = 0$, and gravity $g$ acting downward, obtain the explicit state-space equation in the form $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\tau$ where $x = [q_1, q_2, \dot{q}_1, \dot{q}_2]^T$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Define the state vector (1 mark):**

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \dot{q} \end{bmatrix}, \quad x \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$$

**Step 2 — Kinematic subsystem (2 marks):** By the chain rule, the time derivative of $x_1 = q$ is $x_2 = \dot{q}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{q} = x_2$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic subsystem (3 marks):** From the manipulator equation, solve for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = M(q)^{-1}\left[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q) - F(\dot{q})\right]$$

Substituting $q = x_1$ and $\dot{q} = x_2$:

$$\dot{x}_2 = M(x_1)^{-1}\left[\tau - C(x_1, x_2)x_2 - G(x_1) - F(x_2)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compact nonlinear affine form (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ M(x_1)^{-1}\left[-C(x_1,x_2)x_2 - G(x_1) - F(x_2)\right] \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ M(x_1)^{-1} \end{bmatrix} \tau$$

This identifies the drift $f(x)$ and input $g(x)$ vector fields.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the inertia matrix (2 marks):** With $I_1 = I_2 = 0$ and equal link parameters:

$$M(q) = ml^2 \begin{bmatrix} 2 + 2\cos q_2 & 1 + \cos q_2 \\ 1 + \cos q_2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant and inverse (2 marks):**

$$\det M = ml^4\left[(2 + 2\cos q_2) - (1 + \cos q_2)^2\right] = ml^4\left[1 + 2\cos q_2 - \cos^2 q_2\right]$$

$$M^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det M}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -(1+\cos q_2) \\ -(1+\cos q_2) & 2 + 2\cos q_2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot ml^2$$

**Step 3 — Bias vector $h(q,\dot{q})$ (2 marks):** Including gravity and Coriolis terms:

$$h(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} m l^2(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2)\sin q_2 + (2m)gl\cos q_1 + mgl\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ -m l^2\dot{q}_1^2 \sin q_2 + mgl\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — State-space assembly (1 mark):** Stacking with $x_1 = q_1, x_2 = q_2, x_3 = \dot{q}_1, x_4 = \dot{q}_2$:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_3 \\ x_4 \\ f_3(x) + g_{31}\tau_1 + g_{32}\tau_2 \\ f_4(x) + g_{41}\tau_1 + g_{42}\tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f_i$ and $g_{ij}$ follow the same pattern as the general derivation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the procedure to linearize a nonlinear manipulator state equation $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ about an operating point $(x^*, u^*)$. Derive the expressions for the $A$ and $B$ matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-link manipulator with moment of inertia $I$, mass $m$, length $l$ (center of mass at $l/2$) under gravity satisfies $(I + ml^2/3)\ddot{q} + mgl\sin(q)/2 = \tau$. Linearize this about $q^* = 0$ and write the LTI state-space form. Comment on the stability of the equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Procedure:**

1. Identify an equilibrium $(x^*, u^*)$ satisfying $f(x^*, u^*) = 0$.
2. Define perturbation variables: $\delta x = x - x^*$, $\delta u = u - u^*$.
3. Taylor-expand $f$ to first order about the operating point.
4. The Jacobian $A = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\vert_{(x^*,u^*)}$ and $B = \frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\vert_{(x^*,u^*)}$ define the linearized model: $\delta\dot{x} = A\delta x + B\delta u$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Block diagram description:** A block diagram with three main blocks — (i) Nonlinear System, (ii) Operating Point Selector, (iii) Jacobian Evaluator — connected with feedback from sensors, leading to the LTI form $A, B$.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Derivation of A and B:** For the manipulator, at equilibrium $q^*$, $\dot{q}^* = 0$:

- From kinematic: $\dot{x}_1 = x_2 \Rightarrow A_{11} = 0$, $A_{12} = I_n$
- From dynamic: $\dot{x}_2 = -M^{-1}[\frac{\partial h}{\partial q}\delta q + \frac{\partial h}{\partial \dot{q}}\delta\dot{q}] + M^{-1}\delta\tau$

At $\dot{q} = 0$, Coriolis terms vanish, leaving only $\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}$:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0_n & I_n \\ -M^{-1}\frac{\partial G}{\partial q}\vert_{q^*} & 0_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0_n \\ M(q^*)^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Set up state vector:** Let $x_1 = q$, $x_2 = \dot{q}$. The nonlinear state equation is:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ \frac{1}{I + ml^2/3}\left(\tau - \frac{mgl}{2}\sin x_1\right) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Linearize:** At $q^* = 0$: $\sin(0) = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$. The Jacobian gives:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{mgl/2}{I + ml^2/3} & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{3mgl}{2(3I + ml^2)} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{I + ml^2/3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{3}{3I + ml^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Stability comment:** The eigenvalues of $A$ are $\lambda = \pm \sqrt{\frac{3mgl}{2(3I+ml^2)}}$. Since one eigenvalue is positive real, the equilibrium $q^* = 0$ (pendulum pointing *upward*) is **unstable** — the well-known inverted-pendulum instability.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Control design hint:** The system is controllable (rank of $[B \; AB] = 2$ for 2-state system), so an LQR controller $u = -Kx$ can stabilize it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dimension annotation** — Always state $x \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ and $u \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing dimensions.
> 2. **Confusing state variables with outputs** — Output $y$ is what you *measure*; state $x$ is what *completely describes* the system. They are not always identical.
> 3. **Skipping the determinant step** — When computing $M^{-1}$, always show $\det M$ explicitly. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Dropping gravity terms prematurely** — In a 2R derivation, $G(q)$ must be written with full trigonometric content before any linearization.
> 5. **Sign error in Coriolis** — Note $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ appears with a *positive* sign in $h$ but a *negative* sign in the acceleration formula $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(\tau - h)$.
> 6. **Forgetting the Markov property** — When defining state vector, always mention "minimum set of variables satisfying the Markov property." This is a 1-mark technical term examiners look for.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State vector definition** — $x = [q, \dot{q}]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ for n-DOF manipulator; minimal set, Markov property, includes generalized coordinates and their first time derivatives.
- **Why state-space?** — Converts the second-order, nonlinear, coupled Euler-Lagrange equation into a first-order system amenable to modern control (LQR, MPC, observers).
- **Two-subsystem structure** — Kinematic subsystem ($\dot{x}_1 = x_2$) is trivial; Dynamic subsystem ($\dot{x}_2 = M^{-1}(\tau - h)$) is the heart of the derivation.
- **Affine form** — $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$ is the standard for control design; $f$ is the *drift* and $g$ is the *input* vector field.
- **Invertibility of $M(q)$** — Guaranteed by symmetry and positive-definiteness; $M^{-1}$ always exists.
- **Linearization** — Done via Jacobian; equilibrium is *stable* if all eigenvalues of $A$ have non-positive real parts (Lyapunov's first method).
- **Output equation** — For joint-position control, $y = Cx$ with $C = [I_n \; 0_n]$.
- **Dimensions matter** — State is $2n$, input is $n$, output is $n$. The matrices $A, B, C$ have dimensions $2n \times 2n$, $2n \times n$, and $n \times 2n$ respectively.
- **Engineering applications** — Computed-torque control, LQR, MPC, EKF-based sensor fusion, surgical robots, and space manipulators all rely on this formulation.
- **Exam mantra** — "Show the determinant, label the dimensions, mention the Markov property, and never skip the gravity term."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Control Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** PID Control Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) Control Scheme** is a classical, industry-standard feedback control architecture that computes a corrective control action as a weighted sum of three error-based terms: the **present error (P)**, the **accumulated past error (I)**, and the **predicted future error (D)**. In the context of **robotic manipulator joint control**, the PID regulator forms the innermost feedback loop that drives each joint actuator so that the actual joint position $\theta(t)$ tracks a desired reference trajectory $\theta_d(t)$ while rejecting disturbances such as friction, payload variation, and coupling torques from neighbouring links.

In the **continuous-time Laplace domain**, the PID controller is characterized by the transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d\,s = \frac{K_d\,s^2 + K_p\,s + K_i}{s}$$

where $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ are the non-negative tunable gains (in units of N·m/rad, N·m/(rad·s), and N·m·s/rad, respectively, for a torque-controlled robot joint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The PID control scheme is treated as the baseline single-input single-output (SISO) controller used in *independent joint control* of robotic manipulators, prior to moving on to multivariable computed-torque and feedback-linearization strategies. Mastering tuning, stability margins, and anti-windup is mandatory for university examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PID matters in Robotics:** Even modern model-based controllers (computed torque, adaptive control, sliding-mode) use an inner PID loop as a robustness layer. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the student's ability to **derive**, **analyse**, and **tune** PID for a single robotic joint, including Ziegler–Nichols tuning, steady-state error analysis, and transient-response specification matching.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car-Cruise-Control on a Hilly Road"

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a road that suddenly climbs a hill.

- The **Proportional (P)** term is like *pressing the accelerator harder the further you are from 60 km/h*. The bigger the speed error, the more throttle you apply. It is fast and reactive, but on a long hill it leaves you crawling at 58 km/h — a persistent *steady-state error*.
- The **Integral (I)** term is your *memory of past mistakes*. The longer the car has been below 60 km/h, the more additional throttle you keep adding, until the error is forced to zero. It eliminates the steady-state offset but can over-shoot, making the car lurch.
- The **Derivative (D)** term is your *anticipation of the future*. If the speed is dropping rapidly, you push the accelerator *now* to pre-empt the dip; if speed is rising too fast, you back off to prevent overshoot. It acts as a *damper*.

Together, the three terms produce a smooth, accurate, and well-damped cruise — exactly what a robot joint needs to track a trajectory precisely.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities are **standard benchmarks** the KTU examiner expects you to quote in derivations:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proportional gain | $K_p$ | $0.1$ – $500$ | N·m/rad |
| Integral gain | $K_i$ | $0.01$ – $100$ | N·m/(rad·s) |
| Derivative gain | $K_d$ | $0.001$ – $10$ | N·m·s/rad |
| Sampling period (digital) | $T_s$ | $0.5$ – $5$ | ms |
| Ultimate gain (Z–N) | $K_u$ | System-dependent | N·m/rad |
| Ultimate period | $P_u$ | System-dependent | s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-by-Component Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Error Generation.** The tracking error at the joint is defined as:

$$e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$$

This is the *only* information the controller receives. Its time-derivative and integral form the basis of the D and I actions.

**Step 2 — Proportional Action.** The control output is made directly proportional to the current error:

$$u_P(t) = K_p\,e(t)$$

- **Why it works:** Provides an immediate, large correction when the error is large.
- **Why it fails alone:** Any constant disturbance (gravity on a horizontal-joint arm, Coulomb friction) produces a non-zero error, because a finite $K_p$ can only generate a finite corrective torque to *balance* the disturbance — it never makes the error zero.

**Step 3 — Integral Action.** Accumulates the entire history of the error:

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

- **Why it works:** As long as *any* residual error exists, the integrator keeps increasing the control signal, eventually generating enough torque to cancel constant disturbances exactly → **zero steady-state error** for step inputs and constant load torques.
- **Caution — Integral Wind-up:** If the actuator saturates at $\pm u_{\max}$, the integrator continues accumulating the *un-correla table* error. When the set-point is finally reached, the integrator is bloated and causes a large overshoot (the **wind-up phenomenon**). Always implement **anti-windup** (clamping or back-calculation).

**Step 4 — Derivative Action.** Acts on the rate of change of error:

$$u_D(t) = K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Why it works:** Provides *predictive* damping. By reacting to the *trend* of the error, the D term anticipates overshoot and damps oscillations, increasing the phase margin of the closed-loop system.
- **Caution — Noise Amplification:** The derivative of a noisy measured signal explodes. In practice, a **filtered derivative** is used:

$$u_D(t) = \frac{K_d\,s}{1 + \tau_f\,s}\,e(t)$$

where $\tau_f$ is a small filter time-constant (typically $T_s/5$ to $T_s/10$).

**Step 5 — Summation and Output Saturation.** The total command is:

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\!\int_0^{t}\! e(\tau)d\tau + K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

which is then clamped to the actuator limits $\pm u_{\max}$.

### 2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function for a Single Robot Joint

Consider a single rigid robot joint driven by a DC motor. The linearized plant (armature-controlled, after ignoring coupling) is:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})}$$

where $J_{eq}$ is the equivalent inertia at the motor shaft, $B_{eq}$ is the viscous-friction coefficient, and $K_m$ is the motor-torque constant.

With unity feedback and PID controller $G_c(s)$, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$T(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_d(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_d\,K_m\,s^2 + K_p\,K_m\,s + K_i\,K_m}{J_{eq}\,s^3 + (B_{eq} + K_d\,K_m)\,s^2 + K_p\,K_m\,s + K_i\,K_m}$$

This is a **third-order** closed-loop system, and the three PID gains can be chosen to place the three closed-loop poles arbitrarily in the left-half plane (subject to realizability and physical limits).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table contains *every* equation you are likely to be tested on for the PID topic in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the closed-form and the *role* of each gain in transient response.

| # | Equation / Rule | Physical / Mathematical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | Time-domain PID law | N·m |
| 2 | $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | Laplace-domain PID transfer function | N·m/rad |
| 3 | $G_c(s) = K_p\bigl(1 + 1/(T_i s) + T_d s\bigr)$ | Standard *parallel-to-ISA* form | N·m/rad |
| 4 | $e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = 0$ (with $K_i > 0$) | Zero steady-state error for step input | rad |
| 5 | $e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = 1/K_v$ where $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,G_c G_p$ | Finite ramp error; $K_v$ is velocity-error constant | 1/s |
| 6 | $e_{ss}^{\text{parabola}} = 1/K_a$, $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0} s^2 G_c G_p$ | Acceleration-error constant | 1/s² |
| 7 | $T(s)$ for motor + PID (see §2.2) | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 8 | Characteristic eqn: $J_{eq}s^3 + (B_{eq}+K_d K_m)s^2 + K_p K_m s + K_i K_m = 0$ | Closed-loop poles | — |
| 9 | **Ziegler–Nichols (ultimate cycle):** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$ | Tuning for $\sim 25\%$ overshoot, quarter-decay | — |
| 10 | **ZN reaction-curve:** $K_p = 0.9 \tau / (K_{proc}\,L)$, $T_i = 3L$, $T_d = L/2$ | Open-loop step-response based tuning | — |
| 11 | Derivative filter: $G_d(s) = K_d s / (1 + \tau_f s)$ | Practical noise-robust derivative | N·m·s/rad |
| 12 | Anti-windup: $\dot{x}_i = e + (1/T_t)(u_{sat} - u)$ | Back-calculation integrator state update | — |

### 2.4 Effect of Each Gain on Transient Response (Examiner's Favourite)

> The following *qualitative* table appears almost verbatim in KTU past papers. Commit it to memory.

| Closed-Loop Spec | Increase $K_p$ | Increase $K_i$ | Increase $K_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise time | Decreases | Decreases (minor) | Minor effect |
| Overshoot | Increases | Increases | **Decreases** |
| Settling time | Small change | Increases | **Decreases** |
| Steady-state error | Decreases (not zero) | **Eliminates** | No effect |
| Stability margin | Degrades | Degrades badly | **Improves** |
| Disturbance rejection | Improves | **Best** | No effect (DC) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industrial robotics**, PID remains the workhorse controller because:

1. It requires **no mathematical model** of the robot (only the joint's input–output behaviour).
2. It is **computationally trivial** — three multiply–accumulate operations per control cycle, enabling $1$–$10$ kHz sampling.
3. It is **modular** — each joint gets its own independent PID, simplifying commissioning.
4. It is **industry-certified** (ISO 10218, IEC 61508 SIL-2) when augmented with proper anti-windup and bumpless transfer.

In **space robotics** (e.g., the Canadarm on the ISS), PID is the inner loop wrapped by an outer trajectory-planning layer, because model uncertainties in zero gravity are severe.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function for a Single Robot Joint

**Problem Statement.** A single robot joint, linearized about an operating point, has the plant transfer function (motor + gear + link inertia + friction):

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})}$$

A PID controller with transfer function $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ is placed in the forward path of a unity-feedback loop. Derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \theta(s)/\theta_d(s)$.

**Step 1.** Combine the controller and plant in the forward path:

$$G(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right)\!\cdot\!\frac{K_m}{s\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})}$$

**Step 2.** Take a common denominator for $G_c(s)$:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into $G(s)$:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}\cdot\frac{K_m}{s\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})} = \frac{K_m\,(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s^2\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})}$$

**Step 4.** Apply the standard negative-feedback formula $T(s) = G/(1+G)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_m\,(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s^2\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq}) + K_m\,(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

**Step 5.** Multiply out the denominator:

$$s^2\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq}) = J_{eq}\,s^3 + B_{eq}\,s^2$$

$$K_m\,(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) = K_d K_m\,s^2 + K_p K_m\,s + K_i K_m$$

**Step 6.** Group like powers of $s$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_d K_m\,s^2 + K_p K_m\,s + K_i K_m}{J_{eq}\,s^3 + (B_{eq} + K_d K_m)\,s^2 + K_p K_m\,s + K_i K_m}$$

This is the **final third-order closed-loop transfer function**. The closed-loop poles are the three roots of the characteristic equation (denominator = 0), and can be placed by appropriate choice of $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Error Analysis (Step, Ramp, Parabolic Inputs)

**Problem Statement.** For the closed-loop system above, compute the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ for a unit-step, unit-ramp, and unit-parabolic reference input using the **Final Value Theorem**.

**Step 1.** Recall that for unity feedback, the error transfer function is:

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}\,\theta_d(s)$$

**Step 2.** Steady-state error (FVT) requires all closed-loop poles to lie in $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ (stable). We then have:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\frac{s\,\theta_d(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3.** Define the error constants:

$$K_p^{(err)} = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s)\,G_p(s), \quad K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,G_c(s)\,G_p(s), \quad K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2\,G_c(s)\,G_p(s)$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate $G_c(s)\,G_p(s)$ in the limit $s \to 0$. With $G_c(s) \approx K_i/s$ (dominant at low frequency):

$$G_c(s)\,G_p(s) \approx \frac{K_i}{s}\cdot\frac{K_m}{s\,(J_{eq}\,s + B_{eq})} = \frac{K_i K_m}{s^2 B_{eq}} \quad \text{as } s \to 0$$

So $\lim_{s\to 0} G_c G_p \to \infty$, $\lim_{s\to 0} s\,G_c G_p \to \infty$, and $\lim_{s\to 0} s^2\,G_c G_p = K_i K_m / B_{eq}$.

**Step 5.** Apply the FVT for each input:

**Unit-step:** $\theta_d(s) = 1/s$

$$e_{ss}^{\text{step}} = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{s\cdot(1/s)}{1 + G_c G_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Unit-ramp:** $\theta_d(s) = 1/s^2$

$$e_{ss}^{\text{ramp}} = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{s\cdot(1/s^2)}{1 + G_c G_p} = \frac{1}{K_v} = 0 \quad (\text{since } K_v = \infty)$$

**Unit-parabolic:** $\theta_d(s) = 1/s^3$

$$e_{ss}^{\text{parabola}} = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{s\cdot(1/s^3)}{1 + G_c G_p} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{B_{eq}}{K_i K_m}$$

> **Conclusion:** A PID controller on a *Type-1* plant (with one free integrator in the plant, i.e., $1/s$ from the motor position) plus the *integral action* of the controller elevates the system to **Type 2**, yielding zero steady-state error for step *and* ramp inputs, and a *finite* parabolic error.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate-Cycle Tuning

**Problem Statement.** Using the closed-loop system from §3.1, derive the Ziegler–Nichols (Z–N) tuning rules for a PID controller.

**Step 1.** Set $K_i = K_d = 0$ and gradually increase $K_p$ from zero until the closed-loop response exhibits **sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude. The value of $K_p$ at this point is the **ultimate gain** $K_u$, and the corresponding oscillation period is the **ultimate period** $P_u$.

**Step 2.** With $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$, the closed-loop transfer function reduces to:

$$T(s)\big\vert_{K_i=0,K_d=0} = \frac{K_p K_m}{J_{eq}\,s^2 + B_{eq}\,s + K_p K_m}$$

This is a *second-order* system. Sustained oscillation occurs when the damping ratio $\zeta = 0$, i.e., the real part of the closed-loop poles is zero. Setting $B_{eq} = 0$ momentarily (worst case) gives natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p K_m}{J_{eq}}}$$

At marginal stability ($\zeta = 0$ for $B_{eq}\to 0$), the oscillation period is:

$$P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{J_{eq}}{K_u K_m}}$$

**Step 3.** The Ziegler–Nichols formulas for a PID controller (based on empirical quarter-amplitude-damping criterion) are:

$$K_p = 0.6\,K_u$$

$$T_i = 0.5\,P_u \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{1.2\,K_u}{P_u}$$

$$T_d = 0.125\,P_u \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_d = K_p\,T_d = 0.075\,K_u\,P_u$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** These rules give a controller with approximately **25% overshoot** and a **quarter-decay ratio**. For robotic applications requiring tighter tracking, the gains are often *detuned* (multiplied by $0.5$–$0.7$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classical vs. Robust PID in Robotics

| Property | Classical PID | Filtered + Anti-windup PID | Gain-Scheduled PID |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error (step) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Tracking of fast trajectories | Poor (no model) | Moderate | Good (table lookup) |
| Robustness to payload change | Poor | Moderate | High |
| Computational cost | $O(1)$ per joint | $O(1)$ per joint | $O(\log n)$ per joint |
| Tuning complexity | Low (Z–N) | Low | High |
| Noise sensitivity | High (D term) | Low (filter) | Moderate |
| Typical KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a PID-Controlled Robotic Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Reference Theta_d] --> node2[Summing Junction]
    node2 --> node3[PID Controller Gc of s]
    node3 --> node4[Robot Joint Plant Gp of s]
    node4 --> node5[Output Theta]
    node5 --> node6[Feedback H of s = 1]
    node6 --> node2
    node3 -.-> node7[Proportional Kp Branch]
    node3 -.-> node8[Integral Ki over s Branch]
    node3 -.-> node9[Derivative Kd s Branch]
    node4 --> node10[Disturbance Torque Td]
    node10 -.-> node2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The reference joint angle $\theta_d$ is compared with the measured $\theta$ at the summing junction. The error drives the PID controller, whose three internal branches ($K_p$, $K_i/s$, $K_d s$) act in parallel. The resulting torque command is sent to the plant (motor + gear + link inertia), which produces the actual joint motion. A disturbance torque $T_d$ (e.g., friction, payload, coupling) injects at the plant input.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Multi-Joint Robot with Independent PID

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Trajectory Planner] --> nodeB[Inverse Kinematics]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Theta d1]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Theta d2]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Theta dn]
    nodeC --> nodeF[PID Joint 1]
    nodeD --> nodeG[PID Joint 2]
    nodeE --> nodeH[PID Joint n]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Robot Joint 1]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Robot Joint 2]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Robot Joint n]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Encoder 1]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Encoder 2]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Encoder n]
    nodeL --> nodeF
    nodeM --> nodeG
    nodeN --> nodeH
    nodeI --> nodeO[Gravitational Coupling Torques]
    nodeO -.-> nodeG
    nodeO -.-> nodeH
```

> **Interpretation.** The *outer* trajectory planner generates a sequence of desired joint angles, which are dispatched to *n* independent PID controllers — one per joint. Each PID closes its own loop using the local encoder reading. The *dashed* coupling torques represent the un-modelled disturbances that the P and I terms must reject. This architecture is the **independent-joint PID** strategy of Craig, Spong, and Sciavicco — a KTU Module-3 essential.

### 4.3 Signal-Flow View of the Three PID Branches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Error e of t] --> nodeY1[Multiplier Kp]
    nodeX --> nodeY2[Integrator 1 over s]
    nodeX --> nodeY3[Derivative s times Filter 1 over 1 plus tauf s]
    nodeY2 --> nodeZ1[Multiplier Ki]
    nodeY3 --> nodeZ2[Multiplier Kd]
    nodeY1 --> nodeSUM[Summing Junction]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeSUM
    nodeZ2 --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeOUT[Control u of t]
    nodeOUT --> nodeSAT[Saturation plus minus Umax]
    nodeSAT --> nodeACT[Actuator Torque]
```

> **Note the derivative filter** $1/(1+\tau_f s)$ placed *before* the $K_d$ gain. This is the **filtered-derivative** form mandated by KTU 2024 to suppress measurement-noise amplification, a frequent 7-mark sub-question.
