| 2 | $f = \frac{D_o \cdot D_i}{D_o + D_i}$ | Thin lens formula (Gaussian) | Focus calculation |
| 3 | $M = \frac{D_i}{D_o} = \frac{h_i}{h_o}$ | Lateral magnification | Image scaling |
| 4 | $FOV = 2 d \tan(\theta/2)$ | Field of view | Sensor–lens matching |
| 5 | $N = f / D$ | F-number (aperture) | Exposure control |
| 6 | $G = 2^{bits}$ | Gray levels | Quantization depth |
| 7 | $S = R \times C \times G$ | Image size in bits | Memory calculation |
| 8 | $DOF \approx \frac{2 N c f^2 s^2}{f^4 - N^2 c^2 s^2}$ | Depth of field | Focus range |
| 9 | $\Delta x = \frac{p}{M}$ | Sensor pixel resolution | Smallest detectable feature |
| 10 | $D = B \frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\sin(\beta - \alpha)}$ | Triangulation depth (3D) | Structured light 3D vision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Table:** The vertical bar notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ is replaced with $\mid$ or absolute-value context to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** Weld seam inspection, part identification.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Pill counting, label verification.
- **Agriculture:** Fruit sorting, ripeness detection.
- **Electronics:** PCB defect inspection (AOI).
- **Space (NASA / ISRO):** Autonomous rover navigation (e.g., Mars rovers Perseverance, Pragyan).
- **Medical:** Surgical robotics (Da Vinci system) with stereo vision guidance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Thin Lens Equation (Gaussian Lens Formula)

The thin lens equation relates the **object distance** $D_o$, the **image distance** $D_i$, and the **focal length** $f$ of a thin convex lens.

### Step 1 — Sign Convention (Cartesian)
All distances are measured from the optical center of the lens:
- Object distance: $D_o > 0$ (object on the incoming light side)
- Image distance: $D_i > 0$ (image on the outgoing light side)
- Focal length: $f > 0$ (for convex/converging lens)

### Step 2 — Ray Geometry
Consider a ray from the top of the object passing parallel to the optical axis; it refracts through the focal point on the image side. A second ray passing through the optical center is undeviated. The two refracted rays intersect at the image point.

### Step 3 — Similar Triangles Derivation

From the geometry of similar triangles formed by the parallel ray and the chief ray:

$$
\tan(\theta_1) = \frac{h_o}{D_o}
$$

The refracted ray through the focal point makes an angle:

$$
\tan(\theta_2) = \frac{h_i}{D_i}
$$

Since both angles are equal for the same ray:

$$
\frac{h_o}{D_o} = \frac{h_i}{D_i}
$$

From the definition of magnification $M = h_i / h_o$:

$$
M = \frac{D_i}{D_o}
$$

### Step 4 — Combining the Relationships
From the geometry of the paraxial ray through the focal point $F$ at distance $f$:

$$
\frac{h_o}{f} = \frac{h_i}{D_i - f}
$$

Dividing both sides by $h_i$ and using $M = h_i/h_o$:

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{M}{D_i} \cdot \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{D_i} = \frac{M+1}{D_i}
$$

Substituting $M = D_i/D_o$:

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{D_i/D_o + 1}{D_i} = \frac{1}{D_o} + \frac{1}{D_i}
$$

### Final Equation:

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{D_o} + \frac{1}{D_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Gaussian Thin Lens Equation**, fundamental to all optical design in machine vision lens selection.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Image Storage Size and Memory Calculation

### Step 1 — Define Image Dimensions
Let an image have:
- $R$ = number of rows (height in pixels)
- $C$ = number of columns (width in pixels)
- $b$ = bit-depth (number of bits per pixel)

### Step 2 — Compute Total Pixels
Total pixel count:

$$
N_{pix} = R \times C
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Gray Levels
Number of distinguishable intensity values:

$$
G = 2^{b}
$$

For a standard **8-bit** grayscale image:

$$
G = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ gray levels}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total Memory
Total image data size in bits:

$$
S = N_{pix} \times b
$$

In bytes:

$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{R \times C \times b}{8}
$$

In kilobytes:

$$
S_{KB} = \frac{R \times C \times b}{8 \times 1024}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot vision camera captures a $640 \times 480$ pixel, 8-bit grayscale image. Calculate the memory required.

**Solution:**

$$
N_{pix} = 640 \times 480 = 307{,}200 \text{ pixels}
$$

$$
S = 307{,}200 \times 8 = 2{,}457{,}600 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
S_{bytes} = \frac{2{,}457{,}600}{8} = 307{,}200 \text{ bytes} = 300 \text{ KB}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Field of View (FOV) from Sensor Size

### Step 1 — Define Variables
- $d$ = working distance (object-to-lens distance)
- $S$ = physical size of the sensor (in mm)
- $f$ = focal length of the lens (in mm)
- $\theta$ = total angular field of view (in radians)

### Step 2 — Pin-hole Approximation
For small angles, the paraxial relation holds:

$$
\tan(\theta/2) \approx \frac{S/2}{d_{eff}}
$$

where $d_{eff}$ is the effective image distance, equal to $f$ when the object is at infinity (or $D_i$ in general).

### Step 3 — Solve for FOV
Using $D_i \approx f$ for distant objects:

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\left( \frac{S}{2 f} \right)
$$

The linear FOV (in mm) at working distance $d$ is:

$$
FOV_{linear} = \frac{S \cdot d}{f}
$$

### Worked Example

**Given:** A camera has a 1/3" sensor of horizontal width $S = 4.8 \; \text{mm}$ and a lens of focal length $f = 8 \; \text{mm}$. The working distance is $d = 300 \; \text{mm}$.

**Compute the linear FOV:**

$$
FOV = \frac{4.8 \times 300}{8} = \frac{1440}{8} = 180 \; \text{mm}
$$

Hence, the camera sees a horizontal field of view of **180 mm** at 300 mm working distance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Triangulation Depth (3D Vision with Structured Light)

For a structured light 3D vision system, the depth $D$ of an object point is computed from the triangulation geometry:

### Step 1 — Setup
A laser line is projected at angle $\alpha$ from the camera's optical axis. The reflected line appears on the image at angle $\beta$ from the optical axis.

### Step 2 — Geometry
Using the law of sines in the triangle formed by the projector, object point, and camera:

$$
\frac{B}{\sin(\gamma)} = \frac{D}{\sin(\beta)}
$$

where $B$ is the baseline (projector-to-camera distance) and $\gamma$ is the third angle.

### Step 3 — Simplification
Since the three angles sum to $180°$, and using $\gamma = 180° - \alpha - \beta$:

$$
\sin(\gamma) = \sin(\alpha + \beta)
$$

### Final Depth Equation:

$$
D = B \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta)}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}
$$

In the common case of small angles and $\alpha + \beta \approx 90°$:

$$
D \approx B \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta)}{\cos(\alpha)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This triangulation principle is used in **laser line scanners**, **Kinect depth sensors**, and **LiDAR** systems widely employed in modern robotics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Robot Vision System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal and data flow through all six vision system components, mapped from physical scene capture to robot actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCENE OBJECT] --> B[ILLUMINATION SOURCE]
    B --> C[OPTICAL LENS]
    C --> D[IMAGE SENSOR CCD OR CMOS]
    D --> E[FRAME GRABBER AND ADC]
    E --> F[DIGITAL IMAGE BUFFER]
    F --> G[IMAGE PROCESSOR CPU OR GPU]
    G --> H[FEATURE EXTRACTION MODULE]
    H --> I[RECOGNITION AND CLASSIFICATION]
    I --> J[ROBOT CONTROLLER]
    J --> K[ROBOT MANIPULATOR ACTUATION]
    I --> L[DISPLAY MONITOR FOR OPERATOR]
    G --> M[FEEDBACK TO ILLUMINATION CONTROL]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Component Sub-Architecture — Image Sensor Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[PHOTODIODE ARRAY] --> S2[CHARGE ACCUMULATION]
    S2 --> S3[CHARGE TRANSFER CCD]
    S2 --> S4[ROW SELECT CMOS]
    S3 --> S5[OUTPUT AMPLIFIER]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[ANALOG SIGNAL OUTPUT]
    S6 --> S7[CORRELATED DOUBLE SAMPLING]
    S7 --> S8[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION]
    S8 --> S9[DIGITAL PIXEL VALUES 8 TO 14 BIT]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Image Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[RAW IMAGE INPUT] --> P2[NOISE FILTERING GAUSSIAN OR MEDIAN]
    P2 --> P3[CONTRAST ENHANCEMENT HISTOGRAM]
    P3 --> P4[THRESHOLDING OR EDGE DETECTION]
    P4 --> P5[CONNECTED COMPONENT ANALYSIS]
    P5 --> P6[FEATURE VECTOR EXTRACTION]
    P6 --> P7[OBJECT CLASSIFICATION]
    P7 --> P8[POSE ESTIMATION]
    P8 --> P9[ROBOTIC DECISION OUTPUT]
```

## 4.4 Lighting Configuration Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[ILLUMINATION STRATEGY SELECTION] --> L1[FRONT LIGHT DIFFUSE RING]
    L0 --> L2[BACK LIGHT SILHOUETTE]
    L0 --> L3[STRUCTURED LIGHT LINE OR GRID]
    L0 --> L4[DOMINANT DIRECTIONAL LIGHT]
    L0 --> L5[STROBE LED PULSED]
    L1 --> R[ROBOT VISION CAMERA]
    L2 --> R
    L3 --> R
    L4 --> R
    L5 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Diagrams:**
> - Diagram 4.1 shows the **end-to-end functional flow** — from a physical object in the scene to a physical actuation of the robot arm.
> - Diagram 4.2 shows the **internal signal path** inside the camera (photodiode → amplifier → ADC).
> - Diagram 4.3 shows the **software-side processing pipeline** executed by the CPU/GPU.
> - Diagram 4.4 shows the **lighting strategy decision tree** based on application requirements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the major components of a robot vision system and state the function of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major components of a robot vision system are:
1. Illumination (light source)
2. Optical lens system
3. Image sensor (camera)
4. Frame grabber (digitizer)
5. Image processing unit
6. Display/output interface

**Function of two:**
- **Image Sensor (1.5 marks):** Converts the focused optical image of the object into an analog electrical signal, where each pixel's voltage is proportional to the local light intensity.
- **Frame Grabber (1.5 marks):** Performs analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) of the analog video signal and stores the digitized image in computer memory as a 2D pixel matrix.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for listing, 1.5 marks each for two functions.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between CCD and CMOS image sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) | CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor) |
|:----------|:---------------------------|:----------------------------------------------|
| **Signal readout** | Charge transferred across chip to one output node | Each pixel has its own amplifier and ADC |
| **Noise level** | Lower noise, higher uniformity | Slightly higher noise |
| **Power consumption** | Higher (requires multiple voltage rails) | Lower (single supply) |
| **Speed** | Slower (sequential readout) | Faster (random pixel access possible) |
| **Cost** | Higher manufacturing cost | Lower cost, standard CMOS process |
| **Application** | High-precision metrology, scientific imaging | Consumer cameras, mobile, robotics |

*(Examiner's note: At least 3 valid comparison points = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Describe in detail the various components of a robot vision system with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A robot vision system is an electro-optical instrumentation system that captures, digitizes, processes, and interprets visual information from the environment to enable autonomous robotic actions such as object recognition, localization, and manipulation.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw the block diagram showing: Illumination → Optics → Sensor → Frame Grabber → Processor → Output)*

**Detailed Description of Components (4 marks):**

1. **Illumination / Light Source:**
   - Provides controlled, uniform, and stable light on the object.
   - Types: LED ring, fluorescent, halogen, laser, structured light, strobe.
   - Purpose: Maximize contrast, minimize shadows, and reduce specular reflections.
   - Improper illumination is the leading cause of vision system failure.

2. **Optics / Lens System:**
   - Focuses reflected light from the object onto the image sensor.
   - Key parameters: focal length $f$, aperture $N = f/D$, FOV, DOF.
   - Standard mounts: C-mount, CS-mount.

3. **Image Sensor (Camera):**
   - Converts optical energy into an analog electrical signal.
   - Technologies: CCD (high uniformity) and CMOS (low power, fast).
   - Monochrome or color (with Bayer filter).

4. **Frame Grabber / Digitizer:**
   - Performs analog-to-digital conversion.
   - Samples spatially and quantizes intensity (8-bit to 16-bit depth).
   - Stores image in pixel array form $I(x, y)$.

5. **Image Processing Unit:**
   - CPU, GPU, DSP, or FPGA.
   - Performs pre-processing, segmentation, feature extraction, recognition, and interpretation.

6. **Display / Output Interface:**
   - Monitor for human feedback and communication protocols (EtherCAT, PROFINET) to the robot controller.

**Conclusion (0 marks – implicit):** A vision system is the integrated cooperation of optical, electronic, and computational subsystems.

---

**(b) Explain the working principle of a CCD image sensor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Charge-Coupled Device (CCD)** is a semiconductor image sensor that converts photons into electrons, accumulates charge in potential wells, and transfers the charge sequentially across the chip to a single output amplifier for readout.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Array of **photosensitive MOS capacitors** (pixels) arranged in rows and columns.
- Each pixel is a photodiode or photogate that accumulates electrons proportional to incident light intensity.
- Adjacent pixels form a **horizontal shift register** for charge transfer.
- Covered by a **Bayer color filter array (CFA)** for color imaging.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. **Photon Detection (Photoelectric Effect):**
   Photons striking the pixel release electrons via the photoelectric effect. The number of electrons generated in integration time $T_{int}$ is:

   $$
   N_e = \eta \cdot \Phi \cdot A \cdot T_{int}
   $$

   where $\eta$ is quantum efficiency, $\Phi$ is photon flux (photons/s/m²), and $A$ is pixel area.

2. **Charge Accumulation:**
   Electrons are collected in a potential well created by an applied gate voltage. Charge $Q = N_e \cdot e$ (where $e$ is electron charge).

3. **Charge Transfer:**
   By clocking the gate voltages in a 2-, 3-, or 4-phase sequence, the entire row of charge packets is shifted one pixel at a time toward a **serial register**, then to the **output amplifier**.

4. **Voltage Conversion:**
   The output amplifier converts the charge packet into a voltage $V_{out} = Q/C_{FD}$, where $C_{FD}$ is the floating diffusion capacitance.

5. **Analog Output:**
   The output is a sequential analog waveform corresponding to a **raster scan** of the image.

**Advantages (0 marks optional):** High SNR, low dark current, excellent uniformity — ideal for metrology and scientific imaging.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the different types of illumination techniques used in machine vision with suitable applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 marks):**
Selection of appropriate illumination is critical — it accounts for over 60% of machine vision success. The technique must maximize contrast between the feature of interest and the background.

**Types of Illumination (5 marks total — ~1 mark each + 0.5 application):**

1. **Front Lighting:**
   - Light source placed between the camera and the object on the same side.
   - **Types:** Ring light, bar light, coaxial light.
   - **Application:** Surface inspection, label reading, defect detection on flat surfaces.

2. **Back Lighting:**
   - Light placed behind the object; camera captures a silhouette.
   - **Application:** Dimensional measurement, edge profiling, hole detection, counting.

3. **Structured Lighting:**
   - Projects a known pattern (line, grid, dot matrix) onto the object.
   - Distortion of the pattern reveals 3D shape.
   - **Application:** 3D scanning, solder paste inspection, bin picking.

4. **Diffuse / Dome Lighting:**
   - Uniform hemispherical illumination; eliminates specular reflections.
   - **Application:** Polished or curved surfaces (metals, glass, plastics).

5. **Strobe / Pulsed LED Lighting:**
   - Synchronized with high-speed cameras to freeze motion.
   - **Application:** High-speed production lines, bottle inspection, automotive welding monitoring.

6. **Dark-Field Illumination:**
   - Light is angled so that only scattered light reaches the camera.
   - **Application:** Detection of surface defects, scratches, micro-cracks.

**Selection Criteria (0.5 marks):**
Choice depends on object surface (reflective/transparent), feature size, speed, ambient light rejection, and contrast requirements.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):** A well-designed illumination scheme is the foundation of robust machine vision.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the image formation model in a machine vision system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The image formation model mathematically describes how a real-world 3D scene is projected onto a 2D image plane by the camera. The most common model is the **pinhole camera model**.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw: 3D Scene Object → Lens System → Pinhole Aperture → Image Plane 2D Projection → Image Sensor → Digital Image)*

**Mathematical Model (4 marks):**

The intensity at a pixel $(x, y)$ in the image is given by:

$$
I(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot g(x, y)
$$

where:
- $f(x, y)$ = **object function** (reflectance/intensity distribution of the scene)
- $g(x, y)$ = **illumination function** (light projected on the scene)

**Pinhole Projection:**

For a point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ in 3D space and pinhole at origin, the image coordinates are:

$$
x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

In homogeneous matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point.

**Lens Distortion (optional, 0.5 marks):** Real lenses add radial and tangential distortion, modelled as:

$$
x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4) + \dots
$$

**Conclusion (0 marks — implicit):** The pinhole + lens model is the foundation of camera calibration and 3D reconstruction in robotics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Vision System Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing without explaining:** Students often *list* the six components in a one-line answer and skip the *function* — examiners expect 2–3 line descriptions per component (loses 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Confusing "frame grabber" with "image sensor":** The sensor *creates* the analog signal; the frame grabber *digitizes* it. Mixing these up loses 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram:** A neat labeled block diagram is *mandatory* in any 7-mark question on vision components. Omission costs 1.5–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always specify units for $f$ (mm), $D$ (mm or m), $I$ (gray level), $S$ (bits/bytes). Unitless answers lose 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Confusing "focal length" with "f-number":** Focal length $f$ is a length (mm); f-number $N = f/D$ is a dimensionless ratio. Do not interchange.
> 6. **No real-world application:** Linking the concept to industrial robotics (e.g., pick-and-place, weld inspection) scores higher on KTU's outcome-based CO mapping.
> 7. **Hand-drawn block diagrams are preferred:** Mermaid or scanned sketches are acceptable, but always label every block and every arrow.
> 8. **Vague formulas:** If you write "image size = pixels × bits", also write the explicit equation $S = R \times C \times b$ for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vision System Components (Module 3)**

- ☐ A robot vision system consists of **six sequential components**: illumination, optics, sensor, frame grabber, processor, and output.
- ☐ **Illumination** is the most critical component for vision success; common types include front, back, structured, diffuse, and strobe lighting.
- ☐ The **thin lens equation** is $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{D_o} + \dfrac{1}{D_i}$; magnification is $M = D_i/D_o = h_i/h_o$.
- ☐ The **field of view** of a camera system is $FOV = S \cdot d / f$ (linear form) or $FOV = 2 \arctan(S/2f)$ (angular form).
- ☐ The **aperture / f-number** is $N = f/D$; smaller $N$ means wider aperture and brighter image.
- ☐ **CCD sensors** offer low noise and high uniformity (charge transferred serially).
- ☐ **CMOS sensors** offer low power, fast readout, and on-chip integration (each pixel has its own amplifier).
- ☐ The **frame grabber** performs **A/D conversion** with spatial sampling and intensity quantization.
- ☐ A standard 8-bit grayscale image has **256 gray levels**; image memory is $S = R \times C \times b$ bits.
- ☐ The **image formation model** is $I(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot g(x, y)$.
- ☐ **3D depth recovery** uses the triangulation formula $D = B \cdot \sin(\beta) / \sin(\alpha + \beta)$.
- ☐ Always include a **neat labeled block diagram** in any 7-mark question on this topic.
- ☐ Real-world link: vision systems are used in **pick-and-place**, **AOI**, **autonomous vehicles**, **surgical robots**, and **space rovers**.
- ☐ KTU CO mapping: This topic primarily maps to **CO3 (Apply image processing concepts for robotic perception)** under **Bloom Level 3 (Apply)** and **Bloom Level 4 (Analyze)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frobenius Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frobenius Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Frobenius Theorem** is a fundamental result in differential geometry that establishes the necessary and sufficient conditions under which a system of first-order linear partial differential equations (PDEs) — or equivalently, a regular distribution on a smooth manifold — is **completely integrable**. In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, the Frobenius Theorem is the primary analytical tool used to:

1. Classify velocity constraints (and acceleration constraints) as **holonomic (integrable)** or **nonholonomic (non-integrable)**.
2. Test the **involutivity** of vector fields spanning the control input directions of a driftless robotic system.
3. Determine the **controllability Lie algebra** of a multi-input mechanical system.

The theorem can be stated in two equivalent dual formulations:

- **Distribution Form (Vector Field Form):** A regular distribution $\Delta$ of constant rank on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{Q}$ is completely integrable if and only if it is **involutive**, i.e., the Lie bracket closure property $[X, Y] \in \Delta$ holds for every pair $X, Y \in \Delta$.

- **Pfaffian Form (1-Form Form):** A Pfaffian system of $k$ linearly independent 1-forms $\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$ in $n$ variables is completely integrable if and only if $d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0$ for all $i = 1, \dots, k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PERAT523 Module 4, the Frobenius Theorem is the gateway concept that connects the differential geometry of vector fields and distributions to the existence (or non-existence) of constraint-integrating coordinates in robotic mechanisms. It is **mandatory** for analysing nonholonomic wheeled robots, manipulators with rolling constraints, and driftless kinematic systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking on a **hilly terrain** described by an $n$-dimensional surface. At every point, you are told: *"You are only allowed to walk in $k$ specific directions"* (these directions form a *distribution*). The question is: **can you reach every neighbouring point on the terrain by following only these prescribed directions, without ever leaving the surface?**

- If **YES** → the directions are *integrable* (you can piece together the directions to form a smooth sub-surface — an **integral manifold**). The system is **holonomic**, and a closed-form coordinate description exists.
- If **NO** → the directions are *non-integrable* (no such sub-surface exists). The system is **nonholonomic** — the constraint cannot be reduced to a position-level equation, and the robot can still manoeuvre freely even though velocities are restricted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world robotic examples:**
> - **Unicycle / differential-drive robot** (e.g., TurtleBot): velocity constraint is **nonholonomic** — the car can park sideways without a sideways velocity (parallel parking!). Frobenius test confirms non-integrability.
> - **Rolling wheel on a flat floor** without slipping: rolling constraint is **nonholonomic**.
> - **Two-fingered gripper closing on a sphere**: rolling-without-slipping is nonholonomic (Chasles' theorem on instantaneous screw motion).

### 1.3 Key Symbols & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|:------:|:--------|:------------------|
| $\mathcal{Q}$ | Robot configuration manifold | Smooth manifold, dim $n$ |
| $T_q\mathcal{Q}$ | Tangent space at $q$ | Vector space, dim $n$ |
| $\Delta(q)$ | Distribution (allowable velocities) | Subspace of $T_q\mathcal{Q}$ |
| $\omega^i$ | Pfaffian 1-forms | $i = 1, \dots, k$ |
| $d\omega^i$ | Exterior derivative | Antisymmetric 2-form |
| $[X, Y]$ | Lie bracket | Vector field |
| $L_f g$ | Lie derivative of $g$ along $f$ | Tensor |
| $d\omega \wedge \omega$ | Wedge product | Exterior algebra element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Dimensions:** The dimension of the **integral manifold** equals the **rank** of the distribution (denoted $k$ or $\text{rank}(\Delta)$), not the dimension of the ambient manifold. This is a very common KTU exam pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The Frobenius Theorem has a deeply layered logical structure. Below is a structured walkthrough:

**Step 1 — Define a Pfaffian System.**
A **Pfaffian system** is a set of $k$ linear first-order PDEs in $n$ unknowns:
$$
\omega^i(q) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_j^i(q)\, dq^j = 0, \qquad i = 1, 2, \dots, k
$$
where $q = (q^1, \dots, q^n) \in \mathcal{Q}$. The differentials $dq^j$ are the **unknowns**, and $a_j^i(q)$ are smooth (typically $C^\infty$) functions. The set of $k$ covectors $\{a_j^i(q)\}$ is assumed to be **linearly independent** at every $q \in \mathcal{Q}$, so the system has constant rank $k$.

**Step 2 — Define the Associated Distribution.**
Dually, define the **distribution** $\Delta$ (or **polar distribution**):
$$
\Delta(q) = \left\{ v \in T_q\mathcal{Q} \;\middle|\; \omega^i(q)(v) = 0 \text{ for all } i = 1, \dots, k \right\}
$$
This is the **kernel** of the Pfaffian 1-forms — the set of all velocities permitted by the constraints. The rank is $\dim \Delta = n - k$.

**Step 3 — State the Integrability Question.**
We ask: does there exist a smooth sub-manifold $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{Q}$ through every point $q_0 \in \mathcal{Q}$ such that $T_q\mathcal{S} = \Delta(q)$ for all $q \in \mathcal{S}$? If yes, the system is **completely integrable**; $\mathcal{S}$ is an **integral manifold**.

**Step 4 — Involution (the Geometric Closure Condition).**
A distribution $\Delta$ is **involutive** (or closed under the Lie bracket) if:
$$
\forall X, Y \in \Delta, \qquad [X, Y] \in \Delta
$$
where the Lie bracket of vector fields $X = \sum a^j \partial/\partial q^j$ and $Y = \sum b^j \partial/\partial q^j$ is:
$$
[X, Y] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( a^k \frac{\partial b^j}{\partial q^k} - b^k \frac{\partial a^j}{\partial q^k} \right) \frac{\partial}{\partial q^j}
$$
Geometrically, if you take two allowed vector fields, the "twist" between them must itself be an allowed vector — otherwise you leave the distribution.

**Step 5 — The Pfaffian Integrability Condition.**
Dually, the Pfaffian system is completely integrable **iff**:
$$
d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \omega^2 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0, \qquad \forall i = 1, \dots, k
$$
This is the **exterior-algebraic statement** of Frobenius. It says the exterior derivative of each form, when wedged with all the others, must vanish.

**Step 6 — Application to Driftless Robot Systems.**
For a driftless control-affine robotic system:
$$
\dot{q} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} g_i(q)\, u_i, \qquad q \in \mathcal{Q}, \;\; u \in \mathbb{R}^m
$$
- The **reachable distribution** is $\Delta_m = \text{span}\{g_1, \dots, g_m, [g_i, g_j], \dots\}$.
- **Controllability** requires $\dim \Delta_m = n$ everywhere (Chow–Rashevskii theorem, an immediate corollary of Frobenius).
- The system is **holonomically reducible** if $\Delta_m$ is involutive of dimension $< n$ — then by Frobenius, **integral manifolds** exist and motion is confined to them.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Statement | Used For |
|:-:|:--------------------|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | Pfaffian system | $\omega^i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_j^i(q)\, dq^j = 0$ | Defining velocity constraints |
| 2 | Distribution (kernel) | $\Delta(q) = \{v \in T_q\mathcal{Q} \mid \omega^i(v) = 0\}$ | Defining allowed velocities |
| 3 | Lie bracket | $[X,Y] = X(Y) - Y(X) = \sum (a^k \partial b^j / \partial q^k - b^k \partial a^j / \partial q^k)\,\partial/\partial q^j$ | Checking involution |
| 4 | Frobenius (Vector form) | $\Delta$ integrable $\iff$ $\Delta$ involutive $\iff$ $[X,Y] \in \Delta \; \forall X, Y \in \Delta$ | Robot mechanism analysis |
| 5 | Frobenius (Pfaffian form) | $d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0 \;\; \forall i$ | Constraint classification |
| 6 | Integral manifold | $T_q \mathcal{S} = \Delta(q) \;\; \forall q \in \mathcal{S}$ | Coordinate decoupling |
| 7 | Reachable distribution | $\Delta_m = \overline{\text{span}}\{g_1, \dots, g_m, [g_i,g_j], \dots\}$ | Controllability test |
| 8 | Chow–Rashevskii (corollary) | $\dim \Delta_m = n$ everywhere $\Rightarrow$ controllable | Driftless robot motion planning |
| 9 | Cartan formula | $d\omega(X, Y) = X \cdot \omega(Y) - Y \cdot \omega(X) - \omega([X,Y])$ | Computing $d\omega$ |
| 10 | Coordinate change | Local $y$ such that $\Delta = \text{span}\{\partial/\partial y^1, \dots, \partial/\partial y^k\}$ | Constructing reduced coordinates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Frobenius Theorem is the **first test** applied in the design of:
> - **Underactuated manipulators** (e.g., Acrobot, Pendubot) — checking whether velocity constraints can be reduced to position constraints.
> - **Nonholonomic motion planners** (e.g., Reeds–Shepp, Dubins cars) — verifying the *non-integrability* so the planner retains full manoeuvrability.
> - **Cable-driven parallel robots** — detecting whether cable-length constraints integrate into position-level equations (if yes, the workspace is a lower-dimensional sub-manifold).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Frobenius Theorem |
|:-------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Mobile robotics** (wheeled, legged) | Differentiates holonomic (omnidirectional) vs. nonholonomic (car-like) drives |
| **Underwater ROVs / AUVs** | Classifies hydrodynamic constraint integrability |
| **Rolling contact mechanics** | Verifies Chasles' theorem integrability (yes, instantaneously; no, in finite motion) |
| **Manipulator kinematics** | Tests whether joint constraints can be reduced to fewer DOF coordinates |
| **Optimal control of driftless systems** | Identifies whether problem is differentially flat (a strong consequence of involutivity) |
| **Cable-suspended load systems** | Detects nonholonomic behaviour in swing dynamics |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of the Two Frobenius Statements

**Goal:** Show that $\Delta$ involutive $\iff$ $d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0$.

**Setup:** Let $\Delta$ be a regular distribution of rank $k$ on $\mathcal{Q}$, with co-distribution $\Delta^\perp = \text{span}\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$ of rank $k$ as well.

**Step 1 — Use the Cartan formula for the exterior derivative.**
For any 1-form $\omega$ and vector fields $X, Y$, the **Cartan magic formula** states:
$$
d\omega(X, Y) = X \bigl[\omega(Y)\bigr] - Y \bigl[\omega(X)\bigr] - \omega\bigl([X, Y]\bigr)
$$
This is the foundational identity needed.

**Step 2 — Take $X, Y \in \Delta$.**
By definition of the distribution as the kernel of the Pfaffian forms, we have $\omega^i(X) = \omega^i(Y) = 0$ for all $i = 1, \dots, k$. Substituting into the Cartan formula:
$$
d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0 - 0 - \omega^i([X, Y]) = -\omega^i([X, Y])
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the involution hypothesis.**
If $\Delta$ is involutive, then $[X, Y] \in \Delta$, which means by the kernel definition that $\omega^i([X, Y]) = 0$ for all $i$. Therefore:
$$
d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0 \qquad \forall X, Y \in \Delta
$$
This says $d\omega^i$ **annihilates** on $\Delta \times \Delta$.

**Step 4 — Convert to wedge product form.**
A 2-form $\beta$ satisfies $\beta(X, Y) = 0$ for all $X, Y \in \Delta$ if and only if $\beta$ is in the ideal generated by $\Delta^\perp$ — i.e.,
$$
\beta = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j \wedge \omega^j
$$
for some 1-forms $\alpha_j$. In our case, $d\omega^i$ is a 2-form vanishing on $\Delta$, so:
$$
d\omega^i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j^i \wedge \omega^j
$$

**Step 5 — Wedge with $\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k$.**
Wedging both sides of the above with $\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k$ and using the antisymmetry of the wedge product, every term on the right contains a repeated $\omega^j \wedge \omega^j = 0$, so the right side vanishes:
$$
d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0
$$
This establishes **Frobenius (Pfaffian form) ⟹ Frobenius (Vector form)**.

**Step 6 — Converse direction.**
Conversely, suppose the wedge product condition holds. Then the 2-form $d\omega^i$ lies in the ideal generated by $\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$, which implies $d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0$ for any $X, Y \in \Delta$. By Cartan, $\omega^i([X, Y]) = 0$, hence $[X, Y] \in \Delta$. This proves involutivity. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example: The Unicycle Mobile Robot (Differential-Drive Robot)

A standard **unicycle** in 2D has configuration $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathcal{Q} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, $n = 3$. The kinematic equations are:
$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \qquad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$
where $v$ and $\omega$ are the linear and angular velocity controls. The two control vector fields are:
$$
g_1 = \cos\theta\,\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + \sin\theta\,\frac{\partial}{\partial y}, \qquad g_2 = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}
$$
The associated Pfaffian system (1-form) is obtained from $\dot{q} = g_1 v + g_2 \omega$, giving the single constraint:
$$
\omega^1 = -\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy = 0
$$
This is a rank-$1$ Pfaffian system ($k = 1$) in $n = 3$ variables.

**Step 1 — Compute the exterior derivative.**
$$
d\omega^1 = d(-\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy) = -\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy
$$
Note $d \wedge d = 0$ and we use $d(f\, \alpha) = df \wedge \alpha$. The non-vanishing terms are:
$$
d\omega^1 = -\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Frobenius condition.**
Since $k = 1$, the condition is $d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = 0$:
$$
d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = (-\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy) \wedge (-\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy)
$$
Expanding:
$$
= \cos\theta \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dx \;-\; \cos^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy \;-\; \sin^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dx \;+\; \sin\theta \cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dy
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using $d\alpha \wedge d\alpha = 0$.**
The first and last terms vanish:
$$
= -\cos^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy - \sin^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy \wedge dx
$$
Using $dy \wedge dx = -dx \wedge dy$:
$$
= -\cos^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy + \sin^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy
$$
$$
= (\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta)\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy = -\cos(2\theta)\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
For a nonholonomic constraint, we need this wedge product to be **non-zero**. The wedge product is non-zero **iff** $\cos(2\theta) \neq 0$, i.e., $\theta \neq \pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$. At "generic" orientations:
$$
d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 \neq 0
$$
Hence the unicycle constraint is **non-integrable** (nonholonomic) almost everywhere. The system cannot reduce to a 2-D position constraint, yet via the Lie bracket $[g_1, g_2] = \sin\theta\,\partial_x - \cos\theta\,\partial_y$, the system attains full rank-3 controllability.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Cart-Pole System (Holonomic Reduction)

A cart on a 1-D rail carries a pendulum of angle $\theta$. Configuration: $q = (x, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2$, $n = 2$. Suppose we impose a **virtual** velocity constraint $\omega^1 = \dot{x} - \dot{\theta} = 0$, i.e.,
$$
\omega^1 = dx - d\theta = 0
$$
Compute $d\omega^1 = d(dx - d\theta) = 0$ (exterior derivative of an exact form is zero). Then:
$$
d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = 0 \wedge (dx - d\theta) = 0
$$
The Frobenius condition is satisfied trivially. The system **is** completely integrable: integrating $\omega^1 = 0$ gives $x - \theta = c$, a holonomic constraint reducing the system to a 1-D configuration. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Holonomic vs. Nonholonomic

| Property | Holonomic (Frobenius satisfied) | Nonholonomic (Frobenius violated) |
|:---------|:-------------------------------|:---------------------------------|
| $d\omega \wedge \omega = 0$? | **Yes** | **No** (generically) |
| Involutive distribution? | **Yes** ($[X,Y] \in \Delta$) | **No** (Lie brackets escape $\Delta$) |
| Integral manifold? | **Yes** — lower-dim sub-manifold | **No** — only "leafy" sub-spaces |
| Reduction possible? | **Yes** to fewer coordinates | **No** — full configuration needed |
| Controllability? | Confined to integral manifold | **Full**, by Chow–Rashevskii |
| KTU canonical example | Trolley on a fixed rail (1-D slider) | Unicycle, car, knife-edge |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Frobenius Theorem — Conceptual Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the logical decision flow used to apply the Frobenius Theorem in a robotic-system analysis problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Robot Kinematic System] --> stepB[Identify Configuration Manifold Q]
    stepB --> stepC[Extract Pfaffian Forms omega_i or Vector Fields g_j]
    stepC --> stepD{Choose Test Form}
    stepD -->|Vector form| stepE[Compute Lie Brackets of g_j]
    stepD -->|Pfaffian form| stepF[Compute d omega_i wedge products]
    stepE --> stepG{Check Involution: [X,Y] in Delta?}
    stepF --> stepH{Check d omega_i wedge omega_1 ... omega_k = 0?}
    stepG -->|YES| stepI[Holonomic: Integrable, reduce to sub-manifold]
    stepG -->|NO| stepJ[Nonholonomic: System is richer than constraints]
    stepH -->|YES| stepI
    stepH -->|NO| stepJ
    stepI --> stepK[Apply Position Level Constraints]
    stepJ --> stepL[Apply Lie Algebra Rank Condition]
    stepL --> stepM{rank Delta_m = dim Q?}
    stepM -->|YES| stepN[Controllable: Chow-Rashevskii]
    stepM -->|NO| stepO[Uncontrollable: Motion trapped on integral manifold]
    stepK --> stepP[End: Solution Exists]
    stepN --> stepP
    stepO --> stepP
```

### 4.2 Distribution & Integral Manifold — Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    manifoldQ[Q : Configuration Manifold dim n]
    subS[S : Integral Manifold dim k]
    pointQ[Point q in Q]
    plane1[T_q Q : Tangent Plane dim n]
    plane2[T_q S : Tangent Plane dim k]
    deltavector[Delta q : Distribution dim k]
    dual1[omega_i : 1-forms]
    dual2[Delta perp : Co-distribution dim n-k]
    
    manifoldQ --> pointQ
    pointQ --> plane1
    plane1 --> plane2
    subS --> plane2
    plane2 --> deltavector
    plane1 --> dual1
    dual1 --> dual2
    dual2 -->|kernel| deltavector
```

### 4.3 Lie Bracket Closure — Algebraic Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    baseA[Vector Field g_1 in Delta]
    baseB[Vector Field g_2 in Delta]
    bracket1[Lie Bracket g_1 g_2]
    bracket2[Lie Bracket g_1 g_3]
    bracket3[Lie Bracket g_2 g_3]
    
    deltaNode[Delta : Original Distribution dim k]
    delta2Node[Delta_2 : Involutive Closure dim k+d]
    
    baseA --> deltaNode
    baseB --> deltaNode
    deltaNode --> bracket1
    deltaNode --> bracket2
    deltaNode --> bracket3
    bracket1 --> delta2Node
    bracket2 --> delta2Node
    bracket3 --> delta2Node
```

### 4.4 Real-World Robotic System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    robot[Robotic System Selection]
    robot --> unicycle[Unicycle / Diff Drive]
    robot --> car[Car-Like]
    robot --> omni[Omnidirectional Base]
    robot --> finger[Rolling Disk on Plane]
    
    unicycle --> f1{Frobenius Test}
    car --> f1
    omni --> f1
    finger --> f1
    
    f1 -->|Non integrable| branchA[Nonholonomic]
    f1 -->|Integrable| branchB[Holonomic]
    
    branchA --> appA[Controllability via Lie Algebra]
    branchB --> appB[Coordinate Reduction to Sub-manifold]
    
    appA --> resA[Motion Planning Reeds Shepp Dubins]
    appB --> resB[Mechanism Synthesis on Lower dim Q]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *State the Frobenius Theorem in both its vector-field form and Pfaffian form. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Frobenius Theorem provides the necessary and sufficient conditions for the complete integrability of a regular distribution on a smooth manifold.

- **Vector-field (Distribution) form:** A regular distribution $\Delta$ of constant rank $k$ on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{Q}$ is completely integrable if and only if it is **involutive**, that is, for every pair of vector fields $X, Y \in \Delta$, the Lie bracket satisfies $[X, Y] \in \Delta$.
- **Pfaffian form:** Let $\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$ be $k$ linearly independent 1-forms defining a Pfaffian system on $\mathcal{Q}$. The system is completely integrable if and only if
$$
d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \omega^2 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0, \qquad \forall\, i = 1, 2, \dots, k
$$

Both forms are equivalent via the Cartan magic formula $d\omega(X, Y) = X \cdot \omega(Y) - Y \cdot \omega(X) - \omega([X, Y])$. *(3 Marks)*

---

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Differentiate between a holonomic and a nonholonomic constraint in the context of robot dynamics. How does the Frobenius Theorem help in classifying them? (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Holonomic constraints** are velocity/acceleration constraints that can be **integrated** to yield position-level (geometric) relations among the coordinates. They reduce the effective degrees of freedom of the system.
- **Nonholonomic constraints** are velocity constraints that **cannot be integrated** — although they restrict instantaneous motion, the system retains full manoeuvrability in configuration space (e.g., parallel parking a car).

The **Frobenius Theorem** classifies these via the involutivity test: if the distribution $\Delta$ defined as the kernel of the Pfaffian forms is involutive ($[X, Y] \in \Delta$), then integral sub-manifolds exist and the constraints are **holonomic**. If involution fails, the constraints are **nonholonomic**. Equivalently, the Pfaffian wedge test $d\omega \wedge \omega \neq 0$ signals non-integrability. *(3 Marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(a)* State and prove the Frobenius Theorem in the Pfaffian form, clearly showing the equivalence with the vector-field form using the Cartan formula. *(7 Marks)*

> *(b)* Consider a kinematic unicycle robot with configuration $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and control vector fields
> $g_1 = \cos\theta\,\partial_x + \sin\theta\,\partial_y$ and $g_2 = \partial_\theta$.
> Show that the nonholonomic constraint is **non-integrable** by applying the Frobenius test in both the Pfaffian and vector-field forms. Conclude on the controllability of the system. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Statement:** Let $\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$ be $k$ linearly independent 1-forms on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{Q}$, defining the Pfaffian system $\omega^i = 0$ of constant rank $k$. Then this system is completely integrable if and only if
$$
d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0 \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, k
$$

**Proof (Sketch with full steps):**

**Step 1 (Cartan's formula).** For any 1-form $\omega$ and vector fields $X, Y$, the Cartan magic formula reads:
$$
d\omega(X, Y) = X \cdot \omega(Y) - Y \cdot \omega(X) - \omega([X, Y])
$$
This formula is the bridge between exterior algebra and Lie derivatives.

**Step 2 (Vector fields in the kernel).** Let $X, Y$ be any vector fields in the distribution $\Delta = \ker(\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k)$. By definition, $\omega^i(X) = \omega^i(Y) = 0$ for all $i$. Substituting into the Cartan formula:
$$
d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0 - 0 - \omega^i([X, Y]) = -\omega^i([X, Y])
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 (Involutivity implication).** Suppose $\Delta$ is involutive, i.e., $[X, Y] \in \Delta$. Then $\omega^i([X, Y]) = 0$ for all $i$, and so $d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0$ for all $X, Y \in \Delta$. This means $d\omega^i$ vanishes on $\Delta \times \Delta$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 (Exterior algebra of vanishing forms).** A 2-form vanishing on $\Delta \times \Delta$ must lie in the ideal generated by the annihilator of $\Delta$, which is exactly the co-distribution $\Delta^\perp = \text{span}\{\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k\}$. Hence:
$$
d\omega^i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j^i \wedge \omega^j
$$
for some 1-forms $\alpha_j^i$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 (Wedge product).** Wedge both sides with $\omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k$. Every term on the right contains $\omega^j \wedge \omega^j = 0$ (since the wedge product is antisymmetric). Hence:
$$
d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**Step 6 (Converse).** Conversely, if the wedge product condition holds, then $d\omega^i \in \text{ideal}(\omega^1, \dots, \omega^k)$, which implies $d\omega^i(X, Y) = 0$ for $X, Y \in \Delta$. By the Cartan formula, $\omega^i([X, Y]) = 0$, so $[X, Y] \in \Delta$, proving involutivity. **(2 Marks for completeness + conclusion)**

$\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (Write the Pfaffian system).** The Pfaffian 1-form corresponding to the unicycle's nonholonomic constraint "no sideways velocity" is:
$$
\omega^1 = -\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy = 0
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 (Compute the exterior derivative).**
$$
d\omega^1 = d(-\sin\theta\, dx) + d(\cos\theta\, dy) = -\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 (Apply Frobenius wedge test).** Since $k = 1$, the Frobenius condition reduces to $d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = 0$:
$$
d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = (-\cos\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx - \sin\theta\, d\theta \wedge dy) \wedge (-\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy)
$$
Expanding, the non-vanishing terms give:
$$
d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 = -\cos^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy + \sin^2\theta\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy = -\cos(2\theta)\, d\theta \wedge dx \wedge dy
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 (Conclude non-integrability).** For generic $\theta$ (e.g., $\theta = 0$), $\cos(2\theta) = 1 \neq 0$, so $d\omega^1 \wedge \omega^1 \neq 0$. The Frobenius condition **fails**; the constraint is **non-integrable (nonholonomic)**. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 (Vector-field verification via Lie bracket).** Compute $[g_1, g_2]$:
$$
[g_1, g_2] = g_1(g_2) - g_2(g_1) = -\cos\theta \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} + \dots = \sin\theta \frac{\partial}{\partial x} - \cos\theta \frac{\partial}{\partial y}
$$
This vector is **not** a linear combination of $g_1, g_2$, so the distribution is **not involutive**. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6 (Controllability).** The Lie algebra closure yields:
$$
\Delta_3 = \text{span}\{g_1, g_2, [g_1, g_2]\} = \mathbb{R}^3 \text{ at generic } q
$$
By the **Chow–Rashevskii theorem** (a corollary of Frobenius), $\dim \Delta_3 = n = 3$ implies the unicycle is **controllable** despite being nonholonomic. **(1 Mark)**

---

> **Q3 (B). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(a)* Define a regular distribution on a smooth manifold. Show that involutivity is **necessary** for complete integrability. *(7 Marks)*

> *(b)* A planar robot has configuration $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and a single control vector field $g_1 = x\,\partial_x - y\,\partial_y$. Apply the Frobenius Theorem to test whether the reachable set is a 1-D curve (holonomic) or 2-D (nonholonomic). If 1-D, **find** the integral manifold (the implicit equation $F(x, y) = \text{const}$). *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (Definition).** A **distribution** $\Delta$ of rank $k$ on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{Q}$ assigns to each $q \in \mathcal{Q}$ a $k$-dimensional subspace $\Delta(q) \subset T_q\mathcal{Q}$, smoothly varying with $q$. It is **regular** if $\dim \Delta(q)$ is constant for all $q$. A distribution is **completely integrable** if for every $q_0 \in \mathcal{Q}$ there exists a local sub-manifold $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{Q}$ through $q_0$ with $T_q\mathcal{S} = \Delta(q)$ for all $q \in \mathcal{S}$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 (Necessity of involutivity).** Let $\mathcal{S}$ be an integral manifold and $X, Y$ be two smooth vector fields that are everywhere tangent to $\mathcal{S}$. Then $X(q), Y(q) \in T_q\mathcal{S} = \Delta(q)$ for all $q \in \mathcal{S}$. For a sub-manifold, the coordinate vector fields on $\mathcal{S}$ commute, and the Lie bracket $[X, Y]$ is also tangent to $\mathcal{S}$ (by the standard property: brackets of $\mathcal{S}$-tangent fields are $\mathcal{S}$-tangent). Hence $[X, Y] \in \Delta$, proving involutivity is necessary. **(3 Marks)**

**Step 3 (Pfaffian equivalence).** By the Cartan formula, involutivity is equivalent to $d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0$, which is exactly the Frobenius integrability condition. **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (Single vector field).** A 1-D distribution $\Delta = \text{span}\{g_1\}$ is **automatically involutive** because any 1-D distribution is closed under Lie brackets trivially. Frobenius tells us it is always integrable. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 (Set up the PDE).** The integral curves of $g_1$ satisfy:
$$
\frac{dx}{x} = \frac{dy}{-y} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dx}{x} = -\frac{dy}{y}
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 (Integrate).** Cross-multiplying and integrating:
$$
\int \frac{dx}{x} = -\int \frac{dy}{y} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln \vert x \vert = -\ln \vert y \vert + C
$$
$$
\Longrightarrow \quad \ln \vert x y \vert = C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert x y \vert = e^C = \text{const}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 (Implicit surface).** The integral manifolds are the level sets of $F(x, y) = xy$. In smooth coordinates (away from $x = 0$ or $y = 0$):
$$
xy = K, \qquad K \in \mathbb{R}
$$
These are **hyperbolas** (or coordinate axes when $K = 0$), each forming a 1-D integral curve. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 (Reachable set conclusion).** Since the 1-D distribution is involutive (automatic), the Frobenius Theorem guarantees that the reachable set from any initial condition is confined to **one hyperbola** $xy = K$. The system is **holonomically reducible** despite having only one control. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls in Frobenius Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the dimension of the integral manifold with the dimension of the configuration space.** A rank-$k$ distribution integrates to a $k$-dimensional sub-manifold in an $n$-dimensional $\mathcal{Q}$, **not** an $(n-k)$-dimensional one. Many students get this reversed and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the constant-rank hypothesis.** A non-regular distribution (where rank varies across $\mathcal{Q}$) can fail Frobenius without the constant-rank assumption. Always state the regularity assumption.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute the exterior derivative of the form, not the function.** When the Pfaffian form has $q$-dependent coefficients, $d\omega^i$ has non-zero terms. A common error is writing $d\omega^i = 0$ when the coefficients are non-constant.
> 4. **Mixing the two forms of Frobenius in the same derivation.** Stick to one form throughout, then mention the equivalence. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous logic.
> 5. **Concluding "controllable" without invoking Chow–Rashevskii explicitly.** For nonholonomic driftless systems, you **must** state the Lie algebra rank condition explicitly — it is a corollary of Frobenius, not a substitution for it.
> 6. **Not handling the wedge product correctly.** Remember $d\alpha \wedge d\alpha = 0$ and $dx \wedge dy = -dy \wedge dx$. Getting signs wrong can flip a non-zero wedge product to zero and vice-versa.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Frobenius Theorem**

- **Frobenius Theorem** is the differential-geometric criterion for **complete integrability** of a regular distribution $\Delta$ on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{Q}$.
- Two equivalent forms: **Vector-field** ($[X, Y] \in \Delta$) and **Pfaffian** ($d\omega^i \wedge \omega^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge \omega^k = 0$).
- The **Cartan magic formula** $d\omega(X, Y) = X \cdot \omega(Y) - Y \cdot \omega(X) - \omega([X, Y])$ is the bridge between the two forms.
- **Holonomic** constraints = Frobenius satisfied = involutive distribution = integral sub-manifold exists = coordinates reducible.
- **Nonholonomic** constraints = Frobenius violated = Lie brackets escape $\Delta$ = no integral manifold, but **Chow–Rashevskii** may still allow full controllability.
- The **rank** of $\Delta$ equals the dimension of the integral manifold, **not** the dimension of $\mathcal{Q}$.
- The **Lie algebra rank condition** $\dim \Delta_m = n$ is the controllability test for driftless systems — a direct corollary.
- Canonical KTU examples: **unicycle** (nonholonomic), **rolling wheel** (nonholonomic), **cart on rail** (holonomic), **differential-drive mobile robot** (nonholonomic), **knife-edge on plane** (nonholonomic).
- For a **1-D distribution**, Frobenius is **automatic** — always integrable to a 1-D curve.
- The **exterior derivative** of an exact form (like $dF$) is **zero** — exploit this to quickly integrate Pfaffian constraints of the form $dF = 0$.
- The Frobenius Theorem is the foundation of **involutivity analysis**, **controllability theory**, and **nonholonomic motion planning** — all high-weight topics in KTU 2024 PERAT523.

> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "apply Frobenius Theorem" in a problem, always **state the distribution/Pfaffian form, compute the relevant bracket or wedge product, evaluate the integrability condition, and conclude holonomic vs. nonholonomic + remark on controllability**. A structured 4-step answer routinely scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Input Systems: Introduction to Nonlinear Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single-Input Systems: Introduction to Nonlinear Systems

# 📘 Module 4 — Single-Input Systems: Introduction to Nonlinear Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In control theory, a **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** system is described by a single control input $u(t)$ and a single measured output $y(t)$. When the governing differential equation that relates $u(t)$ and $y(t)$ violates the **principle of superposition** (homogeneity + additivity), the system is classified as a **Nonlinear System**.

Mathematically, a general SISO nonlinear system can be represented in the explicit Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) form:

$$
\ddot{y} = f\big(y, \, \dot{y}, \, u, \, t\big)
$$

or equivalently in state-space form using the state vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^{T}$:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = f(x_1, x_2, u, t)
$$

$$
y = h(x_1, x_2)
$$

A system is **nonlinear** if the function $f(\cdot)$ is not a linear combination of its arguments — that is, if it contains nonlinear operators such as products, powers, trigonometric terms, signum functions, saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis, or discontinuities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For SISO nonlinear systems, focus on first-order and second-order systems of the form $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$. Linear systems satisfy $f(ax_1 + bx_2) = af(x_1) + bf(x_2)$ and $f(\lambda x) = \lambda f(x)$. The violation of either property makes the system nonlinear.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car**:

- On a **straight, flat highway**, a small turn of the steering wheel produces a small predictable change in direction. Press the accelerator gently and the speed increases by a small predictable amount. This is a **linear system** — the response is proportional to the input.

- Now imagine driving on a **winding mountain road with sharp hairpin bends and steep inclines**. The same small steering input may produce drastically different outcomes depending on your current speed, the road curvature, and the slope of the hill. Press the brake too hard and you skid; press too lightly and you cannot stop. This is a **nonlinear system** — the response depends on the current state (speed, position) in a non-proportional way.

In robotics, this is exactly why **robot manipulators** with multiple links rotating in 3D space are fundamentally nonlinear — the same joint torque produces different motions depending on the arm's current configuration. Gear backlash at joints, motor saturation, and Coulomb friction in bearings are all classic nonlinearities that linear models cannot capture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every real physical system is fundamentally nonlinear. Linear models are **approximations** valid only over a small operating range (small-signal linearization around an equilibrium point). Engineers study nonlinear systems because linear models fail when the system operates over **large signals**, near **saturations**, or under **state-dependent dynamics**.

### 1.3 Why Single-Input Nonlinear Systems?

A SISO nonlinear system is the **smallest, most tractable** class of nonlinear systems. Studying them first allows the student to:

1. Understand fundamental nonlinear phenomena (limit cycles, multiple equilibria, chaos) using phase-plane and describing-function techniques.
2. Build intuition before tackling Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) nonlinear systems like robot manipulators.
3. Apply classical nonlinear control design methods (feedback linearization, sliding mode, backstepping) in their simplest form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Dynamics Connection (KTU Course Context):** A single robotic joint driven by a DC motor is a textbook SISO nonlinear system. The nonlinearity arises from gravity torque $\tau_g = m g L \sin\theta$, Coriolis coupling, and motor saturation. Control of this single joint is the foundation upon which multi-joint robot control is built.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties That Distinguish Nonlinear Systems from Linear Systems

| # | Property | Linear Systems | Nonlinear Systems |
|---|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Superposition** | Holds (homogeneity + additivity) | Fails |
| 2 | **Number of Equilibrium Points** | Exactly **one** (for stable LTI systems) | **Multiple**, isolated, or continuous |
| 3 | **Response to Sinusoidal Input** | Sinusoidal output of same frequency (for LTI) | Output contains **harmonics, subharmonics, DC offset** |
| 4 | **Natural Modes** | Exponentially decaying sinusoids | Can have **limit cycles** (sustained oscillations) |
| 5 | **Stability** | Global or unstable — no surprises | **Local**, **regional**, or **global** stability possible |
| 6 | **Finite Escape Time** | Not possible | Possible (state blows up in finite time) |
| 7 | **Chaos** | Not possible | Possible for order $\geq 3$ |
| 8 | **Frequency Response** | Well-defined Bode plot exists | Frequency response is **input-amplitude dependent** |

### 2.2 Common Physical Nonlinearities in SISO Systems

These are the nonlinearities most frequently tested in KTU board examinations and most frequently encountered in robot actuator systems:

| Nonlinearity | Mathematical Model | Physical Cause |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Saturation** | $u_{out} = \text{sat}(u_{in}) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & u_{in} > u_{max} \\ u_{in}, & \vert u_{in} \vert \le u_{max} \\ -u_{max}, & u_{in} < -u_{max} \end{cases}$ | Actuator limits (motor max voltage/torque) |
| **Dead Zone** | $u_{out} = \begin{cases} u_{in} - d, & u_{in} > d \\ 0, & \vert u_{in} \vert \le d \\ u_{in} + d, & u_{in} < -d \end{cases}$ | Static friction, sensor insensitivity |
| **Backlash** | Two-segment hysteresis gap of width $2b$ | Gear play in mechanical transmissions |
| **Relay (On-Off)** | $u_{out} = M \cdot \text{sgn}(e)$ | Thermostats, bang-bang controllers |
| **Relay with Hysteresis** | Schmitt trigger with thresholds $\pm h$ | Systems with inherent hysteresis |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $F_f = \mu_c \, \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Dry friction at joints |
| **Square Law** | $F = c_d \, \dot{x} \vert \dot{x} \vert$ | Aerodynamic drag, fluid flow |

### 2.3 Why Linear Analysis Fails — The Core "Why"

The entire linear control theory (root locus, Bode, Nyquist, Routh-Hurwitz, state feedback with pole placement) is built on **two assumptions**:

1. **Homogeneity:** If input $u$ produces output $y$, then input $\lambda u$ produces output $\lambda y$.
2. **Additivity:** If $u_1 \to y_1$ and $u_2 \to y_2$, then $u_1 + u_2 \to y_1 + y_2$.

For a nonlinear system $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$, homogeneity requires $f(x, \lambda u) = \lambda f(x, u)$, which only holds when $f$ is linear in $u$. Additivity requires $f(x, u_1+u_2) = f(x, u_1) + f(x, u_2)$, again impossible unless $f$ is linear. Therefore **any system where the input appears nonlinearly (squared, inside a sin, inside a signum, multiplied by a state) is nonlinear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** A robot manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ is nonlinear because the inertia matrix $M(q)$ depends on the configuration $q$ (state-dependent). Linear controllers designed at one operating point will degrade at other operating points — this is the practical motivation for nonlinear control.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use / Meaning |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| General SISO nonlinear ODE | $\ddot{y} = f(y, \dot{y}, u, t)$ | Standard form for second-order nonlinear systems |
| State-space form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})u$, $y = h(\mathbf{x})$ | Affine-in-input nonlinear form |
| Equilibrium points | $f(x_e) = 0$ | Solve for all $x_e$ — nonlinear systems can have **multiple** |
| Linearization at $x_e$ | $A = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x_e}$ | Jacobian matrix, valid for small perturbations |
| Linearization error bound | $\vert f(x) - f(x_e) - A(x - x_e)\vert \le L \vert x - x_e \vert^2$ | Quadratic error growth |
| Principle of superposition (linear) | $T\{a u_1 + b u_2\} = a T\{u_1\} + b T\{u_2\}$ | **Fails** for nonlinear systems |
| Homogeneity test | $T\{\lambda u\} = \lambda T\{u\}$ | Fails if output scales non-proportionally |
| Saturation function | $\text{sat}(u) = \text{sgn}(u) \cdot \min(\vert u \vert, u_{max})$ | Most common actuator nonlinearity |
| Dead-zone function | $\text{DZ}(u) = u - \text{sgn}(u) \cdot d$ if $\vert u \vert > d$, else $0$ | Static friction model |
| Signum (relay) | $\text{sgn}(u) = \begin{cases} +1, & u > 0 \\ 0, & u = 0 \\ -1, & u < 0 \end{cases}$ | On-off control law |
| Coulomb friction | $F_c = \mu_c \, \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Dry friction model |
| Viscous + Coulomb friction | $F_f = b\dot{x} + \mu_c \, \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Combined friction model |
| Limit cycle condition | Amplitude and frequency are **independent of initial conditions** | Unique to nonlinear systems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of SISO Nonlinear Analysis |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Robotics** | Single-joint motor control with friction, gravity, saturation |
| **Automotive** | Cruise control with throttle saturation, engine dead-zone |
| **Aerospace** | Missile autopilot with aerodynamic nonlinearities (lift-drag curves) |
| **Process Control** | Valve positioner with hysteresis and stiction |
| **Power Electronics** | DC-DC converters with switching (relay) nonlinearity |
| **Biomedical** | Pacemaker with actuator saturation, drug delivery dead-zone |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proving a System is Nonlinear Using Superposition

**Problem:** Determine whether the system $y(t) = u^2(t)$ is linear or nonlinear.

**Step 1: Test Homogeneity.**

Apply input $u_1(t)$. The output is:

$$
y_1(t) = u_1^2(t)
$$

Apply scaled input $\lambda u_1(t)$. The output becomes:

$$
y_\lambda(t) = \big(\lambda u_1(t)\big)^2 = \lambda^2 u_1^2(t)
$$

For homogeneity we need $y_\lambda = \lambda y_1$, i.e.:

$$
\lambda^2 u_1^2(t) \stackrel{?}{=} \lambda \cdot u_1^2(t)
$$

$$
\lambda^2 u_1^2(t) \neq \lambda u_1^2(t) \quad \text{for} \quad \lambda \neq 0, 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Homogeneity fails.

**Step 2: Test Additivity.**

Apply $u_1(t) + u_2(t)$:

$$
y_{12}(t) = \big(u_1(t) + u_2(t)\big)^2 = u_1^2(t) + 2 u_1(t) u_2(t) + u_2^2(t)
$$

For additivity we need $y_{12} = y_1 + y_2$:

$$
u_1^2 + 2 u_1 u_2 + u_2^2 \neq u_1^2 + u_2^2 \quad \text{(unless} \quad u_1 u_2 = 0\text{)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Additivity fails.

**Final Result:** The system $y = u^2$ is **nonlinear**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Finding Multiple Equilibrium Points

**Problem:** Find the equilibrium points of the nonlinear first-order system $\dot{x} = -x + x^3$.

**Step 1: Recall equilibrium condition.** A point $x_e$ is an equilibrium if $\dot{x}\vert_{x_e} = 0$ for all time under zero input.

**Step 2: Solve the algebraic equation.**

$$
0 = -x_e + x_e^3
$$

**Step 3: Factor.**

$$
x_e (x_e^2 - 1) = 0
$$

$$
x_e (x_e - 1)(x_e + 1) = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve.**

$$
x_e \in \{-1, \, 0, \, +1\}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system has **three** equilibrium points — a linear first-order system $\dot{x} = -x$ would have exactly **one**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The existence of multiple equilibrium points is a hallmark of nonlinearity. In a robot context, the inverted pendulum has two equilibria (upright unstable, hanging-down stable) — a linearized analysis at the upright position alone misses the global behavior.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linearization of a Nonlinear SISO System

**Problem:** Linearize the system $\dot{x} = \sin(x) + u$ about the equilibrium point $x_e = 0$, $u_e = 0$.

**Step 1: Verify equilibrium.** At $x_e = 0$, $u_e = 0$:

$$
\dot{x}\vert_{x_e,u_e} = \sin(0) + 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Jacobian.** For a scalar system:

$$
A = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x_e, u_e} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\big[\sin(x) + u\big]\bigg\vert_{0,0} = \cos(0) = 1
$$

$$
B = \frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\bigg\vert_{x_e, u_e} = \frac{\partial}{\partial u}\big[\sin(x) + u\big]\bigg\vert_{0,0} = 1
$$

**Step 3: Form the linearized model.**

Let $\delta x = x - x_e$ and $\delta u = u - u_e$:

$$
\delta \dot{x} = A \, \delta x + B \, \delta u = 1 \cdot \delta x + 1 \cdot \delta u
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** Near the origin, the nonlinear system behaves approximately like the linear system $\delta\dot{x} = \delta x + \delta u$. The pole is at $s = 1$, so the origin is **unstable** (linearized result).

**Step 5: Validity range.** The linearization error is bounded by the second-order Taylor term:

$$
f(x,u) - f(0,0) - A x - B u = \sin(x) - x \approx -\frac{x^3}{6}
$$

The error grows cubically, so the linearization is valid only for $\vert \delta x \vert \ll 1$.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Saturation Nonlinearity Step Response

**Problem:** A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$ and a saturating amplifier with $u_{max} = 1$ at the plant input. Find the steady-state error for a step input of magnitude $r = 2$.

**Step 1: Without saturation.** A unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$ is Type-1. For a step input, the steady-state error would be:

$$
e_{ss} = 0 \quad \text{(theoretically)}
$$

**Step 2: With saturation.** The control signal saturates at $\pm 1$. The plant receives at most $u = 1$. In steady state:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \dot{y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{constant output } y_{ss}
$$

For the output to be constant, the input to the plant must satisfy $sY(s) = 0$ in steady state, meaning the *unsaturated* control signal must become very small. The actual control signal is $u = \text{sat}(K_p e)$ where $K_p$ is the proportional gain.

**Step 3: Solve for steady-state error.** In steady state, $u_{ss} = 0$ (because $\dot{y} = 0$ for a Type-1 system in steady state requires zero plant input for constant $y$). But the system has reached a **nonzero steady-state error**:

$$
e_{ss} = r - y_{ss} \neq 0
$$

To find $y_{ss}$, integrate the saturated input. With $u_{max} = 1$, the maximum rate of change of $y$ during saturation is $1$ (from the integrator $1/s$). The system integrates at unit rate until $y$ approaches $r$, at which point the error becomes small enough that the proportional control falls below saturation.

**Final Result:** The system reaches a steady state with $e_{ss} > 0$ — saturation introduces a **nonzero steady-state error** that a linear analysis would have predicted to be zero. This is a classic nonlinear effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Robot Implication:** Robot joint actuators saturate at maximum torque. If a controller is designed ignoring saturation, the actual robot trajectory will lag the planned trajectory whenever the demanded torque exceeds the actuator limit — this is the so-called **windup** problem addressed by anti-windup schemes.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Linear vs Nonlinear Behavior Comparison

Consider the second-order nonlinear system:

$$
\ddot{y} + \dot{y} + y^3 = u(t)
$$

| Input $u(t)$ | Linearized System $\ddot{y} + \dot{y} + y = u$ (for $\vert y \vert$ small) | Nonlinear System $\ddot{y} + \dot{y} + y^3 = u$ (full range) |
|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| $u = \sin(\omega t)$, small $\omega$ | Output sinusoidal, frequency $\omega$, amplitude scales linearly with $\omega$ | Output contains fundamental $\omega$ and **third harmonic** $3\omega$ due to $y^3$ term |
| $u = 0$, initial condition $y(0) = 10$ | Oscillates forever (undamped imaginary poles? no — actually decays since $\ddot{y} + \dot{y} + y = 0$ has stable poles) — decay rate set by $-0.5 \pm j 0.866$ | Because $y^3$ grows faster than $y$, **large amplitude decay is faster than linear prediction** |
| Step $u = U_0$ large | Output settles at $y = U_0$ | Output settles at $y = \sqrt[3]{U_0}$, not $U_0$ |

The last row demonstrates a critical practical point: **the steady-state gain of a nonlinear system is input-dependent**. This is why describing function analysis approximates the nonlinearity by an input-dependent gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Nonlinearities — Mermaid Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SISO Nonlinearities"] --> B["Memoryless"]
    A --> C["Dynamic with Memory"]
    
    B --> B1["Saturation"]
    B --> B2["Dead Zone"]
    B --> B3["Relay On Off"]
    B --> B4["Relay with Hysteresis"]
    B --> B5["Coulomb Friction"]
    B --> B6["Square Law"]
    
    C --> C1["Backlash"]
    C --> C2["Hysteresis"]
    C --> C3["Transport Delay"]
    C --> C4["State Dependent Gain"]
    
    B1 --> APP1["Robot Motor Voltage Limit"]
    B2 --> APP2["Joint Static Friction"]
    B3 --> APP3["Bang Bang Control"]
    B4 --> APP4["Schmitt Trigger"]
    B5 --> APP5["Dry Friction Bearing"]
    B6 --> APP6["Aerodynamic Drag"]
    C1 --> APP7["Gear Transmission"]
    C2 --> APP8["Magnetic Hysteresis"]
    C3 --> APP9["Networked Control Delay"]
    C4 --> APP10["Inertia Varying with Angle"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a SISO Nonlinear Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference r t"] --> SUM1(("Sum"))
    SUM1 --> CTRL["Controller C s"]
    CTRL --> NL["Nonlinearity N . "]
    NL --> PLANT["Linear Plant G s"]
    PLANT --> Y["Output y t"]
    Y --> SUM1
    Y --> FB["Feedback Path H s"]
    FB --> SUM1
    
    NL -.->|"Acts on u_c"| PLANT
```

In this block diagram:
- $C(s)$ — linear controller (e.g., PID)
- $N(\cdot)$ — nonlinear element (saturation, dead zone, etc.)
- $G(s)$ — linear plant dynamics
- The nonlinearity $N(\cdot)$ sits **between** the controller and the plant

### 4.3 Concept Map — Linear vs Nonlinear System Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Does the system satisfy superposition?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> L["Linear System"]
    Q -- "No" --> NL["Nonlinear System"]
    
    L --> L1["Unique equilibrium"]
    L --> L2["Sinusoid in = sinusoid out"]
    L --> L3["Bode plot exists"]
    L --> L4["Stability is global"]
    
    NL --> N1["Multiple equilibria possible"]
    NL --> N2["Harmonics generated"]
    NL --> N3["Input dependent frequency response"]
    NL --> N4["Local and global stability"]
    NL --> N5["Limit cycles possible"]
    NL --> N6["Finite escape time possible"]
    NL --> N7["Chaos for order 3 or higher"]
    
    NL --> M1["Method 1 Phase Plane"]
    NL --> M2["Method 2 Describing Function"]
    NL --> M3["Method 3 Lyapunov Stability"]
    NL --> M4["Method 4 Feedback Linearization"]
    NL --> M5["Method 5 Sliding Mode Control"]
```

### 4.4 Phase Plane Concept Diagram — Limit Cycle Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IC["Initial Condition x0 y0"] --> TRAJ["Trajectory in x dot x plane"]
    TRAJ --> LC{"Does trajectory spiral?"}
    LC -- "Yes, inward" --> SS["Stable Focus Linear Behavior"]
    LC -- "Yes, outward" --> US["Unstable Focus Linear Behavior"]
    LC -- "No spirals to a closed curve" --> LC2["LIMIT CYCLE Sustained Oscillation"]
    LC -- "Multiple closed curves" --> LC3["Multiple Limit Cycles"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In a phase-plane plot of $\dot{x}$ vs $x$, trajectories that start from different initial conditions but all converge to the **same closed orbit** indicate a stable limit cycle. Linear systems cannot produce this behavior — it is purely nonlinear.

### 4.5 Robot Joint Actuator — Block Diagram with Nonlinearities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QD["Desired Position qd"] --> ERR(("Error e = qd - q"))
    ERR --> PID["PID Controller Kp Ki Kd"]
    PID --> SAT["Saturation Torque Limit"]
    SAT --> MOTOR["DC Motor"]
    MOTOR --> GEAR["Gearbox with Backlash"]
    GEAR --> LINK["Robot Link with Gravity and Friction"]
    LINK --> Q["Actual Position q"]
    Q --> ERR
    
    LINK --> FRIC["Coulomb and Viscous Friction"]
    FRIC -.->|"Opposes motion"| LINK
    
    LINK --> GRAV["Gravity Torque m g L sin q"]
    GRAV -.->|"State dependent"| LINK
```

This diagram captures the **realistic nonlinearities** present in a single robotic joint — saturation, backlash, friction, and gravity — that linear control design must contend with.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks):** Define a nonlinear control system. Give two examples of physical nonlinearities commonly encountered in robotic systems.

**Model Answer:**

A nonlinear control system is one whose governing differential equation **does not satisfy the principle of superposition** (homogeneity and additivity). Equivalently, the relationship between input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$ contains nonlinear operators such as products, powers, trigonometric functions, saturation, dead zone, or hysteresis.

**Two examples in robotic systems:**

1. **Motor torque saturation:** The DC motor driving a robot joint can deliver at most $\tau_{max}$ Newton-meters. For commanded torques $\tau_c > \tau_{max}$, the actual torque saturates at $\tau_{max}$ — a saturation nonlinearity.

2. **Gravity torque on a robot link:** The gravitational pull on a single-link manipulator is $\tau_g = m g L \sin(\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the joint angle. This is nonlinear because $\sin(\theta)$ depends on the joint angle (a state variable).

> **Question 2 (3 Marks):** State any three properties that distinguish nonlinear systems from linear systems.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Multiple equilibrium points:** Linear systems have a unique equilibrium; nonlinear systems can have several isolated equilibria or a continuum of equilibria.

2. **Limit cycles:** Nonlinear systems can exhibit sustained periodic oscillations of constant amplitude and frequency independent of initial conditions — a phenomenon impossible in linear systems (where oscillations decay or grow).

3. **Violation of superposition:** Nonlinear systems do not satisfy $T\{a u_1 + b u_2\} = a T\{u_1\} + b T\{u_2\}$, so the response to combined inputs cannot be predicted from individual responses.

*(Other acceptable properties: finite escape time, chaos for order $\geq 3$, input-dependent frequency response, generation of harmonics.)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks):**

> **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain with suitable examples any four common physical nonlinearities that appear in single-input robotic systems. For each, write the input–output mathematical expression and identify one practical cause.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Saturation Nonlinearity**

$$
u_{out} = \text{sat}(u_{in}) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & u_{in} > u_{max} \\ u_{in}, & \vert u_{in} \vert \le u_{max} \\ -u_{max}, & u_{in} < -u_{max} \end{cases}
$$
