- **Force-controlled polishing / deburring** where the tool must follow a curved surface with constant normal force.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)** where impedance control enables safe physical human interaction.
- **Surgical robotics** (e.g., da Vinci system) — the surgeon commands motion in camera coordinates.
- **Autonomous mobile manipulators** for grasping, where the object frame is the natural reference.

The unifying engineering benefit is that **task performance becomes a tunable gain-matrix design problem** in the space where the task actually lives.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Dynamics to Operational Space Dynamics

We start from the **Lagrangian joint dynamics** of an $n$-DoF manipulator:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the joint torque vector and $\mathbf{F}_{ext} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the external Cartesian wrench applied at the end-effector.

**Step A — Solve the differential kinematic equations for $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$.**

We begin with the first-order differential kinematics and differentiate it with respect to time to obtain the second-order relationship.

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time using the product rule:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\right]\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

The time derivative of the Jacobian depends on how $\mathbf{J}$ changes as the joints move. We write this explicitly:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \dot{\mathbf{J}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This is the standard **second-order differential kinematic relationship** that connects joint accelerations to Cartesian accelerations. The term $\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ represents a velocity-squared coupling that must be compensated for in any high-performance Cartesian controller.

**Step B — Substitute $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ into the joint dynamics.**

We invert the second-order kinematic relation to express joint accelerations in terms of Cartesian accelerations. Solving the previous equation for $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{J}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

We now substitute this expression for $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ into the joint dynamics equation. The joint inertia matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ multiplies the joint acceleration:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\left[\mathbf{J}^{-1}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step C — Pre-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{J}^{-T}(\mathbf{q})$.**

We pre-multiply by the inverse-transpose of the Jacobian, which is the standard projection operator that converts joint-space quantities into Cartesian-space quantities. This is the projection used in **Virtual Work / D'Alembert's principle**:

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{J}^{-T}\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step D — Define the operational space matrices.**

We now identify the standard operational space quantities. The **operational space inertia** is defined such that the inertial coupling in Cartesian space is captured directly:

$$
\mathbf{M}_x(\mathbf{q}) = \left[\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\right]^{-1}
$$

Using this definition, we can rewrite the inertia term. Note that pre-multiplying $\mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ gives:

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \mathbf{M}_x^{-1}
$$

because $\mathbf{M}_x^{-1} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{J}^T$ implies $(\mathbf{M}_x^{-1})^{-1} = (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1} = \mathbf{J}^{-T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1}$.

The **operational space Coriolis matrix** is defined as:

$$
\mathbf{C}_x(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \mathbf{M}_x(\mathbf{q})\,\left[\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - \dot{\mathbf{J}}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\right]\,\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})
$$

The **operational space gravity vector** is:

$$
\mathbf{g}_x(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{J}^{-T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step E — Write the operational space dynamic equation.**

Substituting these defined matrices, the complete **operational space equation of motion** becomes:

$$
\mathbf{M}_x(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{C}_x(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{g}_x(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}^* - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

Here $\mathbf{F}^* = \mathbf{J}^{-T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the **generalized force** that the joint actuators apply at the end-effector. This equation has the **same form as the joint-space dynamics** but is written entirely in Cartesian coordinates — which is exactly the form needed to design a Cartesian control law using classical linear control techniques.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)

RMRC is the **simplest and most popular** Cartesian scheme, originally proposed by Whitney (1969).

**Step 1 — Define the Cartesian position error.**

$$
\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{x}_d(t) - \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_d$ is the desired Cartesian position and $\mathbf{x}$ is the current position.

**Step 2 — Define the desired Cartesian velocity.**

We make the error decay exponentially with a single scalar rate $\alpha > 0$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) = -\alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse Jacobian to map Cartesian velocity to joint velocity.**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\left[\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t)\right]
$$

This is the **resolved motion rate control law**. For a stationary target ($\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d = 0$):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \alpha\,\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{e}(t)
$$

This is an **outer-loop position controller** — the inner joint servo tracks $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Resolved Motion Acceleration Control (RMAC)

RMRC omits the $\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ term, which leads to tracking errors during fast motions. RMAC eliminates this term by **feeding it forward**.

**Step 1 — Specify the desired Cartesian acceleration.**

Use a second-order error dynamics (PD law in Cartesian space):

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d^* = \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{K}_v\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\,\mathbf{e}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the actual Cartesian acceleration equal to the desired one.**

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d^*
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required joint acceleration from the differential kinematic equation.**

Recall that:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\mathbf{J}\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\left[\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the desired Cartesian acceleration.**

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\left[\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{K}_v\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\,\mathbf{e} - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]
$$

This is the **RMAC law**. The term $\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is the **centrifugal/Coriolis compensation** in Cartesian space. It is computed analytically or estimated numerically.

## 3.4 Derivation: Cartesian Impedance Control

The goal of impedance control is to make the end-effector behave as a **mass–spring–damper** with respect to the desired trajectory.

**Step 1 — Define the error in Cartesian space.**

$$
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d
$$

**Step 2 — Specify the desired impedance behaviour.**

The relationship between the external contact force $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ and the resulting Cartesian error is governed by a second-order linear differential equation:

$$
\mathbf{M}_x\,\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}_x\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_x\,\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

This is **exactly the equation of a 6-DoF mass-spring-damper** anchored at $\mathbf{x}_d$.

**Step 3 — Convert to a joint torque command.**

Solving for the desired Cartesian wrench $\mathbf{F}^*$:

$$
\mathbf{F}^* = \mathbf{M}_x\,\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}_x\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_x\,\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

Mapping to joint torques using the Jacobian transpose:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{J}^{-1}\!\left[\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}^*
$$

The gains $\mathbf{M}_x, \mathbf{B}_x, \mathbf{K}_x$ are user-specified and are the **engineering design knobs** of the system.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: 2-Link Planar RR Manipulator

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** Consider a 2-link planar RR manipulator with link lengths $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$, joint angles $q_1 = 30^\circ$, $q_2 = 45^\circ$. The task is to make the end-effector move with Cartesian velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = [0.1,\, 0.0]^T$ m/s. Compute the required joint velocities using RMRC.

**Step 1 — Forward kinematics.**

$$
x = L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian.**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

With $q_1=30^\circ$, $q_2=45^\circ$:

$$
s_1 = 0.5,\ c_1 = 0.8660,\ s_{12} = 0.9659,\ c_{12} = 0.2588
$$

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 - 0.5(0.9659) & -0.5(0.9659) \\ 0.8660 + 0.5(0.2588) & 0.5(0.2588) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Determinant.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-0.9830)(0.1294) - (-0.4830)(0.9954) = -0.1272 + 0.4808 = 0.3536
$$

Non-zero — configuration is **non-singular**.

**Step 4 — Inverse Jacobian.**

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.3536}\begin{bmatrix} 0.1294 & 0.4830 \\ -0.9954 & -0.9830 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3660 & 1.3659 \\ -2.8152 & -2.7799 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Required joint velocity.**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0366 \\ -0.2815 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{rad/s}
$$

Thus the manipulator should drive joint 1 at **0.0366 rad/s** and joint 2 at **−0.2815 rad/s** (the second joint must retract to allow the tool tip to move purely along the X-axis).

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Cartesian Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TaskSpec["Task Specification Xd t, Fd t"]
    CartError["Cartesian Error ex = Xd - X"]
    CartCtrlLaw["Cartesian Control Law F* = Kp ex + Kd exdot + Mx exddotdot"]
    InvJac["Inverse Jacobian J-1q"]
    FwdDyn["Forward Dynamics / Computed Torque"]
    Robot["Robot Manipulator Mq Cq g"]
    FwdKin["Forward Kinematics X = fq"]
    FKJac["Geometric Jacobian Jq"]
    ForceSensor["Force / Torque Sensor Fext"]
    SelectMatrix["Selection Matrix S"]

    TaskSpec --> CartError
    CartError --> CartCtrlLaw
    ForceSensor --> CartCtrlLaw
    CartCtrlLaw --> InvJac
    InvJac --> FwdDyn
    FwdDyn --> Robot
    Robot --> FwdKin
    Robot --> ForceSensor
    FwdKin --> CartError
    FwdKin --> FKJac
    FKJac --> InvJac
    SelectMatrix --> CartCtrlLaw
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["LEVEL 1 - Task Planning"] --> L2["LEVEL 2 - Cartesian Trajectory Generation"]
    L2 --> L3["LEVEL 3 - Operational Space Controller RMRC or RMAC or Impedance"]
    L3 --> L4["LEVEL 4 - Joint Servo Loops Current and Velocity"]
    L4 --> L5["LEVEL 5 - Mechanical Plant Robot Arm"]
    L5 --> L1

    L1 -- "Desired Xd, Fd" --> L2
    L2 -- "Xd(t), Xddotd(t)" --> L3
    L3 -- "Joint Torque tau" --> L4
    L4 -- "Motor Voltages" --> L5
    L5 -- "Encoder q, qdot" --> L1
    L5 -- "Force Fext" --> L3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TaskFrame["Task Space X Y Z Roll Pitch Yaw"]
        direction LR
        SF["Selection Matrix S = diag s1 ... s6"]
    end

    SF --> P["Position Controlled Directions si = 1"]
    SF --> FC["Force Controlled Directions si = 0"]

    P --> PC["PD Controller on Position Error"]

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q. Define Cartesian-based control. How is it different from joint-based control?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Cartesian-based control** is a robotic control strategy in which the desired motion and/or force of the end-effector is specified in the Cartesian (task) space coordinates $(X, Y, Z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$, and the controller internally maps these specifications to joint-level commands using the Jacobian and inverse kinematics/dynamics.
>
> **Key Differences:**
>
> | **Aspect** | **Joint-Based Control** | **Cartesian-Based Control** |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Control variable | Joint angles $\mathbf{q}$ | End-effector pose $\mathbf{x}$ |
> | User perspective | Motor-centric | Task-centric |
> | Force control | Indirect (joint torque) | Direct (Cartesian wrench) |
> | Computational load | Low | High (Jacobian + IK) |
> | Singularity | Not applicable | Critical issue |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q. What is the role of the Jacobian matrix in Cartesian-based control? Mention one major limitation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **fundamental bridge** between joint space and Cartesian space. It serves three key roles:
> 1. **Velocity mapping** — relates joint velocities to end-effector Cartesian velocities: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> 2. **Static force mapping** — converts end-effector forces to equivalent joint torques: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}$.
> 3. **Singularity analysis** — $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ identifies configurations where Cartesian control fails.
>
> **Major Limitation — Singularity:** Near singular configurations, the inverse Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ becomes ill-conditioned, and joint velocities tend to infinity for finite Cartesian velocities, causing loss of controllability and possible damage to the manipulator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question is **14 marks** and is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the **Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)** law for a robotic manipulator. State clearly the assumptions made and the role of the inverse Jacobian.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 2\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 1\,\text{m}$, with current joint angles $q_1 = 60^\circ$ and $q_2 = 30^\circ$. Using RMRC with rate $\alpha = 5\,\text{s}^{-1}$, compute the joint velocity commands when the desired Cartesian position is $\mathbf{x}_d = [2.5,\ 1.732]^T\,\text{m}$ and the current position is $\mathbf{x} = [2.366,\ 1.366]^T\,\text{m}$.  **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Define the Cartesian position error.**

$$
\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{x}_d(t) - \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Specify the desired decay of the error.**

For exponential convergence at rate $\alpha > 0$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) + \alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d(t) + \alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the differential kinematic relation.**

From the first-order kinematic mapping:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the joint velocity command.**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\left[\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d(t) + \alpha\,\mathbf{e}(t)\right]
$$

**Assumptions:**
- The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is square and non-singular (i.e., $m = n$ and $\det(\mathbf{J}) \neq 0$).
- The desired Cartesian trajectory $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$ is smooth and continuous.
- The inner joint servo loop can perfectly track the commanded joint velocity.

**Role of the Inverse Jacobian:** It **inverts the differential kinematic mapping**, converting the desired Cartesian velocity (or position error scaled by $\alpha$) into the joint-velocity space. It is the **mathematical engine** of the entire controller.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Forward kinematics of 2-link RR.**

$$
x = L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

Given $q_1 = 60^\circ$, $q_2 = 30^\circ$, so $q_1+q_2 = 90^\circ$.

Verify the current position:

$$
x = 2\cos 60^\circ + 1\cos 90^\circ = 2(0.5) + 1(0) = 1.0\,\text{m}
$$

$$
y = 2\sin 60^\circ + 1\sin 90^\circ = 2(0.866) + 1(1) = 2.732\,\text{m}
$$

> (Note: If the values in the problem differ from this verification, students should **state the position error as given** in the problem and proceed.)

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian.**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

With $s_1 = 0.866,\ c_1 = 0.5,\ s_{12} = 1.0,\ c_{12} = 0$:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -2(0.866) - 1(1) & -1(1) \\ 2(0.5) + 1(0) & 1(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.732 & -1.0 \\ 1.0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the determinant.**

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-2.732)(0) - (-1.0)(1.0) = 1.0
$$

Non-singular ✓.

**Step 4 — Inverse Jacobian.**

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.0}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.0 \\ -1.0 & 2.732 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.0 \\ -1.0 & 2.732 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Cartesian error.**

$$
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 - 1.0 \\ 1.732 - 2.732 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -1.0 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply RMRC (with $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d = 0$).**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \alpha\,\mathbf{e} = 5\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -1.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.5 \\ -5.0 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Joint velocity command.**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.0 \\ -1.0 & 2.732 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 7.5 \\ -5.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -5.0 \\ -21.15 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Result:** $\dot{q}_1 = -5.0\,\text{rad/s}$, $\dot{q}_2 = -21.15\,\text{rad/s}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Cartesian Impedance Control** with the relevant equations. Discuss how the choice of $\mathbf{M}_x$, $\mathbf{B}_x$, $\mathbf{K}_x$ shapes the robot's behaviour.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 1-DoF prismatic joint with mass $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ and viscous friction $b = 0.5\,\text{Ns/m}$, design a Cartesian impedance controller with desired inertia $M_x = 1\,\text{kg}$, damping $B_x = 8\,\text{Ns/m}$, and stiffness $K_x = 16\,\text{N/m}$. The desired position is $x_d = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and an external force $F_{ext} = 4\,\text{N}$ is applied at the end-effector. Find the **steady-state position** of the end-effector.  **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** Cartesian impedance control regulates the **dynamic relationship** between the end-effector's position error and the external contact force, making the manipulator behave as a programmable 6-DoF **mass–spring–damper** in task space.

**Governing Equation:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_x\,\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}_x\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_x\,\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

where $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$.

**Interpretation of Gains:**

| **Gain Matrix** | **Effect** | **Physical Meaning** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{M}_x$ ↑ | Slower acceleration response | Heavier virtual mass |
| $\mathbf{B}_x$ ↑ | More damping | Suppresses oscillations faster |
| $\mathbf{K}_x$ ↑ | Smaller steady-state error | Stiffer (less compliant) behaviour |

**Engineering Design Heuristics:**
- **High stiffness** $\mathbf{K}_x$ → position accuracy, suitable for free-space motion.
- **Low stiffness** $\mathbf{K}_x$ → compliance, suitable for contact tasks (assembly, polishing).
- **Critical damping** requires $B_x = 2\sqrt{M_x K_x}$.

**Controller Implementation:** The desired wrench is:

$$
\mathbf{F}^* = \mathbf{M}_x\,\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}_x\,\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_x\,\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

and the joint torque command is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{J}^{-1}\!\left[\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right] + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g} + \mathbf{J}^T\,\mathbf{F}^*
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Steady-state condition.**

At steady state, $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} = 0$ and $\dot{\mathbf{e}} = 0$. The impedance equation reduces to:

$$
\mathbf{K}_x\,\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the steady-state error.**

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{F_{ext}}{K_x} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the steady-state position.**

$$
x_{ss} = x_d + e_{ss} = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75\,\text{m}
$$

**Result:** The end-effector settles at $x_{ss} = 0.75\,\text{m}$ under the 4 N external force.

> **Note on Stability:** The transient response is governed by:
>
> $$
> \ddot{e} + 8\dot{e} + 16e = \frac{F_{ext}}{M_x} = 4
> $$
>
> The natural frequency is $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_x/M_x} = \sqrt{16/1} = 4\,\text{rad/s}$ and the damping ratio is $\zeta = B_x/(2\sqrt{M_x K_x}) = 8/(2\cdot 4) = 1.0$ — **critically damped** — so the response reaches steady state without oscillation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Cartesian Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ term** — In RMAC derivations, students often write $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\ddot{\mathbf{x}}$ which is **mathematically wrong**. The correct expression is $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\left[\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}\right]$. Loss: 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ with $\mathbf{J}^T$** — The inverse Jacobian is used for **velocity** mapping; the transpose is used for **force** mapping. Mixing them is a common error.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the singularity check** — In numerical problems, students compute $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ without verifying $\det(\mathbf{J}) \neq 0$. KTU examiners specifically check this.
>
> 4. **Writing the wrong error direction** — Some texts define $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$ (e.g., impedance control) while others use $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x}$ (e.g., RMRC). Be **consistent** and state the convention explicitly.
>
> 5. **Unit mismatches in numerical answers** — Translational gains must be in N/m and N·s/m, rotational gains in Nm/rad and Nm·s/rad. Mixing units is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cartesian-Based Control Systems**

- ✅ **Cartesian control = task-space control** — specified in $(X, Y, Z, R_x, R_y, R_z)$, executed in joint space.
- ✅ **Jacobian** is the *bridge* between joint and Cartesian space — used for both velocity ($\mathbf{J}$) and force ($\mathbf{J}^T$) mapping.
- ✅ **RMRC** law: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}[\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \alpha \mathbf{e}]$ — simplest, position-based.
- ✅ **RMAC** law: $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}[\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{K}_v\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}]$ — eliminates the $\dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ coupling.
- ✅ **Operational space inertia** is $\mathbf{M}_x = [\mathbf{J}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{J}^T]^{-1}$, **not** simply $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^T$.
- ✅ **Impedance control** is a 6-DoF mass-spring-damper: $\mathbf{M}_x\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}_x\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_x\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}$.
- ✅ **Hybrid control** uses a **selection matrix** $\mathbf{S}$ to switch between position and force control along orthogonal directions.
- ✅ **Singularities** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ — use the **damped least-squares inverse** $\mathbf{J}^\dagger = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T + \lambda^2\mathbf{I})^{-1}$ to avoid infinite joint velocities.
- ✅ **Steady-state error under constant force** in impedance control: $e_{ss} = F_{ext}/K_x$.
- ✅ **Critically damped** impedance response: $B_x = 2\sqrt{M_x K_x}$.
- ✅ **Joint torque command** for full Cartesian decoupling uses the operational-space model: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{J}}\dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g} + \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}^*$.
- ✅ **Advantages:** task-intuitive specification, decoupled force/position control, suitable for contact tasks.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** computationally expensive, sensitive to singularities, requires accurate kinematic and dynamic models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Control** is an advanced feedback control strategy in which the controller parameters are **automatically and continuously adjusted** in real time so that the closed-loop response of a system with **unknown, time-varying, or slowly drifting dynamics** tracks the behavior of a prescribed reference model or optimizer.

In the context of **Robot Dynamics and Control**, adaptive control is employed because the rigid-body dynamics of a manipulator are functions of:

- **Payload mass and inertia** (which change with the grasped object),
- **Joint friction** (Coulomb and viscous terms that vary with temperature and wear),
- **Centrifugal and Coriolis coupling** (which vary nonlinearly with joint velocity),
- **Gear compliance and backlash** in transmissions.

Because the **Lagrangian parameters** $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$ in the manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ cannot be measured exactly at runtime, an adaptive controller "learns" these parameters online.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two principal paradigms dominate the KTU syllabus:**
> 1. **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** — output tracks a stable reference model.
> 2. **Self-Tuning Regulators (STR)** — explicit estimation (e.g., Recursive Least Squares) of parameters followed by controller redesign.

A third, simpler technique, **Gain Scheduling**, is also covered as a precursor where operating-point-dependent gains are pre-computed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a driver who has **never driven a heavy truck before**. The steering is loose, the brakes are slow, and the steering-wheel response changes with road conditions (wet, dry, gravel, snow).

- A **conventional (fixed-gain) PID controller** is like a driver who learned on a small car and applies the same steering and brake inputs to the truck — the truck will overshoot, oscillate, or fail to track the road.
- An **adaptive controller** is like a driver who **continuously recalibrates** his hand movements: at every instant, he observes the deviation between where the truck actually goes and where he wanted it to go (the *tracking error*), and quietly updates his internal mental model of the truck's response.

In robotics: when a manipulator picks up a heavy payload, the joint "feels heavier." An adaptive controller detects the increased tracking error and automatically **increases the effective gain** to compensate, exactly as a human driver would tighten the steering on a windy highway.

## 1.3 Why Adaptive Control is Needed for Robots

| Phenomenon | Effect on Dynamics | Why Fixed Gains Fail |
|---|---|---|
| Variable payload | Inertia matrix $M(q)$ changes | Old gains become too sluggish or unstable |
| Joint friction drift | New disturbance torque | Steady-state error grows |
| Link flexibility | Unmodeled high-frequency modes | Conventional robust margins are exceeded |
| Actuator saturation | Effective gain drops | Tracking deteriorates under high speed |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key engineering metrics used in adaptive control design:**
> - **Adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$** — controls how fast parameters adapt (typical range: **0.1 to 100**).
> - **Tracking error $e(t)$** — deviation between plant and reference model output.
> - **Parameter error $\phi(t) = \theta(t) - \theta^{*}$** — difference between current and ideal controller parameters.
> - **$\sigma$-modification constant $\sigma$** — small positive scalar used to prevent *parameter drift* (typical value: **0.01 to 0.1**).
> - **Lyapunov function $V(e, \phi)$** — scalar energy-like function whose time derivative must be **negative semi-definite** for stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Adaptive Control Schemes

Adaptive controllers are classified into three families. Each addresses a different assumption about what is unknown.

### 2.1.1 Gain Scheduling
- **Idea:** Pre-compute controller gains as a function of a measured *scheduling variable* (e.g., joint angle, velocity, altitude).
- **Architecture:** Lookup table or interpolation in operating point space.
- **Pros:** Simple, no online estimation, fast response.
- **Cons:** Open-loop adaptation; no feedback on actual performance; cannot react to unmeasured disturbances.
- **Use in robotics:** Often used in flight control and as a baseline for trajectory-dependent gain tuning in industrial arms.

### 2.1.2 Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)
- **Idea:** A **stable reference model** specifies the *desired* closed-loop response. The adaptation mechanism adjusts controller gains so the plant output $y_p(t)$ asymptotically tracks the model output $y_m(t)$.
- **Core components:** Reference model, plant, control law, **adaptation mechanism**.
- **Pros:** Direct performance specification, well-developed theory (Lyapunov, Popov, Passivity).
- **Cons:** Sensitive to measurement noise; can suffer parameter drift.

### 2.1.3 Self-Tuning Regulators (STR)
- **Idea:** A **parameter estimator** (Recursive Least Squares, Kalman Filter, etc.) identifies plant parameters online. A **controller design block** then recomputes control gains (e.g., pole-placement, LQG) using the latest estimates.
- **Pros:** Conceptually modular; estimator and designer are decoupled.
- **Cons:** Estimation convergence and closed-loop stability are not automatically guaranteed (the *certainty equivalence principle* is an assumption, not a theorem).

## 2.2 Architecture of the MRAC System

The block-level architecture of a canonical MRAC system has four interacting subsystems:

1. **Plant** with unknown or varying parameters.
2. **Reference Model** $\dot{x}_m = A_m x_m + B_m r$, where $A_m$ is **Hurwitz** (all eigenvalues in the open left half-plane).
3. **Adjustable Controller** $u = \theta^T(t) \, \omega(t)$ where $\omega$ is the regressor vector and $\theta(t)$ is the parameter vector.
4. **Adaptation Mechanism** driven by a stability or optimization criterion (MIT rule, Lyapunov, etc.).

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The "why" and "how" behind every step of MRAC are:

- **Step 1 — Define the tracking error.** $e = x_p - x_m$. This is the immediate measure of performance loss.
- **Step 2 — Express the closed-loop dynamics in terms of parameter error $\phi = \theta - \theta^*$.** When $\theta = \theta^*$, the plant plus controller becomes identical to the reference model, so $e \to 0$.
- **Step 3 — Choose a candidate Lyapunov function $V(e,\phi)$** that is positive definite in both $e$ and $\phi$.
- **Step 4 — Compute $\dot{V}$** along the trajectories of the error system. This reveals an unknown term involving $\dot{\phi}$.
- **Step 5 — Cancel the cross term** by setting $\dot{\phi}$ equal to a function of $e$ and $\omega$. This is the *adaptive law*.
- **Step 6 — Verify that $\dot{V} \le 0$** (negative semi-definite). By **Barbashin–Krasovskii** or **LaSalle's invariance principle**, this guarantees boundedness of $e$ and $\phi$ and asymptotic convergence of $e$ to zero.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **must-know equations** for KTU 2024 examinations on Adaptive Control.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reference Model (LTI) | $\dot{x}_m = A_m x_m + B_m r$ | $A_m$ **Hurwitz** (eigenvalues in LHP) |
| 2 | Plant (uncertain) | $\dot{x}_p = A_p x_p + B_p u$ | $A_p, B_p$ partially/totally unknown |
| 3 | Control Law | $u = \theta^T(t) \, \omega(t)$ | $\omega$ is regressor vector |
| 4 | Tracking Error | $e = x_p - x_m$ | State error vector |
| 5 | Parameter Error | $\phi = \theta - \theta^{*}$ | $\theta^{*}$ = ideal parameter vector |
| 6 | Error Dynamics | $\dot{e} = A_m e + B_m \phi^T \omega$ | Closed form after substitution |
| 7 | MIT Rule (scalar) | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | $\gamma > 0$ adaptation gain |
| 8 | MIT Rule (vector) | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \phi_{\text{sens}}$ | $\phi_{\text{sens}}$ = sensitivity derivative |
| 9 | Lyapunov Function | $V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^T P e + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma} \phi^T \phi$ | $P > 0$ solves $A_m^T P + P A_m = -Q$ |
| 10 | Lyapunov Adaptive Law | $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma \, e \, \omega$ | Gradient-descent-like update |
| 11 | $\sigma$-Modification | $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma e \omega - \sigma \phi$ | $\sigma > 0$ prevents drift |
| 12 | Normalized Adaptive Law | $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma \dfrac{e \, \omega}{1 + \omega^T \omega}$ | Improves robustness to noise |
| 13 | Lyapunov Stability (sufficient) | $\dot{V} \le 0$ | $\Rightarrow$ bounded $e,\phi$ |
| 14 | Robot Adaptive Control Law | $\tau = \hat{M}(q)\ddot{q}_d + \hat{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d + \hat{G}(q) + K_d \tilde{\dot{q}} + K_p \tilde{q}$ | $\tilde{q} = q - q_d$ |
| 15 | RLS Update | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \, \varepsilon(k)$ | Used in STR estimators |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula sheet, **never** write $\vert e \vert$ inside a table cell — it breaks markdown parsing. Use $\lvert e \rvert$ or $\mid e \mid$ instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial manipulators** (KUKA, ABB): MRAC handles varying payloads in pick-and-place.
- **Aerospace**: Gain-scheduled controllers in aircraft over wide flight envelopes.
- **Autonomous vehicles**: STR with RLS for online road-tire friction estimation.
- **Surgical robots**: Adaptive compensation for patient-specific tissue interaction.
- **Quadrotors and drones**: MRAC compensates for battery sag and mass shift as fuel/battery depletes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The MIT Rule

The **MIT Rule** is the historical starting point of adaptive control (Massachusetts Institute of Technology, 1960s). It treats parameter adaptation as a *gradient descent* on a cost function that measures the squared tracking error.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a first-order plant with a single adjustable gain:

$$\dot{y}_p = -a_p \, y_p + b_p \, u$$

where $u = \theta \, y_p$ is the control law, and $\theta$ is the adjustable parameter. The reference model is:

$$\dot{y}_m = -a_m \, y_m + b_m \, r$$

with $a_m > 0$ for stability.

### 3.1.2 Cost Function

The MIT rule defines a loss:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} \, e^2(t)$$

where $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$. The parameter $\theta$ is updated in the direction of steepest descent of $J$:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$$

### 3.1.3 Compute the Sensitivity Derivative

By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = e \cdot \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$$

(The reference model $y_m$ does not depend on $\theta$.)

To find $\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$, differentiate the plant equation with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{\partial \dot{y}_p}{\partial \theta} = -a_p \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} + b_p \, \frac{\partial (\theta y_p)}{\partial \theta}$$

Applying the product rule to $\theta y_p$:

$$\frac{\partial (\theta y_p)}{\partial \theta} = y_p + \theta \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$$

Substitute:

$$\frac{\partial \dot{y}_p}{\partial \theta} = -a_p \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} + b_p \, y_p + b_p \, \theta \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$$

Group the $\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$ terms:

$$\frac{\partial \dot{y}_p}{\partial \theta} = (b_p \theta - a_p) \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} + b_p \, y_p$$

This is itself a linear ODE. If $\theta$ is approximately equal to its ideal value (slow adaptation assumption), the closed-loop pole is approximately $-a_m$, so the transfer function from $b_p y_p$ to $\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$ is approximately $\frac{1}{s + a_m}$. In the steady state, $s = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} \approx \frac{b_p}{a_m} \, y_p$$

This is the **sensitivity function** of the output with respect to the parameter.

### 3.1.4 The Final MIT Rule

Plugging back:

$$\boxed{\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{b_p}{a_m} \, y_p}$$

In practice, $b_p$ and $a_m$ are absorbed into the adaptation gain $\gamma'$:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma' \, e \, y_p$$

> [!NOTE]
> The MIT rule is **heuristic** — it does not by itself guarantee stability. The Lyapunov approach below is mathematically rigorous.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lyapunov-Based Adaptive Control

### 3.2.1 Plant and Reference Model

Consider the scalar first-order system:

$$\dot{x}_p = a_p \, x_p + b \, u, \qquad b > 0$$

where $a_p$ is unknown. The reference model is:

$$\dot{x}_m = -a_m \, x_m + b \, r, \qquad a_m > 0$$

### 3.2.2 Control Law with Two Adjustable Parameters

Let the control law be:

$$u = \theta_1 \, x_p + \theta_2 \, r$$

Substitute into the plant:

$$\dot{x}_p = a_p \, x_p + b \, (\theta_1 x_p + \theta_2 r) = (a_p + b\theta_1) x_p + b\theta_2 r$$

### 3.2.3 Identify the Ideal Parameters

For perfect tracking, we require $\dot{x}_p = \dot{x}_m$ and $x_p = x_m$ for all $r$. This gives:

$$a_p + b\theta_1^{*} = -a_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1^{*} = \frac{-(a_m + a_p)}{b}$$

$$b\theta_2^{*} = b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2^{*} = 1$$

### 3.2.4 Define the Errors

Tracking error:

$$e = x_p - x_m$$

Parameter error vector:

$$\phi = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 - \theta_1^{*} \\ \theta_2 - \theta_2^{*} \end{bmatrix}$$

Regressor vector:

$$\omega = \begin{bmatrix} x_p \\ r \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2.5 Error Dynamics

Differentiate $e$:

$$\dot{e} = \dot{x}_p - \dot{x}_m = (a_p + b\theta_1) x_p + b\theta_2 r - (-a_m) x_m - b r$$

Substitute $\theta_i = \theta_i^{*} + \phi_i$:

$$\dot{e} = (a_p + b\theta_1^{*} + b\phi_1) x_p + (b\theta_2^{*} + b\phi_2) r + a_m x_m - b r$$

Since $\theta_1^{*}, \theta_2^{*}$ make the closed-loop match the reference:

$$\dot{e} = a_m e + b\phi_1 x_p + b\phi_2 r = a_m e + b \, \phi^T \omega$$

### 3.2.6 Lyapunov Function Candidate

Choose a positive definite function:

$$V(e, \phi) = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{b}{2\gamma} \, \phi^T \phi, \qquad \gamma > 0$$

This is positive definite in $(e, \phi)$ and radially unbounded — both required for a valid Lyapunov function.

### 3.2.7 Compute the Time Derivative

$$\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{b}{\gamma} \, \phi^T \dot{\phi}$$

Substitute $\dot{e} = a_m e + b \phi^T \omega$:

$$\dot{V} = e(a_m e + b \phi^T \omega) + \frac{b}{\gamma} \, \phi^T \dot{\phi}$$

$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + b \, e \, \phi^T \omega + \frac{b}{\gamma} \, \phi^T \dot{\phi}$$

Factor out the $\phi^T$ terms:

$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + b \, \phi^T \left( e \, \omega + \frac{1}{\gamma} \dot{\phi} \right)$$

### 3.2.8 Choose the Adaptive Law to Cancel the Cross Term

Set the bracketed term to zero:

$$e \, \omega + \frac{1}{\gamma} \dot{\phi} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\phi} = -\gamma \, e \, \omega$$

This is the **Lyapunov adaptive law**. Component-wise:

$$\dot{\phi}_1 = -\gamma \, e \, x_p, \qquad \dot{\phi}_2 = -\gamma \, e \, r$$

### 3.2.9 Resulting $\dot{V}$

With this choice, the cross term vanishes:

$$\boxed{\dot{V} = a_m e^2 \le 0}$$

since $a_m > 0$ and $e^2 \ge 0$.

### 3.2.10 Stability Conclusion

Because $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} \le 0$:

- $V(t)$ is non-increasing, hence bounded below by 0.
- $e(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ are bounded for all $t \ge 0$.
- By **Barbashin–Krasovskii** (or LaSalle's invariance principle), $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, i.e., asymptotic tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 exam questions on adaptive control. Practice writing it from scratch.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Application to a 1-DOF Robot

Consider a single revolute joint with unknown inertia $J$ and viscous friction $B$:

$$J \, \ddot{q} + B \, \dot{q} = \tau$$

Define $x_1 = q$, $x_2 = \dot{q}$. In state-space form:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -B/J \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/J \end{bmatrix} \tau$$

Let $a = -B/J$ and $b = 1/J$. Choose a reference model with desired damping $-a_m$:

$$\dot{x}_m = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -a_m \end{bmatrix} x_m + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ b \end{bmatrix} r$$

Use feedback linearization control law:

$$\tau = \theta_1 \, x_2 + \theta_2 \, r$$

Following the same Lyapunov procedure, the adaptive law is:

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma \, e_2 \, x_2, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma \, e_2 \, r$$

where $e_2 = x_2 - x_{m,2}$ is the velocity error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MRAC Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

The diagram below illustrates the **canonical MRAC architecture** with all four subsystems and the two feedback paths: one for the tracking error driving the adaptation law, and one for the closed-loop plant output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reference Input r] --> A2[Reference Model]
    A2 --> A3[Reference Output ym]
    B1[Plant Input u] --> B2[Plant with Unknown Parameters]
    B2 --> B3[Plant Output yp]
    A3 --> C1[Summing Junction]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Tracking Error e]
    C2 --> D1[Adaptation Mechanism]
    D1 --> D2[Controller Parameters theta]
    D2 --> E1[Adjustable Controller u equals thetaT omega]
    A1 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    E1 --> B1

    style A2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style B2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style D1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style E1 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C2 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
```

**Reading guide:**
- Blue node = **Reference Model** (specifies the desired dynamics).
- Orange node = **Plant** (the actual robotic system with uncertain $M,C,G$).
- Purple node = **Adaptation Mechanism** (Lyapunov law / MIT rule).
- Green node = **Adjustable Controller** (real-time gain computation).
- Red node = **Tracking Error** $e = y_p - y_m$.

## 4.2 Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Reference r] --> F2[Controller Design Block]
    F3[Plant] --> F4[Output y]
    F1 --> F3
    F4 --> F5[Sensor]
    F5 --> F6[Parameter Estimator RLS]
    F5 --> F2
    F6 --> F7[Estimated Parameters theta hat]
    F7 --> F2
    F2 --> F8[Computed Control u]
    F8 --> F3

    style F3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F6 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style F2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Reading guide:**
- The estimator and the controller are **decoupled** (modular).
- The estimator runs in parallel with the controller, both receiving the same measurement $y$.

## 4.3 Adaptive Robot Manipulator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Trajectory Generator qd qddotd] --> G2[Reference Model]
    G1 --> G3[Regressor Matrix Y]
    G4[Measured q qdot] --> G3
    G3 --> G5[Adaptive Update Law]
    G4 --> G2
    G2 --> G6[Tracking Error e]
    G4 --> G6
    G6 --> G5
    G5 --> G7[Parameter Estimates Mhat Chat Ghat]
    G7 --> G8[Computed Torque tau]
    G1 --> G8
    G8 --> G9[Robot Manipulator]
    G9 --> G4

    style G2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style G9 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style G5 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style G8 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **regressor matrix $Y(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q})$** is the cornerstone of adaptive robot control. It allows the unknown parameters (masses, inertias, friction coefficients) to appear *linearly* in the dynamic equation, enabling clean adaptive updates.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and Self-Tuning Regulators (STR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MRAC | STR |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference Model** | Explicit reference model defines desired response. | No explicit reference model; performance is implicit via pole-placement or LQG. |
| **Adaptation Driver** | Tracking error $e = y_p - y_m$ directly drives the adaptive law. | Parameter estimation error drives an inner-loop controller redesign. |
| **Architecture** | Coupled: adaptation and control are integrated. | Modular: estimator and controller are separate blocks. |
| **Stability Analysis** | Lyapunov / passivity theory gives rigorous proofs. | Stability is not automatic; relies on certainty equivalence. |
| **Tuning** | Adaptation gain $\gamma$ and reference model $A_m$. | Estimator forgetting factor, controller design weights. |

*(Award 3 marks: 1 for tabular distinction, 1 for driver of adaptation, 1 for stability implication.)*

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the MIT rule? State the equation and explain the role of the adaptation gain $\gamma$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The MIT rule is a gradient-descent-based parameter update law. Given a cost function $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2$ where $e = y_p - y_m$:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \approx -\gamma \, e \, \phi_{\text{sens}}$$

- **Role of $\gamma$:** The adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$ sets the *speed* of parameter learning. A larger $\gamma$ produces faster convergence but may cause oscillations or instability; a smaller $\gamma$ yields slow, smooth adaptation at the cost of tracking performance during transients.
- *Trade-off:* Robustness vs. agility. (3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for role, 1 for trade-off.)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an internal choice. The two alternatives below are independent. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Derive the Lyapunov-based adaptive law** for a first-order plant with unknown parameter $a_p$:
$$\dot{x}_p = a_p \, x_p + b \, u, \qquad b > 0, \quad a_m > 0$$
with reference model $\dot{x}_m = -a_m \, x_m + b \, r$ and control law $u = \theta_1 x_p + \theta_2 r$. Clearly show the choice of $V$, the computation of $\dot{V}$, the cancellation step, and the final adaptive law.

**(b)** [7 marks] **State and explain** the role of the $\sigma$-modification in adaptive control. Write the modified adaptive law and discuss its effect on robustness and steady-state tracking.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Lyapunov Adaptive Law Derivation** [7 marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Identify ideal parameters.**
For perfect matching, the closed-loop plant must equal the reference model:
$$a_p + b\theta_1^{*} = -a_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1^{*} = -\frac{(a_m + a_p)}{b}$$
$$b\theta_2^{*} = b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2^{*} = 1$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Define errors.**
$$e = x_p - x_m, \qquad \phi_i = \theta_i - \theta_i^{*}, \qquad \omega = [x_p, \, r]^T$$

**Step 3 [1.5 marks] — Derive error dynamics.**
Substitute the control law into the plant:
$$\dot{x}_p = a_p x_p + b\theta_1 x_p + b\theta_2 r = (a_p + b\theta_1) x_p + b\theta_2 r$$
Substitute $\theta_i = \theta_i^{*} + \phi_i$ and use the matching conditions:
$$\dot{x}_p = -a_m x_p + b\phi_1 x_p + b\phi_2 r$$
Subtract $\dot{x}_m = -a_m x_m + b r$:
$$\dot{e} = -a_m(x_p - x_m) + b\phi_1 x_p + b\phi_2 r - b r = a_m e + b\phi^T \omega$$

**Step 4 [1.5 marks] — Choose Lyapunov function and compute $\dot{V}$.**
$$V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{b}{2\gamma} \phi^T \phi, \qquad \gamma > 0$$
$$\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{b}{\gamma} \phi^T \dot{\phi} = e(a_m e + b \phi^T \omega) + \frac{b}{\gamma} \phi^T \dot{\phi}$$
$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + b\phi^T\left(e \omega + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\phi}\right)$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Choose adaptive law to cancel the cross term.**
Set the bracket to zero:
$$\dot{\phi} = -\gamma \, e \, \omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma e x_p, \quad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma e r$$

**Step 6 [1 mark] — Conclude stability.**
With this law, $\dot{V} = a_m e^2 \le 0$. By LaSalle's invariance principle, $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, and $\phi, e$ remain bounded for all $t \ge 0$. $\blacksquare$

#### **Part (b) — $\sigma$-Modification** [7 marks]

**Statement [2 marks]:** In the presence of unmodeled dynamics, disturbances, or persistent excitation loss, the standard adaptive law $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma e \omega$ can cause $\phi$ to drift unboundedly even when $e \to 0$. This is called **parameter drift**. The **$\sigma$-modification** (Ioannou & Kokotovic, 1984) adds a leakage term:

$$\dot{\phi} = -\gamma \, e \, \omega - \sigma \, \phi, \qquad \sigma > 0$$

**Effect on $\dot{V}$ [2 marks]:** With the modified law:
$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + b\phi^T(e \omega + \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\phi}) = a_m e^2 - \tfrac{b\sigma}{\gamma} \phi^T \phi \le 0$$
So $\dot{V}$ is now negative semi-definite, and the leakage term $-\sigma\phi$ ensures that $\phi$ remains bounded even without persistent excitation.

**Trade-off [2 marks]:**
- *Pro:* Robust to disturbances, prevents drift, well-suited to practical implementation.
- *Con:* The parameter estimate converges only to a *ball* around the true value (not exactly to it). Tracking error has a small steady-state residual proportional to $\sigma / \gamma$.

**Practical guidance [1 mark]:** Choose $\sigma$ small (typically 0.01–0.1) so that drift is suppressed without sacrificing tracking accuracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Explain the architecture of an MRAC system** with the help of a labeled block diagram. Describe the function of each block and the role of the **adaptation mechanism** in detail.

**(b)** [7 marks] **For a robotic manipulator** with dynamics $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$, explain how **adaptive control** can be used to handle unknown payload inertia. Write the **computed torque control law** with adaptive parameter updates and the corresponding **regressor formulation**.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — MRAC Architecture** [7 marks]

**Block diagram description [3 marks]:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)
- **Reference Model:** LTI system $W_m(s)$ that specifies the desired closed-loop behavior. Must be **stable** ($A_m$ Hurwitz).
- **Plant:** System with unknown or time-varying parameters (the robot).
- **Adjustable Controller:** Gains $\theta(t)$ that shape the plant's input.
- **Adaptation Mechanism:** Algorithm that adjusts $\theta(t)$ in real time using tracking error $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$.

**Functional flow [2 marks]:**
1. Reference input $r(t)$ is fed both to the reference model (generating $y_m$) and the plant.
2. The tracking error $e$ is computed and passed to the adaptation mechanism.
3. The adaptation mechanism updates $\theta$ using either MIT rule or Lyapunov law.
4. The updated $\theta$ modifies the controller, which then generates a new control $u$ for the plant.

**Role of the adaptation mechanism [2 marks]:**
- Converts the scalar error $e(t)$ into a vector update $\dot{\theta}(t)$.
- The update law must ensure closed-loop stability (Lyapunov) and parameter convergence (persistent excitation).
- Decouples the *desired performance* (encoded in the reference model) from the *parameter convergence* (encoded in the adaptation gain).

#### **Part (b) — Adaptive Robot Control** [7 marks]

**Computed torque baseline [2 marks]:** For the manipulator $M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$, the standard computed-torque control law is:
$$\tau = M(q)({\ddot{q}_d} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + K_p \tilde{q}) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$
where $\tilde{q} = q - q_d$. When $M, C, G$ are unknown, replace them with estimates $\hat{M}, \hat{C}, \hat{G}$:
$$\tau = \hat{M}(q)({\ddot{q}_d} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + K_p \tilde{q}) + \hat{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + \hat{G}(q)$$

**Regressor formulation [3 marks]:** Because the dynamics are *linear in the parameters*, we can write:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = Y(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q}) \, a$$
where $Y(\cdot) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is the **regressor matrix** and $a \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the unknown parameter vector (masses, inertias, COM offsets, friction coefficients). The control law becomes:
$$\tau = \hat{M}(q)\ddot{q}_r + \hat{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_r + \hat{G}(q) + K_d s = Y(q,\dot{q},\dot{q}_r,\ddot{q}_r) \hat{a} + K_d s$$
where $s = \dot{\tilde{q}} + \Lambda \tilde{q}$ is the **sliding-variable-like filtered error** and $\dot{q}_r = \dot{q}_d - \Lambda \tilde{q}$, $\ddot{q}_r = \ddot{q}_d - \Lambda \dot{\tilde{q}}$.

**Adaptive update law [2 marks]:**
$$\dot{\hat{a}} = -\Gamma \, Y^T(q,\dot{q},\dot{q}_r,\ddot{q}_r) \, s, \qquad \Gamma = \Gamma^T > 0$$

The matrix $\Gamma$ is the **adaptation gain matrix** (positive definite, diagonal in practice). The closed-loop error dynamics become $\dot{\hat{a}} = -\Gamma Y^T s$ with the Lyapunov function:
$$V = \frac{1}{2} s^T M(q) s + \frac{1}{2} \tilde{a}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{a}$$
which can be shown to be non-increasing, ensuring $s \to 0$ and bounded $\hat{a}$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundedness of $\phi$:** Many students prove $e \to 0$ but forget to mention that $\phi$ is *bounded* (a separate consequence of $\dot{V} \le 0$).
> 2. **Wrong sign in adaptive law:** The law must be $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma e \omega$, *not* $+\gamma e \omega$. Sign error gives instability.
> 3. **MIT rule presented as "stable":** The MIT rule is a heuristic. It does **not** guarantee stability. Only Lyapunov-based (or passivity-based) laws do. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for over-claiming MIT rule stability.
> 4. **No mention of $A_m$ Hurwitz:** The reference model must be stable. Forgetting this costs a mark in Part (a) of derivations.
> 5. **Regressor confusion:** Students write $Y$ as a vector when it is a matrix, or vice versa. Be explicit about dimensions.
> 6. **Mixing STR and MRAC:** A common slip is to claim STR has an explicit reference model. It does not.
> 7. **Units in tables:** KTU 2024 expects SI units where applicable. For dimensionless adaptation gain $\gamma$, write "dimensionless" or "s$^{-1}$" depending on normalization.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Adaptive Control**

- **Adaptive control = real-time parameter tuning** to track a reference model (MRAC) or maintain an optimal cost (STR) when plant parameters are unknown or vary.
- **Three families:** Gain Scheduling (offline-lookup), MRAC (online, error-driven), STR (online, estimator-based).
- **MRAC building blocks:** Plant + Reference Model + Adjustable Controller + Adaptation Mechanism.
- **MIT Rule:** $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$. Heuristic, fast, but no stability guarantee.
- **Lyapunov Adaptive Law:** Choose $V = \frac{1}{2} e^T P e + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\phi^T \phi$, compute $\dot{V}$, cancel cross term → $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma e \omega$, conclude $\dot{V} \le 0$.
- **$\sigma$-Modification:** $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma e \omega - \sigma \phi$ with $\sigma > 0$. Prevents parameter drift at the cost of a small residual error.
- **Normalized Law:** $\dot{\phi} = -\gamma \frac{e \omega}{1 + \omega^T \omega}$ improves noise robustness.
- **Robot Adaptive Control:** Uses *linear-in-parameter* property → regressor matrix $Y(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q})$.
- **Computed torque with adaptation:** $\tau = Y \hat{a} + K_d s$, $\dot{\hat{a}} = -\Gamma Y^T s$.
- **Stability conditions:** Reference model Hurwitz; adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$; persistent excitation needed for parameter convergence.
- **Application domains:** Variable-payload manipulators, drones, autonomous vehicles, surgical robots, flight control.
- **Common exam traps:** Sign error in adaptive law, MIT rule stability overclaim, missing regressor dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit Cycle - Describing Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT523 - ROBOT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limit Cycle - Describing Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Limit Cycle — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIMIT CYCLE (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **limit cycle** is an *isolated* closed trajectory (periodic orbit) in the phase plane of an autonomous nonlinear dynamical system, such that all nearby trajectories either spiral *toward* it (stable), *away from* it (unstable), or approach from one side and recede from the other (semi-stable). Unlike the closed orbits of linear conservative systems (which exist in continuous families), limit cycles are **isolated**, making them a uniquely nonlinear phenomenon.

Mathematically, for an autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, a limit cycle is a non-constant periodic solution $\mathbf{x}^{*}(t)$ such that $\mathbf{x}^{*}(t+T) = \mathbf{x}^{*}(t)$ for some period $T > 0$, and no other closed trajectory exists in an open neighborhood of it.

### 1.2 Describing Function — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DESCRIBING FUNCTION (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **describing function** $N(A, \omega)$ is a *quasi-linear* frequency-domain approximation of a nonlinear element. It is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental Fourier component of the steady-state output $y(t)$ to the applied sinusoidal input $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, under the **filtering hypothesis** that the linear part of the system attenuates all harmonics above the fundamental.

$$N(A, \omega) = \frac{Y_1}{A}\, e^{j\phi_1}$$

where $Y_1$ is the amplitude and $\phi_1$ is the phase shift of the fundamental harmonic of the output.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**Limit Cycle Analogy — "The Swinging Clock Pendulum":**
Imagine a grandfather clock pendulum. It does not converge to a static equilibrium at the bottom of its swing (which is what a *linear* damped system would do), nor does it fly off to infinity. Instead, it settles into a **perfectly steady, self-sustaining oscillation** at one specific amplitude and frequency. Surround this steady swing with a slightly smaller or larger push — the smaller one grows *toward* the steady amplitude, and the larger one shrinks *toward* it. This steady, attracting oscillation is a **stable limit cycle**. If you gave the pendulum a perfect push every cycle (energy input exactly matching losses), you would see the same phenomenon in countless engineering systems — robot manipulator joints hunting around a setpoint, tire squeal, or heart-rate pacemaker circuits.

**Describing Function Analogy — "The Distorted Singer in a Concert Hall":**
Picture a singer who cannot hit the exact note and produces a slightly *clipped* or *saturated* waveform — the output is distorted. A describing function says: *"Don't try to compute the entire distorted waveform. Just pretend the singer is singing a pure tone of the same frequency, but with a slightly different effective amplitude and a small phase lag."* That effective complex gain is the describing function. It lets engineers use the powerful machinery of linear Bode/Nyquist plots (which they already understand) on essentially nonlinear problems.

### 1.4 Highlight Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Highlight — Why Limit Cycles Matter in Robotics:**
> In robotic manipulators, joint servo systems driven by saturation amplifiers, backlash in gear trains, and Coulomb friction in bearings all generate nonlinear oscillations called **hunting**. These manifest physically as limit cycles — the robot joint dithers around the desired position without ever settling. KTU questions frequently test whether students can *predict* whether a given nonlinear feedback system will produce hunting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constant / Metric Used:**
> - **Filtering Hypothesis** — the implicit "constant" assumption: the linear plant $G(s)$ must have a low-pass character so $\vert G(jn\omega)\vert \ll \vert G(j\omega)\vert$ for $n \geq 2$. This is **almost always satisfied** for robot joint servos because of inherent electrical and mechanical low-pass filtering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Limit Cycles (Three Types)

| Type | Behavior of Nearby Trajectories | Stability Property | Engineering Reality |
|------|-------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Stable (Attracting)** | Spiral *inward* toward the cycle | Asymptotically stable | Sustained oscillation, e.g., clock pendulum, robot joint hunting |
| **Unstable (Repelling)** | Spiral *outward* away from the cycle | Unstable | Boundary between convergent and divergent behavior |
| **Semi-stable** | Approach from one side, recede from the other | Half-stable | Rare; bifurcation boundary case |

### 2.2 Why Linear Theory Fails — The Fundamental Reason

Linear systems obey the **Principle of Superposition**, which forbids sustained bounded oscillations of a unique amplitude. A linear second-order system $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ produces oscillations that either **decay** ($c > 0$), **grow** ($c < 0$), or **persist forever without damping** ($c = 0$, but this requires infinite precision). Limit cycles of *unique* amplitude and frequency can only be generated by **nonlinearity**.

### 2.3 Derivation Outline of the Describing Function

Consider a memoryless (static) nonlinearity $y = f(x)$ driven by $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$. The output $y(t)$ is a periodic function with period $T = 2\pi/\omega$. Expand it in Fourier series:

$$y(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t)\right]$$

The **fundamental component** is:

$$y_1(t) = a_1 \cos(\omega t) + b_1 \sin(\omega t) = Y_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)$$

where:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} f(A\sin\theta)\cos\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{\omega}, \quad b_1 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} f(A\sin\theta)\sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{\omega}$$

Then:

$$Y_1 = \sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2}, \qquad \phi_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a_1}{b_1}\right)$$

The describing function is:

$$N(A) = \frac{Y_1}{A}\, e^{j\phi_1} = \frac{b_1}{A} + j\frac{a_1}{A}$$

For **odd-symmetric** nonlinearities (which produce no DC offset and no even harmonics for sinusoidal input), $a_1$ reduces to a simpler form, and $N(A)$ depends only on $A$, not $\omega$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Nonlinearity | Describing Function $N(A)$ | Key Parameter |
|---|--------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Relay** (signum) | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | Relay output $\pm M$ |
| 2 | **Relay with Dead Zone** ($\pm\Delta$) | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}$ for $A > \Delta$; else $0$ | Dead zone width $\Delta$ |
| 3 | **Saturation** (linear slope $K$, saturation level $\pm S$) | $N(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{A}\right) + \dfrac{S}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{S}{A}\right)^2}\right]$ for $A > S$; else $K$ | Saturation level $S$ |
| 4 | **Dead Zone** (slope $K$, dead zone $\pm\Delta$) | $N(A) = K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right) + \dfrac{2K\Delta}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}$ for $A > \Delta$; else $0$ | Dead zone $\Delta$ |
| 5 | **Coulomb Friction** | $N(A) = \dfrac{4F_c}{\pi A}$ (with $-90^\circ$ phase) | Friction force $F_c$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Limit-Cycle Equation (KTU's Most-Tested Formula):**
> $$N(A) \cdot G(j\omega) = -1$$
> Split into magnitude and phase:
> $$\vert N(A)\vert \cdot \vert G(j\omega)\vert = 1$$
> $$\angle N(A) + \angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$$

### 2.5 Stability of the Predicted Limit Cycle

Once a candidate $(A_0, \omega_0)$ satisfies $N(A_0)\,G(j\omega_0) = -1$, test stability by checking the encirclement of the critical point $-1/N(A)$ as $A$ varies. A **simpler, equivalent KTU rule** uses the **negative reciprocal of $N(A)$**:

$$\text{Stable limit cycle} \iff -\frac{1}{N(A)} \text{ is NOT encircled by the } G(j\omega)\text{ locus as } A \text{ increases through } A_0$$

Equivalently, if $\dfrac{d}{dA}\big[\,N(A)\,G(j\omega)\,\big]$ at the crossing point lies in the right-half of the complex plane, the limit cycle is **stable**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Robotics

In robotic joint servo design, the describing function method lets the control engineer:
1. **Predict hunting oscillations** in gear-driven joints before fabrication.
2. **Quantify amplitude and frequency** of limit cycles for stability margin assessment.
3. **Compare compensator designs** (PID, lead-lag, state-feedback) for nonlinear suppression.
4. **Diagnose field failures** where linear theory gives wrong predictions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Describing Function for an Ideal Relay

**Setup:** An ideal relay outputs $+M$ when $x > 0$ and $-M$ when $x < 0$ (no dead zone, instantaneous switching). Input: $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the output waveform.**

For $\sin(\omega t) > 0$ (i.e., $0 < \omega t < \pi$), the output is $+M$. For $\sin(\omega t) < 0$ ($\pi < \omega t < 2\pi$), the output is $-M$.

**Step 2 — Compute Fourier coefficients.**

Because the output is odd-symmetric (a square wave), $a_n = 0$ for all $n$ and only $b_n$ are non-zero:

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\theta)\sin\theta\, d\theta$$

Substitute $y(\theta) = +M$ on $(0, \pi)$ and $y(\theta) = -M$ on $(\pi, 2\pi)$:

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} M\sin\theta\, d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\, d\theta\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} M\sin\theta\, d\theta = M[-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = M\left[-(-1) - (-1)\right] = 2M$$

$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\, d\theta = -M[-\cos\theta]_{\pi}^{2\pi} = -M\left[-1 - 1\right] = 2M$$

**Step 4 — Sum and normalize.**

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}(2M + 2M) = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Form the describing function.**

Since $a_1 = 0$, the describing function has zero imaginary part (purely real, zero phase lag):

$$N(A) = \frac{b_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The ideal relay's DF is purely real and **amplitude-dependent only** — it decreases as $1/A$. This is why it always produces limit cycles when placed in any loop with a phase lag exceeding $0^\circ$.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Describing Function for a Relay with Dead Zone

**Setup:** Output $= +M$ when $x > \Delta$, $-M$ when $x < -\Delta$, and $0$ otherwise. Input: $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ with $A > \Delta$.

**Step 1 — Determine switching angles.**

The relay switches ON at $\omega t = \alpha$ where $A\sin\alpha = \Delta$, so:

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)$$

The relay switches OFF at $\omega t = \pi - \alpha$.

**Step 2 — Compute $b_1$ by integrating only over the active region.**

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} M\sin\theta\, d\theta = \frac{M}{\pi}[-\cos\theta]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}$$

$$= \frac{M}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi-\alpha) + \cos\alpha\right] = \frac{M}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\right] = \frac{2M}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\alpha} = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/A)^2}$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{2M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Form the describing function.**

$$\boxed{\,N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\,, \quad A > \Delta\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** At $A = \Delta$, the square root vanishes — meaning the DF is zero. For $A < \Delta$, the relay never switches and $N(A) = 0$ (the loop effectively opens). This non-monotonic behavior is **the single most common KTU exam trap**: students assume DF always decreases with $A$, but the dead-zone relay DF first *rises* from zero, peaks at $A = \Delta\sqrt{2}$, then falls.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Predicting a Limit Cycle in a Robot Servo

**Problem:** A position-control robot joint has a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ and an ideal relay (no dead zone, $M = 1$) in the forward path. Predict the limit cycle, if any.

**Step 1 — Write the limit-cycle equation.**

$$N(A)\cdot G(j\omega) = -1$$

Substitute $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A} = \dfrac{4}{\pi A}$ (since $M = 1$):

$$\frac{4}{\pi A}\cdot \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)} = -1$$

**Step 2 — Separate magnitude and phase conditions.**

Magnitude:

$$\frac{4}{\pi A}\cdot \frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+4}} = 1$$

Phase:

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$$

$$\Rightarrow -\left[90^\circ + \tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2)\right] = -180^\circ$$

$$\Rightarrow 90^\circ + \tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 180^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Solve the phase equation.**

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 90^\circ$$

Using $\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A\tan B}$:

$$\tan 90^\circ \to \infty \implies 1 - \omega\cdot(\omega/2) = 0 \implies \omega^2 = 2 \implies \omega_0 = \sqrt{2}\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the magnitude equation.**

Substitute $\omega_0 = \sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{40}{\pi A \cdot \sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{3}\cdot\sqrt{6}} = 1$$

$$\sqrt{\omega_0^2+1} = \sqrt{3}, \quad \sqrt{\omega_0^2+4} = \sqrt{6}$$

Product: $\omega_0 \cdot \sqrt{3}\cdot \sqrt{6} = \sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{18} = \sqrt{36} = 6$.

$$\therefore \frac{40}{6\pi A} = 1 \implies A_0 = \frac{40}{6\pi} = \frac{20}{3\pi} \approx 2.122$$

**Step 5 — State the predicted limit cycle.**

The robot joint will exhibit sustained oscillations (hunting) of amplitude $A_0 \approx 2.122$ rad at frequency $\omega_0 = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ rad/s. The oscillation is a **stable limit cycle** because $N(A)$ decreases monotonically with $A$ for the ideal relay (no dead zone), so an increase in $A$ reduces the loop gain magnitude below unity — pushing the system back toward the cycle.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Action | Formula / Condition | Output |
|------|--------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | Identify nonlinearity type | From block diagram | $f(x)$ specified |
| 2 | Compute Fourier coefficients | $a_1, b_1$ integrals | Numerical expressions |
| 3 | Form $N(A)$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{b_1 + j a_1}{A}$ | Complex-valued function of $A$ |
| 4 | Write $G(j\omega)$ | Substitute $s = j\omega$ | Magnitude and phase |
| 5 | Solve phase equation | $\angle N + \angle G = -180^\circ$ | $\omega_0$ |
| 6 | Solve magnitude equation | $\vert N(A)\vert\cdot\vert G(j\omega_0)\vert = 1$ | $A_0$ |
| 7 | Stability test | Sign of $\dfrac{d}{dA}[\vert N G\vert]$ at $A_0$ | Stable / Unstable |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phase-Plane Portrait — The Three Limit-Cycle Types

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the qualitative phase-plane behavior of trajectories around each limit-cycle type. Node IDs are alphanumeric per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Trajectory starts at amplitude A_small]) --> innerStable
    innerStable[Spiral trajectory inside the limit cycle] --> convergeStable((Stable Limit Cycle))
    startA -. small perturbation .-> divergeUnstable((Unstable Limit Cycle))
    divergeUnstable --> outwardUnstable[Spiral outward to infinity]
    startB[Trajectory starts far outside] --> outerStable[Spiral inward]
    outerStable --> convergeStable
    semiStable1[Trajectory approaching from outside] --> semiCycle{{Semi-stable Limit Cycle}}
    semiStable1 -- repelled --> awaySemi[Spiral away from outside]
    semiStable1 -- attracted --> semiCycle
    semiStable2[Trajectory approaching from inside] -- attracted --> semiCycle
    semiStable2[Trajectory from inside] -- attracted --> semiCycle
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Describing Function Analysis Loop

The following block diagram maps the analytical flow when applying the Describing Function method to a closed-loop nonlinear robotic joint servo. Each block represents a computational stage; arrows show data and signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[R(t) - Reference Input] --> summingJunction{{Summing Junction}}
    disturbNode[D(t) - Disturbance] --> summingJunction
    summingJunction -- error e(t) --> nonlinBlock[NL - Nonlinear Element e.g. Relay, Saturation]
    nonlinBlock -- y(t) --> linBlock[G(s) - Linear Plant e.g. Robot Joint Dynamics]
    linBlock -- c(t) --> sensorBlock[H(s) - Feedback Sensor]
    sensorBlock --> summingJunction
    nonlinBlock -. Fourier analysis .-> dfCalcNode[F1 - Fundamental Component Extraction]
    dfCalcNode --> dfOutput[N(A) - Describing Function Output]
    linBlock -. Frequency response .-> bodeNode[F2 - Magnitude and Phase Plot of G j omega]
    dfOutput --> limitEqNode[F3 - Limit Cycle Equation N(A) G j omega equals -1]
    bodeNode --> limitEqNode
    limitEqNode --> resultNode[Result - Predicted Limit Cycle Amplitude A0 and Frequency omega0]
```

### 4.3 Nyquist Interpretation — Finding the Limit Cycle Graphically

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 - Plot the polar locus of G(j omega) for omega from 0 to infinity]
    step2[Step 2 - Plot the locus of negative reciprocal of N(A) for A from 0 to infinity]
    step3[Step 3 - Locate the intersection point of the two loci]
    step4[Step 4 - Read off the coordinates of the intersection]
    step4 --> aRead[A0 - read at the A value of the -1/N curve]
    step4 --> wRead[omega0 - read at the omega value of the G curve]
    step5[Step 5 - Test stability by perturbing A]
    step5 --> stableCheck{Direction of G locus as A increases through intersection}
    stableCheck -- not encircled --> stableOut[Stable Limit Cycle]
    stableCheck -- encircled --> unstableOut[Unstable Limit Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Limit Cycle Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1{Does an isolated closed orbit exist in the phase plane?}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> noLC[No Limit Cycle - System is asymptotically stable or unstable]
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{How do neighboring trajectories behave?}
    nodeQ2 -- spiral inward --> nodeStable[Stable Limit Cycle - Sustained oscillation]
    nodeQ2 -- spiral outward --> nodeUnstable[Unstable Limit Cycle - Boundary of stability region]
    nodeQ2 -- one side in, other out --> nodeSemi[Semi-stable Limit Cycle - Bifurcation point]
    nodeQ2 -- converge from both sides --> nodeAttract[Stable Attractor - Common in robot hunting]
    nodeQ2 -- diverge from both sides --> nodeRepel[Unstable Repeller - Critical gain threshold]
```
