## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A.1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Define the term "limit cycle" in the context of nonlinear control systems. How does it differ from a linear system's oscillatory behavior?**

**Model Answer:**

A **limit cycle** is an isolated closed trajectory in the phase plane of a nonlinear autonomous system such that all nearby trajectories either converge to it (stable), diverge from it (unstable), or exhibit mixed behavior (semi-stable). It represents a sustained periodic oscillation of *unique* amplitude and frequency.

| Property | Linear System Oscillation | Nonlinear Limit Cycle |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Amplitude | Depends on initial conditions | **Fixed** (independent of IC) |
| Existence | Continuous family or none | **Isolated** trajectory |
| Stability | Marginally stable (purely conservative) | Asymptotically stable or unstable |
| Caused by | Energy conservation (no damping) | **Nonlinearity + filtering** |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison)**

---

**Q.A.2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]`

**What is a describing function? State the key assumption (filtering hypothesis) on which its validity rests.**

**Model Answer:**

The **describing function** $N(A)$ is a quasi-linear approximation of a nonlinear element, defined as the ratio of the fundamental Fourier component of the output to the input sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$. Mathematically:

$$N(A) = \frac{1}{A}\left[b_1 + j a_1\right]$$

where $a_1, b_1$ are the Fourier coefficients of the fundamental output component.

**Key Assumption — Filtering Hypothesis:** The linear part of the closed-loop system must attenuate all higher harmonics ($n \geq 2$) of the nonlinear output so strongly that they have negligible effect on the system response. Formally, $\vert G(jn\omega)\vert \ll \vert G(j\omega)\vert$ for $n \geq 2$. This is satisfied by robot joint servos due to inherent low-pass mechanical and electrical dynamics.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for filtering hypothesis with justification)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**A robotic manipulator joint has a relay-with-dead-zone nonlinearity in its feedback loop. The plant transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. The relay parameters are $M = 2$ and $\Delta = 1$.**

**(a)** Derive the describing function of the relay-with-dead-zone nonlinearity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the describing function method, determine whether a limit cycle exists, and if so, find its amplitude $A_0$ and frequency $\omega_0$. Also comment on its stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Write the input and switching conditions.**

Input: $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$. The relay outputs $+M$ when $x > \Delta$, $-M$ when $x < -\Delta$, and $0$ otherwise. Define the switching angle $\alpha$ where $A\sin\alpha = \Delta$:

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right), \quad A > \Delta$$

**Step 2 — Compute Fourier coefficient $b_1$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} M\sin\theta\, d\theta = \frac{M}{\pi}\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}$$

$$= \frac{M}{\pi}\bigl[-\cos(\pi-\alpha) + \cos\alpha\bigr] = \frac{2M}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/A)^2}$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{2M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Form the describing function.**

Since the nonlinearity is odd-symmetric, $a_1 = 0$, so:

$$\boxed{\,N(A) = \frac{b_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\,, \quad A > \Delta\,}$$

For $A \leq \Delta$, the relay never switches and $N(A) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Insert numerical values $M = 2$, $\Delta = 1$.**

$$N(A) = \frac{8}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A^2}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for integral evaluation, 2 for final DF with numbers)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Write the limit-cycle equation.**

$$N(A)\cdot G(j\omega) = -1$$

$$\frac{8}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A^2}}\cdot \frac{5}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)} = -1$$

**Step 2 — Phase equation.**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/5) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega/2) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/5) = 90^\circ$$

Using the tangent addition identity:

$$1 - \frac{\omega}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega}{5} = 0 \implies \frac{\omega^2}{10} = 1 \implies \omega_0 = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude equation at $\omega_0 = \sqrt{10}$.**

$$\vert G(j\omega_0)\vert = \frac{5}{\omega_0\sqrt{\omega_0^2+4}\sqrt{\omega_0^2+25}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{14}\cdot\sqrt{35}}$$

$$\sqrt{14}\cdot\sqrt{35} = \sqrt{490} = 7\sqrt{10}$$

$$\vert G(j\omega_0)\vert = \frac{5}{\sqrt{10}\cdot 7\sqrt{10}} = \frac{5}{70} = \frac{1}{14}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_0$.**

$$\frac{8}{\pi A_0}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A_0^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{14} = 1$$

$$\frac{8}{14\pi A_0}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A_0^2}} = 1$$

$$\frac{4}{7\pi A_0}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{A_0^2}} = 1$$

Let $u = A_0^2$. Then:

$$\frac{4}{7\pi}\sqrt{\frac{u-1}{u}} = \sqrt{u}$$

$$\frac{16}{49\pi^2}\cdot\frac{u-1}{u} = u$$

$$\frac{16(u-1)}{49\pi^2} = u^2$$

$$49\pi^2 u^2 - 16 u + 16 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$u = \frac{16 \pm \sqrt{256 - 4\cdot 49\pi^2 \cdot 16}}{2\cdot 49\pi^2} = \frac{16 \pm \sqrt{256(1 - 49\pi^2)}}{98\pi^2}$$

Since $49\pi^2 \approx 483.6 \gg 1$, the discriminant is negative — **no real solution exists**, meaning no limit cycle is predicted.

**Step 5 — Stability & interpretation.**

Because no real $A_0 > \Delta = 1$ satisfies the equation, the system has **no limit cycle** for the given relay parameters. The joint servo is either asymptotically stable or unstable depending on the linear part's gain margin; the dead zone has suppressed oscillation entirely.

**(7 marks: 2 phase, 3 magnitude, 2 conclusion + stability)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]`

**Consider a saturation-type nonlinearity in a robot joint position control loop. The linear plant is $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+a)}$ where $K$ and $a$ are positive real constants. The saturation has linear slope $K_s$ in the active region and saturates at output level $\pm S$.**

**(a)** Derive the describing function of the saturation nonlinearity for $A > S$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If $K = 10$, $a = 1$, $K_s = 2$, and $S = 1$, determine the limit cycle amplitude $A_0$ and frequency $\omega_0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the saturation characteristic.**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} K_s A\sin\omega t, & A\sin\omega t \leq S \\ K_s S \cdot\text{sgn}(\sin\omega t), & A\sin\omega t > S \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the saturation angle.**

Let $\beta = \sin^{-1}(S/A)$. For $A > S$, the output follows the linear law for $\theta \in [0, \beta) \cup (\pi - \beta, \pi]$ and saturates on $[\beta, \pi - \beta]$.

**Step 3 — Compute $b_1$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\beta} K_s A\sin^2\theta\, d\theta + \int_{\beta}^{\pi-\beta} K_s S\sin\theta\, d\theta + \int_{\pi-\beta}^{\pi} K_s A\sin^2\theta\, d\theta\right]$$

By symmetry, the first and third integrals are equal:

$$2\int_{0}^{\beta} K_s A\sin^2\theta\, d\theta = K_s A\!\left[\beta - \sin\beta\cos\beta\right]$$

The middle integral:

$$\int_{\beta}^{\pi-\beta} K_s S\sin\theta\, d\theta = K_s S\,[-\cos\theta]_{\beta}^{\pi-\beta} = 2K_s S\cos\beta$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\left\{K_s A(\beta - \sin\beta\cos\beta) + 2K_s S\cos\beta\right\}$$

With $\sin\beta = S/A$ and $\cos\beta = \sqrt{1-(S/A)^2}$:

$$b_1 = \frac{K_s}{\pi}\left\{A\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{A}\right) - S\sqrt{1-\frac{S^2}{A^2}} + 2S\sqrt{1-\frac{S^2}{A^2}}\right\}$$

$$= \frac{K_s}{\pi}\left\{A\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{A}\right) + S\sqrt{1-\frac{S^2}{A^2}}\right\}$$

**Step 5 — Form $N(A)$.**

Since $a_1 = 0$ (odd-symmetric output):

$$\boxed{\,N(A) = \frac{2K_s}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{A}\right) + \frac{S}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{S}{A}\right)^2}\right]\,}$$

For $A \leq S$, $N(A) = K_s$ (linear region, no saturation).

**(7 marks: 2 setup, 3 integration, 2 final form)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Substitute numerical parameters into $G(j\omega)$ and $N(A)$.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)}, \qquad N(A) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{A^2}}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Phase equation.**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) = 90^\circ \implies \omega \to \infty$$

This indicates that $\omega_0 \to \infty$, which is non-physical. Therefore, **no finite-frequency limit cycle** can exist for this plant with these parameters. The system instead settles to a static equilibrium (asymptotically stable behavior), since saturation at high amplitudes effectively reduces the loop gain below unity for all finite $\omega$.

**Step 3 — Numerical verification.**

At any finite $\omega$, $\angle G = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\omega$ which is always in $(-180^\circ, -90^\circ)$. Since $N(A)$ is purely real (zero phase, odd-symmetric saturation), the phase sum $\angle N + \angle G = \angle G$ can never reach $-180^\circ$. Hence no limit cycle exists.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

For $K = 10$, $a = 1$, $K_s = 2$, $S = 1$, the robot joint is **asymptotically stable with no limit cycle**. The saturation acts as an automatic gain-reducer at large amplitudes, preventing self-sustaining oscillation.

**(7 marks: 2 setup, 3 phase analysis, 2 conclusion)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Often Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the filtering hypothesis.** A describing function analysis is *only* valid when the linear plant has low-pass character. Stating this assumption explicitly at the start of your answer can earn 1–2 easy marks and demonstrates examiner awareness.
>
> 2. **Confusing the limit-cycle equation sign.** Students write $N(A)G(j\omega) = +1$ instead of $-1$. Recall: $G$ is in the forward path with **negative feedback**; closing the loop around a negative sign flips the sign. Always draw the block diagram first.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the $a_1$ imaginary component for non-odd-symmetric nonlinearities.** Hysteresis and biased relays produce a non-zero $a_1$. Computing only $b_1$ (real part) yields a wrong DF and a wrong limit cycle prediction.
>
> 4. **Stability misjudgment.** Finding $(A_0, \omega_0)$ is only half the answer. KTU boards *require* an explicit stability statement using either the encirclement criterion or the monotonicity test on $N(A)$.
>
> 5. **Units of $\omega_0$.** Always state $\omega_0$ in **rad/s** and $A_0$ in the **same units as the input** (e.g., radians for joint angle). Mixing degrees with radians or Hz with rad/s costs marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **MUST-KNOW Bullet Points Before the Exam:**
>
> - [ ] **Limit cycle** = isolated closed orbit in phase plane; **unique amplitude & frequency**, unlike linear oscillations.
> - [ ] Three types: **stable** (spiral in), **unstable** (spiral out), **semi-stable** (one-sided).
> - [ ] **Describing function** $N(A) = \dfrac{Y_1}{A}e^{j\phi_1}$ — fundamental component ratio.
> - [ ] **Filtering hypothesis**: linear plant must attenuate harmonics $\geq 2$.
> - [ ] **Limit-cycle equation**: $N(A)\cdot G(j\omega) = -1$.
> - [ ] **Phase** gives $\omega_0$; **magnitude** gives $A_0$.
> - [ ] **Ideal relay DF**: $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ — purely real, decreasing in $A$.
> - [ ] **Relay-with-dead-zone DF**: $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-(\Delta/A)^2}$ — non-monotonic, peaks at $A = \Delta\sqrt{2}$.
> - [ ] **Saturation DF**: $\dfrac{2K_s}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}(S/A) + (S/A)\sqrt{1-(S/A)^2}\right]$ — approaches $K_s$ as $A \to S^+$.
> - [ ] **Stability test**: Encirclement of $-1/N(A)$ by $G(j\omega)$ locus, or sign of $\dfrac{d}{dA}[NG]$.
> - [ ] **Always state** units ($A$ in input units, $\omega$ in rad/s).
> - [ ] **Always state** filtering hypothesis explicitly in derivations.
> - [ ] Robot hunting in joint servos = direct physical manifestation of stable limit cycles.
> - [ ] Dead-zone nonlinearities can **suppress** limit cycles — used deliberately in some anti-hunting circuits.
> - [ ] Saturation is **self-stabilizing** — limits amplitude growth, often prevents limit cycles.
